question: Bellman–Ford relaxes every edge V−1 times. Why exactly V−1?

choices.1: Relaxing fewer than V−1 times risks integer overflow when summing negative weights
choices.2: The algorithm must traverse all V−1 spanning-tree edges exactly once to guarantee correctness
choices.3: Each edge can only be safely relaxed once per vertex, and there are V−1 vertices other than the source
choices.4: A shortest path in a graph of V vertices uses at most V−1 edges

explanation: So V−1 passes give every shortest path the chance to fully form.

question: After the V−1 relaxation passes, Bellman–Ford runs one additional pass. What does it mean if some distance still decreases during this extra pass?

choices.1: The graph is disconnected, so some vertices remain unreachable
choices.2: The earlier passes terminated early and simply need to be repeated
choices.3: The source vertex was chosen incorrectly and the algorithm must restart
choices.4: A negative cycle reachable from the source exists

explanation: So no well-defined shortest path exists for the affected vertices.

question: What distinguishes Bellman–Ford from Dijkstra's algorithm?

choices.1: It correctly handles negative edge weights and can detect reachable negative cycles
choices.2: It computes all-pairs shortest paths instead of single-source
choices.3: It runs faster on dense graphs because it avoids a priority queue
choices.4: It still finds an optimal path even when edge weights change during execution, unlike Dijkstra's greedy approach

question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: O((V + E) log V)
choices.2: O(V²)
choices.3: O(V · E)
choices.4: O(E log V)

question: The implementation tracks an `updated` flag and breaks out early when a full pass makes no changes. What does this optimization accomplish?

choices.1: It lowers the worst-case complexity to O(E log V)
choices.2: It is required for correctness; without it, negative cycles could not be detected
choices.3: It guarantees the algorithm finishes in at most O(V) passes regardless of graph shape
choices.4: It lets typical graphs settle and terminate early, but the worst case stays O(V · E)

question: Relaxation is the algorithm's only primitive. For a directed edge u → v of weight w, when is dist[v] lowered?

choices.1: When dist[u] is not infinite and dist[u] + w < dist[v]
choices.2: When dist[v] + w < dist[u], regardless of whether dist[u] is finite
choices.3: Whenever w is negative, since a negative edge always shortens a path
choices.4: When v has not yet been touched during the current pass

question: What invariant holds after the i-th relaxation pass over all edges?

choices.1: The i vertices closest to the source are finalized, as in Dijkstra
choices.2: Every shortest path that uses at most i edges has its correct distance recorded
choices.3: All paths of length exactly i are discovered, but shorter ones may still be wrong
choices.4: Exactly i vertices have reached their final shortest-path distance

question: Given the edges 0→1 (5), 1→2 (−2), and 0→2 (10) with source 0, what is the final shortest distance to vertex 2?

choices.1: Infinity, because the negative edge forms a cycle
choices.2: 5, since that is the cost to first reach vertex 1
choices.3: 10, via the direct edge 0→2
choices.4: 3, via the path 0→1→2 (5 + (−2))

question: Bellman–Ford's negative-cycle detection makes it the standard tool for which of the following?

choices.1: Computing minimum spanning trees in graphs with negative weights
choices.2: Finding strongly connected components in a directed graph
choices.3: Detecting currency-arbitrage opportunities
choices.4: Maximizing flow through a network with capacity constraints

explanation: It is also the per-vertex subroutine inside Johnson's all-pairs shortest-path algorithm.

question: How does the SPFA variant relate to standard Bellman–Ford?

choices.1: It only queues the vertices whose distance just changed
choices.2: It improves the worst-case bound to O(E + V log V) by using a Fibonacci heap
choices.3: It processes edges in sorted weight order, like Kruskal's algorithm
choices.4: It drops negative-cycle detection in exchange for guaranteed linear time

explanation: Often faster in practice but with the same O(V · E) worst case.

question: What is the space complexity of Bellman–Ford, and what does it store?

choices.1: O(V), one distance value per vertex
choices.2: O(V²), an adjacency matrix of distances
choices.3: O(E), one entry per edge
choices.4: O(V · E), one distance per edge per pass

question: Binary lifting precomputes only Θ(log n) different jump lengths per node, yet can answer a jump of any length k. Which property of integers makes that sufficient?

choices.1: Every tree has at most log n distinct depths, so only log n jump lengths can ever occur
choices.2: Tree paths always have a power-of-two length, so non-power-of-two jumps are never requested
choices.3: Applying the single parent pointer log n times can reach any ancestor
choices.4: Any k can be decomposed into the set bits of its binary representation

explanation: So composing the precomputed 2^i jumps reproduces a jump of length k.

question: Let up[k][v] be the 2^k-th ancestor of v. Which recurrence correctly builds row k from the row below it?

choices.1: up[k][v] = up[k-1][v] + up[k-1][v]

question: Why does the doubling recurrence up[k][v] = up[k-1][up[k-1][v]] give the 2^k-th ancestor?

choices.1: It averages two adjacent rows, which doubles the implied depth
choices.2: It multiplies the parent index by k, scaling the jump to 2^k
choices.3: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then a single extra step, giving 2^(k-1)+1 levels rather than 2^k
choices.4: It jumps 2^(k-1) steps and then 2^(k-1) more, and 2^(k-1)+2^(k-1) = 2^k

question: What are the time and space costs of building the binary-lifting table for an n-node tree?

choices.1: O(n^2) preprocessing time and O(n^2) space for the table
choices.2: O(n log n) time and O(n log n) space
choices.3: O(n log n) time but only O(n) space
choices.4: O(n) time and O(n) space

question: How many rows (power-of-two levels) does the table need for an n-node tree?

choices.1: ceil(log2 n) + 1 rows
choices.2: n / log2 n rows
choices.3: Exactly 2 rows, the parent and grandparent
choices.4: Exactly n rows

question: kthAncestor loops over bit positions i and does `if (k & (1<<i)) v = up[i][v];`. Why is a single k-th-ancestor query O(log n)?

choices.1: Each jump halves the remaining distance, like a binary search on edge count
choices.2: k < n has at most ceil(log2 n) bits
choices.3: The loop always runs exactly n / log n iterations
choices.4: Each up[i][v] lookup internally recurses log n times

explanation: So at most that many power-of-two jumps are taken across the log n rows.

question: In LCA, phase 1 lifts the deeper node up by k = |depth(u) - depth(v)| levels. What is its purpose, and what does it mean if the two nodes coincide afterward?

choices.1: It moves both nodes to the root; coinciding means the tree is a single path
choices.2: It finds the midpoint of the path; coinciding means they were the same node to begin with
choices.3: It makes the two nodes siblings; coinciding means the tree is malformed
choices.4: It equalises their depths

explanation: Coinciding means the shallower node was an ancestor of the deeper one and is itself the LCA.

question: In phase 2 of the LCA algorithm, iterating k from the highest power down, when are BOTH u and v jumped up by 2^k?

choices.1: Only when up[k][u] != up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors still differ
choices.2: Whenever 2^k is less than or equal to the remaining depth difference
choices.3: On every iteration unconditionally, until k reaches 0
choices.4: Only when up[k][u] == up[k][v], i.e. their 2^k-th ancestors already match

question: After phase 2 of LCA finishes, why is the returned answer up[0][u] (u's parent) rather than u itself?

choices.1: u and v are now equal, and up[0] retrieves the deepest distinct ancestor
choices.2: u has been lifted exactly one level too high, and up[0] corrects that off-by-one
choices.3: The greedy lifting keeps u and v strictly below the LCA
choices.4: up[0][u] is always the root, the only guaranteed common ancestor

explanation: So their shared parent up[0][u] is the LCA. Stopping when they are distinct children of it.

question: The rows up[k][v] for k >= 1 are filled inside the DFS, before recursing into v's children. Why is that ordering correct?

choices.1: Because the recurrence reads only v's own column, which never depends on any other node
choices.2: In a pre-order traversal every ancestor of v is visited before v
choices.3: Because v's children are visited first, so their columns are ready to compose
choices.4: Because DFS visits nodes in increasing index order, so dependencies are always met

explanation: So the lower-row entries that up[k][v] composes are already complete.

question: The build sets the root's parent to itself (up[0][root] = root). What does this sentinel accomplish?

choices.1: It forces the root's depth to be computed as 1 instead of 0
choices.2: It lets kthAncestor return -1 when k exceeds the node's depth
choices.3: It marks the root so the LCA routine can detect when both nodes reach the top
choices.4: Any jump that would go above the root harmlessly stays at the root

explanation: So over-jumps never read an invalid node.

question: Per the comparison of LCA methods, when is Euler tour + sparse-table RMQ the better choice than binary lifting?

choices.1: When preprocessing must be O(n) rather than O(n log n)
choices.2: When all queries are known up front and can be answered offline
choices.3: When you specifically need O(1) queries and can accept more code for an LCA-only structure
choices.4: When you also need k-th-ancestor or level-ancestor queries answered from the same single structure

question: Why is binary lifting described as an 'online' LCA method while Tarjan's Union-Find approach is 'offline'?

choices.1: Tarjan's is online because Union-Find processes unions incrementally; binary lifting must batch its queries
choices.2: Binary lifting runs over a live network connection while Tarjan's runs locally
choices.3: Binary lifting answers queries one at a time as they arrive; Tarjan's needs all queries known in advance
choices.4: Binary lifting mutates the tree during each query while Tarjan's leaves it unchanged

question: Binary lifting generalises to any functional graph (each node has a single succ(v) pointer). With edge data stored beside the 2^k jumps, which extra query can the same power-of-two decomposition answer?

choices.1: The exact number of distinct cycles reachable from a node
choices.2: The total number of nodes in the subtree rooted at v
choices.3: Path aggregates over the same jumps
choices.4: The shortest path between any two nodes in an arbitrary weighted graph

explanation: Min/max/sum of edge weights combine over the 2^k segments being jumped.

question: Dijkstra is greedy: it always finalises the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance. Why is a vertex's distance final the moment it is popped from the min-heap?

choices.1: Because each vertex is pushed to the heap exactly once, so the first pop is the only pop
choices.2: Because edges are non-negative
choices.3: Because the heap guarantees no other vertex can ever later be pushed with a smaller key
choices.4: Because relaxation always processes a vertex's edges in ascending weight order

explanation: So any later path to it can only add non-negative weight and can never beat the distance it was popped with.

question: Why does Dijkstra's correctness require non-negative edge weights?

choices.1: Negative weights force the algorithm into an infinite loop re-relaxing the source
choices.2: A negative edge could make a cheaper route to an already-settled vertex appear later
choices.3: Negative weights make the distance array hold negative values, which a min-heap cannot order
choices.4: Negative weights cause the min-heap to extract vertices in the wrong order because of integer overflow

explanation: Breaking the greedy finality invariant and silently producing wrong answers.

question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra implemented with a binary min-heap?

choices.2: O(E + V log V)
choices.3: O(V * E), one heap op per edge-vertex pair
choices.4: O((V + E) log V)

question: Python's heapq has no decrease-key operation. How does the implementation handle a vertex whose tentative distance improves during relaxation?

choices.1: It pushes a new, smaller (distance, node) entry and leaves the old one
choices.2: It removes the old entry from the middle of the heap and reinserts the smaller one
choices.3: It updates the distance in place and re-heapifies the whole heap in O(V)
choices.4: It locates the old entry via an index map and decreases its key in place

explanation: So a vertex may appear several times (lazy deletion).

question: What is the purpose of the guard `if current_dist > dist[u]: continue` at the top of the pop loop?

choices.1: It stops the source vertex from being relaxed against itself
choices.2: It terminates the loop early once every reachable vertex is settled
choices.3: It detects a negative-weight edge and aborts the algorithm
choices.4: It discards stale duplicate heap entries

explanation: So each vertex's edges are relaxed only once, at its final distance.

question: Because duplicates are pushed instead of using decrease-key, how many entries can the heap hold, and what complexity bound does that yield?

choices.1: O(V^2) entries, giving O(V^2 log V)
choices.2: O(E) entries, giving O((V + E) log V)
choices.3: O(V + E) entries, giving O((V + E)^2)
choices.4: O(V) entries, giving O(V log V)

question: While relaxing edge (u, v, weight) with u's settled distance current_dist, under what condition is dist[v] updated?

choices.1: When current_dist + weight < dist[v], i.e. a shorter tentative path is found
choices.2: Whenever v has not yet been popped from the heap, regardless of the tentative distance value
choices.3: When weight < dist[u], regardless of dist[v]
choices.4: When current_dist + weight > dist[v]

question: How is the dist[] array initialised before the main loop runs?

choices.1: Every vertex set to infinity, including the source
choices.2: The source set to infinity and every other vertex set to 0
choices.3: Every vertex, including the source, set to 0
choices.4: The source set to 0 and every other vertex set to infinity

question: How does the implementation reconstruct the actual shortest path, not just its length?

choices.1: It sorts all vertices by final distance; consecutive vertices form the path
choices.2: Walk predecessors backward, reverse
choices.3: It re-runs Dijkstra from the target and intersects the two distance arrays
choices.4: It stores the full path so far inside each heap entry alongside the distance

question: Per the Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford comparison, which capability does Bellman-Ford have that Dijkstra lacks?

question: What precisely is a strongly connected component (SCC) of a directed graph?

question: Why does processing vertices in reverse finish order on the transpose keep each DFS tree confined to a single SCC?

choices.2: It never moves backward

choices.4: It is reset to 0 on each mismatch

explanation: Every text character is processed at most once.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/KMP String Matching.md
question: What is the pathological input that makes naive matching O(n*m)?

choices.1: A pattern with all distinct characters
choices.2: An empty pattern
choices.3: Repetitive text and pattern
choices.4: A pattern longer than the text

explanation: Like text 'AAAAAAAAAAAB' with pattern 'AAAAAB' — the same characters get re-checked repeatedly.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/KMP String Matching.md
question: After a full match (j == m) is reported, how does KMP continue searching for more occurrences?

choices.1: It stops, since only the first match matters
choices.2: It resets both i and j to 0
choices.3: It moves i back to the match start + 1
choices.4: It sets j = lps[j-1] and keeps scanning

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/KMP String Matching.md
question: Why is the overall KMP algorithm O(n + m)?

choices.1: Because the text is scanned m times, once per pattern character
choices.2: Because building the LPS array is O(m) and the search is O(n)
choices.3: Because the LPS array makes every comparison O(1/m)
choices.4: Because hashing avoids character comparisons

explanation: I only moves forward and each lps fallback strictly decreases j while j only grows on matches.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/KMP String Matching.md
question: What is lps[0] always equal to, and why?

choices.1: 1, because a single character always matches itself
choices.2: 0, because a single character has no proper prefix
choices.3: Undefined until the search begins
choices.4: The length of the pattern

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/KMP String Matching.md
question: Per the comparison table, which matcher is highlighted as great for MULTIPLE patterns (with a verify step for hash collisions)?

choices.1: Naive matching
choices.2: Boyer-Moore
choices.3: Rabin-Karp
choices.4: KMP

explanation: String hashing / rolling hash shines for multiple-pattern search.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: How does a polynomial (rolling) hash represent a string?

choices.1: As the XOR of its character codes
choices.2: Digits of a base-B number mod M
choices.3: As a bit array with one bit per character
choices.4: As a sorted multiset of its characters

explanation: The string is treated as a number in base B, taken modulo a large prime.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: What is the headline benefit of precomputing prefix hashes and base powers?

choices.1: It sorts the string in O(n)
choices.2: It compresses the string to O(log n) bits
choices.3: O(1) substring hashes after O(n) prep
choices.4: It removes all collisions

explanation: Any substring's hash can be read in constant time.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: What is the formula for the hash of substring s[l:r] from prefix hashes?

choices.1: pref[r] - pref[l]
choices.2: pref[r] + pref[l] * B^(r-l)
choices.3: (pref[r] * pref[l] * B^(r-l)) mod M
choices.4: (pref[r] - pref[l] * B^(r-l)) mod M

explanation: The standard rolling-hash subtraction formula.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: Why is the pref[l] term scaled by B^(r-l) in the substring formula?

choices.1: To convert the hash to a different modulus
choices.2: Because B^(r-l) is the number of characters in the prefix
choices.3: To avoid negative numbers after the modulo
choices.4: Because the s[:l] portion sits

explanation: R-l) digits to the left of s[l:r], so its contribution is scaled by B^(r-l.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: How is the basic polynomial hash computed iteratively (Horner's method)?

choices.1: h = (h + code * base) mod M, summing independently
choices.2: h = max(h, code) over all characters
choices.3: h = h XOR (code << base)
choices.4: h = (h * base + code) mod M for each character

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: Since a hash maps infinitely many strings into M values, what must correctness-critical matching do on a hash hit?

choices.1: Verify the hit with a real character-by-character comparison
choices.2: Trust the hash, since collisions are impossible with a prime modulus
choices.3: Discard the match as a collision
choices.4: Rehash with the same base and compare again

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: What does using two independent (base, mod) pairs (double hashing) achieve?

choices.1: It makes substring hashing O(log n) instead of O(1)
choices.2: It halves the preprocessing time
choices.3: Collisions become ~1/(M1*M2)
choices.4: It removes the need for a modulus

explanation: Two independent (base, mod) pairs make collisions effectively impossible.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: Why map the alphabet starting from 1 (e.g. ord(ch) - ord('a') + 1) rather than 0?

choices.1: To fit the codes in a single byte
choices.2: So leading characters aren't 'swallowed'
choices.3: To make the base larger than the alphabet
choices.4: Because 0 is reserved for the modulus

explanation: Strings like 'a', 'aa' must hash differently.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: How does Rabin-Karp use rolling hashes to search, and where does it shine?

choices.1: It builds an LPS array from the hashes; best for repetitive text
choices.2: It sorts the text by hash; best for single-pattern search
choices.3: O(1) hash updates per slide
choices.4: It hashes only the first character; best for prefixes

explanation: One char leaves, one enters; compare against the pattern hash — and it shines for multiple patterns.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: What is Rabin-Karp's average and worst-case search time?

choices.1: O(n log m) average
choices.2: O(n + m) in all cases, like KMP
choices.3: O(n + m) average, O(n*m) worst
choices.4: O(m) regardless of text length

explanation: Collisions forcing verification create the worst case.

source: C++/Algorithms & Data Structures/String Algorithms/String Hashing & Rolling Hash.md
question: Why randomise the base at startup for untrusted input?

choices.1: To speed up the modulo operation
choices.2: Because the modulus itself changes on each run
choices.3: To guarantee zero collisions
choices.4: Fixed constants invite collision attacks

explanation: Randomising the base defends against adversarial inputs engineered to collide.

question: What algorithm does std::sort implement, and what worst-case guarantee does that give?

choices.1: Heapsort only, O(N log N) but cache-unfriendly
choices.2: Pure quicksort, with O(N^2) worst case
choices.3: Merge sort, O(N log N) but always stable
choices.4: Introsort - quicksort with a heapsort fallback

explanation: Guaranteeing O(N log N) worst case. Plus insertion sort for tiny ranges.

question: When does introsort (std::sort) switch from quicksort to heapsort?

choices.1: When a custom comparator is supplied
choices.2: When the data is already sorted
choices.3: When recursion depth exceeds ~2*log2(N)
choices.4: When the range has fewer than ~16 elements

explanation: Signalling quicksort is degenerating toward O(N^2).

question: How does std::stable_sort differ from std::sort?

choices.1: It guarantees O(N) on nearly-sorted input
choices.2: Equal elements keep their order
choices.3: It is faster and uses less memory
choices.4: It requires only bidirectional iterators

explanation: Typically merge sort, at the cost of extra memory.

question: What does std::nth_element do, and at what average cost?

choices.1: Quickselect placement, average O(N)
choices.2: Fully sorts the range in O(N log N)
choices.3: Returns the k smallest elements sorted, in O(N log K)
choices.4: Performs a binary search in O(log N)

explanation: The element that would be at position n if sorted ends up there, smaller before and larger after (neither side sorted).

question: What is the difference between std::lower_bound and std::upper_bound on a sorted range?

choices.1: They are identical except upper_bound returns a bool
choices.2: lower_bound is O(N); upper_bound is O(log N)
choices.3: lower_bound returns the smallest element; upper_bound the largest
choices.4: lower_bound returns the first element >= x

explanation: Upper_bound returns the first element > x.

question: Why can std::binary_search and std::lower_bound degrade to O(N) on a std::list?

choices.1: Because a list offers only bidirectional iterators
choices.2: Because list elements are unsorted
choices.3: Because list stores duplicates
choices.4: Because the comparator cannot be inlined for a list

explanation: So advancing the iterator to the midpoint is linear.

question: What is the key difference between std::accumulate and std::reduce, and why does it matter for floating point?

choices.1: accumulate is a strict left-to-right fold
choices.2: accumulate is parallel; reduce is sequential
choices.3: reduce only works on integers
choices.4: They are identical aliases

explanation: Reduce requires an associative and commutative op and may reorder/parallelize - which changes FP rounding.

question: Why is the erase-remove idiom needed (e.g. v.erase(std::remove_if(...), v.end()))?

choices.1: Because remove sorts the range first
choices.2: Because erase is faster than remove
choices.3: Because remove_if invalidates all iterators
choices.4: Because std::remove can't actually erase

explanation: It shifts kept elements forward and returns the new logical end, which you then erase. Algorithms don't know the container.

question: What does std::partial_sort cost and produce?

choices.1: O(N log K); sorted top-K
choices.2: O(N log N); a fully sorted range
choices.3: O(N); the median element only
choices.4: O(log N); a boolean presence check

explanation: The smallest K elements, sorted, at the front — heap-based.

question: std::unique has what important precondition and effect?

choices.1: It removes all duplicates anywhere in the range, which need not be sorted
choices.2: It returns the count of distinct elements
choices.3: It collapses only ADJACENT duplicates, so you normally sort first
choices.4: It sorts and deduplicates in one pass

question: Why should bit manipulation be done in unsigned types?

choices.1: To get defined wraparound and avoid sign-bit surprises
choices.2: Unsigned types are faster on all CPUs
choices.3: Because unsigned types are larger
choices.4: Because signed integer types cannot use bitwise operators

explanation: Left-shifting signed/negative values is UB.

question: Which of these shift operations is undefined behaviour?

choices.1: ANDing two signed values
choices.2: Right-shifting an unsigned value by 1
choices.3: Shifting a 32-bit value by >= 32 bits
choices.4: Left-shifting an unsigned value that doesn't overflow

question: What does the expression x & (x - 1) do?

choices.1: Tests whether x is negative
choices.2: Rounds x up to the next power of two
choices.3: Clears the lowest set bit of x
choices.4: Isolates the lowest set bit

question: What does x & (-x) compute (in two's complement)?

choices.1: It isolates the lowest set bit of x
choices.2: It returns the absolute value of x
choices.3: It flips all bits of x
choices.4: It clears the lowest set bit that is in x

question: What does (x + (a-1)) & ~(a-1) compute, where a is a power of two?

choices.1: x rounded up to the next multiple of a
choices.2: x with its lowest a bits cleared, rounding down
choices.3: x divided by a
choices.4: the number of set bits in x

explanation: Alignment round-up.

question: How do you clear bit n of x with a mask?

choices.3: x ^ (1u << n) (toggles bit n)

question: What is the advantage of C++20 <bit> functions like std::popcount and std::countl_zero?

choices.1: Portable single-instruction bit ops
choices.2: They are constexpr but slower than loops
choices.3: They handle signed overflow safely
choices.4: They only work on std::bitset

explanation: popcnt, lzcnt, tzcnt without non-portable intrinsics.

question: Why use std::bit_cast instead of reinterpret_cast to reinterpret an object's bytes as another type?

choices.1: bit_cast is shorter to type
choices.2: No aliasing violation, constexpr-friendly
choices.3: bit_cast performs a runtime bounds check
choices.4: reinterpret_cast can't change the type at all

explanation: Unlike reinterpret_cast punning.

question: When should you use std::bitset<N> versus std::vector<bool>?

choices.1: bitset<N> for runtime sizes; vector<bool> for compile-time sizes
choices.2: bitset<N> for a compile-time-known size
choices.3: bitset<N> only stores up to 8 bits
choices.4: They are interchangeable in every situation

explanation: Vector<bool> (or vector<uint64_t>) for a runtime-sized bit array.

question: How do you test whether bit n of x is set, yielding 0 or 1?

choices.1: ~x >> n

question: What are the four key stages of building C++ code, in order?

choices.1: Preprocessing, compilation, assembly, linking
choices.2: Compilation, linking, assembly, preprocessing
choices.3: Parsing, optimization, linking, loading
choices.4: Tokenizing, compiling, interpreting, running

question: What is a translation unit?

choices.1: The final linked executable
choices.2: One preprocessed .cpp file
choices.3: A shared library loaded at runtime
choices.4: A single function within a source file

explanation: The source file together with all the headers it includes.

question: How are translation units related to .cpp files and compilation?

choices.1: All .cpp files form one translation unit compiled together
choices.2: One .cpp, one unit, one object file
choices.3: A translation unit is produced only after linking
choices.4: Headers each become their own translation unit

explanation: Each .cpp compiles independently into an object file.

question: What does an object file (.o) contain?

choices.1: The preprocessed source text
choices.2: A copy of every header it included
choices.3: Only human-readable assembly
choices.4: Code, symbols, relocations

explanation: Machine code, a symbol table (defined and undefined), and relocation information.

question: What is the linker's job?

choices.1: To expand macros and #include directives
choices.2: Resolve symbols, produce the binary
choices.3: To check syntax and types
choices.4: To translate C++ into assembly

explanation: It collects object files, resolves references, and combines code and data.

question: An 'undefined reference to add' error means what?

choices.1: add was called with the wrong arguments
choices.2: A header for add is missing during preprocessing
choices.3: Declared but never defined
choices.4: add was defined twice

explanation: No object file or library supplied add's definition.

question: Which tasks does the preprocessor handle?

choices.1: Type checking and assembly generation
choices.2: Symbol resolution across object files
choices.3: Includes, defines, conditionals
choices.4: Loading shared libraries at runtime

explanation: #include, #define, #if/#ifdef, and macro expansion.

question: What is the trade-off between static and dynamic linking?

choices.1: Static produces a self-contained but larger binary
choices.2: Dynamic linking removes the need for a linker
choices.3: Static linking loads libraries at runtime
choices.4: Static is always smaller; dynamic is always larger

explanation: Dynamic gives smaller binaries and shared memory but adds a runtime dependency.

question: What does the One Definition Rule (ODR) require?

choices.1: Every translation unit must define main
choices.2: Every symbol must be declared exactly once
choices.3: Each header may be included only once
choices.4: One definition per program

explanation: Each function or variable must be defined exactly once in the final program.

question: What benefit does compiling each translation unit independently (separate compilation) provide?

choices.1: Fast incremental parallel builds
choices.2: Elimination of all linker errors
choices.3: Automatic resolution of the ODR
choices.4: A smaller final executable

explanation: And modular code.

question: What does the One Definition Rule (ODR) require for ordinary (non-inline) entities?

choices.1: They may be declared many times but defined at most once per program
choices.2: They must be both declared and defined exactly once per translation unit
choices.3: They may never be declared in a header
choices.4: They may be defined once per translation unit if identical

question: What does the inline keyword on a function actually mean?

choices.1: A command that forces the compiler to inline every call
choices.2: Permission for repeated definitions
choices.3: A guarantee the function has internal linkage
choices.4: A hint that disables optimization for the function

explanation: The entity may be defined identically in multiple TUs; the linker merges them.

question: Why do functions defined inside a class body work in headers without ODR errors?

choices.1: Because functions in a class body have internal linkage
choices.2: Because the linker ignores class methods
choices.3: Because they are implicitly inline (as are templates)
choices.4: Because each TU renames them uniquely

question: Putting `int global;` (a non-inline definition) in a header included by many TUs causes what?

choices.1: Nothing - each TU gets its own copy
choices.2: A compile error in every TU
choices.3: Internal linkage by default
choices.4: Multiple definitions across TUs

explanation: An ODR violation and link error.

question: Which of these has INTERNAL linkage by default?

choices.1: A non-const global variable at namespace scope
choices.2: Namespace-scope const and static
choices.3: A normal (non-static) free function
choices.4: A non-static class member

explanation: Also anything in an unnamed namespace.

question: What is the modern, preferred way to give a helper internal (TU-private) linkage?

choices.1: Declare it inline
choices.2: An unnamed namespace
choices.3: Define it in a header
choices.4: Mark it extern

explanation: Preferred over file-scope static.

question: What problem do inline variables (C++17) solve?

choices.1: One header definition, shared by TUs
choices.2: They force variables into registers
choices.3: They make variables thread-safe
choices.4: They give variables internal linkage automatically

explanation: A global or static data member defined in a header without ODR trouble.

question: Why are cross-TU ODR violations (e.g. a class defined differently behind #ifdefs in different TUs) especially dangerous?

choices.1: They are automatically fixed by the linker
choices.2: Silent undefined behaviour
choices.3: They always produce a clear compile error
choices.4: They only slow down linking

explanation: They are often not diagnosed at all.

question: What belongs in headers versus source files to keep ODR happy?

choices.1: Headers: all definitions. Source files: only main
choices.2: Headers: only macros. Source files: everything else
choices.3: Both should contain identical definitions
choices.4: Declarations up top, definitions in one TU

explanation: Headers: declarations, inline functions/variables, templates, type definitions, constexpr. Source files: the single definition of each non-inline function and global.

question: What does declaring a static data member `static inline constexpr int size2 = 4;` (C++17) avoid?

choices.1: The need to mark the enclosing class final
choices.2: Zero-initialization of the member
choices.3: The need for the member to be const
choices.4: The separate out-of-line definition

explanation: Historically required when the member is ODR-used; inline removes it.

question: What is copy elision?

choices.1: Deleting the copy constructor automatically
choices.2: A runtime optimization that caches copies
choices.3: Replacing all copies with references
choices.4: Constructing directly at the destination

explanation: The compiler skips the copy/move the source code describes and builds the object in its final location.

question: When is copy elision MANDATORY in C++17?

choices.1: Only for trivially copyable types
choices.2: When initializing an object from a prvalue
choices.3: Whenever -O2 is enabled
choices.4: When returning a named local variable (NRVO)

explanation: Even for non-movable types. A pure temporary.

question: How does NRVO (named return value optimization) differ from C++17 prvalue elision?

choices.1: NRVO is mandatory; prvalue elision is optional
choices.2: NRVO is permitted but NOT guaranteed
choices.3: NRVO only works for non-movable types
choices.4: They are the same rule

explanation: When it doesn't apply you get a move (not a copy).

question: Why is `return std::move(local);` a pessimization?

choices.1: It disables NRVO
choices.2: It leaks the local's resources
choices.3: It causes a compile error
choices.4: It copies instead of moving

explanation: A candidate for zero-cost elision becomes a guaranteed move.

question: Why can a factory function return an immovable type (move ctor deleted) by value in C++17?

choices.1: Because immovable types are always copied
choices.2: Because the return is converted to a pointer
choices.3: Guaranteed elision skips the move
choices.4: Because the compiler secretly adds a move constructor

explanation: The prvalue materializes directly into the destination — no move needed.

question: In which case does NRVO typically FAIL to apply, falling back to a move?

choices.1: Different named objects per branch
choices.2: When the function is marked noexcept
choices.3: When the function returns a single named local
choices.4: When the returned type is movable

explanation: E.g. return b ? a : b2 — the compiler can't elide when the returned object varies.

question: Per the note, how should you return a large std::vector or std::string from a function?

choices.1: With return std::move(v) to ensure a move
choices.2: By value - guaranteed elision or a move makes it cheap
choices.3: Through a void f(vector<int>& out) out-parameter to avoid copies
choices.4: As a raw pointer to a heap allocation

explanation: Prefer it to out-parameters.

question: Which value category triggers the C++17 GUARANTEED elision case?

choices.1: An lvalue (a named variable)
choices.2: Any glvalue
choices.3: An xvalue produced by std::move
choices.4: A prvalue

explanation: A pure temporary with no identity, e.g. Widget{} or make().

question: What is a key difference between copy initialization (T obj = value) and direct initialization (T obj(value))?

choices.1: Direct initialization forbids implicit conversions
choices.2: Direct initialization cannot use braces
choices.3: Copy initialization cannot use explicit constructors; direct initialization can
choices.4: Copy initialization always makes an extra copy that is never elided by the compiler

question: During brace (list) initialization, how is constructor selection changed?

choices.1: Only the default constructor is considered
choices.2: initializer_list constructors win
choices.3: Parentheses constructors always win
choices.4: Explicit constructors are skipped

explanation: They are preferred whenever the braced arguments can convert to the element type.

question: Given Foo with Foo(int,double) and Foo(std::initializer_list<int>), what does Foo f{10, 3.14} call?

choices.1: It is ambiguous and fails to compile
choices.2: Foo(int, double), the exact match
choices.3: The default constructor
choices.4: Foo(std::initializer_list<int>)

explanation: Because braces prefer the initializer_list ctor and 10 and 3.14 convert to int.

question: What does a delegating constructor do?

choices.1: Forwards to another constructor of the same class
choices.2: Inherits constructors from a base class
choices.3: Disables the default constructor
choices.4: Calls a specific base-class constructor explicitly by name

explanation: E.g. G(int x) : G(x, 0) {}.

question: What does `using Base::Base;` in a derived class accomplish?

choices.1: It deletes the base constructors
choices.2: It delegates to a sibling constructor of the class
choices.3: It inherits the base class's constructors
choices.4: It makes Base a friend

question: What does marking a single-argument converting constructor explicit prevent?

choices.1: It forces the constructor to always be inline
choices.2: It allows narrowing conversions
choices.3: It prevents accidental implicit conversions
choices.4: It prevents the class from being copied

explanation: E.g. E e = 3.14 no longer compiles.

question: From best to worst, what is the conversion ranking used in overload resolution?

choices.1: Exact match, standard conversion, user-defined, promotion, ellipsis
choices.2: Exact, promotion, standard, user, ellipsis
choices.3: User-defined, standard conversion, promotion, exact match, ellipsis
choices.4: Ellipsis, exact match, promotion, standard, user-defined

explanation: From best to worst in overload resolution.

question: Given Bar(int) and Bar(double), why does Bar d(3U) (unsigned int) fail to compile?

choices.1: Because Bar has no matching constructor
choices.2: Because unsigned arguments require an explicit constructor
choices.3: Two conversions of equal rank
choices.4: Because 3U cannot be stored in an int

explanation: 3U->int and 3U->double are both standard conversions, making the call ambiguous.

question: What does the value-init form T obj{} do?

choices.1: Is a syntax error for built-in types
choices.2: Value-initializes it, e.g. int x{} gives 0
choices.3: Always calls an initializer_list constructor
choices.4: Leaves the object uninitialized

explanation: Zero-initialize first.

question: In copy-initialization Foo c = otherFoo, which constructor is preferred?

choices.1: Always an initializer_list constructor
choices.2: Always the default constructor
choices.3: A converting constructor
choices.4: Copy, or move for rvalues

question: What does a move operation do compared to a copy?

choices.1: It transfers ownership of the resource
choices.2: It deep-copies the resource then deletes the original
choices.3: It marks the source const
choices.4: It always allocates a new buffer

explanation: E.g. hands over the pointer and empties the source. Turning an O(n) copy into an O(1) transfer.

question: What does std::move actually do?

choices.1: It frees the object's memory
choices.2: It physically moves the object's resources
choices.3: An unconditional cast to T&&
choices.4: It swaps two objects

explanation: static_cast<T&&> merely enables a move by selecting the && overload — it moves nothing itself.

question: What happens if you std::move an object whose type has no move constructor or move assignment?

choices.1: It leaves the object in an invalid state
choices.2: A compile error
choices.3: It throws std::bad_move
choices.4: It silently falls back to a copy

question: What is the state of an object after it has been moved from?

choices.1: Identical to before the move
choices.2: Always exactly empty/zero
choices.3: Valid but unspecified
choices.4: Destroyed and unusable

explanation: Safe to destroy or reassign, but you must not rely on its value.

question: Why is it important that move constructors/assignments be noexcept?

choices.1: noexcept makes the move twice as fast
choices.2: vector only moves noexcept moves
choices.3: It is required to take an rvalue reference
choices.4: Otherwise they cannot compile

question: Why does move assignment need a self-assignment guard and to release before stealing?

choices.1: To support copy elision
choices.2: Because rvalue references can alias const objects
choices.3: Self-move would free the stolen buffer
choices.4: To make the operation noexcept

question: What silently suppresses the compiler-generated move operations for a class?

question: What is the Rule of Zero, recommended as the best approach?

choices.1: Declare every class final
choices.2: Always write all five special members explicitly

choices.4: Never use move semantics

question: Why does a named rvalue reference parameter still require std::move (or std::forward) to move from it?

question: Which reference overload binds ONLY to rvalues, marking the operand as safe to move from?

choices.2: Widget&& (rvalue reference)
choices.3: Widget& (non-const lvalue reference)
choices.4: const Widget& (lvalue reference to const)

choices.1: Destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, and move assignment
choices.2: Constructor, destructor, getter, setter, and swap

question: What does the Rule of Three state?

choices.2: Move operations must come in groups of three
choices.3: Every class needs three constructors
choices.4: Define one, define all three

choices.4: It removes the destructor requirement

question: What is the Rule of Zero, the modern best practice?

choices.1: Define only the destructor
choices.2: Define all five special members as = default

choices.4: Never use constructors

question: In the BadBuffer example, why does `BadBuffer b = a;` followed by destruction crash?

question: Why does the correct copy assignment operator begin with `if (this == &other) return *this;`?

choices.3: To make the operation noexcept

question: What classes of bugs do these rules exist to prevent?

choices.4: Data races and deadlocks

question: What is the Rule of One (the modern guideline)?

choices.1: A class should have exactly one constructor
choices.2: A class should have a single responsibility
choices.3: A class should define exactly one special member
choices.4: Only one object of a class may exist at a time

question: In the Rule-of-Five Buffer, what does the move constructor do?

choices.1: Allocates a new buffer and copies element by element
choices.2: Throws if the source is empty
choices.3: Deep-copies the buffer then frees the source
choices.4: Steal, then null the source

question: What does std::expected<T, E> (C++23) hold?

choices.2: Always a T, with E stored separately

question: Why is std::expected preferred over exceptions on an HFT hot path?

choices.3: Exceptions are slower to compile
choices.4: std::expected automatically retries failed operations

question: How does std::expected<T, E> improve on std::optional<T>?

choices.3: It can hold multiple values at once

question: What is the difference between value() and operator* on a std::expected?

choices.1: They are identical
choices.2: value() returns the stored error while operator* returns the value

question: How do you construct the error path of a std::expected?

choices.3: throw the error

question: In the monadic interface, what does and_then(f) do?

choices.3: Calls f (which returns another expected) only on success
choices.4: Calls f only on error to recover

question: What is the difference between transform(f) and and_then(f)?

choices.1: transform's f returns a plain value
choices.2: transform runs on error; and_then runs on success
choices.3: transform short-circuits; transform never runs f

explanation: Infallible step) which gets rewrapped; and_then's f returns another expected (fallible step.

question: For HFT, what kind of error type E does the note recommend?

choices.1: A std::string for rich messages
choices.2: A small, trivially-copyable E
choices.3: A std::shared_ptr to an error object
choices.4: A polymorphic exception class

explanation: An enum, std::error_code, or POD. So returns stay cheap and allocation-free.

question: Which accessor safely returns a default if the expected holds an error?

choices.1: value_or(default)
choices.2: error_or(default)
choices.3: value()
choices.4: operator*

question: In this std::function implementation, what role does the abstract Concept base class (with virtual operator() and clone) play?

choices.3: It stores the captured arguments
choices.4: It implements small-buffer optimization

question: Why does Impl store its callable as `using FuncType = std::decay_t<Func>;` rather than Func directly?

choices.4: To enable reference collapsing

question: How is copying a function object implemented, given the callable is held behind a unique_ptr<Concept>?

question: What happens when operator() is invoked on an empty function (d_ptr is null)?

choices.1: It is undefined behavior
choices.2: It returns a default-constructed ReturnType
choices.3: It silently does nothing
choices.4: It throws std::bad_function_call()

choices.2: To require the callable be noexcept

question: How are the copy-assignment and move-assignment operators implemented here?

choices.3: By manually deleting and reallocating d_ptr

question: What is the second condition in the constructor's requires-clause (besides excluding function itself)?

question: How does this optional implementation choose its storage strategy?

choices.2: It always heap-allocates the T

question: What does `alignas(T) std::byte obj[sizeof(T)];` provide, and what does it NOT do?

choices.2: It creates a live T object ready to use
choices.3: It allocates the T on the heap

question: Why is alignas(T) necessary on the byte buffer?

question: In the small-object version, how does a T's lifetime begin and end inside the buffer?

question: What is the distinction between raw_ptr() and ptr() in the small-object specialization?

choices.2: Construct-into versus use-after

choices.4: raw_ptr() heap-allocates; ptr() frees

question: What does std::launder communicate to the compiler in ptr()?

choices.3: A real T now lives at this address

question: How does this implementation differ from the real std::optional, per the caveats?

question: Why should std::launder NOT be used before construction (i.e., raw_ptr instead of ptr)?

question: What does constexpr on a function actually mean?

choices.4: It makes the return value const

question: How does consteval differ from constexpr?

choices.2: They are synonyms
choices.3: consteval permits runtime evaluation; constexpr forbids it
choices.4: consteval is an immediate function

question: What does constinit guarantee, and what does it NOT do?

question: What problem does constinit exist to solve?

choices.1: The static initialization order fiasco

choices.3: Data races on atomics
choices.4: Stack overflow caused by very deep recursion

question: Why is the static initialization order fiasco dangerous across translation units?

choices.2: Cross-TU init order is unspecified
choices.3: Globals are never zero-initialized first
choices.4: Constant initialization runs in random order

explanation: One global's dynamic initializer may read another that is still only zero-initialized.

question: What are the two ordered phases of initialization for non-local static-storage variables before main?

choices.2: Static then dynamic initialization
choices.3: Stack init then heap init
choices.4: Compile-time init then link-time init

explanation: Static = zero-init then constant initialization (no ordering hazard); dynamic = code that runs at startup.

question: What happens if a constinit variable's initializer is NOT a constant expression?

choices.2: It initializes on first use
choices.3: A compile error
choices.4: It silently falls back to dynamic initialization

question: What makes if constexpr different from a plain if in a template?

choices.1: if constexpr only works with arithmetic types
choices.2: The untaken branch isn't instantiated
choices.3: if constexpr is faster at runtime
choices.4: if constexpr requires both branches to compile for every type

question: What is the key difference between const and constexpr?

choices.3: const means runtime-immutable (value may be computed at runtime)
choices.4: const is a compile-time constant; constexpr is runtime-immutable

question: When constinit cannot apply (a genuinely runtime-constructed global like a Logger), what is the classic fix?

choices.2: Declare it const
choices.3: The construct-on-first-use idiom

question: What is the formula for speedup S(N) in Amdahl's Law (P = parallel fraction, N = processors)?

choices.4: S(N) = N * P

question: What is the central insight of Amdahl's Law?

choices.1: Parallel code is always faster than serial code
choices.2: Speedup grows without bound as processors increase
choices.3: The serial part caps speedup
choices.4: Doubling processors always doubles speed

explanation: The non-parallelisable fraction limits the maximum possible gain.

question: If 90% of a program is parallelisable (P = 0.9), what speedup does Amdahl's Law predict with 10 processors?

choices.1: About 10x speedup
choices.2: About 9x
choices.3: About 2x
choices.4: About 5.26x

question: What is the difference between vertical and horizontal scalability?

choices.1: They mean the same thing
choices.2: Vertical makes one machine more powerful (more RAM/faster CPU)
choices.3: Vertical is for CPUs; horizontal is for memory only
choices.4: Vertical adds more machines while horizontal upgrades a single machine

explanation: Horizontal adds more machines (e.g. servers in a cluster).

question: According to the note, which strategies improve scalability?

choices.1: Always scale vertically rather than horizontally
choices.2: Increase communication between processors
choices.3: Less serial work, less sharing
choices.4: Add more serial checkpoints and shared locks

explanation: Split tasks into independent parts, reduce inter-processor communication, and avoid shared-resource bottlenecks.

question: In Amdahl's Law, what does the term (1 - P) represent?

choices.1: The number of processors
choices.2: The serial fraction
choices.3: The parallel speedup
choices.4: The communication overhead

explanation: The part of the program that must run sequentially.

question: What is a deadlock?

choices.1: A mutex locked twice by the same thread
choices.2: A thread that spins forever on a CPU
choices.3: Mutual waiting with no exit
choices.4: A thread that crashes while holding a lock

explanation: Two or more threads each wait for the other to release something, so none continue.

question: What is the safest rule regarding nested locks?

choices.1: Don't hold one lock while taking another
choices.2: Hold all of the locks for the entire function
choices.3: Always acquire locks in reverse order
choices.4: Use one global lock for everything

explanation: The fewer locks held at once, the lower the deadlock risk.

question: Why is calling user-supplied code (callbacks, virtual functions, operator==) while holding a lock dangerous?

choices.1: It is always slower
choices.2: It corrupts the mutex state
choices.3: Unknown code may lock in a bad order
choices.4: It silently bypasses the mutex entirely

explanation: The callback might acquire another lock (or re-lock the same mutex), causing deadlock.

question: What is the safer pattern for invoking a user callback that needs shared data?

choices.1: Use a recursive mutex
choices.2: Copy under lock, call outside
choices.3: Hold the lock for the whole callback to be safe
choices.4: Call the callback first, then lock

explanation: Copy the data while holding the lock, release, then call the callback with the copy.

question: How does acquiring multiple locks in a fixed global order prevent deadlock?

choices.1: It makes locks faster to acquire
choices.2: It eliminates the need for mutexes
choices.3: It allows two threads to hold the same lock
choices.4: Same order means no cycle

explanation: If every thread locks in the same order, circular waiting cannot form.

question: What is a lock hierarchy, and what does it add over plain fixed ordering?

choices.1: It lets any thread lock in any order safely
choices.2: It replaces mutexes with atomics
choices.3: It removes the need for fixed ordering by allowing nesting
choices.4: Levels locked in one direction

explanation: A hierarchical_mutex can detect incorrect lock order at runtime. Each mutex gets a level; acquiring only downward makes violations detectable.

question: The four deadlock conditions reduce to one thing these guidelines mainly target. Which?

choices.1: Preventing any resource from being held
choices.2: Eliminating all waiting
choices.3: Preventing the circular wait
choices.4: Forcing preemption of locks

explanation: Don't let threads acquire locks in inconsistent orders.

question: Why is plain lazy initialization (if (!ptr) ptr.reset(new Resource)) not thread-safe?

choices.1: reset() is never thread-safe
choices.2: The Resource constructor always throws
choices.3: Both threads can initialize
choices.4: shared_ptr cannot be used across threads at all

explanation: Two threads may both see ptr empty — a race.

question: What is the drawback of guarding the whole lazy-init operation with a mutex?

choices.1: It can deadlock with itself
choices.2: It is incorrect under concurrency
choices.3: It only works for one thread
choices.4: Every call pays the lock

explanation: It is correct, but the mutex is locked even after initialization is already done — unnecessary overhead.

question: Why is naive double-checked locking dangerous in older C++ memory models?

choices.1: Non-null before fully constructed
choices.2: It leaks the Resource
choices.3: It locks too many times
choices.4: It never enters the critical section

explanation: Another thread might observe the pointer before the Resource's construction completes.

question: What does std::call_once guarantee for one-time initialization?

choices.1: That init runs once per thread
choices.2: Exactly once; others wait
choices.3: That init never blocks any thread
choices.4: That init is retried on failure automatically

explanation: The init function runs once even if multiple threads race; the rest block until it completes.

question: Why is a function-local static (static Resource resource; inside a function) a clean thread-safe option?

choices.1: Because statics are never shared between threads
choices.2: Because it uses a mutex on every access
choices.3: C++11 guarantees the init is safe
choices.4: Because it avoids constructing the Resource at all

explanation: Function-local static initialization is thread-safe by the standard.

question: Which two techniques does the note recommend for safe once-only shared initialization?

choices.1: std::call_once or a function-local static variable
choices.2: Plain lazy init or double-checked locking
choices.3: A global mutex or a spinlock
choices.4: volatile pointers or atomics with relaxed ordering

question: What is the core rule for exception-safe concurrent C++?

choices.1: Catch every exception at the top of main
choices.2: Only throw exceptions while holding a lock
choices.3: No half-updated state on throw
choices.4: Disable exceptions entirely with -fno-exceptions

explanation: Design so exceptions can't leave shared state inconsistent, locks held, or threads abandoned.

question: Why prefer std::lock_guard over manual m.lock()/m.unlock()?

choices.1: It still unlocks if the code throws
choices.2: It allows recursive locking
choices.3: It locks faster
choices.4: It avoids needing a separate mutex at all

explanation: Manual unlock() would be skipped when the protected work throws; the RAII guard releases automatically.

question: What is the recommended structure for a multi-step shared-state update that might throw?

choices.1: Use two separate locks for the two steps
choices.2: Hold the lock across the whole function and ignore exceptions
choices.3: Do all the throwing/expensive work inside the lock
choices.4: Prepare outside, commit inside

explanation: Do expensive/throwing work outside the lock, then commit with short, ideally noexcept steps (e.g. swap) inside.

question: What invariant should hold at every lock boundary?

choices.1: The mutex must be recursive
choices.2: The lock must always be held for at least one complete operation
choices.3: Only one thread may ever unlock the mutex
choices.4: Whenever a mutex is unlocked, the protected data must be valid

question: Why must destructors used for cleanup (lock wrappers, thread joiners, rollback guards) be noexcept?

choices.1: So an exception cannot escape a destructor
choices.2: To allow them to be invoked manually later
choices.3: To run faster
choices.4: Because destructors cannot allocate

explanation: Which would risk std::terminate during stack unwinding.

question: What happens to an uncaught exception thrown inside a std::thread, and what's the fix?

choices.1: It propagates to the joining thread automatically
choices.2: It unwinds the main thread's stack
choices.3: std::terminate is called
choices.4: It is silently ignored

explanation: Propagate deliberately via std::promise/std::future, std::async, or catch inside the thread (std::exception_ptr).

question: Why prefer std::scoped_lock when acquiring multiple mutexes?

choices.1: It converts the mutexes into a single recursive mutex
choices.2: It is the only lock that is exception-safe
choices.3: Deadlock-safe multi-lock RAII
choices.4: It allows locking them in any inconsistent order safely one at a time

explanation: All mutexes are locked with a deadlock-avoidance algorithm and unlocked automatically.

question: What are the four exception guarantees, from strongest to weakest?

choices.1: No-throw, basic, none, strong
choices.2: Strong, no-throw, none, basic
choices.3: Basic, strong, no-throw, none
choices.4: No-throw, strong, basic, none

explanation: State still valid but may change. State unchanged if it throws. State may be corrupted.

question: Why should a condition variable always be waited on with a predicate (cv.wait(lock, pred))?

choices.1: It absorbs spurious wakeups
choices.2: It is strictly required in order to compile
choices.3: To avoid taking the lock
choices.4: To make wait faster

explanation: The predicate handles spurious wakeups and missed notifications, keeping state consistent.

question: What is data contention, and why does it limit parallelism?

choices.1: When too much memory is allocated
choices.2: When separate threads access different data and never actually conflict
choices.3: When multiple threads need the same shared resource at once
choices.4: When the CPU runs out of registers

explanation: Per Amdahl's Law. Creating a serialization point that bounds speedup.

question: What is cache ping-ponging?

choices.1: A logical conflict where two threads need the same lock
choices.2: A scheduler bug that pauses threads
choices.3: The CPU prefetching the wrong data
choices.4: A line bouncing between cores

explanation: Different threads keep modifying the same cache line, so it migrates back and forth.

question: What is false sharing?

choices.1: Sharing read-only immutable data
choices.2: Two threads intentionally sharing one variable
choices.3: Different variables, same cache line
choices.4: A thread reading stale data due to a missing memory fence

explanation: The line bounces between cores even though the threads never share a variable.

question: What is a typical fix for false sharing between per-thread counters?

choices.1: Use one single shared atomic counter for all
choices.2: Use memory_order_relaxed
choices.3: Give each counter its own cache line
choices.4: Increase the number of threads

explanation: Pad/align with alignas(64) or std::hardware_destructive_interference_size.

question: What is a typical cache line size assumed in the note?

choices.1: 8 bytes
choices.2: 64 bytes
choices.3: 4 KB
choices.4: 256 bytes

question: What is the difference between data contention and cache ping-ponging?

choices.1: Contention only happens with mutexes; ping-ponging only with atomics
choices.2: Data contention is a logical/program-level conflict
choices.3: Data contention is hardware; ping-ponging is logical
choices.4: They are the same thing

explanation: Cache ping-ponging is a hardware/cache-level effect (they often co-occur).

question: What is a key mental model about shared reads vs shared writes?

choices.1: Shared writes are cheap while shared reads are the expensive part
choices.2: Shared reads are usually cheap (cache lines can be shared)
choices.3: Neither has any cost on modern CPUs
choices.4: Both are equally expensive

explanation: Shared writes are expensive (they force ownership transfers).

question: How does the NUMA first-touch policy affect where memory is placed?

choices.1: Memory is striped evenly across all nodes automatically
choices.2: Pages land where first written
choices.3: Memory is always placed near socket 0
choices.4: First-touch has no effect on placement

explanation: Physical pages go to the NUMA node of the first-touching thread, so each worker should initialize the data it will later use.

question: Why can a structure-of-arrays (SoA) layout beat array-of-structs (AoS) for a loop that uses only some fields?

choices.1: SoA loads only the fields the loop needs
choices.2: SoA uses less total memory
choices.3: AoS cannot be iterated
choices.4: SoA avoids essentially all cache misses entirely

explanation: With AoS, accessing one field still pulls the others into cache; SoA improves cache use by keeping each field contiguous.

question: What is oversubscription, and when can it still be useful?

choices.1: Using fewer threads than cores; always good
choices.2: Extra threads; fine when they block
choices.3: Pinning threads to cores; only useful on NUMA
choices.4: Allocating too much memory per thread

explanation: More runnable threads than the hardware runs efficiently — usually bad for CPU-bound work but helpful for I/O-bound work where threads often block.

question: How does nested parallelism cause accidental oversubscription?

choices.1: Libraries spawning threads under threads
choices.2: It never does; nesting is always coordinated
choices.3: It forces all work onto one core
choices.4: It reduces the thread count below the core count

explanation: App threads each call a library that spawns its own (e.g. 8 x 8 = 64 runnable threads) — far more than intended.

question: Why are tasks that are too small (poor granularity) a performance problem?

choices.1: They cannot be submitted to a thread pool
choices.2: Overhead swamps the useful work
choices.3: They use too much memory
choices.4: They always cause false sharing

explanation: Scheduling costs dominate tiny tasks and cause excessive switching; batch items into larger tasks instead.

question: How does reducing sharing via local accumulation help (e.g. local_total then one global update)?

choices.1: It removes the need for any synchronization or memory-ordering guarantees
choices.2: It makes the result incorrect but faster
choices.3: It increases the number of shared writes
choices.4: Each thread works on private data and only combines occasionally

explanation: Avoiding constant contention and cache-line movement on the shared variable.

question: What is the main lesson about fast concurrent code?

choices.1: Always use global atomics for counters
choices.2: More threads is always faster
choices.3: Locks should always be held as long as possible
choices.4: Fewer, better-fed threads beat more threads

explanation: Each thread should have enough work, work on private/partitioned data, touch nearby memory, and communicate rarely.

question: Why is the separate top() then pop() pattern dangerous in multithreaded code?

choices.1: top() throws if the stack is non-empty
choices.2: pop() returns the wrong type
choices.3: The state can change in between
choices.4: top() is always slower than pop()

explanation: Another thread might mutate the structure between top() and pop(); they should be one atomic operation.

question: What exception-safety problem does a combined `auto v = stack.pop();` have?

choices.1: A throwing return loses the item
choices.2: It always returns a null-valued item
choices.3: pop() can deadlock
choices.4: It double-frees the item

explanation: If the item is removed and then copying/moving it to the caller throws, the item is gone forever.

question: How does the 'pass in a reference' method (void pop(T& result)) achieve strong exception safety?

choices.1: It removes the item first, then copies
choices.2: It never throws because references can't fail
choices.3: Copy out before removing
choices.4: It returns a shared_ptr instead

explanation: If the copy throws, the item is still in the structure.

question: Returning T by value from pop() is only safely exception-proof under what condition?

choices.1: Only if T is an integer
choices.2: Only if the whole stack is empty
choices.3: Only if T can't throw on return
choices.4: Always, regardless of T

explanation: The return after data.pop() must not throw, or the popped element is lost.

question: Why does returning std::shared_ptr<T> from pop() avoid the dangerous value-return problem?

choices.1: Because shared_ptr can never be null
choices.2: Because it stores the item on the stack
choices.3: Because it removes the need for a lock
choices.4: Copying a pointer is safe and cheap

explanation: The T doesn't have to be copied out after the pop, so no throwing copy can lose the item.

question: What is the main downside of the shared_ptr-returning approach?

choices.1: It restricts T to no-throw types
choices.2: The caller must always pre-construct a T
choices.3: An allocation and nullable results
choices.4: It cannot be made thread-safe

explanation: Extra dynamic allocation, more complex ownership, and callers must handle a possible nullptr.

question: What problem do hazard pointers solve in lock-free data structures?

choices.1: Use-after-free on removed nodes
choices.2: Deadlock between threads
choices.3: Priority inversion
choices.4: Integer overflow in shared counters

explanation: A node removed by one thread may still be referenced by another that holds a raw pointer to it.

question: What is the core hazard-pointer protocol for safely reading a shared node?

choices.1: Lock the node, read it, unlock it
choices.2: Increment a reference count and decrement after
choices.3: Copy the whole structure before reading
choices.4: Publish, re-check, then dereference

explanation: Read the shared pointer, publish it in your hazard slot, re-read to confirm it didn't change, dereference, then clear the slot.

question: Why is the re-read (validation) step `while (head.load() != p)` necessary after publishing p?

choices.1: To confirm p is still reachable
choices.2: To free the old node
choices.3: To make the load atomic
choices.4: To increment an internal version counter

explanation: If it changed between the first read and the publish, the thread retries so it never protects/dereferences a stale pointer.

question: How do hazard pointers separate logical removal from physical deletion?

choices.1: They use a garbage collector to free nodes
choices.2: They never delete nodes at all
choices.3: A removed node is retired, not deleted
choices.4: They delete the node immediately on removal

explanation: It is freed later only after a scan confirms no thread has it in a hazard slot. Added to a per-thread retired list.

question: During a reclamation scan, when is a retired node actually freed?

choices.1: Always immediately
choices.2: Only when the retired list is full
choices.3: After a fixed time delay
choices.4: When no hazard slot holds it

explanation: A retired node is freed only when it appears in no thread's hazard pointers.

question: In the Michael-Scott lock-free queue dequeue, what does a thread do with the old head after a successful CAS moves head forward?

choices.1: delete head immediately
choices.2: retire(head)
choices.3: Leave head leaked
choices.4: Push head back onto the queue

explanation: Another thread may still be reading it via a hazard pointer, so it can't be deleted immediately.

question: Which property describes hazard pointers' protection granularity?

choices.1: They protect whole epochs of allocations at once
choices.2: They protect only the most recently allocated node
choices.3: They protect the entire data structure with one flag
choices.4: They are precise: they protect specific nodes

explanation: Not whole epochs.

question: With T threads and H hazard slots each, what is the cost of a reclamation scan?

choices.1: It scans the entire heap
choices.2: It must stop all threads first
choices.3: It is O(1) regardless of thread count
choices.4: It inspects up to T*H hazard pointers

question: Which problem do hazard pointers NOT automatically solve?

choices.1: Publishing a pointer before dereferencing
choices.2: Use-after-free
choices.3: Retiring removed nodes
choices.4: The ABA problem and reference cycles

explanation: CAS fooled when memory is reused at the same address.

question: What are the three progress guarantees for nonblocking data structures, from weakest to strongest?

choices.1: Obstruction-free, lock-free, wait-free
choices.2: Blocking, lock-free, wait-free
choices.3: Wait-free, lock-free, obstruction-free
choices.4: Lock-free, wait-free, obstruction-free

question: What does lock-free guarantee (as opposed to wait-free)?

choices.1: Every thread completes in a bounded number of steps
choices.2: Someone always progresses
choices.3: Progress only when a thread runs alone without interference
choices.4: No thread ever retries an operation

explanation: At least one thread makes progress system-wide, though an individual thread may starve.

question: What does wait-free guarantee?

choices.1: Some thread eventually finishes
choices.2: Progress only without interference
choices.3: Threads never use atomics
choices.4: Bounded steps for every thread

explanation: Every thread completes in a bounded number of steps regardless of others — no starvation.

question: What does obstruction-free mean?

choices.1: Threads block on a mutex
choices.2: At least one thread always progresses
choices.3: Progress only when running alone
choices.4: Every thread finishes in bounded time

explanation: The weakest guarantee — a thread progresses only without interference.

question: What is 'helping' in wait-free algorithms?

choices.1: Threads back off randomly on contention
choices.2: The runtime garbage-collects nodes
choices.3: Finishing someone else's operation
choices.4: A thread waits for another to finish

explanation: One thread completes another's pending work so progress is never blocked.

question: What is the most common practical implementation family for lock-free data structures?

choices.1: CAS (compare-and-swap)-based
choices.2: Mutex-based
choices.3: Descriptor-based
choices.4: Tracing-garbage-collection-based

question: What does linearizability mean?

choices.1: Threads never contend
choices.2: Atomic at a single instant
choices.3: Operations run in numeric order
choices.4: The structure is implemented as a linear array

explanation: Each operation appears to occur at one point in time.

question: What is the ABA problem?

choices.1: Two threads alternate on a lock forever
choices.2: A thread aborts before another begins
choices.3: A-to-B-to-A fools CAS
choices.4: An allocator runs out of memory

explanation: The location returns to its old value, so CAS thinks nothing changed.

question: Which of these is a memory-reclamation strategy for nonblocking structures?

choices.1: Priority inheritance
choices.2: The double-checked locking pattern
choices.3: Hazard pointers or epochs
choices.4: Spinlocks

explanation: Also reference counting, GC, or RCU.

question: Why do lock-free queues often use a manual reference-counting scheme instead of std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<Node>>?

choices.1: shared_ptr cannot point to queue nodes at all
choices.2: shared_ptr is too small
choices.3: atomic<shared_ptr> may not be lock-free
choices.4: Manual counting is always simpler

explanation: If std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<Node>> takes a lock on the platform, using it defeats the purpose of the lock-free structure.

question: In the counted-pointer scheme, what is the purpose of the EXTERNAL count?

choices.1: It counts how many times the node was deleted
choices.2: It protects a node while a thread is acquiring it from a shared atomic pointer
choices.3: It stores the node's data size
choices.4: It tracks the references already acquired by threads, and is stored inside the node

explanation: Head/tail/next.

question: Why can't you simply put one atomic refcount inside the node and increment it?

choices.1: Incrementing a refcount requires a lock
choices.2: Atomics can't live inside nodes
choices.3: Dereferencing it first is a chicken-and-egg race
choices.4: The node has no valid address until it is fully constructed

explanation: To increment node->refcount you must first dereference the node, but it might already be deleted — so the first reference must be acquired atomically through the shared pointer.

question: How does a thread safely acquire a node from head before dereferencing it?

choices.1: It deletes the node and then re-creates it
choices.2: It locks a mutex around head
choices.3: One CAS bumps the count and checks head
choices.4: It copies the whole node first

explanation: It CASes head to a copy with external_count incremented; increment and pointer check happen in one atomic step, retrying if head changed.

question: When is a node actually deleted in this scheme?

choices.1: Immediately when removed from the queue
choices.2: No external and no internal refs
choices.3: After a fixed timeout
choices.4: When the external count reaches 1

explanation: Deletion happens only when both counts are exhausted.

question: When a shared atomic pointer (e.g. head) stops referencing a node, what happens to its external count?

choices.1: It is reset to zero
choices.2: It is discarded
choices.3: It moves into the node
choices.4: It is copied into tail

explanation: The external count is folded/transferred into the node's internal count — the acquired references become internal references.

question: How does the counted pointer help mitigate the ABA problem?

choices.1: It uses a mutex on every CAS
choices.2: It never reuses addresses
choices.3: The CAS compares both the pointer AND the count
choices.4: It prevents freed memory from ever being reused again

explanation: So even if a raw address is reused, the counted value is much less likely to look identical.

question: What is the main platform dependency / downside of the counted-pointer scheme?

choices.1: It only works single-threaded
choices.2: It needs double-width CAS
choices.3: It cannot store any data in nodes
choices.4: It requires garbage collection

explanation: std::atomic<counted_node_ptr> (pointer + counter, often 16 bytes) must be lock-free, or the whole scheme isn't.

question: Why does an SPSC ring buffer need no CAS at all?

choices.1: Each index has exactly one writer
choices.2: Because the buffer is single-threaded
choices.3: Because reads and writes never overlap in time
choices.4: Because it uses a mutex internally

explanation: One thread owns the write index and one the read index, so no two threads modify the same variable — only atomic loads/stores with the right ordering are needed.

question: Why must the capacity be a power of two?

choices.1: To avoid false sharing
choices.2: To make the buffer fit in a cache line
choices.3: To allow CAS on the indices
choices.4: Masking replaces modulo

explanation: Wrapping uses & (Capacity-1) instead of the costlier % operation.

question: Using free-running counters (head_, tail_) that only increment, how are empty and full detected?

choices.1: Empty when both are zero; full when both equal Capacity
choices.2: Empty when head_ == tail_; full when head_ - tail_ == Capacity
choices.3: Empty when head_ - tail_ == Capacity; full when head_ == tail_
choices.4: Empty when head_ < 0; full when tail_ overflows

question: What is the single memory-ordering rule for the SPSC buffer?

choices.1: Relaxed everywhere, since one thread owns each index
choices.2: Acquire on your own index, release on the other's
choices.3: Relaxed own, acquire theirs, release publish
choices.4: Use seq_cst everywhere for correctness

explanation: Relaxed on your own index, acquire when loading the other thread's index, release when publishing after the payload write or slot free.
