question: In push, why is head_.store(head+1) done with memory_order_release?

choices.1: Because release is faster than relaxed
choices.2: To prevent the producer from ever reading tail_ early
choices.3: To make the store atomic
choices.4: Release publishes the payload, not just the index

explanation: The payload write buf_[...] = item happens-before the consumer's acquire-load of head_ seeing the new index.

question: What is the classic bug if you use relaxed ordering on the CROSS-thread loads/stores?

choices.1: Garbage reads past the payload
choices.2: The producer silently overwrites tail_
choices.3: The capacity must double
choices.4: The buffer deadlocks

explanation: The consumer could see the incremented head_ before the payload write lands.

question: Why are head_ and tail_ each given alignas(64)?

choices.1: To make them atomic
choices.2: To put each index on its own cache line
choices.3: To pad the struct to exactly 64 bytes total
choices.4: To align them for SIMD

explanation: Often a 2-3x throughput difference. Avoiding false sharing where one thread's write invalidates the other's cached line.

question: What does the 'cached indices' optimization achieve?

choices.1: It makes the queue support multiple producers
choices.2: Re-read the atomic only when needed
choices.3: It removes the need for a payload buffer
choices.4: It replaces acquire/release with seq_cst

explanation: Each side caches the other's last-seen index and re-reads the real atomic only when the cache says full/empty, keeping the common path cache-local.

question: What progress guarantee do push and pop provide, and what is the hard usage constraint?

choices.1: Lock-free; any number of producers and consumers
choices.2: Wait-free (bounded steps, no retries)
choices.3: Blocking; multi-producer safe
choices.4: Obstruction-free; single-threaded only

explanation: Strictly one producer and one consumer - two producers racing on head_ corrupts it.

question: What is the problem with a single shared task queue in a threadpool?

choices.1: It can only hold one task
choices.2: It cannot store tasks
choices.3: All workers contend for the same lock
choices.4: It requires full lock-free programming

explanation: Which scales poorly.

question: What is the usual high-performance alternative to one central queue?

choices.1: One thread per task
choices.2: Only a single global lock-free stack
choices.3: Per-worker queues with stealing
choices.4: A single bigger queue

explanation: Each worker mostly uses its own queue and only steals when out of work.

question: In work stealing, why do the owner and thieves take from opposite ends of the deque?

choices.1: They touch opposite ends
choices.2: To guarantee wait-freedom
choices.3: To preserve FIFO order
choices.4: Because deques only allow one end

explanation: The owner pops from the back and thieves steal from the front, so they usually contend less.

question: What is the single easiest, highest-impact rule (level 1) for a threadpool?

choices.1: Create one new thread per task
choices.2: Use lock-free queues
choices.3: Always notify_all
choices.4: Run tasks outside the lock

explanation: Move the task out under the lock, then run it outside.

question: Why prefer cv.notify_one (or a counting semaphore) over cv.notify_all when adding a task?

choices.1: notify_all is deprecated
choices.2: Mass wakeups just contend
choices.3: notify_one wakes more threads
choices.4: Semaphores cannot wake workers

explanation: Waking too many workers causes contention; notify_one or a semaphore reduces pointless wakeups.

question: How does batching pops from the shared queue help?

choices.1: It forces strictly single-threaded execution
choices.2: It takes several tasks per lock acquisition
choices.3: It runs tasks while holding the lock
choices.4: It eliminates the queue entirely

explanation: Especially helpful when tasks are small. Turning many lock acquisitions into fewer.

question: Why push a worker-generated (internal) child task to that same worker's LOCAL queue?

choices.1: To deliberately force the task onto a different core
choices.2: To guarantee FIFO ordering globally
choices.3: Because the global queue is read-only
choices.4: For locality and to reduce global contention

explanation: Useful for divide-and-conquer workloads (parallel_sort, tree_search).

question: What does the note advise about starting with lock-free queues?

choices.1: Always start lock-free for best performance
choices.2: Lock-free queues completely remove the need for any work stealing
choices.3: Don't start with lock-free; start with good queue topology
choices.4: Lock-free queues are never useful

explanation: A std::mutex + std::deque often beats a complex lock-free queue unless task granularity is tiny.

question: Which C++20 facility gives a condition-variable wait that wakes on either work availability OR cancellation?

choices.1: std::condition_variable with a timeout
choices.2: std::binary_semaphore
choices.3: std::atomic<bool> with busy-waiting
choices.4: condition_variable_any + stop_token

explanation: cv.wait(lock, stop, pred) wakes on either work or cancellation.

question: Why must you use std::condition_variable_any rather than std::condition_variable for stop-token waits?

choices.1: Only _any supports stop_token
choices.2: condition_variable_any is faster
choices.3: condition_variable_any avoids needing a mutex
choices.4: condition_variable cannot take a predicate

explanation: std::condition_variable does not take a stop_token; condition_variable_any does.

question: What is the main rule for the wait predicate in an interruptible worker?

choices.1: Always include cancellation in the predicate
choices.2: Wait on the condition variable alone, with no predicate
choices.3: Only check tasks.empty()
choices.4: Never use a predicate with stop tokens

explanation: The real condition is your predicate, not the cv itself. E.g. stop.stop_requested() || hasWork.

question: What is the difference between the two shutdown modes shown?

choices.1: Return with tasks queued vs finish the queue
choices.2: One of them uses a mutex, the other does not
choices.3: One is for C++17, the other for C++20
choices.4: They are identical

explanation: 'Stop immediately' returns even with tasks queued (checks stop_requested); 'drain first' stops accepting new work but keeps processing while the queue is non-empty.

question: Why call cv.notify_all() during shutdown even though condition_variable_any is stop-aware?

choices.1: notify_all is the only way to wake any worker
choices.2: Everyone rechecks the predicate
choices.3: It is required or the program crashes
choices.4: It cancels the stop request

explanation: All sleeping workers promptly reevaluate and observe the stop.

question: How do you achieve interruptible waiting before C++20 (no stop_token)?

choices.1: Busy-spin on tasks.empty()
choices.2: An atomic stopping flag
choices.3: Use std::condition_variable_any anyway
choices.4: Use pthread_cancel

explanation: Check it in the predicate and call cv.notify_all() on shutdown.

question: What is the modern, clean way to interrupt a thread in C++?

choices.1: Cooperative cancellation
choices.2: Set the thread priority to zero
choices.3: Throw an exception into the thread
choices.4: Forcefully kill it with TerminateThread / pthread_cancel

explanation: Std::jthread + std::stop_token, where the function checks stop.stop_requested() at safe points.

question: What does a std::jthread do automatically when it is destroyed?

choices.1: It detaches the thread
choices.2: Nothing; you must join manually
choices.3: It requests stop and then joins
choices.4: It throws if the thread is still running

question: Why is a task like doOneHugeBlockingOperation() not meaningfully interruptible?

choices.1: It never checks the stop token
choices.2: Because stop tokens only work with condition variables
choices.3: Because blocking operations ignore mutexes
choices.4: Because jthread can't pass a stop token to it

explanation: Cooperative cancellation only works if code periodically checks; a 30-second blocking call can't be cancelled until it returns.

question: How do you make a long sleep interruptible?

choices.1: Lower the thread priority
choices.2: Use std::this_thread::sleep_for once with the full duration
choices.3: Use pthread_cancel during the sleep
choices.4: Sleep in stop-checked slices

explanation: Break it into smaller waits that check stop.stop_requested(), or use a stop-aware cv.wait_for.

question: Since std::future::get() is not stop-token-aware, how do you wait on another task interruptibly?

choices.1: Detach the producing thread
choices.2: Call get() and hope it returns
choices.3: Poll wait_for in a loop
choices.4: Use pthread_cancel on the future

explanation: Use future.wait_for(small_timeout) and check stop.stop_requested() between polls.

question: Why avoid forcibly killing threads (TerminateThread, pthread_cancel)?

choices.1: They are not available on Linux
choices.2: They break invariants mid-flight
choices.3: They require C++20
choices.4: They are slower than cooperative cancellation

explanation: Mutexes stay locked, memory half-mutated, files half-written.

question: What task signature does the note recommend for clean, composable interruptible threadpool tasks?

choices.1: using Task = void*
choices.2: using Task = std::packaged_task<void()>
choices.3: using Task = std::function<void()>
choices.4: function<void(stop_token)>

explanation: Workers run task(stop), so any task can observe cancellation.

question: What is the simple way to make task B depend on task A in a threadpool?

choices.1: Move A's future into B
choices.2: Use a global mutex shared by A and B
choices.3: Submit B before A
choices.4: Run both on the same thread sequentially

explanation: B's lambda calls a.get() before doing its work.

question: How can naively blocking on dependent tasks deadlock the pool?

choices.1: If two tasks share a mutex
choices.2: All workers waiting on queued-but-unrun tasks
choices.3: If get() happens to be called from the main thread
choices.4: If the future is moved twice

explanation: E.g. a 1-thread pool where B occupies the only worker and waits for A, which can never run.

question: For a simple dependency chain, what ordering avoids the deadlock?

choices.1: Submit the dependent tasks before their own dependencies
choices.2: Submit the dependencies first, then the dependent tasks
choices.3: Use a single-thread pool
choices.4: Submit everything from inside a single worker task

question: Why are then()-style continuations a better design than blocking inside a worker?

choices.1: They guarantee FIFO ordering
choices.2: They remove the need for futures completely
choices.3: Waiting tasks stop wasting workers
choices.4: They run faster on a single core

explanation: Dependent tasks don't block worker threads; the continuation runs only after the dependency completes.

question: How do you handle a task that depends on MULTIPLE prior tasks?

choices.1: Merge the tasks into one before submitting
choices.2: Only one dependency is allowed per task
choices.3: Capture and get() each future
choices.4: Use a single shared future for all of them

explanation: E.g. a.get() and b.get() before combining.

question: What is the best general solution for complex dependencies in a robust threadpool?

choices.1: Call get() from the destructor
choices.2: Dependency-aware scheduling
choices.3: Block inside workers but add more threads
choices.4: Always use a single worker thread

explanation: Represent dependencies explicitly and only enqueue a task after its dependencies finish (e.g. submit_after), so workers aren't wasted waiting.

question: The simple move-the-future approach is fine under which conditions?

choices.1: Simple graphs with enough workers
choices.2: Only when tasks return void
choices.3: Any conditions; it never deadlocks
choices.4: Only with exactly one worker thread

explanation: Dependencies submitted before dependents, a simple dependency graph, enough worker threads, and tasks that don't recursively submit-and-wait on more pool tasks.

question: How does a threadpool make a submitted task waitable?

choices.1: By blocking submit() until the task finishes
choices.2: By writing the result to a global variable
choices.3: By returning the thread id
choices.4: By wrapping the task in a std::packaged_task

explanation: Storing it in the queue, and returning the task's std::future to the caller.

question: What does std::packaged_task provide?

choices.1: A callable wired to a future
choices.2: A lock-free queue
choices.3: Automatic thread creation
choices.4: A stop token for cancellation

explanation: The worker runs the packaged task while the submitter keeps the future.

question: What is a key restriction on calling get() on a std::future?

choices.1: It never blocks
choices.2: It can only be called once on a given future
choices.3: It must be called before the task starts
choices.4: It can only ever be called from the main thread

question: How are exceptions from a submitted task handled via std::future?

choices.1: They are only logged
choices.2: They are swallowed silently
choices.3: Rethrown at future.get()
choices.4: They terminate the program

explanation: They propagate automatically through the shared state.

question: How do you wait for multiple submitted tasks?

choices.1: Call pool.join()
choices.2: Collect futures, get() each
choices.3: Use a single future for all
choices.4: Submit them as one combined task

explanation: E.g. in a vector.

question: For a task with no return value, how is waiting handled?

choices.1: std::future<void>
choices.2: Poll a global flag
choices.3: Return a dummy int instead
choices.4: You cannot wait on void tasks

explanation: get() or wait() blocks until done and rethrows any exception.

question: If you only need to wait for a BATCH to finish (no return values), what C++20 primitive is suggested?

choices.1: std::mutex
choices.2: std::future<void> for the whole batch
choices.3: std::atomic<int> with busy-waiting
choices.4: std::latch

explanation: Initialize with the count, count_down() in each task, wait() to block.

question: What guarantee do std::atomic types provide?

choices.1: They make all surrounding code thread-safe automatically
choices.2: Race-free access when all accesses are atomic
choices.3: They replace the need for any memory ordering
choices.4: They guarantee numerical reproducibility

explanation: An atomic operation is indivisible with respect to other atomic operations on the same object.

question: Which statement about lock-freedom of std::atomic is correct?

choices.1: Every single std::atomic<T> is guaranteed lock-free
choices.2: Most std::atomic<T> are NOT guaranteed lock-free
choices.3: No atomic type is ever lock-free
choices.4: Only std::atomic<bool> is lock-free

explanation: Std::atomic_flag is the exception whose operations are required to be lock-free. Check is_lock_free() / is_always_lock_free.

question: What is the default memory order for most atomic operations?

choices.1: memory_order_relaxed
choices.2: memory_order_acquire
choices.3: memory_order_seq_cst
choices.4: memory_order_release

question: What is special about std::atomic_flag?

choices.1: It supports load(), store(), and arithmetic
choices.2: Guaranteed lock-free test-and-set
choices.3: It is the only atomic with fetch_add
choices.4: It is never lock-free

explanation: Two states with test_and_set/clear, required to be lock-free — meant for low-level spinlocks.

question: Why is compare_exchange_weak typically used inside a loop?

choices.1: Because it locks the atomic on each call
choices.2: It may fail even when the value matched
choices.3: Because it is slower than a single call
choices.4: Because it cannot update expected

explanation: Spurious failure is allowed, so the loop retries (and expected is updated with the current value on failure).

question: Which operations does std::atomic<bool> NOT support?

choices.1: load() and store()
choices.2: exchange() and compare_exchange_*
choices.3: fetch_add/fetch_sub and ++/--
choices.4: wait()/notify_* in C++20

explanation: It uses the primary template, not the integral specialization.

question: Which extra operations do atomic INTEGRAL specializations add over the primary template?

choices.1: None; they are identical
choices.2: Arithmetic and bitwise RMW
choices.3: Only pointer arithmetic
choices.4: Only floating-point min/max

explanation: fetch_add/fetch_sub and fetch_and/fetch_or/fetch_xor, plus operators like ++, +=, &=.

question: What does std::atomic_ref<T> provide?

choices.1: A new atomic object that owns its value
choices.2: An atomic VIEW over an existing object
choices.3: A reference-counted atomic pointer
choices.4: A lock-free guarantee for any T

explanation: You must avoid mixing ordinary non-atomic access to that same object concurrently. Non-atomic-typed.

question: What is the mental model for choosing atomics vs a mutex?

choices.1: Always use atomics; mutexes are obsolete
choices.2: Use atomics for a single small naturally-atomic value
choices.3: Use a mutex for counters but atomics for whole vectors
choices.4: They are interchangeable in all cases

explanation: Use a mutex when you must protect multiple values together as one invariant. Counter, flag, pointer.

question: What is the difference between compare_exchange_strong and compare_exchange_weak?

choices.1: Strong fails only if the value doesn't match expected
choices.2: Weak compares only two values while strong compares three
choices.3: Strong may fail spuriously; weak never fails
choices.4: They are identical in all respects

explanation: Weak may ALSO fail spuriously even when the value did match.

question: Why does the weak form exist at all?

choices.1: To save memory
choices.2: It maps directly onto LL/SC
choices.3: Because strong CAS is not implementable
choices.4: To avoid using the expected parameter

explanation: On load-linked/store-conditional architectures a reservation can be lost for reasons other than a value change, and weak exposes that cheaply.

question: On native CAS hardware like x86 (CMPXCHG), how do weak and strong compare?

choices.1: Weak is always much faster
choices.2: They often compile identically
choices.3: Strong is unavailable on x86
choices.4: Weak requires two instructions

explanation: There's no natural reason for a single CMPXCHG to fail when the value matched, so weak and strong usually emit the same instruction.

question: Why can an LL/SC store-conditional fail even when the value still matched expected?

choices.1: Because LL/SC always fails the first time
choices.2: Because the reservation can be lost for non-value reasons
choices.3: Because the store-conditional ignores the compared value entirely
choices.4: Because expected is never compared

explanation: Another core writing the reservation set, interrupts, context switches, reservation granularity, or microarchitectural limits.

question: On LL/SC hardware, why can compare_exchange_strong be more expensive than weak?

choices.1: Strong allocates memory
choices.2: Strong hides spurious SC failures
choices.3: Strong uses two separate reservations
choices.4: Strong disables the cache

explanation: Strong cannot expose a spurious store-conditional failure as failure, so it may need an internal retry loop — wasted work when the caller already has an outer loop.

question: Why is weak the natural choice inside a typical CAS loop?

choices.1: Because weak supposedly never fails inside a loop
choices.2: The algorithm's outer loop already retries
choices.3: Because weak skips the comparison
choices.4: Because strong cannot be used in loops

explanation: Another thread may legitimately win the race. So a spurious weak failure just causes a harmless retry.

question: When is compare_exchange_strong the better choice?

choices.1: Always, since it's simpler
choices.2: For one-shot, non-looping decisions
choices.3: Only inside tight retry loops
choices.4: Never at all; weak is always preferable

explanation: Where a spurious failure would change the meaning of the code — e.g. acquiring a lock with a single compare_exchange_strong.

question: What happens to the 'expected' argument when a compare-exchange fails?

choices.1: It is left unchanged
choices.2: It gets the current value
choices.3: It is set to the desired value
choices.4: It is set to zero

explanation: expected is updated with the atomic's current value.

question: What does an atomic fence (memory barrier) do?

choices.1: It atomically reads and writes a shared variable
choices.2: It controls memory ordering
choices.3: It provides mutual exclusion like a lock
choices.4: It makes ordinary variables atomic

explanation: It tells the compiler/CPU not to move certain memory operations across that point - without itself reading/writing a shared variable.

question: What does a release fence do?

choices.1: It prevents any later memory operations from being reordered before the fence
choices.2: It prevents earlier memory operations from being reordered after the fence
choices.3: It locks the atomic variable
choices.4: It makes all operations sequentially consistent

explanation: So writes before it are visible before a subsequent signal.

question: What does an acquire fence do?

choices.1: It prevents earlier operations from moving after the fence
choices.2: Later ops stay after the fence
choices.3: It flushes the cache
choices.4: It atomically increments a counter

explanation: Once you observe a signal, later reads can't be reordered before the synchronization point.

question: Does adding a fence make ordinary (non-atomic) shared variables safe to race on?

choices.1: Yes, a fence makes any nearby variable atomic
choices.2: No - a fence only constrains ordering
choices.3: Yes, but only on x86
choices.4: Only if the fence is seq_cst

explanation: Shared data still needs atomics, locks, or another synchronization mechanism to avoid a data race.

question: What does the note recommend over standalone fences for most code?

choices.1: Always prefer using seq_cst fences
choices.2: Ordering attached to atomic ops
choices.3: Use volatile variables instead
choices.4: Avoid atomics entirely

explanation: Store with memory_order_release, load with memory_order_acquire; reserve explicit fences for low-level lock-free code.

question: How does the same C++ fence differ across architectures like x86 vs ARM/POWER?

choices.1: It is illegal on ARM
choices.2: x86 has relatively strong ordering
choices.3: ARM fences are always cheaper than x86
choices.4: Architecture has no effect on fences

explanation: So many fences are cheap or compile-only; ARM/POWER are weakly ordered so fences often require real CPU barrier instructions.

question: Is a fence about mutual exclusion or ordering?

choices.1: Mutual exclusion - it locks a critical section
choices.2: Ordering
choices.3: Both equally
choices.4: Neither; it only affects the cache

explanation: It creates an ordering boundary and locks nothing.

question: For fences to enable inter-thread communication, what must they generally pair with?

choices.1: A mutex
choices.2: Matching atomics
choices.3: A condition variable
choices.4: A volatile read

explanation: So one thread's ordering is actually observed by another.

question: When does an evaluation A happen-before B?

choices.1: Only when A and B are on the same thread
choices.2: Sequenced, synchronizes, or transitive
choices.3: Only when both are seq_cst
choices.4: Whenever A executes earlier in wall-clock time

explanation: A is sequenced-before B (same thread), A synchronizes-with B, or the relation follows transitively through other evaluations.

question: How does a release store create a happens-before edge with an acquire load?

choices.1: It never does
choices.2: When the load reads the stored value
choices.3: Only if both use relaxed ordering
choices.4: Only if they both run on the same thread

explanation: If the acquire load reads the value written by the release store, the release store synchronizes-with the acquire load, creating a happens-before edge.

question: How is 'strongly happens-before' stricter than 'happens-before' for the synchronizes-with case?

choices.1: It requires the operations to be on the same thread
choices.2: Both ops must be seq_cst
choices.3: It requires relaxed ordering
choices.4: It only applies to non-atomic operations

explanation: For the synchronizes-with case, both operations must be memory_order_seq_cst.

question: For an acquire/release pair where the acquire reads the release (not seq_cst), which holds?

choices.1: Both happens-before and strongly happens-before hold
choices.2: happens-before only
choices.3: Neither holds
choices.4: Only strongly happens-before holds

explanation: Since they aren't both seq_cst. Not necessarily strongly happens-before.

question: Why does 'strongly happens-before' exist?

choices.1: To make even relaxed atomics synchronize
choices.2: To constrain the seq_cst total order S
choices.3: To replace happens-before entirely
choices.4: To order non-atomic writes

explanation: The single global total order over all memory_order_seq_cst operations must respect strongly happens-before, but not every ordinary happens-before relation.

question: What do relaxed atomic operations contribute to happens-before?

choices.1: They create the same happens-before edges as acquire/release
choices.2: Atomicity, but no edges
choices.3: They prevent all happens-before relationships
choices.4: They force a seq_cst total order

explanation: They are indivisible but create no synchronization or happens-before by themselves.

question: What is the simplest mental model contrasting the two relations?

choices.1: They are identical
choices.2: happens-before is the visibility relation (which writes a load can observe)
choices.3: happens-before constrains seq_cst; strongly happens-before is about visibility
choices.4: Both only matter for relaxed atomics

explanation: Strongly happens-before is the part of ordering strong enough to constrain sequential consistency.

question: How does happens-before affect which value an atomic load may observe?

choices.1: It has no effect on observed values
choices.2: That write or a later one
choices.3: The load always sees the initial value
choices.4: The load may see any write regardless of ordering

explanation: If a write happens-before a load, the load can't see anything earlier in the object's modification order.

question: When you write x.fetch_add(1) on a std::atomic<int>, what does the compiler actually do?

choices.1: It wraps the int in a hidden mutex for every access
choices.2: It makes the int a special compiler-only type having no runtime cost at all
choices.3: It always asserts a physical bus lock for the whole operation
choices.4: It emits machine instruction(s) the target CPU guarantees are atomic

explanation: E.g. an atomic RMW instruction or an LL/SC loop.

question: What does the C++ standard actually specify about an atomic operation, leaving the rest to the implementation?

choices.1: That every atomic is lock-free
choices.2: That all atomics use sequential consistency
choices.3: Indivisibility and the requested ordering
choices.4: The exact machine instructions to emit

explanation: The access is indivisible relative to other atomic accesses to that object, and memory ordering matches the requested std::memory_order — instruction choice is the implementation's.

question: Although a std::atomic<int> may be only 4 bytes, at what granularity does the cache-coherency system typically arbitrate ownership?

choices.1: Per individual byte
choices.2: Per virtual memory page
choices.3: Per cache line (commonly 64 bytes)
choices.4: Per 4-byte word matching the atomic's size

explanation: Contains the atomic plus surrounding bytes.

question: In the MESI protocol, what does the Modified (M) state mean for a cache line?

choices.1: Multiple cores hold clean copies
choices.2: This cache's copy is invalid and must be refetched
choices.3: This core has the only valid, dirty copy
choices.4: This core has the only valid, clean copy

explanation: Main memory may be stale.

question: With write-back caching, after x.store(42, release) succeeds in a core's cache, what is true of main memory (DRAM)?

choices.1: The value is written only to DRAM, never cached
choices.2: The store is lost unless flushed explicitly
choices.3: DRAM is immediately updated to 42 before the store returns
choices.4: DRAM may still hold the stale value

explanation: The new value can live in the core's cache in Modified state, and coherency supplies it to other readers later.

question: On modern x86, what is the common-case behaviour of a `lock xadd`/`lock cmpxchg` on cached write-back memory within one cache line?

choices.1: It disables interrupts on all cores
choices.2: It always asserts a physical bus lock (LOCK#) for the whole system
choices.3: Cache locking, not a bus lock
choices.4: It falls back to a software mutex

explanation: The core takes exclusive ownership of the line and coherency blocks other cores — usually without asserting the bus lock.

question: Which situation still forces a real x86 bus lock rather than cheap cache locking?

choices.1: A relaxed-ordered load
choices.2: Any fetch_add regardless of alignment
choices.3: A naturally aligned atomic on write-back memory
choices.4: Split locks and uncacheable memory

explanation: An access crossing cache lines, or memory types other than write-back (UC/WC/WP/WT).

question: On classic ARM/RISC hardware without single-instruction atomics, how is a fetch_add typically implemented?

choices.1: A single locked add instruction just like x86
choices.2: A load-linked/store-conditional retry loop
choices.3: By disabling the cache
choices.4: By calling into the OS kernel

explanation: Ldaxr, add, stlxr, branch-back-on-failure. Exclusive load/store.

question: What do ARM's Large System Extensions (LSE) add?

choices.1: Hardware transactional memory only
choices.2: Single-instruction atomics
choices.3: A larger cache line size
choices.4: They remove the need for cache coherency

explanation: E.g. CAS, atomic add. So the compiler can emit one-instruction RMWs instead of an LL/SC loop.

question: What is the difference between cache coherency and memory ordering?

choices.1: Coherency is a compiler concept; ordering is a hardware concept
choices.2: They are two names for the same mechanism
choices.3: Coherency only matters for relaxed atomics; ordering only for seq_cst
choices.4: One location versus across locations

explanation: Coherency makes cores agree on the latest value of one cache line; memory ordering governs how operations on different locations may be observed.

question: Does memory_order_relaxed avoid the cost of obtaining exclusive cache-line ownership for an RMW under contention?

choices.1: Yes, relaxed makes the operation non-atomic and therefore free
choices.2: Yes, relaxed RMWs skip coherency entirely
choices.3: No - relaxed removes ordering constraints
choices.4: No, but only because relaxed forces a bus lock

explanation: Not the need for exclusive ownership; a contended relaxed RMW still causes cache-line ping-pong.

question: Why can two atomics on the same cache line slow each other down even when different threads touch different variables (false sharing)?

choices.1: Coherency tracks the whole cache line
choices.2: The two atomics share the same memory address
choices.3: Atomics are globally serialized regardless of address
choices.4: The compiler merges the two atomics into one

explanation: So a write to one variable invalidates the other core's copy of the line; fix with alignas(64) / cache-line padding.

question: Why does a spinlock built on atomic_flag often spin on a relaxed test() before attempting test_and_set()?

choices.1: To avoid needing acquire/release semantics anywhere
choices.2: Because test() is more accurate than test_and_set()
choices.3: Because test_and_set cannot be used in a loop
choices.4: Reads share the line; RMWs demand it

explanation: Plain reads keep the line in Shared state, while test_and_set needs exclusive ownership; read-spinning first reduces failed RMWs and coherency traffic.

question: How do 'lock-free' and 'wait-free' differ at the hardware level per the note?

choices.1: They are identical guarantees
choices.2: Wait-free allows unbounded retries; lock-free does not
choices.3: Lock-free means the operation has zero cost
choices.4: Lock-free means the system as a whole makes progress

explanation: Wait-free means each thread completes in a bounded number of steps. An LL/SC store-conditional may fail and retry many times under contention.

question: According to the C++ memory model, when do two accesses to the same non-atomic object form a data race (undefined behaviour)?

choices.1: Whenever any object is shared between threads, even with synchronization
choices.2: Only when both accesses are writes through different pointers
choices.3: Whenever two threads read the same object simultaneously
choices.4: Concurrent, a write, no ordering

explanation: Potentially concurrent accesses, at least one write, at least one non-atomic, and neither happens-before the other.

question: What is a 'modification order' in the C++ atomics model?

choices.1: The order in which threads were created
choices.2: The per-atomic-object total order of all writes to that one object
choices.3: A single global total order across all atomic objects in the program
choices.4: The compiler's instruction-scheduling order

question: With two separate relaxed atomics x and y (both starting 0), thread 1 stores x=1 then loads y, thread 2 stores y=1 then loads x. Is the outcome r1==0 && r2==0 allowed?

choices.1: Yes - x and y each have their own modification order
choices.2: No - the stores must be visible before the loads on any hardware
choices.3: No - relaxed atomics still impose one global total order across all objects
choices.4: Only if the two atomics share a cache line

explanation: There is no single global order relaxed ops must agree on.

question: Even with memory_order_relaxed, what 'per-object coherence' guarantee still holds for a single atomic? (Suppose x's modification order is 0->1->2.)

choices.1: No going backward within a thread
choices.2: None; relaxed means any read can return any prior value in any order
choices.3: Relaxed loads always return the initial value 0
choices.4: All threads must observe x change at the same wall-clock instant

explanation: After a relaxed load reads 2, a later load in the same thread cannot read the older 1.

question: Why are relaxed atomic operations described as NOT being synchronization operations?

choices.1: Because they are not actually atomic
choices.2: They create no happens-before edges
choices.3: Because the compiler ignores them entirely
choices.4: Because they require a mutex internally

explanation: They provide atomicity and per-object modification order, but no synchronizes-with relationships between threads.

question: Can a compiler reorder two relaxed stores to the SAME atomic object x that are sequenced in one thread (x=1 then x=2)?

choices.1: No — per-object coherence forbids it
choices.2: Yes, relaxed lets the compiler do anything
choices.3: Yes, because they target the same address
choices.4: Only on weakly-ordered hardware

explanation: x's modification order must be 0->1->2; it cannot appear as 0->2->1.

question: How is the happens-before relation built in the C++ model?

choices.1: From the combined modification order of every single object
choices.2: From sequenced-before, synchronizes-with, and transitivity
choices.3: Purely from wall-clock timestamps
choices.4: Only from mutex lock/unlock pairs

explanation: E.g. release/acquire. Single-thread order.

question: In what restricted situation does the standard guarantee sequential consistency in the simple, intuitive sense?

choices.1: Only for single-threaded programs
choices.2: For programs that use only relaxed atomics
choices.3: For any program that uses atomics at all
choices.4: Race-free plus all-seq_cst programs

explanation: Data-race-free programs using exclusively memory_order_seq_cst atomics behave sequentially consistently.

question: What are the three logical steps of an atomic read-modify-write (RMW) operation, performed indivisibly?

choices.1: Read, compute, write back
choices.2: Allocate, then copy, then free
choices.3: Load, fence, store
choices.4: Lock, compute, unlock

explanation: Read the current value, compute a new value from it, write it back — all appearing as one indivisible step.

question: Why is `x.store(x.load() + 1)` wrong for a shared counter where fetch_add(1) is right?

choices.1: store and load are not atomic individually
choices.2: Two atomics don't make one RMW
choices.3: fetch_add is slower but always correct
choices.4: load() returns the new value, causing double counting

explanation: The load and store are individually atomic, but two threads can read the same old value and lose an increment.

question: What does fetch_add return?

choices.1: The old value
choices.2: A boolean success flag
choices.3: The new value after the addition
choices.4: The amount added

explanation: The value immediately before the operation.

question: For compare_exchange, how do success and failure differ in what kind of atomic operation occurs?

choices.1: Success is an RMW; failure a load
choices.2: Both are plain non-atomic operations
choices.3: Both are always full RMWs
choices.4: Success is a load; failure is a store

explanation: A failed one is only an atomic load (and updates 'expected' with the observed value). Only a successful compare_exchange performs the read-modify-write.

question: Why must the FAILURE memory order of compare_exchange not be release or acq_rel?

choices.1: Because release is slower on failure
choices.2: Because failure is undefined behaviour otherwise
choices.3: Failure is only a load
choices.4: Because the failure order must always be seq_cst

explanation: release/acq_rel imply a store, which a failed exchange doesn't perform.

question: How do atomic RMW operations relate to a release sequence?

choices.1: They have no relationship to release sequences
choices.2: Only seq_cst RMWs can be in a release sequence
choices.3: They terminate any release sequence immediately
choices.4: RMWs extend a release sequence

explanation: Atomic RMW operations on the same object continue a sequence begun by a release store, so a later acquire reading from them still synchronizes with the original release.

question: In a lock-free push using compare_exchange_weak in a loop, why does the loop work correctly on spurious or contended failure?

choices.1: Failure refreshes 'expected'
choices.2: Because failure switches to compare_exchange_strong automatically
choices.3: Because weak CAS never fails
choices.4: Because the node is freed and reallocated each time

explanation: compare_exchange updates old_head with the current value, so the retry works against the latest head.

question: What is the role of the synchronizes-with relationship?

choices.1: The cross-thread edge in happens-before
choices.2: It guarantees two threads run at the same time
choices.3: It replaces the need for atomics
choices.4: It orders operations within a single thread

explanation: Combined with sequenced-before and transitivity, it builds happens-before so one thread's work becomes visible to another.

question: For a release store to synchronize-with an acquire load on the same atomic, what must be true?

choices.1: The value must come from the release
choices.2: The store must happen later in wall-clock time
choices.3: They must be on the same thread
choices.4: Both must use relaxed ordering

explanation: Or from its release sequence.

question: A release store publishes data, but the consumer's acquire load reads the OLD (pre-store) value. Is there a synchronizes-with edge?

choices.1: Yes, but only on x86
choices.2: Yes, the acquire always synchronizes regardless of value read
choices.3: Only if the data is also atomic
choices.4: No — wrong value, no edge

explanation: The acquire didn't read the release's value, so no synchronizes-with is established.

question: Does a release store synchronize-with a RELAXED load that reads its value?

choices.1: Only if they are on the same thread
choices.2: No — the load must be acquire
choices.3: Yes, reading the value is sufficient
choices.4: Yes, because the store was release

explanation: A relaxed load reading the value does not establish synchronizes-with; acquire (or seq_cst) is required.

question: A release store to x is followed by a relaxed fetch_add on x (making it 2); a third thread's acquire load reads 2. Can it synchronize with the original release?

choices.1: No, release sequences require seq_cst
choices.2: Only if the third thread also writes x
choices.3: No, because the RMW used relaxed ordering
choices.4: Yes — the RMW continues the sequence

explanation: A relaxed fetch_add on the same object extends the release sequence, so an acquire reading from it synchronizes with the original release store.

question: When does synchronizes-with also contribute to 'strongly happens-before'?

choices.1: Only when both are seq_cst
choices.2: Always, whenever it contributes to happens-before
choices.3: Only for relaxed operations
choices.4: Never; strongly happens-before ignores synchronizes-with

explanation: Both synchronized operations must be sequentially consistent atomics.

question: What does a release operation guarantee about earlier memory operations?

choices.1: It allows them to be reordered after it for speed
choices.2: Earlier ops can't move after it
choices.3: It discards them
choices.4: It makes them atomic

explanation: Prior writes are published before the atomic value is observed.

question: What does an acquire operation guarantee about later memory operations?

choices.1: Later ops can't move before it
choices.2: It allows later reads to move before the load
choices.3: It flushes the cache to DRAM
choices.4: It makes later writes atomic

explanation: After observing the released value, the thread sees the prior writes.

question: What is the central rule of acquire-release synchronization?

choices.1: Any two atomic ops synchronize regardless of value
choices.2: Release and acquire must use the same thread
choices.3: Acquire-release only works with seq_cst
choices.4: Observation orders the two threads

explanation: If an acquire observes a value written by a release, everything before the release happens-before everything after the acquire.

question: Why must the acquire and release operations act on the SAME atomic object?

choices.1: For performance only
choices.2: The atomic object is the synchronization channel
choices.3: They don't have to; any two atomics synchronize
choices.4: Because different atomics share a modification order

explanation: An acquire on one atomic does not acquire releases made to a different, unrelated atomic.

question: How does acquire-release ordering chain across three threads (A releases, B acquires then releases, C acquires)?

choices.1: By transitivity of happens-before
choices.2: Only if all three use the same atomic variable
choices.3: Only with seq_cst on every operation
choices.4: It doesn't chain; each pair is isolated

explanation: A's writes -> B observes -> B publishes -> C observes, so C sees A's earlier writes too.

question: In a spinlock built on atomic_flag, why does lock() use acquire and unlock() use release?

choices.1: For symmetry only, with no semantic effect
choices.2: lock should really use release and unlock should use acquire
choices.3: unlock's release publishes the critical section's writes
choices.4: Both should be relaxed for speed

explanation: Lock's acquire ensures the new owner sees the previous owner's writes.

question: How does acquire-release differ from sequential consistency?

choices.1: seq_cst is weaker than acquire-release
choices.2: Acquire-release provides one single global order, whereas seq_cst does not
choices.3: Acquire-release orders only through matching synchronization edges
choices.4: They are identical

explanation: Seq_cst additionally puts all seq_cst operations into a single global total order (stronger, sometimes more expensive).

question: For a read-modify-write that must both consume prior state and publish new state, which order is appropriate?

choices.1: memory_order_acq_rel
choices.2: memory_order_release only
choices.3: memory_order_acquire only
choices.4: memory_order_relaxed

explanation: Acquire on the read, release on the write.

question: What is the key difference between memory_order_consume and memory_order_acquire on a load?

choices.1: consume is for stores; acquire is for loads
choices.2: They are identical in every respect
choices.3: consume orders ALL later operations; acquire orders only dependent ones
choices.4: acquire orders all later operations in the consumer thread

explanation: Consume orders only operations that carry a dependency on the loaded value.

question: After p = published.load(consume), which access is guaranteed ordered, and which is not?

choices.1: Neither is ordered
choices.2: p->payload (depends on p) is ordered
choices.3: Both p->payload and an unrelated side_counter load are guaranteed
choices.4: Only the unrelated side_counter load is ordered

explanation: An unrelated side_counter.load(relaxed) (no dependency on p) is not generally protected.

question: What makes p->payload 'dependent' on a consume-loaded pointer p?

choices.1: It is written before the load
choices.2: It is an atomic access
choices.3: Its address needs p's value
choices.4: It uses the same memory order

explanation: The address of p->payload cannot be computed without p, so it carries a dependency.

question: What does the note recommend for portable real-world C++ regarding consume?

choices.1: Usually use memory_order_acquire instead
choices.2: Always use consume for pointer-based publication
choices.3: Use seq_cst for all loads to avoid consume
choices.4: Use relaxed instead of consume

explanation: Consume is hard to specify/implement and current compilers essentially map it to acquire.

question: What is the status of memory_order_consume per the WG21 direction noted?

choices.1: It is the recommended default for loads
choices.2: Slated to become acquire
choices.3: It was removed in C++20
choices.4: It is faster than acquire on all hardware

explanation: Its current definition is widely considered not useful.

question: What does memory_order_relaxed guarantee?

choices.1: Atomicity and per-object order only
choices.2: Full ordering of all surrounding memory operations
choices.3: A single global order across all atomics
choices.4: Nothing; it is non-atomic

explanation: No ordering or visibility guarantees for other memory.

question: Why is using a relaxed store as a 'ready' flag for published data unsafe?

choices.1: Relaxed flags deadlock
choices.2: No publication of the prior writes
choices.3: Relaxed stores are not actually atomic at all
choices.4: Relaxed stores always tear

explanation: A consumer seeing ready==true via a relaxed load is not guaranteed to see the earlier non-atomic writes to the data.

question: Which is a GOOD use case for relaxed atomics?

choices.1: Implementing a lock's acquire/release
choices.2: Signaling that initialization is complete
choices.3: Publishing a fully-initialized object to other threads
choices.4: Counters and ID generators

explanation: Where only the atomic value itself matters — nothing else is being published.

question: Why is 'relaxed means random' a misconception?

choices.1: Because per-object coherence still holds
choices.2: Because relaxed forbids any reordering at all
choices.3: Because relaxed acts as a full memory barrier
choices.4: Because relaxed is actually the strongest order

explanation: For one atomic, if a reader sees value 2 it can't subsequently see the older value 1.

question: What is the rule of thumb for when relaxed is appropriate?

choices.1: Whenever you want maximum performance, regardless of use
choices.2: When nothing else depends on it
choices.3: Only inside a mutex
choices.4: Only for boolean flags

explanation: Only when the atomic doesn't communicate ownership, readiness, initialization, or protection of other data.

question: What does memory_order_seq_cst provide?

choices.1: One global order everyone observes
choices.2: Ordering only for the single atomic touched
choices.3: Atomicity only, like relaxed
choices.4: A per-object order but no cross-object order

explanation: All seq_cst operations from all threads behave as if placed in a single total order.

question: In the classic two-flag test (T1: store x=1 then load y; T2: store y=1 then load x), all seq_cst - is r1==0 && r2==0 possible?

choices.1: No — that would be a cycle
choices.2: Only if x and y share a cache line
choices.3: Yes, on weakly-ordered hardware
choices.4: Yes, seq_cst doesn't constrain different objects

explanation: seq_cst places all four operations in one global order, and both-zero would contradict it.

question: How can seq_cst be summarized relative to acquire/release?

choices.1: It only adds atomicity
choices.2: Acquire/release plus a global order
choices.3: It is weaker than acquire/release
choices.4: It is identical to relaxed plus a fence

explanation: All seq_cst operations additionally participate in one globally consistent total order.

question: Does seq_cst make ordinary non-atomic concurrent access safe?

choices.1: Yes, but only for integers
choices.2: Yes, if at least one seq_cst atomic exists in the program
choices.3: Yes, seq_cst protects all nearby data
choices.4: No - it only orders atomic operations

explanation: Unsynchronized non-atomic concurrent access (e.g. two threads doing data++) is still a data race.

question: When does the note recommend using seq_cst?

choices.1: When correctness outranks peak speed
choices.2: Never; acquire/release is always better
choices.3: Always, even in the hottest loops
choices.4: Only for relaxed counters

explanation: Or while still designing/debugging the synchronization — it gives the simplest mental model.

question: What is the difference between a mutex and a lock in modern C++?

choices.1: A mutex is the synchronization object
choices.2: A lock is the primitive; a mutex is the wrapper
choices.3: A mutex is only for reading; a lock is for writing
choices.4: They are the same thing

explanation: A lock is usually an RAII wrapper that locks/unlocks the mutex for you.

question: Why prefer std::lock_guard over manual m.lock()/m.unlock()?

choices.1: RAII makes it exception-safe
choices.2: It supports timed locking
choices.3: It is faster at runtime
choices.4: It also allows recursive locking

explanation: The mutex is released automatically at scope exit even if the body throws or returns early.

question: When should you choose std::unique_lock over std::lock_guard?

choices.1: Only when locking multiple mutexes
choices.2: For every lock, since it's strictly better
choices.3: Only for shared/read locks
choices.4: When you need flexibility

explanation: Deferred locking, early unlock/relock, timed locking, movability, or condition-variable compatibility.

question: What is the main advantage of std::scoped_lock when locking multiple mutexes?

choices.1: It supports early unlock
choices.2: Deadlock-free multi-mutex locking
choices.3: It is movable
choices.4: It additionally allows shared/read access

explanation: It locks all the mutexes using a deadlock-avoidance algorithm (like std::lock), avoiding lock-ordering deadlocks.

question: What does std::shared_mutex provide?

choices.1: Recursive locking for one thread
choices.2: Timed locking only
choices.3: Only exclusive locking, like std::mutex
choices.4: Shared and exclusive modes

explanation: Multiple concurrent readers via shared_lock, one writer via unique_lock.

question: Why must you re-check the condition after releasing a shared_lock and taking a unique_lock on a std::shared_mutex?

choices.1: Another thread may act in the gap
choices.2: Because shared_lock is not RAII
choices.3: Because unique_lock is slower
choices.4: Because the mutex becomes recursive

explanation: Standard shared mutexes don't support direct shared-to-exclusive upgrade, so between unlocking shared and locking exclusive another thread may have changed the data.

question: What is a common downside of std::recursive_mutex?

choices.1: It cannot be locked at all
choices.2: It can hide design problems
choices.3: It only works with shared_lock
choices.4: It is not exception-safe

explanation: Redesign is often cleaner. Making it harder to reason about which function protects the object's invariant.

question: What does the std::defer_lock tag do when constructing a unique_lock?

choices.1: Locks immediately and adopts ownership
choices.2: Tries to lock without blocking
choices.3: Construct now, lock later
choices.4: Assumes the mutex is already locked

explanation: The mutex is not locked at construction; call lock() when ready.

question: What does std::try_lock(a, b) return?

choices.1: true if all locked, false otherwise
choices.2: The number of mutexes locked
choices.3: A pointer to the failed mutex
choices.4: -1 if all locks were acquired

explanation: Otherwise the zero-based index of the object it failed to lock (releasing any it already took).

question: Why is `std::lock_guard<std::mutex>{m};` (an unnamed temporary) a bug?

choices.1: It fails to compile
choices.2: The temporary unlocks immediately
choices.3: It locks the mutex twice
choices.4: It secretly makes the mutex recursive

explanation: It is destroyed at the end of the full expression, so the mutex is not held for the scope.

question: Per the note, can std::mutex::try_lock fail even when the mutex is currently free?

choices.1: Yes — spurious failure is allowed
choices.2: No, only timed mutexes can fail
choices.3: Yes, but only on recursive mutexes
choices.4: No, try_lock is always exact

explanation: Treat failure only as 'not acquired', not as proof another thread owns it.

question: What does a semaphore's counter represent?

choices.1: Whether the resource is dirty
choices.2: The owning thread's ID
choices.3: The number of threads currently blocked
choices.4: How many permits are available

explanation: Units of a resource.

question: What do acquire() and release() do on a semaphore?

choices.1: Both increment the counter
choices.2: acquire decrements the counter (waiting if it is 0)
choices.3: acquire increments and then waits, while release decrements
choices.4: acquire locks a mutex; release unlocks it

explanation: Release increments it and wakes a waiter.

question: What is a key ownership difference between a semaphore and a mutex?

choices.1: A semaphore must be released by the same thread that acquired it
choices.2: A mutex has no owner; a semaphore does
choices.3: A semaphore has no strict ownership
choices.4: Neither has any concept of ownership

explanation: One thread can acquire and a different thread can release, which makes it useful for signaling.

question: In the producer-consumer example, what is the division of labour between the semaphore and the mutex?

choices.1: The semaphore signals that an item is available
choices.2: The semaphore protects the queue; the mutex signals availability
choices.3: The semaphore replaces the queue entirely
choices.4: Both protect the queue redundantly

explanation: The mutex protects the queue's internal state.

question: What is the rule of thumb for choosing a semaphore vs a mutex?

choices.1: Use a semaphore for protecting a single variable; a mutex for counting
choices.2: Use a mutex to protect shared data
choices.3: They are interchangeable in all cases
choices.4: Always prefer a binary_semaphore over a mutex

explanation: Use a semaphore to count or signal availability (a binary_semaphore can act mutex-like but a mutex has clearer ownership).

question: What deadlock pattern does std::lock / std::scoped_lock prevent?

choices.1: Circular wait across mutexes
choices.2: A thread locking the same mutex twice
choices.3: Priority inversion
choices.4: A thread forgetting to unlock

explanation: Threads acquiring the same mutexes in different orders, each holding one while blocking forever on another.

question: What is the core deadlock-avoidance strategy used to lock multiple mutexes?

choices.1: Use a single global lock for all mutexes
choices.2: Blockingly lock one mutex, try_lock the rest
choices.3: Sort all mutexes by address and lock in that fixed global order
choices.4: Lock them all simultaneously in hardware

explanation: If any try_lock fails, unlock everything and retry (often starting from the failed mutex).

question: Why does the retry strategy often start from the mutex that caused the failure?

choices.1: To impose a fixed global order
choices.2: Because try_lock cannot be called twice on the same mutex
choices.3: Because the first mutex is always the contended one
choices.4: Block where you failed, then retry

explanation: Waiting on the contended mutex first avoids repeatedly failing the same try_lock in a loop.

question: What does std::scoped_lock add on top of std::lock's deadlock-avoidance?

choices.1: RAII on top of std::lock
choices.2: Support for recursive locking
choices.3: Timed locking
choices.4: A globally fixed lock order

explanation: Automatic unlocking, exception safety, and cleaner syntax — the deadlock prevention itself comes from std::lock.

question: What is a limitation of std::lock's deadlock prevention?

choices.1: It only works with exactly two locked mutexes
choices.2: It only covers mutexes locked in that call
choices.3: It requires a global mutex registry
choices.4: It cannot be used with RAII

explanation: Locking another mutex separately while holding them can still form a larger circular wait.

question: Why is std::lock safer than a manual fixed lock order across a large codebase?

choices.1: It cannot deadlock
choices.2: It never blocks
choices.3: It is faster
choices.4: It sorts mutexes by name

explanation: A manual fixed order only works if every call site uses the same order — fragile at scale. std::lock stays deadlock-free even when different code passes the mutexes in different orders.

question: What is the key behavioural difference between std::thread and std::jthread on destruction?

choices.1: std::thread calls std::terminate() if still joinable
choices.2: Both call std::terminate if joinable
choices.3: std::thread joins automatically; std::jthread calls terminate
choices.4: Both detach automatically

explanation: Std::jthread requests stop and then joins.

question: Does joinable() mean the thread is currently running?

choices.1: It means join() has already been called
choices.2: No — it may have already finished
choices.3: Yes, joinable means actively executing
choices.4: It means the thread has been detached

explanation: joinable means the object still represents a thread (owns a join/detach responsibility); the function may already have returned.

question: Why does a std::thread destroyed while still joinable (e.g. when an exception skips join()) call std::terminate?

choices.1: Because join() always throws
choices.2: Because the OS forcibly killed the process
choices.3: Because the thread leaked memory
choices.4: The standard defines it to terminate

explanation: std::thread does not auto-join or auto-detach; a joinable thread at destruction calls std::terminate(). Use std::jthread or a scope guard for exception safety.

question: What memory-visibility guarantee does a successful join() provide?

choices.1: The worker's writes become visible
choices.2: It guarantees visibility even before join is called
choices.3: None; join is purely a wait
choices.4: It makes all the worker's variables atomic

explanation: Thread completion synchronizes-with the return from join().

question: How does std::jthread pass cancellation to its callable?

choices.1: A stop_token first parameter
choices.2: It calls pthread_cancel
choices.3: It sets a global flag the callable must poll
choices.4: It throws an exception into the thread

explanation: jthread passes its token automatically; the worker checks st.stop_requested().

question: Why are std::thread objects move-only (copy deleted)?

choices.1: Thread ownership must be unique
choices.2: Because copying would duplicate the OS thread
choices.3: To save memory
choices.4: Because threads cannot be stored in containers

explanation: Two thread objects must not own the same thread of execution; ownership transfers only by move.

question: What does std::thread::hardware_concurrency() return?

choices.1: The number of currently running threads
choices.2: The precise exact number of threads you must create
choices.3: The number of CPU sockets
choices.4: A hint for the number of hardware thread contexts

explanation: It may return 0 if not computable, so code should provide a fallback.

question: What happens if an exception escapes the initial function of a std::thread or std::jthread?

choices.1: It detaches the thread
choices.2: It calls std::terminate()
choices.3: It propagates to the joining thread
choices.4: It is silently swallowed

explanation: Exceptions are not auto-propagated, so catch inside the thread and convey via exception_ptr/promise.

question: What is the danger of detaching a thread that captures local data by reference?

choices.1: Detached threads cannot access captured data at all
choices.2: The data becomes atomic automatically
choices.3: detach() throws if data is captured
choices.4: It may outlive the referenced data

explanation: The detached thread can use the data after destruction (dangling); pass by value or via shared_ptr instead.

question: What does a condition variable do, and what does it NOT store?

choices.1: Sleeping until state may have changed
choices.2: It also stores the condition and the data
choices.3: It protects shared data like a mutex
choices.4: It queues notifications as permits

explanation: It does NOT store the condition itself — the condition lives in your shared data.

question: Why must cv.wait be used with a predicate (or a while loop), not a bare wait?

choices.1: For performance only
choices.2: Because a thread can experience spurious wakeups
choices.3: Because the predicate itself notifies other threads
choices.4: Because wait cannot take a lock otherwise

explanation: The predicate re-checks the actual state. Wake with the condition still false.

question: What is a 'lost wakeup', and how does putting the condition in shared state prevent it?

choices.1: A wakeup that wakes far too many threads at once; use notify_one
choices.2: A thread that never calls notify
choices.3: A notification that fires before the consumer starts waiting
choices.4: A spurious wakeup that returns early

explanation: A predicate over shared state lets the waiter notice the change and not sleep. So a bare wait sleeps forever.

question: Why must wait() atomically release the mutex and put the thread to sleep?

choices.1: To avoid holding the mutex too long
choices.2: Because the mutex must stay locked during sleep
choices.3: To make the predicate atomic
choices.4: A gap would swallow the notification

explanation: Between deciding to wait and sleeping, a notification could arrive and be missed forever.

question: When should you use notify_one vs notify_all?

choices.1: Always notify_all for safety
choices.2: notify_one when only one thread can make progress
choices.3: notify_one wakes everyone while notify_all wakes just one
choices.4: They are identical

explanation: Notify_all when multiple threads may proceed or during shutdown. E.g. one item pushed.

question: Does a condition variable remember a notify_one() if no thread is waiting?

choices.1: No — it is simply lost
choices.2: Yes, it queues all notifications
choices.3: Yes, the next waiter consumes it
choices.4: Yes, it stores it as a permit like a semaphore

explanation: Notifications aren't queued; the real state must be stored separately (e.g. a bool).

question: When does notify_one() hand the mutex to the woken thread?

choices.1: Immediately; the woken thread resumes already holding the mutex
choices.2: Only if notify_all is used
choices.3: It does not - notify_one only wakes the thread
choices.4: The mutex is never reacquired

explanation: The woken thread must still compete to reacquire the mutex before wait() returns.

question: What does a std::future<T> represent?

choices.1: A queue of tasks
choices.2: A mutex protecting a value
choices.3: A thread of execution
choices.4: A handle to a later value

explanation: Connected to a shared state that will eventually hold the result or an exception.

question: What three things can a future's shared state hold?

choices.1: Only a return value
choices.2: A value, an exception, or a not-ready status
choices.3: A thread id, a mutex, and a counter
choices.4: A promise, a packaged_task, and an async handle

question: What does future.get() do?

choices.1: Starts the task and returns immediately
choices.2: Returns without blocking
choices.3: Wait, retrieve, invalidate
choices.4: Can be called any number of times on a std::future

explanation: It blocks until ready, rethrows any stored exception, and leaves a std::future invalid.

question: How does std::shared_future differ from std::future?

choices.1: It is copyable with repeatable get()
choices.2: It cannot store exceptions
choices.3: It is strictly move-only and single-use
choices.4: It runs the task eagerly

explanation: get() can be called multiple times (often returning a const reference), so many consumers can read the same result.

question: What is a 'broken promise' as observed through a future?

choices.1: A promise whose value happened to be set twice
choices.2: Destroyed without a value or exception
choices.3: A future that was never waited on
choices.4: A future copied without share()

explanation: The future becomes ready with a std::future_error.

question: What does std::async(std::launch::deferred, task) do?

choices.1: Nothing until get(), then inline
choices.2: Runs the task immediately on a new thread
choices.3: Schedules the task on a thread pool
choices.4: Runs the task on the GPU

explanation: The task runs synchronously in the calling thread when get() or wait() is called.

question: Why might std::async(task) (no launch policy) not start a new thread?

choices.1: It requires a packaged_task first
choices.2: The default policy may defer
choices.3: It never starts threads
choices.4: It always defers

explanation: Without an explicit policy the implementation chooses async OR deferred; use std::launch::async to force a new asynchronous task.

question: What is the key difference between std::latch and std::barrier?

choices.1: A latch has phases; a barrier does not
choices.2: They are identical
choices.3: A latch is reusable, whereas a barrier is single-use
choices.4: A latch is single-use (opens permanently at zero)

explanation: A barrier is reusable and resets automatically after each phase.

question: How does a std::latch work?

choices.1: It starts with a counter
choices.2: It counts up to a target
choices.3: It resets after reaching zero
choices.4: It blocks all threads until one calls open()

explanation: Count_down() decrements it, and threads in wait() proceed once the counter reaches zero (permanently).

question: What does latch.arrive_and_wait() do?

choices.1: Only waits, without decrementing at all
choices.2: Decrements the counter and then waits
choices.3: Resets the latch
choices.4: Increments the counter

explanation: Equivalent to count_down() followed by wait().

question: What does a std::barrier's completion function do?

choices.1: Once-per-phase work between phases
choices.2: It runs only when the barrier is destroyed
choices.3: It runs once per thread on arrival
choices.4: It cancels the barrier

explanation: It runs after all threads arrive but before they are released — for shared work done exactly once.

question: What does barrier.arrive_and_drop() do?

choices.1: Drops the barrier entirely for all threads
choices.2: Resets the barrier to its initial count
choices.3: Arrive and shrink future phases
choices.4: Arrives and waits like arrive_and_wait

explanation: It counts for the current phase and permanently reduces the expected arrival count.

question: For repeated phase-based synchronization (e.g. finish phase 1 together, then phase 2 together), which tool fits?

choices.1: std::counting_semaphore
choices.2: std::barrier — it's reusable
choices.3: std::latch, because it can be reset
choices.4: A single std::future

explanation: A latch is single-use; a barrier resets each phase.

question: What is the core difference between std::promise and std::packaged_task?

choices.1: packaged_task cannot capture exceptions
choices.2: promise wraps a callable; packaged_task does not
choices.3: Only promise produces a future
choices.4: A promise is a manual result provider

explanation: A packaged_task wraps a callable so its return value/exception is captured automatically into the future. You call set_value/set_exception.

question: How does std::promise handle exceptions?

choices.1: You must set_exception yourself
choices.2: It ignores them
choices.3: It catches all of them automatically
choices.4: It terminates the program

explanation: It does not auto-catch; catch and call p.set_exception(std::current_exception()), which is rethrown at future.get().

question: Conceptually, what does invoking a packaged_task do internally?

choices.1: Roughly: try { promise.set_value(callable(args...))
choices.2: It only stores the callable for later, never running it
choices.3: It copies the result to every shared_future
choices.4: It blocks until a promise is set elsewhere

explanation: } catch (...) { promise.set_exception(std::current_exception()); }.

question: Why are std::promise and std::packaged_task move-only?

choices.1: The shared state must be unique
choices.2: Because copying them would deadlock
choices.3: To save memory
choices.4: Because they store raw threads

explanation: Each owns a shared state that must not be duplicated.

question: How many times can you normally call get_future() on a promise or packaged_task?

choices.1: Exactly twice
choices.2: As many times as you like
choices.3: Never; the future is always returned automatically
choices.4: Once; calling it again throws std::future_error

explanation: Use shared_future via .share() for multiple consumers.

question: Why is std::packaged_task especially useful for task queues and thread pools?

choices.1: It cannot be moved, so it stays in place
choices.2: A schedulable unit with a future
choices.3: It runs on the GPU
choices.4: It auto-creates threads

explanation: It wraps a callable into stored work that runs later and delivers its result through a future.

question: What are the four standard execution policies?

choices.1: sync, async, deferred, lazy
choices.2: read, write, shared, exclusive
choices.3: fast, slow, safe, unsafe
choices.4: seq, par, par_unseq, unseq

explanation: Sequenced, parallel, parallel + vectorized, and vectorized single-thread (C++20).

question: What is the correct mental model for an execution policy like std::execution::par?

choices.1: A permission, not a command
choices.2: A command that forces multi-threaded execution
choices.3: A guarantee of a specific thread count
choices.4: A vectorization-only hint

explanation: The implementation MAY parallelize and may fall back to sequential execution (e.g. if resources are unavailable).

question: Under std::execution::par, who is responsible for avoiding data races (e.g. a lambda doing out.push_back)?

choices.1: The library handles all synchronization
choices.2: The caller synchronizes
choices.3: Data races are impossible under par
choices.4: par forbids any side effects at compile time

explanation: Shared mutation needs a mutex/atomic or a reduction; par calls may run concurrently.

question: Why is using a mutex inside an unseq or par_unseq element-access function dangerous?

choices.1: Mutexes are slower under these policies
choices.2: Mutexes are not allowed to compile
choices.3: Unsequenced calls can deadlock it
choices.4: It forces sequential execution

explanation: Element-access calls may be interleaved within a thread, so blocking synchronization is unsafe.

question: What happens if an element-access function exits via an uncaught exception under a standard execution policy?

choices.1: The exception propagates to the caller normally
choices.2: The algorithm silently skips that element
choices.3: The policy downgrades to seq
choices.4: std::terminate is invoked

explanation: Separately, std::bad_alloc may be thrown if temporary parallelization memory is unavailable.

question: Even under par, why shouldn't you assume iteration 0 runs before iteration 1?

choices.1: Because iterations always run in reverse
choices.2: Element order is not guaranteed
choices.3: Because par runs everything sequentially
choices.4: Because par sorts the range first

explanation: Code that depends on iteration order (e.g. spin-waiting on another iteration) may hang.

question: Which policy is best for a pure, independent, SIMD-friendly operation over large data, allowing both threads and vectorization?

choices.1: par_unseq
choices.2: unseq only
choices.3: No policy
choices.4: seq

question: What does std::async return and conceptually create?

choices.1: A hidden mutex guarding the result
choices.2: A raw pointer to the result
choices.3: A std::thread you must join
choices.4: A future tied to a shared state

explanation: The shared state holds the returned value, a thrown exception, or a deferred callable.

question: What does the default std::async(f, args...) overload (no policy) mean for execution?

choices.1: It always defers
choices.2: Either eager or deferred — its choice
choices.3: It always runs on a separate new thread
choices.4: It runs synchronously in place

explanation: It is equivalent to launch::async | launch::deferred, so the implementation may run eagerly on a thread OR defer until get()/wait().

question: With std::launch::deferred, when and where does the callable run?

choices.1: Immediately on a new thread
choices.2: In a library thread pool at construction
choices.3: On the first get(), in that thread
choices.4: It never runs

explanation: It runs lazily in whichever thread calls the first non-timed get()/wait().

question: Why can `std::async(std::launch::async, []{ do_work(); });` (result not stored) behave as if synchronous?

choices.1: The future's destructor blocks
choices.2: async always runs synchronously
choices.3: The lambda is deferred
choices.4: do_work runs in the destructor

explanation: The returned future is a temporary destroyed at the end of the full expression, and a future from std::async can block in its destructor until the work completes.

question: What should you use when you need GUARANTEED concurrent execution from std::async?

choices.1: Plain std::async(f) and simply hope
choices.2: Pass std::launch::async explicitly
choices.3: std::launch::deferred
choices.4: std::async with no arguments

explanation: The default policy may defer instead of running concurrently.

question: How are exceptions from an async callable surfaced?

choices.1: Stored, then rethrown at get()
choices.2: They terminate the program immediately
choices.3: They propagate on the worker thread to the OS
choices.4: They are logged and discarded

explanation: The shared state carries the exception to the caller.

question: What must a C++20 coroutine 'task type' (e.g. Task<T>) provide?

choices.1: A mutex and condition variable
choices.2: A nested promise_type
choices.3: Just a return statement
choices.4: A std::thread member

explanation: Plus a coroutine handle, result/exception storage, and typically an operator co_await and a continuation.

question: What does the 'continuation' stored in the promise enable?

choices.1: Running the coroutine on a GPU
choices.2: Detaching the coroutine
choices.3: Restarting the coroutine from the beginning
choices.4: Resuming the awaiting coroutine

explanation: When this one finishes (symmetric transfer), so one coroutine can co_await another.

question: Is a coroutine-based Task<T> automatically asynchronous (multi-threaded)?

choices.1: No - by itself it only suspends/resumes coroutines
choices.2: Yes, the promise_type spawns a thread
choices.3: Only if T is void
choices.4: Yes, because co_await always runs on another thread

explanation: Real asynchrony requires connecting it to a scheduler, executor, thread pool, event loop, or async I/O.

question: How can a simple awaitable make a coroutine resume on a new thread?

choices.1: By calling std::async from within await_ready
choices.2: await_suspend launches a resuming thread
choices.3: By returning true from await_ready
choices.4: By storing the value in the promise

explanation: E.g. std::thread([h]{ h.resume(); }).detach().

question: Why does Task<void> need a separate specialization from Task<T>?

choices.1: optional<void> doesn't exist
choices.2: Because void tasks cannot have a promise_type
choices.3: Because void coroutines cannot suspend
choices.4: Because exceptions can't be captured for void

explanation: And the promise uses return_void() instead of return_value().

question: What does the note recommend for real production coroutine code?

choices.1: Prefer a library task type
choices.2: Use std::async instead of any coroutine
choices.3: Always hand-roll your own Task type
choices.4: Avoid coroutines entirely

explanation: Rolling your own is mainly for learning. E.g. cppcoro::task, Folly coro::Task, Boost.Asio awaitable.

question: What defines a C++20 coroutine - what can it do that an ordinary function cannot?

choices.1: It cannot take arguments
choices.2: It can suspend and resume
choices.3: It can return multiple types
choices.4: It runs on a separate thread automatically

explanation: Via co_await/co_yield/co_return, resuming later from where it left off.

question: What do the three coroutine keywords do?

choices.1: They are interchangeable
choices.2: co_await and co_return both yield values
choices.3: co_await yields a value; co_yield ends; co_return waits
choices.4: co_await suspends until a result is ready

explanation: Co_yield suspends and returns a value (generators); co_return ends the coroutine, optionally with a value.

question: What is the role of the promise_type in a coroutine?

choices.1: The coroutine's manager object
choices.2: It is the coroutine handle
choices.3: It stores a std::promise for the result
choices.4: It is the awaiter

explanation: It controls the return object, suspend points, and value/exception handling.

question: Which three methods does an awaiter define to control how co_await behaves?

choices.1: await_ready(), await_suspend(), await_resume()
choices.2: initial_suspend(), final_suspend(), yield_value()
choices.3: lock(), wait(), unlock()
choices.4: begin(), next(), end()

question: Where is the coroutine frame/state stored in C++?

choices.1: On the heap
choices.2: In the promise object only
choices.3: Always on the calling thread's stack
choices.4: In a CPU register

explanation: Unlike some languages that keep coroutine state on the stack.

question: In a generator promise_type, what does yield_value typically return and why?

choices.1: the value itself
choices.2: std::suspend_never, to keep producing without stopping
choices.3: a coroutine handle to the consumer
