choices.4: std::suspend_always

explanation: The coroutine suspends after storing each yielded value until the consumer resumes it.

question: What are coroutines well-suited for?

choices.1: Guaranteeing real-time deadlines
choices.2: Async I/O, generators, cooperation
choices.3: Replacing all mutexes
choices.4: Forcing parallel execution across cores

explanation: Lazy sequences and cooperative multitasking.

question: What is std::generator (C++23)?

choices.1: An async I/O awaitable
choices.2: A container that eagerly stores all values
choices.3: A thread-pool task type
choices.4: A coroutine-based lazy range

explanation: The coroutine runs until co_yield, hands one value to the caller, suspends, and resumes on the next request.

question: Why can a std::generator represent an infinite sequence safely?

choices.1: It runs on a background thread that you stop
choices.2: Values are produced lazily
choices.3: It precomputes a very large buffer
choices.4: It caps the sequence at INT_MAX

explanation: E.g. combined with std::views::take. So you only generate what you consume.

question: What is the big gotcha about std::generator being single-pass?

choices.1: It can only yield ints
choices.2: It cannot be used in a range-for loop
choices.3: It models an input range
choices.4: It copies all elements on each pass

explanation: Calling begin() more than once on the same generator object is undefined behaviour, so create a fresh generator per iteration.

question: How do you yield every element of another range from a std::generator?

choices.1: co_yield elements_of(r)
choices.2: co_return the whole range
choices.3: return r.begin()
choices.4: co_await the range

explanation: std::ranges::elements_of yields every element of another range.

question: What is a key limitation of std::generator regarding asynchrony?

choices.1: It cannot yield references
choices.2: It is synchronous
choices.3: It must run on a separate thread
choices.4: It only works with std::views

explanation: You cannot co_await an asynchronous operation inside a coroutine returning std::generator.

question: When is std::generator NOT the right tool?

choices.1: Random access, re-iteration, async
choices.2: When producing a sequence lazily
choices.3: When you want to avoid writing a custom iterator
choices.4: When composing with std::views::filter/transform

explanation: Or maximum performance in hot inner loops.

question: In a hand-written Generator<T>, which promise_type function makes co_yield work?

choices.1: get_return_object()
choices.2: yield_value(T)
choices.3: return_value(T)
choices.4: initial_suspend()

explanation: It stores the value into current_value and returns std::suspend_always to suspend the coroutine.

question: In the iterator-based Generator, what does operator++ do?

choices.1: Copies the current value
choices.2: Destroys the coroutine
choices.3: Allocates a new coroutine frame
choices.4: It resumes the coroutine

explanation: coro.resume() advances to the next yielded value, rethrowing any stored exception.

question: How does the Generator's iterator detect the end of the sequence?

choices.1: Comparing against the default sentinel
choices.2: By checking whether current_value is empty
choices.3: By a fixed length counter
choices.4: By catching an exception

explanation: operator== returns true when the handle is null or coro.done().

question: What does this Generator's begin() do before returning an iterator?

choices.1: Nothing; it returns immediately
choices.2: It runs to the first co_yield
choices.3: It destroys the previous frame
choices.4: It copies all values into a vector

explanation: It resumes the coroutine once (rethrowing any stored exception) before returning the iterator.

question: What kind of generator is this hand-written Generator<T>?

choices.1: A lazy SYNCHRONOUS generator
choices.2: A multi-threaded parallel generator
choices.3: An asynchronous generator that waits on I/O
choices.4: An eager generator that precomputes everything

explanation: It does not automatically wait for async I/O, timers, or background work.

question: What does a trailing const on a member function promise?

choices.1: No change to observable state
choices.2: That the function is thread-local
choices.3: That the return value is const
choices.4: That it runs at compile time

explanation: Inside it, members are treated as const and only other const members may be called.

question: What is the difference between low-level and top-level const for a pointer?

choices.1: Low-level const only applies to references
choices.2: Pointee const versus pointer const
choices.3: Top-level affects the pointee; low-level affects the variable
choices.4: They are the same

explanation: Low-level const (const int*) is what's pointed at and matters for conversions/overloads; top-level const (int* const) is the variable itself and is ignored for parameter matching.

question: What is the difference between bitwise const and logical const, and what bridges them?

choices.1: Logical const is enforced by the compiler; bitwise is a convention
choices.2: const_cast bridges them
choices.3: They are identical; nothing bridges them
choices.4: The compiler enforces bitwise const (the bits don't change)

explanation: Logical const means observable value doesn't change; the mutable keyword lets a specific member change inside a const function.

question: Why is a std::mutex used inside a const getter typically declared mutable?

choices.1: Locking isn't 'logical' mutation
choices.2: To allow the getter to change the cached value
choices.3: To make the getter faster
choices.4: Because mutexes cannot be const

explanation: The convention is that const members are safe to call concurrently, so the mutex must be mutable to be locked within a const function.

question: What convention links const member functions and thread-safety?

choices.1: const member functions should be safe to call concurrently
choices.2: const has no relationship to threading
choices.3: Only non-const members are thread-safe
choices.4: const members must never ever be called from multiple threads

explanation: Read-only implies thread-safe to share. Which is why mutating through a const interface is dangerous.

question: Why should you avoid const-by-value return types (e.g. const T foo())?

choices.1: They are mostly pointless and block moves
choices.2: They prevent the function being constexpr
choices.3: They are a syntax error
choices.4: They force a heap allocation

explanation: The returned value can't be moved from.

question: What does std::as_const(x) do?

choices.1: Makes x constexpr
choices.2: Removes constness from x
choices.3: Deep-copies x as const
choices.4: A const reference to x

explanation: E.g. read-only iteration. Forcing const access / the const overload. No copy, just a const view.

question: Why does the note say big-O alone misleads when choosing a container?

choices.1: Because allocation is free
choices.2: Because cache effects dominate
choices.3: Because big-O is always wrong
choices.4: Because all containers are O(1)

explanation: A cache miss (~100ns) vs L1 hit (~1ns) means contiguous containers (vector/array) with prefetching often beat pointer-chasing ones despite worse big-O.

question: For a small key-value lookup (N <= ~32), what does the note recommend?

choices.1: std::list
choices.2: std::map for ordering
choices.3: std::unordered_map in all cases
choices.4: A sorted vector or linear scan

explanation: lower_bound over a sorted vector — or even a plain scan — often beats a hash map for small N.

question: Why is 'use a list for lots of insertions' usually wrong?

choices.1: Prefetched memmove beats per-node misses
choices.2: std::list doesn't support insertion
choices.3: list insertion is O(N)
choices.4: vector simply cannot insert in the middle

explanation: vector middle-insertion is a bandwidth-bound memmove; list insertion is O(1) but costs a cache miss plus an allocation per node.

question: Which container does the note recommend for growing at BOTH ends?

choices.1: std::vector
choices.2: std::deque
choices.3: std::array
choices.4: std::list

question: For pure point-lookup throughput at scale, what is preferred over std::unordered_map?

choices.1: std::map
choices.2: std::list
choices.3: A flat hash map
choices.4: A sorted vector

explanation: E.g. absl::flat_hash_map or boost::unordered_flat_map — open addressing, no per-node allocations. Use std::unordered_map only if you need its reference-stability guarantee.

question: What is described as the single biggest performance win regardless of container choice?

choices.1: Using a custom key comparator
choices.2: Always using std::map
choices.3: Disabling exceptions
choices.4: Avoiding repeated allocation

explanation: E.g. reserve(n) ahead of known sizes and reusing containers (clear() keeps capacity) on hot paths.

question: What is std::array<T, N> and a key advantage over a raw C array?

choices.1: A view over existing memory
choices.2: A linked list of fixed size
choices.3: A heap-allocated dynamic resizable array
choices.4: A stack wrapper with value semantics

explanation: Zero-overhead and fixed-size (the size is part of the type); it copies/assigns like a value and offers .size(), iterators, and .at().

question: How is std::deque laid out, and what does that imply?

choices.1: A doubly-linked list of single elements
choices.2: A 'map' array of pointers to fixed-size chunks
choices.3: One contiguous buffer like vector, so data() works
choices.4: A red-black tree

explanation: It gives O(1) push at both ends but is NOT contiguous (no data()) and operator[] does two indirections.

question: What stability guarantee does inserting/erasing at the ENDS of a std::deque provide?

choices.1: It invalidates everything
choices.2: It only invalidates the end iterator
choices.3: Other references stay valid
choices.4: It invalidates pointers but not iterators

explanation: Middle insertion invalidates everything. A key difference from vector. End insert/erase invalidates iterators but not references to other elements.

question: What are std::list's strengths and its main practical drawback?

choices.1: O(1) splice/insert but cache-hostile
choices.2: Random access and great cache behaviour
choices.3: Compile-time fixed size
choices.4: Contiguous storage with O(1) indexing

explanation: O(1) insert/erase given an iterator, O(1) splice, and the strongest iterator/reference stability — but a random heap address and allocation per node makes it almost always the wrong choice for performance.

question: What is std::priority_queue built on and what can you access?

choices.1: A red-black tree; you can iterate in order
choices.2: A binary heap over a vector
choices.3: A hash table; O(1) lookup
choices.4: A doubly-linked list; O(1) splice

explanation: O(log N) push/pop, O(1) top, but no iteration and no random access (you only see the top).

question: What is the default underlying container for std::stack and std::queue?

choices.1: std::deque
choices.2: std::vector
choices.3: std::array
choices.4: std::list

question: What is std::span<T> (C++20)?

choices.1: A tightly bit-packed boolean array
choices.2: A non-owning pointer plus length
choices.3: An owning dynamic array
choices.4: A hash table view

explanation: Over any contiguous sequence.

question: What problem does std::span solve as a function parameter?

choices.1: It copies the data for safety
choices.2: It deep-copies into a vector
choices.3: It magically makes functions run in parallel
choices.4: One signature for any contiguous buffer

explanation: A single non-template signature accepts vector, array, or C array, replacing error-prone (T* ptr, size_t n) pairs by carrying the length with it.

question: What is the difference between span<T> and span<const T>?

choices.1: span<T> can modify the underlying elements (writes through)
choices.2: span<const T> owns its data
choices.3: They are identical
choices.4: span<T> is read-only while span<const T> writes through elements

explanation: Span<const T> is read-only - either way the span never resizes/reallocates.

question: What does a static extent span (span<T, N>) provide over a dynamic-extent span?

choices.1: It owns N elements
choices.2: The size is baked into the type
choices.3: It does automatic bounds checking on []
choices.4: It can resize at runtime

explanation: Unroll/vectorize. Just a pointer. Enabling compile-time size checks and better codegen, and it's smaller.

question: What is the key lifetime caveat with std::span?

choices.1: It dangles when the backing dies
choices.2: It cannot be passed to functions
choices.3: It always extends the lifetime of its backing data
choices.4: It copies its data on construction

explanation: It owns nothing, so destruction or reallocation of the container invalidates it; use it for parameters and short-lived locals, not storage.

question: How does slicing a span (first/last/subspan) work?

choices.1: It just adjusts pointer/length - nothing is copied
choices.2: It returns a vector
choices.3: It copies the selected elements into a brand new buffer
choices.4: It reallocates the backing container

question: How is std::map implemented and what does that give you?

choices.1: A linked list; O(1) splice
choices.2: A balanced BST (red-black tree)
choices.3: A hash table; average O(1) unordered lookup
choices.4: A contiguous array; O(1) indexing

explanation: O(log N) guaranteed lookup/insert/erase, sorted iteration, lower_bound/range queries, and stable references.

question: Why does std::unordered_map use separate chaining rather than faster open addressing?

choices.1: Because open addressing can't hash strings
choices.2: To support ordered iteration
choices.3: Because chaining is always faster
choices.4: The standard mandates reference stability

explanation: Node-based chaining keeps element nodes in place — only relinked on rehash.

question: What does a rehash in unordered_map invalidate?

choices.1: Iterators, not pointers or references
choices.2: Only references, and never any iterators
choices.3: Both iterators and references
choices.4: Nothing

explanation: The bucket array is rebuilt but nodes are not moved, only relinked.

question: What triggers a rehash, and how do you avoid it during bulk inserts?

choices.1: load_factor exceeding max_load_factor (default 1.0)
choices.2: Calling find(); use at() instead
choices.3: It happens on every single insert and cannot be avoided
choices.4: Erasing elements; call clear()

explanation: Call reserve(n) to pre-size buckets.

question: What happens to unordered_map lookups with a bad hash function (e.g. one that collides heavily)?

choices.1: One bucket, O(N) lookups
choices.2: It silently switches to a tree
choices.3: They stay O(1)
choices.4: The map refuses to compile

explanation: Everything collides into the same chain.

question: Why is operator[] dangerous for a read-only lookup (e.g. if (m[key] == 0))?

choices.1: It is slower than at()
choices.2: It throws if the key is absent
choices.3: It returns a copy, not a reference
choices.4: It inserts a default value on miss

explanation: The map is silently mutated when the key is absent; use find/contains for read-only checks.

question: What is the difference between a vector's size and capacity?

choices.1: capacity is always twice the size
choices.2: size is how many elements currently exist (are constructed)
choices.3: They are always equal
choices.4: size is the total byte count while capacity is the element count

explanation: Capacity is how many the current allocation could hold before it must reallocate.

question: Why does geometric (multiplicative) growth make push_back amortized O(1)?

choices.1: Because push_back is always O(1) regardless of growth
choices.2: Because it preallocates infinite memory
choices.3: Because it never reallocates
choices.4: Multiplicative growth spreads the cost of relocations

explanation: So N pushes total O(N); constant-increment growth would make N pushes O(N^2) from repeated copying.

question: What does a vector reallocation invalidate?

choices.1: Every iterator, pointer, reference
choices.2: Only the end iterator
choices.3: Nothing
choices.4: Only references, but never iterators

explanation: Reserve up front to avoid it.

question: During reallocation, when does a vector MOVE elements vs COPY them, and why?

choices.1: It always moves for speed
choices.2: It moves only if the move ctor is noexcept
choices.3: It moves only for the trivially-copyable types
choices.4: It always copies to be safe

explanation: Otherwise it copies — for exception safety, so a throw mid-relocation can roll back to the intact original (std::move_if_noexcept).

question: What is the difference between push_back and emplace_back?

choices.1: emplace_back copies; push_back moves
choices.2: They are identical for all types
choices.3: emplace_back constructs in place
choices.4: emplace_back is always safer

explanation: push_back takes an already-constructed object (copy/move); emplace_back forwards its arguments to construct the element in place, avoiding a temporary.

question: Why is std::vector<bool> considered a trap?

choices.1: It cannot store false
choices.2: It is slower than vector<int> for all operations
choices.3: It is not part of the standard
choices.4: It is a bit-packed specialization

explanation: Operator[] returns a proxy (not bool&), you can't take an element's address, and data() doesn't exist; use vector<char>/uint8_t or std::bitset instead.

question: What does clear() do to a vector's capacity?

choices.1: It releases the allocation, setting capacity to 0
choices.2: It destroys all elements but keeps the allocation
choices.3: It halves the capacity
choices.4: It calls shrink_to_fit automatically

explanation: Capacity unchanged. So a cleared vector can be reused without reallocating.

question: What two problems with unscoped (C-style) enums does enum class fix?

choices.1: Inability to be used in switch statements
choices.2: Lack of a default value and missing iteration
choices.3: Slow lookup and large storage
choices.4: Name leakage and implicit int conversion

explanation: enum class scopes the names (E::Value) and forbids implicit conversion to int.

question: How do you convert an enum class value to its underlying integer?

choices.1: With a reinterpret_cast
choices.2: Explicitly via static_cast
choices.3: It converts implicitly
choices.4: By assigning it to an int directly

explanation: There is no implicit conversion. Or std::to_underlying in C++23.

question: Why fix an enum's underlying type, e.g. enum class Side : std::uint8_t { Buy, Sell }?

choices.1: To enable implicit int conversion
choices.2: To make the enum sortable
choices.3: Size, ABI, forward declaration
choices.4: To add iteration support

explanation: 1 byte instead of 4, stable serialization, and the ability to forward-declare the enum.

question: Why might you omit default in a switch over an enum?

choices.1: -Wswitch flags unhandled enumerators
choices.2: To make the switch run faster
choices.3: To allow fallthrough between cases
choices.4: Because default is a syntax error for enums

explanation: Adding a new enum value becomes a compiler-guided refactor.

question: For an enum class used as bit flags, why must you define operator| yourself?

choices.1: Because enum class can't hold flag values
choices.2: No implicit conversion to the underlying int
choices.3: Because flags require a separate header
choices.4: Because operator| is implicitly deleted for all enums

explanation: Bitwise operators must be defined explicitly (cast to the underlying type and back).

question: Does std::vector require T's move constructor to be noexcept?

choices.1: No, but it strongly prefers one
choices.2: Yes, it is a hard requirement
choices.3: No, and it never moves elements
choices.4: Yes, otherwise it won't compile

explanation: A noexcept move lets it move (rather than copy) elements during reallocation.

question: Why is moving with a potentially-throwing move constructor dangerous during reallocation?

choices.1: It corrupts the allocator
choices.2: A mid-relocation throw is unrecoverable
choices.3: It is always slower than copying
choices.4: Moves simply can't be used in vectors at all

explanation: Some elements are moved-from and some untouched, and the original can't be restored — breaking the strong exception guarantee.

question: Why is copying safer than a throwing move during reallocation?

choices.1: Copying never throws
choices.2: Copying skips over the old buffer entirely
choices.3: Copying is always faster
choices.4: Copying leaves the old elements untouched

explanation: So if a copy throws the vector can destroy the partial new buffer and keep using the original intact.

question: What does std::move_if_noexcept express?

choices.1: Move only when it cannot throw
choices.2: Move only trivially-copyable types
choices.3: Never move; always copy
choices.4: Always move regardless of noexcept

explanation: Move if the move is noexcept (or the type isn't copyable), otherwise copy — the logic vector uses to stay exception-safe.

question: What is the practical rule of thumb for types stored in a vector?

choices.1: Delete the move constructor
choices.2: Mark moves noexcept when they can't throw
choices.3: Always provide only a copy constructor instead
choices.4: Make the move constructor explicit

explanation: T(T&&) noexcept and T& operator=(T&&) noexcept let reallocation move rather than copy.

question: In what sense are C++ exceptions 'zero-cost'?

choices.1: The success path costs nothing
choices.2: They use no binary space
choices.3: Throwing is free
choices.4: They never flush the CPU pipeline

explanation: The normal path executes no extra instructions (no per-call flags); the cost is paid on the throw path and in optimisations the compiler must forgo.

question: What does the table-based (zero-cost) model emit off to the side instead of per-call checks?

choices.1: Unwind tables and landing pads
choices.2: Inline destructor calls on the hot path
choices.3: A try-block flag checked after every call
choices.4: A global exception counter

explanation: .eh_frame / LSDA describe cleanup/handlers per call site, with out-of-line landing pads running destructors.

question: Why does the mere possibility of a throw inhibit compiler optimisation?

choices.1: It disables the optimiser entirely
choices.2: Every may-throw call is a barrier
choices.3: It doesn't; throws only cost at runtime
choices.4: It forces all functions to be inlined

explanation: It adds an invisible control-flow edge to a landing pad, restricting hoisting/sinking/reordering/CSE/DSE across the call.

question: Why does exception handling raise register pressure even when nothing throws?

choices.1: It reserves all registers for the unwinder
choices.2: It disables register allocation
choices.3: It adds a flag register per call
choices.4: Unwind-live locals must stay addressable

explanation: Locals with non-trivial destructors must be kept in a well-defined place — typically spilled to memory and kept live across calls — so the runtime can destroy them while unwinding.

question: How does marking a function noexcept help the optimiser?

choices.1: It can prove no unwinding happens
choices.2: It forces full tail-call optimisation
choices.3: It makes the function inline
choices.4: It removes all error handling

explanation: The compiler can drop landing pads, reorder freely across the call, and pick faster library paths (e.g. vector move-on-grow).

question: Why is the throw path itself a hardware worst case?

choices.1: Cold code, guaranteed mispredicts
choices.2: It disables the cache
choices.3: It blocks all other threads
choices.4: It runs twice as many instructions on the hot path

explanation: The unwinder jumps to unpredicted destructor/landing-pad code: branch predictor and return-stack misses (pipeline flush) plus I-cache/I-TLB misses.

question: What are the four exception-safety guarantees from weakest to strongest?

choices.1: no guarantee, basic, strong, no-throw
choices.2: strong, basic, no-throw, no guarantee
choices.3: basic, strong, no-throw, no guarantee
choices.4: no-throw, strong, basic, no guarantee

question: What does the BASIC guarantee promise if an exception is thrown?

choices.1: The operation never throws
choices.2: The operation has no observable effect
choices.3: The object is destroyed
choices.4: Valid state, possibly changed

explanation: No leaks, invariants preserved, objects remain valid — but state may be partially changed.

question: What does the STRONG guarantee promise, and what pattern implements it?

choices.1: That destructors run
choices.2: No effect on throw; copy-then-swap
choices.3: That the function never throws; mark it noexcept
choices.4: That state may be partially changed but valid

explanation: Commit-or-rollback semantics, commonly implemented by modifying a temporary copy and swapping.

question: What is the no-throw guarantee, and which operations typically aim for it?

choices.1: The operation rolls back on throw; constructors
choices.2: The operation has basic safety; all member functions
choices.3: The operation may throw but won't leak; getters
choices.4: The operation is guaranteed not to throw at all

explanation: Destructors (ideally), swap for some types, and move operations marked noexcept.

question: What level does a lot of well-written library code aim for at minimum (and often stronger for modifying operations)?

choices.1: Basic at minimum, strong when practical
choices.2: No guarantee
choices.3: Only the no-throw guarantee
choices.4: None at all; exceptions are avoided entirely

explanation: Modifying operations often aim for the strong guarantee.

question: Which fstream types are used for reading vs writing vs both?

choices.1: ifstream and ofstream both only read
choices.2: They are interchangeable
choices.3: ofstream reads, ifstream writes, fstream neither
choices.4: ifstream in, ofstream out, fstream both

explanation: Reading, writing, and both respectively.

question: By default, what does opening a file with std::ofstream do to an existing file, and how do you append instead?

choices.1: It fails if the file exists
choices.2: It overwrites (truncates) the file by default
choices.3: It appends by default; use std::ios::trunc to overwrite
choices.4: It opens in read-only mode

explanation: Pass std::ios::app to append to the end.

question: What is the difference between std::getline(file, line) and file >> word for reading?

choices.1: getline reads a single word; >> reads a full line
choices.2: They are identical
choices.3: getline reads a full line (up to newline)
choices.4: getline only works on cin

explanation: >> reads a single whitespace-separated token.

question: What is the recommended safety check after opening a file?

choices.1: Check if (!file) after opening
choices.2: No check is needed
choices.3: Always immediately call file.clear()
choices.4: Call file.eof() first

explanation: Or file.is_open() — catch a failed open before reading/writing.

question: Which flag combination opens a file for both reading and writing while appending?

choices.1: std::ios::trunc alone
choices.2: in | out | app
choices.3: std::ios::binary only
choices.4: std::ios::in alone

explanation: std::ios::in | std::ios::out | std::ios::app.

question: What is the core type of the <filesystem> library (C++17)?

choices.1: std::filesystem::path
choices.2: std::fstream
choices.3: std::filesystem::file
choices.4: std::string

explanation: The core type representing a filesystem path.

question: Why prefer the / operator over string concatenation to join paths?

choices.1: / converts the path to absolute
choices.2: Correct separators everywhere
choices.3: It is shorter to type
choices.4: String concatenation doesn't compile

explanation: path's operator/ handles platform differences.

question: What is the difference between fs::create_directory and fs::create_directories?

choices.1: They are identical
choices.2: create_directories deletes existing directories first
choices.3: create_directory makes nested folders; create_directories makes one
choices.4: create_directory makes a single directory

explanation: Create_directories also creates missing parent folders (and does nothing if it already exists).

question: What is the difference between fs::canonical and fs::weakly_canonical?

choices.1: canonical requires the path to exist
choices.2: They are the same
choices.3: weakly_canonical only works on directories
choices.4: canonical works on non-existent paths; weakly_canonical requires existence

explanation: Weakly_canonical works when part of the path may not exist (and lexically_normal normalizes without requiring existence).

question: How do most filesystem functions report errors, and what is the non-throwing alternative?

choices.1: They return -1; the alternative is a global errno
choices.2: They throw std::filesystem::filesystem_error
choices.3: They print to stderr; no alternative exists
choices.4: They abort the program

explanation: Passing a std::error_code& overload makes them report errors without throwing.

question: What is the difference between directory_iterator and recursive_directory_iterator?

choices.1: directory_iterator lists one directory's entries
choices.2: They are the same
choices.3: directory_iterator descends into subdirectories; recursive does not
choices.4: recursive_directory_iterator only lists files, not directories

explanation: Recursive_directory_iterator descends into subdirectories too.

question: What is a stream in C++?

choices.1: A flow of data in or out
choices.2: A synchronization primitive
choices.3: A thread of execution
choices.4: A fixed-size buffer

explanation: The source/destination can be the console, a file, or a string.

question: Why does mixing std::cin >> x followed by std::getline often misbehave?

choices.1: getline clears the stream state
choices.2: The >> leaves the trailing newline in the buffer
choices.3: getline cannot read from cin
choices.4: >> consumes the entire line including the newline

explanation: So the next getline reads an empty line; fix with std::cin.ignore(...).

question: Why is `while (!file.eof()) { file >> value; }` the wrong loop?

choices.1: It reads each value twice
choices.2: It never terminates
choices.3: eof() is not even a member of streams
choices.4: eof() is set only after a failure

explanation: So the last (failed) read is still processed; loop on the read itself: while (file >> value).

question: What is std::stringstream useful for?

choices.1: Treating a string as a stream
choices.2: Synchronizing threads
choices.3: Reading bytes from the network
choices.4: Writing directly to disk

explanation: Parsing/extracting tokens with >> or splitting with getline(ss, field, ',').

question: After a failed extraction (e.g. non-numeric input to >> int), what two steps recover the stream?

choices.1: clear() then ignore(...)
choices.2: Just call clear()
choices.3: Call eof() and then good()
choices.4: close() then open()

explanation: std::cin.clear() resets the error state, then std::cin.ignore(...) discards the bad input.

question: When overloading operator<< for a custom type, why return the stream (return os;)?

choices.1: To allow chaining
choices.2: To flush the buffer
choices.3: To avoid a memory leak
choices.4: Because os is const

explanation: std::cout << a << b works because each call returns the stream for the next insertion.

question: What is the difference between int f; and int f{}; at block scope?

choices.1: int f{}; leaves it uninitialized
choices.2: They are identical
choices.3: int f; zero-initializes
choices.4: int f; default-initializes

explanation: Int f{}; value-initializes to 0. Indeterminate value for built-ins - reading it is UB.

question: What safety property does brace {} initialization add over = or () init?

choices.1: It always allows the most vexing parse
choices.2: It forbids narrowing conversions
choices.3: It runs at compile time
choices.4: It zero-initializes pointers

explanation: E.g. int x{3.9} is an error, while int x = 3.9 silently truncates.

question: Why does std::vector<int> v{3, 0} differ from std::vector<int> v(3, 0)?

choices.1: Parentheses make a 2-element vector
choices.2: Braces always call the default constructor
choices.3: They are identical
choices.4: Braces pick the initializer_list ctor

explanation: {3,0} is a 2-element vector [3,0], while (3,0) is the sizing constructor giving [0,0,0].

question: What is an aggregate that can be brace-initialized member-by-member?

choices.1: A plain struct with public members only
choices.2: Any class with a constructor
choices.3: Only std::array
choices.4: Absolutely any class with a virtual destructor

explanation: No user-declared constructors, no private/protected non-static data members, no virtual functions, and no non-public bases.

question: What do C++20 designated initializers allow, with what restriction?

choices.1: Initializing members in any order, mixed with positional
choices.2: Named members, in declaration order
choices.3: Calling constructors by name
choices.4: Initializing private members directly

explanation: E.g. .width = 1920 — but no reordering and no mixing with positional initializers.

question: What is the 'most vexing parse', and how does brace-init avoid it?

choices.1: An ambiguous operator overload; adding braces disambiguates it
choices.2: A narrowing conversion error
choices.3: Anything that could be parsed as a function declaration is
choices.4: A template instantiation failure

explanation: Widget w{}; instead declares a value-initialized object. E.g. Widget w(); declares a function.

question: In a structured binding like `auto& [a, b] = p;`, what exactly does the `&` (and the introducer's qualifiers in general) apply to?

choices.1: Each named binding individually, so you can declare `a` as a reference while `b` is a copy in the same statement
choices.2: The hidden anonymous object that is created from the initializer
choices.3: Only the first name in the list; subsequent names are always deduced as plain values
choices.4: The initializer expression `p`, forcing `p` itself to be re-declared as a reference

explanation: The qualifier governs that whole object, so all names share the same value/reference semantics.

question: Iterating a `std::map<std::string, int>` with `for (auto& [name, score] : scores)`, you can do `score += 1` but cannot reassign `name`. Why?

choices.1: Because `auto&` only grants write access to the final binding in the list
choices.2: Because the range-based for loop copies the key into a const temporary while it leaves the value as a live reference
choices.3: Because a map's element type is `std::pair<const K, V>`, so the key member is const and the value member is mutable
choices.4: Because structured bindings always make the first name read-only and the rest writable

explanation: The bindings inherit those member qualifiers.

question: When a structured binding decomposes a struct/aggregate, which members does it bind to, and in what order?

choices.1: All data members including private and static ones, in alphabetical order
choices.2: The public non-static data members, in their declaration order
choices.3: Only members that have a default member initializer, in reverse declaration order
choices.4: Whichever members are returned by the type's `get<>()` overloads

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three categories that structured bindings can decompose, per the note?

choices.1: Built-in arrays such as `int arr[3]`
choices.2: Tuple-like types (`pair`, `tuple`, `array`, or anything providing `tuple_size`/`get`)
choices.3: Structs/aggregates, binding to their public non-static data members in order
choices.4: A raw pointer, which is decomposed into its address and the pointee

question: What is the purpose of writing `auto&& [a, b] = make_pair(...);` rather than `auto [a, b] = ...`?

choices.1: Forwarding/universal-reference binding
choices.2: It deep-copies the temporary twice to guarantee exception safety
choices.3: It forces both `a` and `b` to be const, preventing any modification
choices.4: It is required syntax whenever the right-hand side is a function call

explanation: It binds to the temporary without making a copy of it.

question: How does `std::tie(x, y) = std::make_pair(1, 2);` differ from `auto [x, y] = std::make_pair(1, 2);`?

choices.1: `std::tie` works only on `std::array`, while structured bindings work only on `std::pair`
choices.2: `std::tie` assigns into pre-existing variables, whereas a structured binding declares brand-new names in that statement
choices.3: `std::tie` always binds purely by reference while a structured binding always copies, with no way to change either one at all
choices.4: There is no difference; `std::tie` is just the C++17 spelling of a structured binding

question: Why is `if (auto [it, inserted] = m.try_emplace(key, value); inserted) { ... }` a clean idiom?

choices.1: A scoped pair with a direct bool test
choices.2: Because the init-statement makes the structured binding `constexpr`, evaluating the insertion at compile time
choices.3: Because `try_emplace` returns a `tuple<iterator, bool, size_t>` that only an if-statement can unpack
choices.4: Because structured bindings are only legal inside `if`/`switch` init-statements

explanation: try_emplace returns pair<iterator, bool>; the if-init-statement scopes the lookup tightly while letting you test inserted directly.

question: Which statement about pre-C++26 limitations of structured bindings is correct?

choices.1: You may name fewer variables than there are elements; the trailing elements are silently discarded
choices.2: You must name every element, and you cannot make the declaration `constexpr`
choices.3: You can mix references and values per name as long as the declaration is `constexpr`
choices.4: You can decompose only types whose element count is known at runtime

explanation: No placeholder to skip one before C++26's `_`.

question: In `auto [a, b] = p;`, what does the `[a, b]` syntax denote?

choices.1: A lambda capture list that captures `a` and `b` by value
choices.2: An array subscript selecting elements `a` and `b` of `p`
choices.3: The structured-binding introducer
choices.4: An attribute specifier applied to the declaration

explanation: It is not a lambda capture. It names the decomposed elements.

question: What constraint governs the number of names in a structured binding declaration?

choices.1: There must be at least one name; extras beyond the element count are default-initialized
choices.2: The number of names must exactly match the number of elements/members being decomposed
choices.3: The number of names may be any value; unmatched names become uninitialized
choices.4: The count must be a power of two for tuple-like types

question: Given `std::pair<int, std::string> p{1, "hi"}; const auto& [id, name] = p;`, which description is accurate?

choices.1: `id` and `name` are read-only references that alias `p`'s members without copying them
choices.2: `id` and `name` are independent copies of `p`'s members that may still be freely modified
choices.3: `p` is moved-from, leaving `id` and `name` as the only valid handles to the data
choices.4: Only `name` is a reference; `id` is copied because `int` is a scalar

question: Why can you not call std::sort on a std::list, forcing the list to provide its own member .sort()?

choices.1: std::sort cannot relink nodes, and list elements must be relinked rather than swapped
choices.2: std::sort is not declared as a friend of std::list, so it cannot access the nodes
choices.3: std::sort requires random-access iterators, but list's iterators are only bidirectional
choices.4: std::sort requires contiguous iterators so it can memcpy elements, which a list cannot provide

explanation: It jumps around while partitioning.

question: std::lower_bound compiles for any forward iterator. Why is it genuinely O(log N) on a std::vector but effectively O(N) on a std::list?

choices.1: Midpoint advancement costs O(N) on a list
choices.2: Because list iterators cannot be compared with <, so lower_bound falls back to a linear scan
choices.3: Because list stores no size, so lower_bound must first count the elements in O(N)
choices.4: Because vector caches previous search results while list does not

explanation: lower_bound does O(log N) comparisons either way, but reaching the midpoint is O(N) on a list and O(1) on a vector.

question: What does end() refer to, and how is the range [begin, end) characterised?

choices.1: end() points one past the last element and is a non-dereferenceable sentinel
choices.2: end() points at the first element; iteration runs backwards to begin()
choices.3: end() points at the last element; the range is closed on both sides
choices.4: end() points at a copy of the last element used solely for internal bounds checking

explanation: The range is half-open, and an empty range is begin == end.

question: What is the correct weak-to-strong ordering of the iterator category hierarchy?

choices.1: forward -> input/output -> bidirectional -> contiguous -> random-access
choices.2: input/output -> bidirectional -> forward -> random-access -> contiguous
choices.3: input/output -> forward -> bidirectional -> random-access -> contiguous
choices.4: random-access -> contiguous -> bidirectional -> forward -> input/output

question: What does the contiguous iterator category (C++17) guarantee beyond random-access?

choices.1: That dereferencing is O(1) instead of O(log N)
choices.2: That elements are physically adjacent in memory
choices.3: That the iterator can move both forwards and backwards
choices.4: That the container will never invalidate the iterator

explanation: So &*it yields a real pointer (std::to_address works) and the data is memcpy-able / SIMD- and C-passable.

question: Which is the weakest iterator category that std::find requires?

choices.1: Input
choices.2: Bidirectional
choices.3: Random-access
choices.4: Contiguous

question: What is the role of std::iterator_traits in generic code, as used by std::distance / std::advance?

choices.1: It guarantees the iterator is never invalidated during the algorithm
choices.2: It exposes value_type, difference_type, reference, pointer and iterator_category
choices.3: It rebinds the iterator's allocator to the algorithm's needs
choices.4: It automatically converts any iterator into a random-access iterator at compile time

explanation: So algorithms can dispatch on the category and pick the optimal implementation.

question: When writing a custom iterator, which category should you advertise?

choices.1: Whichever category matches the value_type's size
choices.2: Always forward, because it is the safest default for any container
choices.3: The weakest honest category
choices.4: Always random-access, since it enables the most algorithms

explanation: Claiming random-access you can't deliver in O(1) breaks algorithms' complexity guarantees. Advertise only what the structure supports at the stated complexity.

question: What does assigning through a std::back_inserter adaptor do?

choices.1: It overwrites the element that the underlying iterator currently points at
choices.2: It calls push_back on the underlying container, growing it as you copy
choices.3: It reverses the direction of iteration
choices.4: It moves the element out of the source, leaving it empty

question: What is a lambda, fundamentally?

choices.1: A compiler-generated closure struct with an operator()
choices.2: A special stack-allocated function pointer with no state
choices.3: A macro that the preprocessor expands into a free function
choices.4: A runtime object allocated on the heap and managed by std::function

explanation: Captured variables become its data members.

question: By default, can a lambda modify variables it captured by value inside its body, and what changes that?

choices.1: No, because operator() is const by default
choices.2: No, and there is no way to allow it short of capturing by reference
choices.3: Yes by default; const removes the ability
choices.4: Yes, and the modification is reflected in the original outer variable

explanation: Adding mutable removes the const so the body can modify its own copies.

question: At what moment is a by-value capture's value fixed?

choices.1: When the lambda is created — the value is copied at that point
choices.2: When the enclosing scope exits
choices.3: It is never fixed; by-value captures always alias the original
choices.4: When the lambda is invoked (it re-reads the variable each call)

explanation: So later changes to the outer variable are not seen.

question: Inside a member function, what does [this] actually capture, and what is the safe way to snapshot the whole object?

choices.1: It captures a reference to *this that is somehow automatically extended to match the lambda's lifetime
choices.2: It captures nothing; member access inside a lambda is illegal
choices.3: It copies all the object's members; no snapshot mechanism is needed
choices.4: It captures the object pointer (members are accessed through it, so it dangles if the object dies)

explanation: [*this] (C++17) stores an independent copy of the object.

question: Why is [=] inside a member function a notorious surprise?

choices.1: It silently converts the lambda into a function pointer
choices.2: It deep-copies every member including base subobjects, which is very expensive
choices.3: It also captures the this pointer (not copies of the members)
choices.4: It captures nothing at all, despite appearances

explanation: So member access still dangles if the object is destroyed.

question: A function returns a lambda that captured a local by reference ([&local]); the returned lambda is later invoked. What is the rule and fix?

choices.1: It is UB because local is destroyed when the function returns
choices.2: It is fine because the compiler extends local's lifetime to match the lambda
choices.3: It is UB only if local is larger than a cache line
choices.4: It is fine because references are reference-counted

explanation: Capture by value/move anything that must outlive the enclosing scope.

question: What problem does init-capture (e.g. [ptr = std::move(p)]) solve?

choices.1: Move captures and computed captures
choices.2: It converts a capturing lambda into a function pointer
choices.3: It allows one binding to be a reference and another a value
choices.4: It makes the lambda implicitly constexpr

explanation: A new closure member with an initializer enables capturing a unique_ptr by move or storing a computed value.

question: Which lambdas are convertible to a plain function pointer, and why?

choices.1: Only generic (auto-parameter) lambdas
choices.2: Only mutable lambdas, because they expose their state
choices.3: Only capture-less (stateless) lambdas
choices.4: Any lambda, because operator() is always static

explanation: Because a capturing lambda carries state that a function pointer cannot hold.

question: With auto f = [x]() mutable { return ++x; }; called twice, what is observed?

choices.1: f() returns 1 then 2, while the outer x is unchanged
choices.2: It fails to compile because ++ cannot apply to a captured value
choices.3: f() returns 1 then 2, and the outer x becomes 2 as well
choices.4: f() returns 1 then 1, because the copy is reset each call

explanation: State persists in the closure's copy.

question: What is the overarching principle for hot-path code in this note?

choices.1: Inline every function to remove all call overhead
choices.2: Always prefer branchless code over branchy code
choices.3: Do no work that isn't essential
choices.4: Use as many threads as cores to maximise throughput

explanation: No allocation, no virtual dispatch, no exceptions, no cache misses, no mispredicted branches.

question: What does [[likely]]/[[unlikely]] (or __builtin_expect) achieve?

choices.1: It forces the branch predictor to always predict taken
choices.2: The common path falls through
choices.3: It pins the branch to a specific CPU core
choices.4: It removes the branch entirely, producing branchless code

explanation: Code layout is steered so the hot branch has no taken-branch penalty and the cold path moves out of line.

question: What does __restrict promise the compiler, and what is the danger?

choices.1: That the pointed-to memory is read-only for the function's duration
choices.2: That memory is cache-aligned, enabling wider loads
choices.3: That a pointer is never null, enabling null-check elimination
choices.4: That two pointers don't alias

explanation: The compiler keep values in registers and vectorize — but it is UB if the pointers actually do alias.

question: Why prefer static dispatch (templates/CRTP, variant + visit) over virtual calls in an inner loop?

choices.1: Virtual functions cannot be marked noexcept
choices.2: Virtual calls always throw exceptions on the hot path
choices.3: Virtual calls allocate memory on every call
choices.4: Indirect branches block inlining

explanation: A virtual call is an often-mispredicted indirect branch and stops cross-call optimization.

question: How does the note characterise the cost of exceptions on the hot path?

choices.1: They have no performance impact in optimised builds
choices.2: They are always expensive, so the hot path should catch them eagerly
choices.3: They are cheaper than return codes, so prefer them everywhere
choices.4: Free until thrown, then very costly

explanation: Zero-cost when not thrown, but the machinery inhibits some optimizations — keep the hot path exception-free and validate at the boundary.

question: Which of these is called out as a source of hidden allocations to watch for on the hot path?

choices.1: constexpr variables
choices.2: string_view and span
choices.3: std::array plus small stack buffers
choices.4: std::function with large callables

explanation: Also std::string past SSO, std::stringstream, and lambda-to-function conversions.

question: What is the recommended remedy for false sharing of hot per-thread data?

choices.1: Make the data const so threads cannot write it
choices.2: Disable the hardware prefetcher
choices.3: Align/pad the data to a cache line
choices.4: Store the data in a single global atomic

explanation: E.g. alignas(std::hardware_destructive_interference_size). So independent fields sit on different lines.

question: What is the purpose of std::unreachable() / [[assume(cond)]] on the hot path?

choices.1: They force the enclosing function to be inlined
choices.2: They give the optimizer facts (impossible paths / invariants)
choices.3: They emit a runtime trap if the condition is violated, for debugging
choices.4: They mark the branch as unlikely for layout purposes

explanation: So it can prune branches and bounds work it would otherwise keep.

question: What is the note's caution about branchless code?

choices.1: Branchless code cannot be vectorized
choices.2: It is always faster, so it should be the default
choices.3: Branchless code is undefined behaviour on x86
choices.4: Predictable branches are free

explanation: Branchless code can be slower; reserve it for unpredictable data-dependent branches and measure.

question: Why does the compiler insert padding between struct members?

choices.1: To satisfy member alignment
choices.2: To store hidden type information for RTTI
choices.3: To make the struct size a power of two
choices.4: To reserve space for future members

explanation: Each member must sit at an address matching its alignment requirement.

question: To minimise wasted space from padding, how should you order a struct's fields?

choices.1: By how frequently each field is accessed
choices.2: By increasing alignment requirement (smallest first)
choices.3: Alphabetically by member name
choices.4: By decreasing alignment requirement

explanation: Largest-aligned members first.

question: For struct Example { char c; int i; double d; }, what is sizeof(Example) and why?

choices.1: 24, because each member is rounded up to 8 bytes
choices.2: 12, because char and int pack into the first 8 bytes
choices.3: 16, from alignment padding
choices.4: 13, because that is the exact sum of the member sizes

explanation: 3 bytes after c, 4 after i, and the struct rounds up to the largest member's 8-byte alignment.

question: Why is the struct itself padded at the end to a multiple of its largest member's alignment?

choices.1: To allow memset to zero it in one instruction
choices.2: Arrays need aligned elements
choices.3: To match the cache line size exactly
choices.4: To leave room for a vtable pointer

explanation: Every element of an array of the struct must start properly aligned.

question: How do you stop two threads' independent hot counters from causing false sharing?

choices.1: Declare them both thread_local in the same struct
choices.2: Mark both counters volatile
choices.3: Combine them into one atomic variable
choices.4: Put each on its own cache line using alignas(64)

explanation: Or std::hardware_destructive_interference_size.

question: What is the cost when a misaligned double straddles an alignment boundary?

choices.1: The compiler refuses to compile the program
choices.2: It is always faster because it skips alignment checks
choices.3: The value is silently truncated to fit
choices.4: Two accesses, or a fault

explanation: The CPU may need two memory accesses instead of one; some architectures fault outright.

question: When is removing padding via #pragma pack(1) / __attribute__((packed)) appropriate, per the note?

choices.1: Always, since smaller structs are universally faster
choices.2: Whenever a struct exceeds 64 bytes
choices.3: Only for I/O / serialization layouts — not hot data
choices.4: For hot, compute-heavy data structures, to save cache

explanation: Because misaligned access can be slower or even fault.

question: When you access one field across many objects, why can Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) beat Array-of-Structs (AoS)?

choices.1: SoA packs each field contiguously
choices.2: SoA uses less total memory because it has no padding at all
choices.3: SoA guarantees alignment to 64 bytes automatically
choices.4: AoS cannot be iterated with range-based for

explanation: Better cache utilization and SIMD-friendliness and avoiding dragging unused fields into cache.

question: Which tools/operators does the note suggest to confirm a struct's actual layout?

choices.1: valgrind and gdb breakpoints
choices.2: strace and ltrace
choices.3: nm and objdump symbol tables
choices.4: sizeof/alignof and pahole

explanation: Or clang -fdump-record-layouts.

question: What is the minimal modern Allocator interface a container needs?

choices.1: A single allocate function returning constructed objects
choices.2: Only operator new and operator delete
choices.3: construct, destroy, max_size and rebind<U>::other
choices.4: value_type, allocate, deallocate, ==

explanation: Plus a templated converting (rebind) constructor.

question: What does allocate(n) return, and what constructs the elements?

choices.1: Raw, uninitialized storage for n objects
choices.2: Fully constructed objects ready to use
choices.3: A reference-counted block that auto-destroys on deallocate
choices.4: Zero-initialized objects that still need their constructors run

explanation: The container constructs and destroys the elements separately (allocation is decoupled from object lifetime).

question: Why does the rebind (templated converting) constructor matter for a std::list?

choices.1: Because rebind disables exceptions during allocation
choices.2: Because list allocates internal node objects, not T
choices.3: Because list needs to allocate T plus a reference count
choices.4: Because list stores elements contiguously and needs alignment metadata

explanation: So the allocator must be rebindable to MyAllocator<Node>.

question: What does allocator equality (operator==) decide?

choices.1: Whether the two allocators were constructed in the same translation unit
choices.2: Whether the allocator supports the rebind constructor
choices.3: Whether the allocator is thread-safe
choices.4: Whether memory from one allocator instance can be freed by another

explanation: Interchangeability. Which governs cheap move/swap of containers.

question: Why do containers go through std::allocator_traits rather than calling the allocator directly?

choices.1: Because the allocator's allocate method is private
choices.2: Because allocator_traits supplies sensible defaults
choices.3: Because allocator_traits is required to allocate on the stack
choices.4: To bypass the allocator's operator== for speed

explanation: Construct/destroy, rebind, max_size, propagation traits. So a minimal allocator just works.

question: In a pool/free-list allocator, what does the union Slot { T value; Slot* next; } trick accomplish?

choices.1: It allows the pool to serve allocations of arbitrary size
choices.2: It guarantees the object is always cache-line aligned
choices.3: It stores the free-list pointer inside the slot while it is unused
choices.4: It lets a slot hold both an object and its free-list link simultaneously

explanation: So the pool needs zero extra metadata, with O(1) lock-free allocate/deallocate.

question: How does an arena / bump allocator work, and what standard resource embodies it?

choices.1: It frees each object individually via a per-size free list; std::pmr::synchronized_pool_resource
choices.2: It coalesces adjacent free blocks on every free; std::pmr::unsynchronized_pool_resource
choices.3: It allocates by advancing a pointer and frees everything at once by resetting the pointer
choices.4: It uses reference counting to reclaim memory; std::pmr::new_delete_resource

explanation: Std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource.

question: Why do custom pool/arena allocators help latency versus the global new/delete?

choices.1: They guarantee allocations never fail
choices.2: They run constructors in parallel
choices.3: No locks, syscalls, or tail spikes
choices.4: They compress objects to use less memory

explanation: Plus better cache locality and deterministic O(1) allocation.

question: What is the pragmatic advantage of PMR (polymorphic_allocator + memory resources) over hand-rolled allocators?

choices.1: Arenas without allocator templates
choices.2: It is always faster than any hand-rolled allocator
choices.3: It eliminates the need for allocate/deallocate entirely
choices.4: It makes all allocations thread-safe by default

explanation: Containers of different resources share one type — no templating on the allocator.

question: Is malloc a system call, and how does it usually satisfy a request?

choices.1: Yes, but only the first malloc call ever touches the kernel
choices.2: Yes, every malloc traps into the kernel to get fresh pages
choices.3: No — it is a library function
choices.4: No — malloc is a compiler intrinsic resolved at compile time

explanation: A user-space allocator (ptmalloc/tcmalloc/jemalloc) manages heaps obtained from the OS and sub-allocates chunks, usually with no syscall on the fast path.

question: How does glibc malloc typically satisfy small/medium versus large allocations?

choices.1: Both via mmap, differing only in flags
choices.2: Small via mmap, large via sbrk
choices.3: sbrk for small, mmap for large
choices.4: Both via sbrk only

explanation: Small/medium allocations extend the heap with sbrk/brk; large ones (roughly > 128 KiB) get a separate mmap region that can be individually returned with munmap.

question: How do new and malloc differ on allocation failure?

choices.1: Both throw std::bad_alloc
choices.2: new throws std::bad_alloc, whereas malloc returns NULL
choices.3: Both return NULL
choices.4: new returns NULL, whereas malloc throws std::bad_alloc

question: What two actions does the new expression perform?

choices.1: It only allocates memory; the constructor must be called manually
choices.2: Allocate, then construct
choices.3: It runs the constructor first, then allocates memory to hold the result
choices.4: It calls malloc and then memsets the memory to zero

explanation: operator new obtains raw memory, then the constructor runs in it (placement-new internally).

question: If a constructor throws during evaluation of a new expression, what happens to the allocated memory?

choices.1: It is zeroed and returned to the caller anyway
choices.2: It becomes undefined-behaviour territory the runtime cannot handle
choices.3: It is automatically freed via the matching operator delete
choices.4: It leaks, because the constructor never finished

question: What does delete do that C's free does not?

choices.1: It checks for double-free and aborts safely
choices.2: It runs the destructor first
choices.3: It nulls out the pointer after freeing
choices.4: It always returns the memory to the OS immediately

explanation: Then deallocates via operator delete — free never calls destructors.

question: Why must delete[] be used (not delete) for memory from new[]?

choices.1: delete[] returns the memory to a different heap arena
choices.2: delete[] disables exceptions during destruction
choices.3: Element count lives in hidden metadata
choices.4: delete[] is merely a stylistic alias for delete

explanation: delete[] runs every element's destructor using it; plain delete on a new[] block is UB.

question: Why does a multithreaded ptmalloc use multiple arenas (often one per thread)?

choices.1: To reduce lock contention, since each arena has its own lock
choices.2: To keep each thread's memory in a different NUMA node automatically
choices.3: To give each thread a separate address space
choices.4: To allow each thread a different allocation size limit

question: Why does free() often not reduce the process's memory as seen in top/htop?

choices.1: Chunks return to bins, not the OS
choices.2: Because the kernel lazily reclaims freed pages after a fixed delay
choices.3: Because top/htop cannot measure heap memory
choices.4: Because free zeroes the memory but keeps the mapping for security

explanation: free() usually returns memory to the allocator's internal caches; it only truly returns for mmap'd blocks (munmap) or when a coalesced top-of-heap block lets brk shrink (rare).

question: What does placement new do?

choices.1: Constructs an object in already-allocated storage, performing no allocation itself
choices.2: Allocates memory at a kernel-chosen placement address and then constructs an object
choices.3: Relocates an existing object to a new address
choices.4: Allocates memory but skips construction

question: When realloc cannot grow a block in place, what is the slow path, and how does this relate to std::vector?

choices.1: It always uses mremap, so it is O(1)
choices.2: malloc, memcpy, free — O(n)
choices.3: It zeroes the extra space and returns the same pointer
choices.4: It throws std::bad_alloc and leaves the old block untouched

explanation: It copies min(old, new) bytes to a new block; that moving cost is why vector grows by doubling to amortise it.

question: What is the key difference between memcpy and memmove?

choices.1: memcpy runs constructors; memmove does not
choices.2: memcpy is fully overlap-safe; memmove requires non-overlapping regions
choices.3: memcpy requires the source and destination regions not to overlap
choices.4: memcpy copies element counts; memmove copies bytes

explanation: Memmove behaves correctly even when they overlap. Overlap is UB.

question: Why is using the mem* functions on a non-trivially-copyable type undefined behaviour?

choices.1: Raw bytes ignore the object model
choices.2: Because mem* cannot handle types larger than a cache line
choices.3: Because the compiler forbids passing class types to void* parameters
choices.4: Because mem* functions are slower than the type's copy constructor

explanation: No constructors/destructors run — a memcpy'd std::string aliases the same heap buffer, leading to a double free.

question: What does memset(arr, 1, n) do to an array of ints, and why?

choices.1: Is undefined behaviour for any non-char array
choices.2: Sets each int to 1, because memset is value-aware
choices.3: Sets each int to 0x01010101, because memset fills identical bytes
choices.4: Sets only the very first int to 1 and leaves all the rest unchanged

explanation: Only 0 and 0xFF are meaningful for multi-byte types.

question: Why can a plain memset(buf, 0, n) fail to securely erase secrets?

choices.1: The compiler can delete dead stores
choices.2: Because memset cannot write the value 0
choices.3: Because memset only writes the first byte of buf
choices.4: Because the OS re-fills freed memory with the old contents

explanation: If buf is never read again, memset may legally be eliminated; use explicit_bzero, memset_s, or a volatile-pointer loop.

question: Why is memcmp == 0 not a reliable test of logical equality?

choices.1: Because memcmp always returns 0 for equal-length buffers
choices.2: Because byte-identical is not logically equal
choices.3: Because memcmp compares as signed char, inverting high bytes
choices.4: Because memcmp ignores the length argument

explanation: Struct padding bytes are indeterminate, floats have +0.0/-0.0 and NaN quirks, and multi-byte integer ordering reflects byte order (endianness), not numeric value.

question: The mem* functions take a length argument measured in what units?

choices.1: Machine words (8 bytes each)
choices.2: Element counts, like the STL algorithms
choices.3: Bytes
choices.4: Bits

explanation: For arrays multiply by sizeof(T): memcpy(d, s, n * sizeof(T)).

question: Passing a null pointer to memcpy when n == 0 is:

choices.1: Undefined behaviour regardless of length
choices.2: Well-defined only if the destination is also null
choices.3: Implementation-defined but never UB
choices.4: Always well-defined, since no bytes are touched

explanation: A null pointer is invalid even when n == 0.

question: How does memchr differ from strchr, making it safe on binary data?

choices.1: memchr returns an index rather than a pointer
choices.2: It honors n, not NUL
choices.3: memchr is NUL-terminated like strchr but faster
choices.4: memchr searches backwards from the end of the buffer

explanation: memchr respects the explicit length and doesn't stop at a terminator, so binary bytes are fine.

question: Which C++ typed equivalents does the note recommend in place of raw mem* calls?

choices.1: std::memcpy is always preferred; there are no typed equivalents
choices.2: std::move and std::forward for all copies
choices.3: copy, fill, and bit_cast
choices.4: operator new and operator delete

explanation: std::copy/copy_n lower to memmove for trivially copyable types, std::fill lowers to memset, and std::bit_cast replaces reinterpret_cast punning.

question: What does the note say about memcpy and object creation in C++20+?

choices.1: memcpy always requires std::launder before the bytes can be read back
choices.2: memcpy can construct objects of any type, including those with constructors
choices.3: Implicit-lifetime objects appear
choices.4: memcpy now runs the copy constructor for class types automatically

explanation: memcpy into suitable storage implicitly creates objects of implicit-lifetime type (no std::launder needed) — but never non-trivial objects.

question: What is the central idea of RAII?

choices.1: Tie a resource's lifetime to an object's lifetime
choices.2: Use reference counting on every resource so it is freed when the last user drops it
choices.3: Defer all cleanup to a manual goto cleanup label at the end of each function
choices.4: Allocate all resources at program start and release them all at program end

explanation: Acquire it in the constructor and release it in the destructor, so cleanup runs on every exit path.

question: When an exception is thrown, what guarantees RAII cleanup happens but manual cleanup may not?

choices.1: Exceptions trigger garbage collection, which reclaims both RAII objects and manually allocated resources
choices.2: Manual cleanup runs first via atexit handlers, then RAII destructors run
choices.3: The runtime scans the heap and frees any unreferenced memory before propagating the exception
choices.4: Unwinding runs destructors; code is skipped

explanation: Every fully-constructed local's destructor runs during unwinding, while manual cleanup after the throw point never executes.

question: When does an object's lifetime begin and end?

choices.1: It begins when its constructor completes (and storage is obtained) and ends when its destructor starts
choices.2: It begins when the object is first read and ends when it is last read
choices.3: It begins at the opening brace of its scope and ends at the closing brace
choices.4: It begins when storage is allocated and ends when storage is freed, regardless of constructor/destructor

explanation: Accessing the object outside that window is UB.

question: How do placement new and an explicit destructor call relate storage duration and lifetime?

choices.1: They decouple lifetime from storage
choices.2: Placement new ends a lifetime and the explicit destructor call begins a new one
choices.3: They merge the two so that lifetime and storage duration always coincide
choices.4: They are illegal outside of constexpr contexts

explanation: Placement new begins a lifetime in existing storage; an explicit ~T() ends it while the storage remains.

question: Why does const char* p = make().c_str(); leave p dangling (where make returns std::string by value)?

choices.1: The compiler copies the string into p, but the copy is const and immediately destroyed
choices.2: make() returns a reference to a local, which is the actual bug
choices.3: The temporary string dies at the end of the full expression (the semicolon)
choices.4: c_str() always returns a pointer to a static empty buffer

explanation: So p points into freed memory; lifetime extension does not chain through the c_str() call.

question: Which binding extends a temporary's lifetime beyond the end of the full expression?

choices.1: Assigning it to a plain auto variable by value
choices.2: Binding it to a const& or an rvalue reference
choices.3: Passing it by value to a function
choices.4: Taking its address with &

explanation: And range-for over the temporary extends it across the loop.

question: In what order are a class's non-static data members constructed and destroyed?

choices.1: Constructed in declaration order and destroyed in the same (declaration) order
choices.2: Constructed alphabetically by name, destroyed in reverse
choices.3: Constructed in declaration order (not member-initializer-list order) and destroyed in the reverse of that order
choices.4: Constructed strictly in the order they appear in the constructor's initializer list, and destroyed in the same order

question: Why does a polymorphic base class need a virtual destructor?

choices.1: Without it, deleting a derived object through a base pointer is UB
choices.2: Without it, the base constructor would run after the derived constructor
choices.3: Because non-virtual destructors cannot be called during stack unwinding
choices.4: Because the compiler refuses to compile any class with virtual functions otherwise

explanation: Because the derived destructor never runs.

question: Which standard-library types are RAII owners that release in their destructors?

choices.1: None at all — the standard library requires manual cleanup
choices.2: Only the smart pointers unique_ptr and shared_ptr
choices.3: Only container types such as vector and string
choices.4: unique_ptr, shared_ptr, lock_guard/scoped_lock, vector

explanation: String, fstream.

question: What does std::pmr (polymorphic memory resources) let you do?

choices.1: Make every allocation thread-safe and lock-free by default
choices.2: Runtime allocators, one container type
choices.3: Replace new/delete globally for the whole program
choices.4: Allocate memory that is automatically garbage collected

explanation: The allocation strategy is chosen at runtime while the container's type stays unchanged.

question: What is the central abstract interface in PMR?

choices.1: std::allocator_traits, specialised for pmr
choices.2: std::pmr::memory_resource
choices.3: std::pmr::polymorphic_allocator, a concrete final class
choices.4: std::pmr::vector, which all resources derive from

explanation: An abstract base with virtual do_allocate/do_deallocate/do_is_equal.

question: What is the call chain when a std::pmr::vector allocates?

choices.1: pmr::vector -> operator new -> memory_resource -> the kernel
choices.2: vector, allocator, resource, strategy
choices.3: pmr::vector -> memory_resource -> polymorphic_allocator<T> -> the heap
choices.4: pmr::vector -> allocator_traits -> std::allocator -> malloc

explanation: pmr::vector -> polymorphic_allocator<T> -> memory_resource -> the actual allocation strategy.

question: What does a std::pmr::polymorphic_allocator<T> actually store, and why is it 'polymorphic'?

choices.1: It stores a vtable of allocation functions generated per type T
choices.2: It stores a pointer to a memory_resource
choices.3: It stores a template parameter selecting the strategy at compile time
choices.4: It stores an inline copy of the buffer it allocates from, fixed at compile time

explanation: It is polymorphic because the exact resource (and thus strategy) can vary at runtime through that pointer.

question: Why can two std::pmr::vector<int> objects share one type even with different allocation strategies, unlike std::vector<int, MyAllocator<int>>?

choices.1: The strategy hides behind a pointer — so changing it does not change the type
choices.2: Because polymorphic_allocator is type-erased into void* at every call
choices.3: Because std::vector<int, MyAllocator<int>> is actually the same type as std::vector<int>
choices.4: Because pmr containers ignore their allocator entirely

explanation: A runtime memory_resource pointer, not the container's static type.

question: What is the behaviour of std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource?

choices.1: It always forwards directly to global new/delete
choices.2: It maintains size-class pools and frees each object individually
choices.3: It reference-counts allocations and frees them when the count hits zero
choices.4: It allocates from a buffer and typically releases everything at once

explanation: Ideal for short-lived groups of objects.

question: How do unsynchronized_pool_resource and synchronized_pool_resource differ?

choices.1: They are identical aliases kept for backward compatibility
choices.2: unsynchronized is for single allocations; synchronized is for arrays
choices.3: unsynchronized uses the heap; synchronized uses a stack buffer
choices.4: unsynchronized uses pools without thread synchronization

explanation: Synchronized adds synchronization so it is safe across threads. Faster, not thread-safe.

question: What does std::pmr::new_delete_resource() use under the hood?

choices.1: A thread-local pool per calling thread
choices.2: A stack-allocated monotonic buffer
choices.3: The normal global new and delete
choices.4: mmap directly, bypassing the C++ allocator

question: In the example using a std::array<std::byte,1024> with a monotonic_buffer_resource, where does the pmr::vector get its memory?

choices.1: From the local stack buffer first
choices.2: From a global memory pool shared by all pmr containers
choices.3: From thread-local storage allocated at thread start
choices.4: Always directly from the heap, ignoring the buffer

explanation: Falling back to the heap only if the buffer is exhausted.

question: What does std::launder(p) do?

choices.1: It starts the lifetime of a new T object at that address
choices.2: It reinterprets the bytes at p as a T, enabling type punning
choices.3: It fixes a misaligned pointer so the object can be accessed safely
choices.4: A provenance barrier to a live object

explanation: It returns a usable pointer to the already-live T at that address.

question: Where is std::launder declared and what is its signature?

choices.1: In <type_traits>: template<class T> constexpr T launder(T p) noexcept
choices.2: In <memory>: template<class T> T* launder(void* p)
choices.3: In <new>: template<class T> constexpr T* launder(T* p) noexcept
choices.4: In <cstring>: void* launder(void* p, size_t n)

question: Which of these does std::launder NOT do?

choices.1: Return a valid pointer that the abstract machine may use to refer to the current object at that address
choices.2: Help after certain placement-new reuse cases where old pointers are stale
choices.3: Create an object, start a lifetime, fix bad alignment, change the bytes, or make type punning legal
choices.4: Act as a provenance/optimization barrier

question: Under the transparent replacement rule, when do you NOT need std::launder after destroying and reconstructing an object in the same storage?

choices.1: Whenever the new object has a different type than the old one
choices.2: Only when the storage is dynamically allocated
choices.3: Only when the old object was const
choices.4: Same storage, type, non-const, complete

explanation: Same storage, same type (ignoring top-level cv), old object not const, and a complete object (or matching subobject) — then the old name/pointer retargets automatically.

question: Which situation requires std::launder because transparent replacement fails?

choices.1: Any time you call placement new, without exception
choices.2: Const objects, bases, or raw-storage pointers
choices.3: Whenever the object is larger than a cache line
choices.4: Only when allocating with malloc instead of new

explanation: Transparent replacement fails when the old object was const, for base-class subobjects, or when you only hold a pointer derived from raw byte storage.

question: After new (const_cast<X*>(p)) const X{5}; over a previously const X, why is reading p->n directly UB while std::launder(p)->n is OK?

choices.1: The const old object breaks retargeting
choices.2: Because launder physically copies the new value to where p points
choices.3: Because p->n reads uninitialized memory until launder zero-initializes it
choices.4: Because const_cast itself is UB and launder repairs it

explanation: The old pointer doesn't automatically refer to the new object; launder yields a valid pointer to it.

question: What is the important caveat about placement-new'ing into the storage of a const complete object?

choices.1: It is fine for static objects but UB for automatic ones
choices.2: It is always fine as long as you launder the result afterward
choices.3: Const complete objects are off-limits
choices.4: It is fine only if the new object is also const

explanation: Placement-new into storage of a const complete object with static/thread/automatic storage duration is UB, even with launder.

question: Why can std::launder NOT be used to make *p valid over raw uninitialized storage?

choices.1: Because launder zeroes the storage, destroying any value you wrote
choices.2: Because launder requires the storage to be heap-allocated
choices.3: Because launder does not begin an object's lifetime
choices.4: Because launder only works on trivially copyable types

explanation: You must first create the object (e.g. placement new or std::construct_at) — launder only yields a pointer to an object that is already alive.

question: Why is std::launder not a type-punning tool (e.g. launder an int* over a float)?

choices.1: Because launder<int> would just silently convert the float's value to an int
