explanation: Stronger consistency, less duplication, and easier/safer updates (good for transactional OLTP systems).

question: What is a downside of a highly normalised schema?

choices.1: Data becomes duplicated and can go stale
choices.2: It uses far more storage than denormalised data
choices.3: Common queries may need to join several tables
choices.4: Writes must update many tables at once

explanation: Queries more complex and read performance potentially worse — and it is less convenient for reporting.

question: What is a key advantage of denormalisation?

choices.1: It makes writes simpler and safer
choices.2: It guarantees there is exactly one single copy of each fact
choices.3: Related data is stored together so reads need fewer joins
choices.4: It always reduces total storage

explanation: Faster, simpler reads — which suits reporting, dashboards, search indexes, and read-heavy/OLAP workloads.

question: What is the main risk of denormalisation?

choices.1: It prevents the database from scaling reads
choices.2: Reads become much slower due to extra joins
choices.3: It makes foreign-key constraints impossible
choices.4: Duplicates drifting out of sync

explanation: The same value exists in several places and can become inconsistent/stale; writes get more complex because one logical change may need to update many tables/systems.

question: Why can storing customer_name directly on an invoice be valid rather than bad denormalisation?

choices.1: Because names are never updated
choices.2: Because invoices never reference customers by id
choices.3: It can be a deliberate historical snapshot
choices.4: Because it always keeps the customer table in sync automatically

explanation: The invoice should show the name as it was at the time of purchase, even if the customer later changes their name.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: How do OLTP and OLAP workloads differ?

choices.1: OLTP scans billions of rows; OLAP does point lookups
choices.2: OLTP is read-only; OLAP is write-only
choices.3: Few rows all columns, or the reverse
choices.4: Both touch few rows and all columns, differing only in latency

explanation: OLTP: many small fast point reads/writes touching few rows but all columns. OLAP: large scans and aggregations touching many rows but few columns.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: What is the physical-storage consequence of the OLTP/OLAP split?

choices.1: OLTP favours column stores; OLAP favours row stores
choices.2: Both favour column stores
choices.3: Both favour row stores
choices.4: OLTP favours row stores; OLAP favours column stores

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: In a row store, why is 'SUM(amount)' over a wide table wasteful?

choices.1: Because row stores store each column in a separate file
choices.2: A row is contiguous on disk
choices.3: Because SUM requires reconstructing rows from many files
choices.4: Because row stores cannot compute aggregates

explanation: I/O is proportional to the full table width, not just the column touched. So reading one column (amount) forces reading every column of every row.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: Why does columnar storage win for analytics?

choices.1: Because it makes single-row updates cheap
choices.2: Each column is contiguous
choices.3: Because it avoids any compression overhead
choices.4: Because columns are stored row-by-row for locality

explanation: Columns of one type with low cardinality/sorted runs compress far better (RLE/dictionary/delta), and vectorized SIMD/cache-friendly execution processes a column as a batch. So a query reads only the columns it needs.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: Why does columnar storage lose for OLTP?

choices.1: Because columnar stores cannot be queried with SQL
choices.2: Because OLTP never reads individual rows
choices.3: Row writes and row reads touch many columns
choices.4: Because columns are too compressed to read

explanation: Inserting or updating one row writes many column segments (write amplification), and reading a full row scatters across many column files — columnar stores are append/batch-oriented, not point-update systems.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: How do you connect the OLTP and OLAP worlds in practice?

choices.1: Store both row and column copies in the same OLTP table
choices.2: Run analytical queries directly on the production OLTP database
choices.3: Replace the OLTP database with a columnar store
choices.4: ETL/ELT or CDC into a warehouse

explanation: Move data OLTP -> OLAP in batch (ETL/ELT) or near-real-time (CDC streaming WAL/binlog changes) into a columnar warehouse/lake — don't run analytics on the production DB.

topic: OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores
source: C++/Databases/OLTP vs OLAP and Column Stores.md
question: Which databases are typical examples of OLTP row stores vs OLAP column stores?

choices.1: OLTP: Redis; OLAP: Memcached
choices.2: OLTP row stores: Postgres, MySQL, DynamoDB
choices.3: Both use the same engines, differing only in indexes
choices.4: OLTP: Snowflake and BigQuery; OLAP: Postgres and MySQL

explanation: OLAP column stores: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, ClickHouse, DuckDB.

question: What is a geohash?

choices.1: A Base32 cell-encoding of lat/lon
choices.2: A checksum of GPS hardware output
choices.3: An exact lat/lon coordinate stored as a hash
choices.4: A hash of a city name used for routing

explanation: Longer strings mean smaller, more precise rectangular cells.

question: How does geohash encoding work at the core?

choices.1: It looks the coordinates up in a global table of cells
choices.2: It repeatedly splits the world in half
choices.3: It stores the binary IEEE-754 bits of the two coordinates
choices.4: It multiplies latitude by longitude and hashes the result

explanation: Writing a 0/1 bit per split, then groups the bits into chunks of 5 and encodes each as a Base32 character. Alternating longitude and latitude.

question: What is notable about the geohash Base32 alphabet?

choices.1: Each character encodes 8 bits (one byte)
choices.2: It is case-sensitive, using both upper and lower case
choices.3: It uses all 26 letters plus 6 symbols
choices.4: It drops a, i, l, and o

explanation: Avoiding confusion with similar-looking characters; each character encodes 5 bits.

question: Why do nearby places often share a geohash prefix, and how is that exploited?

choices.1: Because the prefix encodes the exact distance between points
choices.2: Because geohashes are sorted by physical distance from the equator
choices.3: Each character nests a smaller cell
choices.4: Because all points in a country share an identical prefix

explanation: Databases can do prefix scans (e.g. WHERE geohash LIKE 'ezs4%'). So a shared prefix means the same larger cell. Shared prefixes mean nested cells, so prefix search finds nearby candidates.

question: What is the geohash boundary problem, and how is it mitigated in nearby search?

choices.1: The boundary problem only affects points near the equator
choices.2: Neighbors can straddle a cell edge
choices.3: Geohashes overflow at cell boundaries, corrupting the string
choices.4: Points exactly on a boundary cannot be encoded at all

explanation: Two close points on opposite sides of a boundary get very different geohashes, so nearby search must also query neighboring cells and filter by true distance.

question: Why are geohashes good for coarse spatial filtering but not final distance ranking?

choices.1: Because longer geohashes are always farther apart
choices.2: Because geohashes are only valid at the equator
choices.3: Because geohashes store distance directly and ranking is automatic
choices.4: String closeness isn't physical closeness

explanation: Cells are rectangular and not equal-size everywhere (east-west width shrinks toward the poles by ~cos(latitude)), so a true-distance check is still needed.

question: What is a quadtree?

choices.1: A balanced tree of sorted key ranges
choices.2: Recursive four-way spatial splits
choices.3: A tree that divides space into hexagonal cells
choices.4: A binary tree where each node has at most two children

explanation: 2D space divides into NW/NE/SW/SE quadrants, each splittable again.

question: What is the main advantage of a quadtree for geospatial data?

choices.1: It stores all points in a single sorted list
choices.2: Every cell is always the same physical size regardless of density
choices.3: It guarantees O(1) point insertion with no subdivision
choices.4: It adapts to data density

explanation: A leaf subdivides into four children once it exceeds its capacity, so dense areas get fine cells while sparse areas stay coarse.

question: How does a bounding-box query work in a quadtree?

choices.1: Only the root node is checked; children are ignored
choices.2: Scan every point in the tree and keep those inside the box
choices.3: Hash the box corners and look up matching cells directly
choices.4: Prune non-overlapping regions, then filter

explanation: Skip any node whose region doesn't overlap the query box, recurse into overlapping nodes, and finally check individual points against the query shape.

question: Why are depth limits important in a quadtree?

choices.1: Because deeper trees use less memory
choices.2: Because depth limits make queries return exact distances
choices.3: Because quadtrees can only have four levels by definition
choices.4: Clustered points can subdivide forever

explanation: Near-identical points cause excessive subdivision without a max depth or minimum cell size; safeguards include max depth, min cell size, and multiple points per leaf.

question: How does a radius query typically use a quadtree?

choices.1: The quadtree converts the circle into a geohash prefix
choices.2: Bounding box first, exact math second
choices.3: The quadtree directly returns only points within the exact radius with no post-filtering
choices.4: The quadtree cannot answer radius queries at all

explanation: Candidates inside a box around the circle are filtered by distance <= radius.

question: How does a quadtree differ from an R-tree?

choices.1: A quadtree cannot adapt to density while an R-tree can
choices.2: They are the same structure with different names
choices.3: A quadtree uses variable rectangles; an R-tree uses fixed quadrants
choices.4: A quadtree uses fixed 4-way spatial subdivision

explanation: Great for points, tiles, grids, map viewports); an R-tree uses data-driven bounding rectangles (better for rectangles, polygons, and mixed geometries.

question: What is an R-tree?

choices.1: A tree that sorts one-dimensional numeric keys
choices.2: A grid of fixed-size square cells
choices.3: A hash table mapping coordinates to objects
choices.4: A hierarchy of bounding rectangles

explanation: It indexes multi-dimensional spatial objects by their minimum bounding rectangles (MBRs) — the spatial equivalent of a B-tree.

question: What do internal node entries store in an R-tree, versus leaf entries?

choices.1: Both store only object IDs with no bounding boxes
choices.2: Internal entries store points; leaf entries store rectangles
choices.3: Internal entries store actual geometries; leaf entries store covering boxes
choices.4: Covering boxes versus object boxes

explanation: Internal entries hold a box covering everything below plus a child pointer; leaf entries hold an object's box plus a pointer to the actual geometry/row.

question: How does an R-tree prune the search for a query rectangle?

choices.1: It scans every object's geometry against the query
choices.2: It hashes the query box to find one matching leaf
choices.3: It skips subtrees whose boxes miss the query
choices.4: It always visits every node in the tree

explanation: It recurses only into children whose bounding boxes intersect the query rectangle, and exact-tests candidates at the leaves.

question: Why does an R-tree index bounding boxes rather than exact geometries, and what two-step process results?

choices.1: Because the database forbids polygon comparisons
choices.2: Because exact geometries cannot be stored on disk
choices.3: Rectangle tests are cheap
choices.4: Because bounding boxes are always identical to the geometry

explanation: E.g. ST_Intersects. So the index does a fast bounding-box filter to find candidates, then runs the exact spatial predicate only on those. Overlap checks on boxes cost far less than exact polygon intersection; exact tests run only on final candidates.

question: How is a new object inserted into an R-tree?

choices.1: Insert into the leaf with the largest bounding box
choices.2: Insert into a random leaf and rebuild the whole tree
choices.3: Always insert into the root and never split
choices.4: Least-enlargement leaf, then fix boxes upward

explanation: Compute the bounding box, insert at the leaf needing least enlargement, update boxes up to the root, and split any node that overflows.

question: What is the main challenge that distinguishes R-trees from B-trees?

choices.1: R-trees require the data to be one-dimensional
choices.2: R-trees cannot store more than one object per leaf
choices.3: R-trees never need node splits
choices.4: Bounding rectangles can overlap

explanation: Unlike a B-tree where a key belongs to exactly one sorted range, a query intersecting two boxes must follow both paths — performance depends on minimizing bbox area and overlap.

question: Which R-tree variant or implementation is correctly described?

choices.1: The R+ tree deliberately increases overlap to speed up inserts
choices.2: R*-tree improves insertion/splitting to reduce overlap
choices.3: PostGIS uses a hash index for spatial queries
choices.4: The Hilbert R-tree removes all hierarchy

explanation: R+ tree avoids overlap by duplicating objects across nodes; PostGIS commonly implements R-tree-like behavior via a GiST index.

question: What is an inverted index?

choices.1: A B-tree mapping document IDs to their byte offsets
choices.2: A word-to-documents mapping
choices.3: A mapping from documents to their word counts only
choices.4: A reversed copy of each document's text

explanation: The inverse of storing document -> words — like a book's back-of-book index.

question: What does the analysis step do before terms enter an inverted index (e.g. in Elasticsearch)?

choices.1: It encrypts the text for storage
choices.2: Analyzed terms, not raw text
choices.3: It compresses the document into a single token
choices.4: It stores the document exactly as written with no transformation

explanation: Lowercasing, stop-word removal, punctuation splits, stemming, and synonyms happen before terms enter the index.

question: Beyond document IDs, what do real inverted-index posting lists commonly store?

choices.1: The full original document text for each term
choices.2: The disk block address of each character
choices.3: The user who created each document
choices.4: Frequency, positions, offsets, field

explanation: Everything ranking and phrase search need.

question: Why is an inverted index fast for full-text search?

choices.1: It sorts documents by length so search is binary
choices.2: Jump straight to the posting list
choices.3: It compresses every document to one byte
choices.4: It keeps all documents in CPU registers

explanation: No scanning every document — the term maps directly to its matching documents.

question: Why are term positions needed for a phrase search like "quick brown fox"?

choices.1: Phrase search ignores positions and matches any document with all words
choices.2: Positions are only used to highlight results, not to match phrases
choices.3: Knowing all three words appear in a document isn't enough
choices.4: Positions store the document's relevance score

explanation: Positions confirm the words are adjacent in the right order, distinguishing the exact phrase from documents where the words are scattered.

question: What does the BM25 scoring model favour when ranking results?

choices.1: The document that happens to have the smallest ID
choices.2: Frequent in the doc, rare in the corpus
choices.3: The document indexed most recently
choices.4: The document with the most total words

explanation: Also: the term appears in important fields, more query terms match, and (for phrases) the order matches — a refined TF-IDF style ranking.

question: What is the difference between a 'text' and a 'keyword' field in Elasticsearch?

choices.1: A text field can't be searched; only keyword fields can
choices.2: A keyword field is analyzed; a text field is exact
choices.3: Both are analyzed identically; the names are interchangeable
choices.4: A text field is analyzed into terms for full-text search

explanation: A keyword field is indexed as one exact value for filtering, aggregations, and sorting (good for status = "In Progress", not for searching just "progress").

question: What is an LSM (Log-Structured Merge) tree optimized for, and how?

choices.1: Fast in-place random updates like a B-tree
choices.2: Read-only analytical scans
choices.3: Minimizing disk usage above all else
choices.4: Fast writes via sequential appends

explanation: Instead of updating in place, it writes changes sequentially (buffering in memory) and reorganizes in the background, turning random writes into mostly sequential ones.

question: What is the LSM write path?

choices.1: WAL, memtable, flush to SSTable
choices.2: Find the key on disk and overwrite it in place immediately
choices.3: Broadcast the write to all replicas before storing it
choices.4: Write directly to a B-tree page and split if needed

explanation: Append to the log, insert into the sorted memtable, and later flush it as an immutable sorted file.

question: Why can reads be slower in an LSM tree?

choices.1: A key may exist in several places
choices.2: Because every read rewrites the data first
choices.3: Because the memtable is on disk, not in memory
choices.4: Because reads must wait for compaction to finish

explanation: Active memtable, immutable memtables, and multiple SSTables across levels. So the read may check several before finding the newest value or proving the key is absent.

question: How are deletes handled in an LSM tree?

choices.1: By writing a special tombstone marker
choices.2: By moving the key to a separate deleted file instantly
choices.3: By immediately erasing the key from every SSTable
choices.4: By marking the disk block as bad

explanation: Reads use it to ignore older versions of the key, and compaction physically removes the tombstone and old values later when safe.

question: What is compaction in an LSM tree?

choices.1: The process of compressing the WAL into a single byte
choices.2: Rebuilding the memtable from scratch on each write
choices.3: Encrypting SSTables before storage
choices.4: Background merging of SSTables

explanation: It discards obsolete versions and expired tombstones and produces new sorted files, gradually pushing data down through levels.

question: What are the three 'amplification' costs of LSM trees?

choices.1: Write amplification, read amplification, and space amplification
choices.2: Latency, bandwidth, and CPU amplification
choices.3: Index, cache, and log amplification
choices.4: Only write amplification exists; both reads and space are unaffected

explanation: Compaction rewrites a key multiple times as it moves through levels. Reads consult several files; Bloom filters help. Old versions/tombstones linger until compaction.

question: How does an LSM tree compare to a B-tree?

choices.1: They are identical in write pattern and read cost
choices.2: Sequential-write throughput versus read latency
choices.3: LSM trees do in-place updates; B-trees buffer in memory
choices.4: B-trees have higher write throughput than LSM trees

explanation: LSM trees turn writes sequential for higher write throughput but point reads may check multiple files; B-trees update in place with direct page lookups, suiting read-heavy or low-latency mixed workloads.

question: What is a memtable?

choices.1: An on-disk index of all table names
choices.2: An in-memory write buffer
choices.3: A cache of compiled SQL query plans
choices.4: A persistent log of every write ever made

explanation: LSM-tree engines store recent writes in RAM before flushing them to disk as immutable sorted files.

question: Why does a memtable speed up writes?

choices.1: It compresses each write before storing it on disk
choices.2: It avoids writing to disk entirely, even on flush
choices.3: Random small writes become batched appends
choices.4: It writes each update straight to disk twice for safety

explanation: Buffering in memory and flushing in batches turns scattered writes into sequential ones.

question: What is the typical memtable write path?

choices.1: Broadcast to replicas before touching local memory
choices.2: Write to an SSTable first, then the memtable
choices.3: WAL, memtable, then ack
choices.4: Insert into the memtable, then acknowledge, with no logging

explanation: Append to the commit log, insert into the memtable, acknowledge the client.

question: Why write to a WAL if the memtable already holds the data in memory?

choices.1: RAM is volatile; the WAL is replayable
choices.2: Because the memtable cannot store deletes
choices.3: Because the WAL is faster to read than the memtable
choices.4: Because the WAL replaces the need for SSTables

explanation: A crash before the memtable flushes would lose in-memory data; the durable WAL rebuilds it (memtable = fast, WAL = durable).

question: Why is a memtable usually kept sorted by key?

choices.1: Because the WAL requires sorted input
choices.2: Because sorting reduces RAM usage to zero
choices.3: Because unsorted memtables cannot accept writes
choices.4: So flushes emit a sorted SSTable directly

explanation: It also supports ordered reads/range scans; typically a skip list or balanced tree.

question: What happens during a memtable flush?

choices.1: The memtable is deleted and all its data lost
choices.2: It is frozen and written out as an SSTable
choices.3: The WAL is flushed into the memtable
choices.4: All SSTables are merged into the memtable

explanation: When the active memtable fills it is made immutable, a new memtable accepts writes, and the old one is written to disk as an SSTable — after which the corresponding WAL section can be discarded.

question: What is an SSTable (Sorted String Table)?

choices.1: A sorted immutable on-disk file
choices.2: A B-tree page that is updated in place
choices.3: A temporary log of uncommitted transactions
choices.4: A mutable in-memory hash table of recent writes

explanation: Key-value pairs used by LSM-tree NoSQL databases.

question: Why are SSTables immutable, and what is the benefit?

choices.1: Immutability lets the database edit any record instantly
choices.2: They are immutable to save RAM during reads
choices.3: They are immutable only until the first read
choices.4: Once written they are never modified in place

explanation: Updates/deletes write new versions elsewhere and old data is removed during compaction — this avoids random in-place disk updates, making writes sequential and fast.

question: Why does storing keys in sorted order help an SSTable?

choices.1: Sorting makes writes random instead of sequential
choices.2: Sorting lets the file be compressed to a single key
choices.3: Binary search and range scans
choices.4: Sorting removes the need for a memtable

explanation: Sorted keys enable efficient lookups via indexes and efficient range reads.

question: Why might a read consult multiple SSTables, and what reduces the cost?

choices.1: Because reads always rewrite the SSTable first
choices.2: The same key can appear in several SSTables
choices.3: Because SSTables must be read in random order
choices.4: Because each SSTable holds only one key

explanation: Bloom filters, indexes, partition summaries, and caches reduce unnecessary disk access. The newest version wins. So the engine may check several.

question: How are updates and deletes represented given SSTable immutability?

choices.1: An update writes a newer record, and a delete writes a tombstone marker
choices.2: Both updates and deletes are rejected on immutable files
choices.3: Updates edit the old SSTable directly in place, while deletes erase the row
choices.4: Deletes immediately rewrite every SSTable

explanation: The older data may persist in older SSTables until compaction removes it. Treated as correct.

question: What is the specific purpose of a Bloom filter for SSTables?

choices.1: It guarantees with certainty that a key exists in the SSTable
choices.2: It compresses the SSTable on disk
choices.3: It stores the exact value for every key
choices.4: 'Probably not here' in O(1)

explanation: Letting it skip a disk read for that file. It quickly rules SSTables out; false positives just cost one wasted read.

question: What is a Write-Ahead Log (WAL), and what is its basic rule?

choices.1: A sorted on-disk file of all current key-values
choices.2: An in-memory cache of recently read rows
choices.3: Log the change first, then modify
choices.4: A log written only after the data files are updated

explanation: A durable append-only log records changes before they are applied to the main data files.

question: How does a WAL enable crash recovery?

choices.1: It deletes any data written before the crash
choices.2: It restores the database from a nightly full backup
choices.3: It prevents crashes from happening at all
choices.4: Replay or roll back from the log

explanation: On restart the database inspects the WAL, replays completed operations, and discards incomplete ones — the log is the source of truth during recovery.

question: What role does the WAL play in an LSM-tree engine's write path?

choices.1: It stores the final query results
choices.2: It rebuilds the memtable after a crash
choices.3: It replaces the memtable entirely
choices.4: It is written only after the memtable is flushed

explanation: Writes go to the WAL then the in-memory memtable before acknowledging; the WAL section is discarded once the memtable is flushed to an SSTable.

question: Why are WALs fast?

choices.1: They skip writing to disk and only use memory
choices.2: Appending beats scattered updates
choices.3: They compress every write to a single bit
choices.4: They keep the entire database in CPU cache

explanation: Appending to the end of a file is much cheaper than modifying many scattered disk locations.

question: How does the WAL differ from the main data files?

choices.1: Both are append-only and identical in purpose
choices.2: Append-only log versus query-shaped files
choices.3: The WAL is the permanent storage and the data files are temporary
choices.4: The WAL is read-optimized while the data files are write-optimized

explanation: The WAL is write-optimized, temporary/recyclable, sequential; the main data files are structured for querying, read/storage-optimized, and long-lived.

question: What is a trade-off introduced by using a WAL?

choices.1: It makes writes slower than random in-place updates
choices.2: It removes the need for any data files
choices.3: It eliminates all disk I/O
choices.4: Double writes and fsync cost

explanation: Data is written to the WAL and again to data files, recovery takes longer for large WALs, disk is used until truncation, and strong durability pays fsync.

question: Why do databases use B-Trees for indexes rather than binary trees?

choices.1: B-Trees avoid the need for any disk pages
choices.2: Binary trees cannot store more than 256 keys
choices.3: B-Trees store data in random order for faster inserts
choices.4: A B-Tree node holds many keys and child pointers

explanation: Important because reading a disk/SSD page is far more expensive than comparing in-memory values. High branching factor. So a lookup touches only a few pages. Keeping the tree shallow.

question: Which is a core property of a B-Tree?

choices.1: Each node has at most two children
choices.2: Keys are stored unsorted within each node
choices.3: Sorted keys, wide fanout, uniform leaf depth
choices.4: Leaves sit at varying depths in order to save space

explanation: Nodes stay at least partially full, and inserts/deletes split/merge to rebalance.

question: Why are B-Trees good for range queries like WHERE order_date BETWEEN ... AND ...?

choices.1: Because B-Trees hash each key for O(1) range lookup
choices.2: Because B-Trees store ranges as a single key
choices.3: Because range queries scan the whole table regardless of the index
choices.4: Seek once, walk the leaves

explanation: Sorted keys let the database find the first match and scan forward through linked leaf entries — also helping >, ORDER BY, MIN, and MAX.

question: How does a B+ Tree (what most DB indexes really are) differ from a classic B-Tree?

choices.1: A B+ Tree has no leaf nodes at all
choices.2: Data only in linked leaves
choices.3: A B+ Tree is unbalanced to speed up inserts
choices.4: A B+ Tree stores all data in internal nodes and none in leaves

explanation: Internal nodes are pure navigation; the leaves hold the data/row-pointers and are linked left-to-right for sequential range scans.

question: What is the difference between a clustered and a non-clustered (secondary) index?

choices.1: A clustered index determines the physical/logical order of the table rows
choices.2: A clustered index is always smaller than a secondary index
choices.3: They are identical; the names depend on the vendor
choices.4: A secondary index stores the full row; a clustered index stores only pointers

explanation: A secondary index stores the indexed column plus a reference to the row, often requiring a second lookup to fetch it. Its leaves contain the rows.

question: What is a covering index?

choices.1: An index that automatically rebuilds itself on every write
choices.2: An index that covers multiple tables at once
choices.3: An index that covers every column in the table
choices.4: An index that contains all the columns a query needs

explanation: So the database can answer the query directly from the index without reading the table rows.

question: What happens during a B-Tree insertion when the target leaf is full?

choices.1: The key is appended to the end of the file outside the tree
choices.2: The whole tree is rebuilt from scratch
choices.3: Split the leaf, push up a separator
choices.4: The new key is dropped to preserve balance

explanation: The leaf becomes two, and the parent gains a separator key routing future lookups.

question: What is a composite index?

choices.1: An index that covers multiple tables
choices.2: A hash index over a concatenated string of all columns
choices.3: Two separate single-column indexes merged at query time
choices.4: A B-Tree over a column tuple

explanation: E.g. (country, city, age), sorted lexicographically like a dictionary.

question: Under the leftmost-prefix rule, which query can an index on (country, city, age) NOT serve efficiently?

choices.1: WHERE city = 'London' alone —
choices.2: WHERE country = 'UK' AND city = 'London' AND age = 35
choices.3: WHERE country = 'UK' AND city = 'London'
choices.4: WHERE country = 'UK'

explanation: Because the B-Tree is sorted by country first, so London rows are scattered across different country groups.

question: For the query pattern WHERE country = ? AND city = ? AND age > ?, why is (country, city, age) better than (country, age, city)?

choices.1: Equality columns should come before the range column
choices.2: Because range columns must always be first in the index
choices.3: Because city values are larger than age values
choices.4: Because (country, age, city) is not a valid index

explanation: After a range condition like age > ?, the index can no longer use a later column (city) to narrow the search, since within the range city is not a single contiguous block.

question: Why is a composite index on (country, city, age) not equivalent to three separate single-column indexes?

choices.1: The composite index stores one combined sorted key
choices.2: Separate indexes are always faster than a composite index
choices.3: A composite index can only be used for the first column
choices.4: They are exactly equivalent in all cases

explanation: So it can directly locate rows matching a combination (e.g. country='UK' AND city='London'); with separate indexes the database must find matching row IDs from each and combine them, which is less efficient.

question: How can a composite index help with sorting?

choices.1: It removes the need for ORDER BY entirely
choices.2: It sorts the table physically on every query
choices.3: Within a prefix, the index is already sorted
choices.4: Composite indexes cannot help with sorting

explanation: With country='UK' fixed, the index is ordered by the remaining columns (city, age), so a query filtering on country with ORDER BY city, age may avoid a separate sort.

question: What is the recommended column-ordering strategy for a composite index?

choices.1: Always alphabetical order of column names
choices.2: Equality, then range, then sort
choices.3: Lowest-cardinality columns first regardless of the query
choices.4: Range columns first, then equality columns

explanation: Equality columns first, range columns next, sorting/grouping last.

question: What is the core idea of MVCC (Multiversion Concurrency Control)?

choices.1: Only one version of each row ever exists, protected by a mutex
choices.2: Every transaction acquires an exclusive lock on the whole table
choices.3: Readers and writers are serialized into a single queue
choices.4: The database keeps multiple versions of a row

explanation: So a reader sees the version valid when its transaction began, while a writer creates a newer version without immediately overwriting the old one.

question: What is MVCC's main concurrency benefit?

choices.1: It guarantees full serializable isolation with no extra mechanisms
choices.2: Non-blocking reads plus consistent snapshots for each transaction
choices.3: It makes all writes instantaneous
choices.4: It eliminates the need for any storage of old data

explanation: Readers usually don't block writers and writers don't block readers.

question: How does an UPDATE behave under MVCC?

choices.1: It usually does not overwrite the old row in place
choices.2: It locks the entire table until all readers finish
choices.3: It appends the change to the WAL but never to the row
choices.4: It overwrites the row in place and discards the old value immediately

explanation: It creates a new version (with metadata like created_by / deleted_by transaction), and the old version remains for transactions that still need it.

question: How does MVCC decide which row version a transaction reads?

choices.1: It returns the oldest version on disk
choices.2: It always returns the most recently written version regardless of snapshot
choices.3: It returns a random version for load balancing
choices.4: When the transaction starts it gets a snapshot

explanation: A read returns the newest version visible to that snapshot (created by a transaction committed before the snapshot and not deleted before it).

question: Why must old row versions eventually be cleaned up, and what is this called?

choices.1: They are never cleaned up; storage grows forever by design
choices.2: They are cleaned up immediately on every commit, before the next read
choices.3: Cleanup is done by the client application, not the database
choices.4: Old versions can't be kept forever

explanation: Once no active transaction needs them they are removed — e.g. PostgreSQL vacuuming dead tuples, InnoDB purging old undo versions.

question: Does MVCC eliminate all locking, and what is a common MVCC problem?

choices.1: No — concurrent writes to the same row still conflict
choices.2: MVCC blocks all of the readers whenever a writer is active
choices.3: Yes, MVCC removes all locks and conflicts entirely
choices.4: MVCC requires every transaction to be serializable

explanation: A common problem is a long-running transaction that keeps an old snapshot open, preventing cleanup of old versions. One waits or aborts/retries.

question: Which anomalies does each standard isolation level allow?

choices.1: Read Uncommitted allows dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads
choices.2: All four levels allow dirty reads
choices.3: Read Committed prevents all anomalies; Serializable allows phantoms
choices.4: Serializable allows non-repeatable reads but prevents dirty reads

explanation: Read Committed prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable and phantom; Repeatable Read prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads; Serializable prevents all.

question: What is a dirty read?

choices.1: Reading the same row twice and getting different values
choices.2: Reading data written by another transaction that later rolls back
choices.3: A query returning brand-new rows that appeared between two executions
choices.4: Two transactions overwriting each other's updates

explanation: So you saw data that never truly existed.

question: What is a non-repeatable read?

choices.1: Reading uncommitted data that is later rolled back
choices.2: A query returning extra rows on a second execution
choices.3: Losing an update because two writers raced
choices.4: The same row reads differently twice

explanation: Another transaction committed an update between the two reads within your transaction.

question: What is a phantom read?

choices.1: A deadlock between two transactions
choices.2: Repeating the same query and seeing new rows appear
choices.3: Reading a value that was never committed
choices.4: Reading the same exact row twice with different values

explanation: Or disappear. Because another transaction inserted/deleted matching rows and committed.

question: What is write skew, and which isolation level fails to prevent it?

choices.1: Disjoint writes breaking a shared constraint; Snapshot Isolation
choices.2: A reader blocking a writer; Read Committed allows it
choices.3: Write skew is identical to a dirty read
choices.4: Two transactions both writing to the same row; Serializable allows it

explanation: Two transactions read overlapping data, decide based on what they saw, then update different rows in a way that together violates a constraint (e.g. both doctors going off-call); SI prevents many anomalies but still allows write skew.

question: How does MVCC implement Read Committed versus Repeatable Read / Snapshot Isolation?

choices.1: Both give one snapshot per transaction
choices.2: Read Committed locks rows until commit; Repeatable Read uses no snapshot
choices.3: Read Committed gives each statement a fresh committed snapshot
choices.4: Both give a fresh snapshot per statement

explanation: Repeatable Read / Snapshot Isolation gives one stable snapshot for the whole transaction. So two statements in one transaction may see different states.

question: Why does true Serializable isolation need more than MVCC snapshots?

choices.1: MVCC snapshots are automatically serializable with no extra work
choices.2: Serializable only requires preventing dirty reads
choices.3: MVCC alone gives snapshots, not serializability
choices.4: Serializable just disables MVCC entirely

explanation: Serializable needs extra machinery such as Serializable Snapshot Isolation conflict detection, predicate/range locks, or commit-time validation to rule out anomalies like write skew.

question: What does a document-based database store?

choices.1: Only key-value pairs with opaque values
choices.2: Rows split across many normalized tables joined by foreign keys
choices.3: Self-contained JSON-like documents
choices.4: Fixed-width binary records with a rigid schema

explanation: E.g. BSON in MongoDB — fields hold scalars, nested objects, and arrays, grouped into collections.

question: What core problem do document databases address?

choices.1: Running large analytical scans over many columns
choices.2: Guaranteeing global serializable transactions across all documents
choices.3: Enforcing strict foreign-key constraints across tables
choices.4: Storing and querying semi-structured

explanation: Easing object-relational mismatch, schema evolution, and data locality. Evolving, application-shaped data without forcing every entity into many normalized tables.

question: What is the central design principle of a document database?

choices.1: Index every field by default to allow any query
choices.2: Store what's read together, together
choices.3: Normalize all data into the smallest possible tables
choices.4: Always reference other entities by ID and never embed

explanation: An aggregate is one unit when the app usually reads and writes it as one — design around access patterns.

question: What is the common rule for embedding versus referencing data?

choices.1: Always embed everything to avoid joins
choices.2: Embed owned, reference shared
choices.3: Embed shared data and reference owned data
choices.4: Always reference everything to avoid duplication

explanation: Embed when data is owned by the parent and read with it; reference when shared, large, or independently updated.

question: Which data shape is usually NOT ideal for a document database?

choices.1: User profiles with optional, evolving fields
choices.2: Many-to-many heavy, normalized data
choices.3: Orders read together with their line items and address
choices.4: Product catalogues with category-specific attributes

explanation: Also complex transactional constraints, ad-hoc analytics across many fields, or rapidly growing embedded arrays.

question: What does 'flexible schema does not mean schema-free' mean for document databases?

choices.1: Document databases always enforce a strictly rigid schema like relational tables
choices.2: Even without a database-enforced schema, the application still has a schema
choices.3: There is genuinely no schema anywhere, so any shape is fine
choices.4: The schema must be defined in SQL DDL before inserting

explanation: Letting documents drift into inconsistent shapes makes queries and indexes painful. In code, API contracts, validation, or types.

question: What does a graph database store?

choices.1: JSON documents grouped together into collections
choices.2: Sorted key-value pairs in SSTables
choices.3: Rows in tables joined by foreign keys
choices.4: Nodes and edges with properties and labels

explanation: Data as a network of entities connected by relationships, with key-value properties and labels/types — useful when relationships matter as much as the entities.

question: What is the core performance idea distinguishing graph from relational databases?

choices.1: Relationships are first-class stored objects (stored edges)
choices.2: Relationships are computed lazily only at query time in both
choices.3: Graph databases avoid indexes entirely
choices.4: Graph databases keep all data in memory while relational databases use disk

explanation: So the database follows relationship pointers directly instead of reconstructing connections through foreign-key joins.

question: What is index-free adjacency?

choices.1: Every hop requires a fresh global index lookup
choices.2: Adjacency is recomputed from foreign keys on each query
choices.3: The database has no indexes at all, anywhere
choices.4: Hops follow stored pointers, not an index

explanation: Once the database finds a starting node, traversal follows edge pointers directly — cost is ~O(visited nodes + visited edges) with no per-hop index lookups.

question: What are the two major graph data models?

choices.1: The relational model and the columnar model
choices.2: The document model and the key-value model
choices.3: The B-tree model and the LSM model
choices.4: Property graphs and RDF triples

explanation: Property graph (Neo4j, queried with Cypher/Gremlin) versus subject-predicate-object triple stores (queried with SPARQL).

question: What does the rule 'indexes get you into the graph; adjacency gets you through the graph' mean?

choices.1: Index in, pointers through
choices.2: Neither indexes nor adjacency are used; everything is a full scan
choices.3: Indexes are used for every hop and adjacency is never used
choices.4: Adjacency finds the start and indexes do the traversal

explanation: An index (e.g. Person.email) finds starting nodes; adjacency pointers traverse relationships from there.

question: Why can graph traversal still suffer combinatorial explosion despite avoiding global joins?

choices.1: Branching compounds per hop
choices.2: Because edges are stored without types, forcing full scans
choices.3: Because graph databases must scan every node for each hop
choices.4: They cannot — graph traversal is always linear in the graph size

explanation: Depth k with branching factor d can expand to ~d^k paths, so broad multi-hop queries can blow up.

question: When is a graph database usually NOT the best choice?

choices.1: Tabular reports and whole-table scans
choices.2: For multi-hop relationship queries like friends-of-friends
choices.3: For recommendation queries exploring a local neighborhood
choices.4: For fraud detection over shared devices and cards

explanation: Heavy aggregations over all records or large scans with little relationship logic suit relational or columnar analytical databases better.

question: What is a key-value store, conceptually?

choices.1: A table of rows linked by foreign keys
choices.2: A sorted, immutable on-disk file of column-family data
choices.3: A graph of nodes connected by edges
choices.4: A database that maps a unique key directly to a value

explanation: Essentially a dictionary/hash map exposed as a database, where a pure store treats the value as an opaque blob of bytes.

question: What is the typical key-value store API?

choices.1: MATCH, TRAVERSE, and EXPAND
choices.2: A tiny set of operations
choices.3: MAP, FILTER, and REDUCE
choices.4: SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY, and ORDER BY

explanation: Get(key), put(key, value), and delete(key).

question: What is the defining access trait of a key-value store?

choices.1: You traverse relationships between values
choices.2: You query data by arbitrary field conditions like a relational database
choices.3: You fetch a value by its key, not by querying the contents of values
choices.4: You scan all values and filter server-side

explanation: So 'all users where age > 30' or sorting by value is not directly possible. An ~O(1) hash-based lookup.

question: What data is a key-value store best for?

choices.1: Complex multi-record ACID transactions
choices.2: Data accessed by a single known key and read/written as a whole unit
choices.3: Ad-hoc analytical aggregations over many fields
choices.4: Highly relational data that needs joins across many different entities

explanation: Sessions, caching, config/feature flags, shopping carts, rate-limit counters — often with TTLs for ephemeral data.

question: Which is a drawback of key-value stores?

choices.1: They require a fixed schema migrated on every change
choices.2: They cannot store strings, only numbers
choices.3: Key-only access, no querying by value
choices.4: They are always slower than relational databases

explanation: No joins/relationships, limited indexing, no server-side filtering/aggregation, and often weaker consistency.

question: How do 'data-structure stores' like Redis extend the pure key-value model?

choices.1: They drop keys entirely and query by content
choices.2: Structured values behind the key
choices.3: They enforce relational foreign keys between values
choices.4: They add full SQL querying over values

explanation: Lists, hashes, sorted sets instead of opaque blobs — the key remains the primary access path.

question: What is the core idea of a relational database?

choices.1: It stores opaque values fetched only by key
choices.2: Tables connected by shared values
choices.3: It stores documents as JSON blobs in collections
choices.4: It stores data as nodes and edges

explanation: Foreign keys referencing primary keys — best for structured, related data.

question: What is the difference between a primary key and a foreign key?

choices.1: Both uniquely identify rows in the same table
choices.2: A foreign key is just an index on the primary key
choices.3: A primary key points to another table; a foreign key is unique within its table
choices.4: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in its table

explanation: A foreign key is a value that points to a row in another table, forming the relationship.

question: What is the role of the buffer pool / cache in a relational engine?

choices.1: It enforces foreign-key constraints
choices.2: It logs every change before applying it
choices.3: It keeps frequently used data pages in RAM
choices.4: It stores the query results permanently on disk

explanation: So the engine reads from memory when a page is cached and only loads from disk on a miss — a major reason databases are fast.

question: What does the query optimizer do?

choices.1: It writes changes to the write-ahead log
choices.2: It physically reads pages from disk
choices.3: It picks the execution plan
choices.4: It parses SQL syntax and checks permissions

explanation: Which table or index to use first, which join algorithm, etc. — by estimating which plan will be fastest. The most efficient way to run the query.

question: What does ACID stand for in relational databases?

choices.1: Availability, Concurrency, Indexing, Durability
choices.2: Atomicity, Caching, Isolation, Distribution
choices.3: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
choices.4: Access, Constraints, Integrity, Durability

question: What is the purpose of a write-ahead log (WAL) in a relational database?

choices.1: Logging changes before applying them
choices.2: It chooses the query execution plan
choices.3: It caches frequently read pages in memory
choices.4: It stores the system catalog/metadata

explanation: Before changing data files, the database writes the change to a log so a crash can be recovered by replaying or undoing log entries — a backbone of durability.

question: How do relational databases let many users read and write safely at once?

choices.1: By disabling transactions under load
choices.2: Through concurrency control mechanisms
choices.3: By copying the whole database per user
choices.4: By allowing only one connection at a time

explanation: Locks, MVCC (multi-version concurrency control), and isolation levels that govern how much one transaction sees of another.

question: What is a wide-column database?

choices.1: An in-memory cache of opaque key-value blobs
choices.2: A graph of nodes connected by typed edges
choices.3: A columnar analytics warehouse for scanning and aggregating columns
choices.4: Sparse dynamic columns at cluster scale

explanation: Rows identified by keys hold many sparse, dynamic columns, distributed for huge write throughput, horizontal scale, and fast key/range lookups (Cassandra, HBase, Bigtable).

question: How does a wide-column database differ from a columnar analytics database like ClickHouse or BigQuery?

choices.1: A wide-column DB is single-node; a columnar DB is distributed
choices.2: Key-oriented rows versus column scans
choices.3: A wide-column DB scans columns; a columnar DB does point lookups
choices.4: They are the same thing with different names

explanation: A wide-column DB is closer to a distributed key-value store with structured rows, optimized for key access; a columnar analytics DB is optimized to scan and aggregate columns.

question: What is the typical primary-key shape in a Cassandra-style wide-column table?

choices.1: primary key = just a single auto-increment integer
choices.2: primary key = the timestamp alone
choices.3: primary key = a hash of every column
choices.4: primary key = partition key + clustering columns

explanation: The partition key decides which node stores the row, and clustering columns order rows within that partition.

question: What is the central schema-design principle for wide-column databases?

choices.1: Normalize fully and always rely on joins at query time
choices.2: Index every column so any query is fast
choices.3: Model around the queries you need to answer quickly
choices.4: Use one giant table for all entities

explanation: Not around abstract entities) — often creating one table per query pattern and deliberately duplicating data (denormalization.

question: What is the wide-column write path (Cassandra-style)?

choices.1: Update the target row in place on disk, then log it
choices.2: Buffer writes in the client until a batch threshold
choices.3: Write directly to all replicas synchronously before acknowledging
choices.4: Log, memtable, ack, flush to SSTable

explanation: Mostly sequential appends — the source of very high write throughput.

question: Why is partition-key choice critical, and how do you avoid hot partitions?

choices.1: Always use just a single global partition for consistency
choices.2: A poor key concentrates data and load on a few partitions
choices.3: Partition keys never affect performance
choices.4: Use the largest possible value as the partition key

explanation: Add entropy such as country+user_id or device+date, and use bucketing to avoid unbounded partitions. US enormous, IS tiny. E.g. country.

question: What does tunable consistency (ONE/QUORUM/ALL) with R + W > N achieve?

choices.1: Choosing how many replicas must acknowledge a read (R) and write (W)
choices.2: It means writes never need acknowledgement
choices.3: It disables replication to reduce latency
choices.4: It guarantees strong consistency completely regardless of the R, W, and N values

explanation: If R + W > N (replication factor), reads and writes overlap on at least one replica, improving the chance of reading the latest value.

question: What is offset-based pagination?

choices.1: It returns rows after a known position using a cursor
choices.2: Skip N rows, return the next batch
choices.3: It caches each page in memory keyed by page number
choices.4: It returns all rows and paginates client-side

explanation: E.g. LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40 — page navigation by page number.

question: What is cursor-based pagination?

choices.1: It loads the entire table and slices it
choices.2: It skips a number of rows by page number
choices.3: It requires no ordering at all
choices.4: Fetching rows after a known position

explanation: E.g. WHERE id > last_seen_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 20 — 'the next N rows after the last item I saw'.

question: Why does offset pagination perform poorly for large offsets?

choices.1: Offset pagination locks the whole table
choices.2: Offset queries cannot use any index ever
choices.3: Offset always returns the wrong rows past 10000
choices.4: The database still walks all skipped rows

explanation: OFFSET 100000 processes 100k rows even though few are returned; cursor pagination instead jumps directly into the index near the cursor value.

question: Why can offset pagination show duplicates or skip rows when data changes between requests?

choices.1: Because offset pagination caches stale pages forever
choices.2: Because offset pagination reverses the sort order each page
choices.3: Because offset pagination ignores ORDER BY
choices.4: The offset shifts under you

explanation: Cursor pagination ('rows after item J') is more stable. E.g. LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10. So the next page may repeat or miss items. Inserts or deletes between pages move the boundary, duplicating or skipping rows.

question: Why must cursor pagination use a stable and unique ordering?

choices.1: Ties cause missed or duplicated rows
choices.2: Because cursors require a hash index
choices.3: Because unique ordering makes the query slower but simpler
choices.4: Because cursors can only order by the primary key

explanation: Ordering by non-unique created_at alone breaks pagination at ties; use (created_at, id) and a composite cursor to make the order stable and unique.

question: When should you prefer offset versus cursor pagination?

choices.1: They are interchangeable with identical behaviour
choices.2: Always use offset; cursor pagination is deprecated
choices.3: Offset for pages, cursor for feeds
choices.4: Cursor for numbered page navigation; offset for infinite scroll

explanation: Cursor for infinite scroll, feeds, large changing datasets, and high performance. Numbered pages and small static datasets suit offset; infinite scroll and live data suit cursors.

question: What is database contention?

choices.1: When a single transaction runs too slowly
choices.2: When indexes become fragmented
choices.3: Concurrent access to the same hot data
choices.4: When the database runs out of disk space

explanation: Multiple transactions reading and writing the same rows at once — without coordination this causes anomalies like lost updates and write skew.

question: What is the core difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control?

choices.1: Optimistic assumes conflict is rare
choices.2: Optimistic is for reads; pessimistic is for writes only
choices.3: Optimistic locks first; pessimistic checks at commit
choices.4: They are identical except optimistic is faster always

explanation: Pessimistic assumes conflict is likely (lock first, then work) — pick based on how often you actually expect a conflict. Do the work, then check for conflicts at commit and retry if needed.

question: How does a conditional write (compare-and-set) prevent a lost update?

choices.1: You write only if the data still looks as expected, e.g.
choices.2: It blocks all other writers until you commit
choices.3: It locks the row for the entire transaction
choices.4: It silently overwrites whatever value happens to be present

explanation: UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE id = 1 AND version = 7; if someone changed it first, zero rows are updated and you know you lost the race and must retry — the check and write are one atomic operation.

question: What does SELECT ... FOR UPDATE do, and what is its main danger?

choices.1: It reads a consistent snapshot without any locking
choices.2: It guarantees no other transaction can ever read the row
choices.3: It creates a new version of the row like MVCC
choices.4: It pessimistically locks the selected row

explanation: So other FOR UPDATE readers block until you commit, preventing lost updates; the main danger is deadlocks (mitigated by acquiring locks in a consistent order and keeping transactions short).

question: Why do distributed locks need TTLs and fencing tokens?

choices.1: They are only needed for single-node Redis locks
choices.2: TTLs free crashed holders
choices.3: TTLs make the lock faster; fencing tokens compress it
choices.4: They eliminate the need for the lock entirely

explanation: A monotonically increasing fencing token (which the protected resource checks, rejecting stale ones) prevents a paused/delayed holder from acting after its lock expired and was re-acquired. And fencing tokens stop a stale holder from acting after expiry.

question: What is the guiding principle for choosing a contention-control tool?

choices.1: Always use the heaviest tool (distributed locks) for safety
choices.2: Always use optimistic concurrency regardless of conflict rate
choices.3: Never use locks; only conditional writes are correct
choices.4: The cheapest correct tool wins

explanation: Conditional write, then optimistic concurrency, then isolation levels / pessimistic locks, with distributed locks as a last resort — escalate only as the data spans more rows, tables, or machines.

question: What is the recommended ladder for scaling reads, in order?

choices.1: First add a cache, then optimise queries, then shard
choices.2: First buy bigger hardware, then rewrite in C++
choices.3: First shard the database, then add replicas, then index
choices.4: Optimise, replicate, then cache

explanation: First cheaper reads in the database, then read replicas, then an external caching layer.

question: Why is indexing described as the single biggest lever for read performance?

choices.1: Indexes guarantee strong consistency across replicas
choices.2: Indexes make writes faster
choices.3: Indexes remove the need for a buffer pool
choices.4: An index turns a full table scan

explanation: O(N)) into a B-tree seek (O(log N)); a covering index that contains every column the query needs lets the database answer from the index alone (an index-only scan.

question: What do materialized views / precomputation achieve for read scaling?

choices.1: Compute once, serve many reads
choices.2: They eliminate the need for any indexes
choices.3: They make every write recompute all aggregates
choices.4: They replicate data to other machines

explanation: An expensive aggregate is stored, shifting work from read time (every query) to write/refresh time (occasionally) — great when reads greatly outnumber writes.

question: How do read replicas scale reads?

choices.1: A primary streams changes to replicas
choices.2: Replicas shard the data so each holds a slice
choices.3: Replicas cache query results in memory only
choices.4: Every replica accepts writes independently

explanation: SELECTs are load-balanced across the replicas, so read throughput scales with the replica count.

question: What problem does replication lag cause, and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: It only affects writes, never reads
choices.2: Replication lag corrupts the primary's data
choices.3: Stale reads from lagging replicas
choices.4: It is fixed by adding more primaries

explanation: Mitigate by routing a user's reads to the primary for a few seconds after they write, or tolerating staleness. A user reloads and a lagging replica shows old data. Breaking read-your-own-writes. Mitigate with read-your-writes routing, sticky primaries, or bounded staleness.

question: What is the difference between replication and sharding?

choices.1: Replication scales writes; sharding scales reads
choices.2: Both keep a full copy of the data on every node
choices.3: Replication copies all data; sharding slices it
choices.4: Sharding is just replication with more copies

explanation: Replication gives every node all the data and scales reads; sharding gives each node some of the data and scales writes plus storage. Replication doesn't help write throughput since every write still goes through one primary.

question: What is the 'thundering herd' problem with an external cache?

choices.1: When replication lag makes cached data stale
choices.2: When the cache node runs out of memory and evicts everything
choices.3: Expiry stampedes on a hot key
choices.4: When too many keys are written at once

explanation: Thousands of simultaneous misses hit the database at once; mitigate with locks/single-flight or stale-while-revalidate so only one request refills.

question: Why are writes harder to scale than reads?

choices.1: Reads require locks but writes do not
choices.2: Writes are larger in bytes than reads
choices.3: Writes funnel through one ordered primary
choices.4: Writes cannot be cached but reads always can

explanation: Every write must be durable, ordered, and agreed upon — unlike reads, which scale via cheap stale-tolerant copies.

question: Why do indexes tax write throughput?

choices.1: Indexes are rebuilt from scratch on every insert
choices.2: Every index must be updated on each write
choices.3: Indexes lock the whole table on every write
choices.4: Indexes force writes to go to disk synchronously

explanation: E.g. an INSERT into a table with 6 indexes is 1 heap write plus 6 index writes. So dropping unneeded indexes speeds up write-heavy tables.

question: How does an LSM-tree storage engine help write-heavy workloads versus a B-tree?

choices.1: An LSM-tree appends to an in-memory memtable and flushes sequentially
choices.2: An LSM-tree updates rows in place faster than a B-tree
choices.3: A B-tree always uses sequential writes, whereas an LSM uses random writes
choices.4: They have identical write performance

explanation: Sequential I/O optimized for writes; a B-tree updates pages in place, causing read-optimized random I/O. Compacting later.

question: What is the real horizontal fix for write throughput, and how does it work?

choices.1: Vertical scaling to the biggest possible machine
choices.2: Adding read replicas, since every replica can also accept writes
choices.3: Caching writes in Redis before the database
choices.4: Sharding across many primaries

explanation: Each shard owns a slice and handles its own writes, so throughput scales with shard count.

question: Why is a sequential shard key (timestamp or auto-increment id) a poor choice?

choices.1: Sequential keys violate ACID guarantees
choices.2: All new writes land on the last shard
choices.3: Sequential keys make reads slower but writes faster
choices.4: Sequential keys cannot be indexed

explanation: A good key has high cardinality and even write distribution (fix by hashing or adding a random component). Creating a hotspot and gaining no scaling.

question: What is the key limitation of using a queue as a write buffer?

choices.1: A queue absorbs temporary spikes only
choices.2: Queues guarantee exactly-once delivery automatically
choices.3: Queues make writes synchronous and slower
choices.4: Queues cannot retry failed writes

explanation: If the sustained arrival rate exceeds what consumers can drain, the backlog grows without bound — you still need enough write capacity for the average load.

question: How does hierarchical aggregation avoid a single hot row for massive write fan-in (e.g. likes on a viral post)?

choices.1: It stores each like in a separate table forever
choices.2: It funnels every increment to one row protected by a lock
choices.3: It disables the counter under high load
choices.4: Sharded counters rolling up periodically

explanation: Many shard-local counters absorb slices of the writes, roll up into regional counters, then one global total computed periodically — no single-row hotspot, at the cost of a slightly stale total.

question: What shape of data makes time-series databases special?

choices.1: Data dominated by in-place updates to existing rows
choices.2: Highly relational data with many foreign keys
choices.3: Small datasets accessed by random key
choices.4: Timestamped, append-mostly, range-queried

explanation: Writes arrive at 'now', rarely updated or deleted, huge in volume, ordered by time, and queried by time range — with old data losing granularity value.

question: Why does a TSDB store points by appending to per-series logs?

choices.1: New data arrives at now; old is immutable
choices.2: Because it avoids needing any compression
choices.3: Because random writes are faster than sequential ones
choices.4: Because appending lets it update old points in place

explanation: Appends give fast sequential I/O, free time-ordering, and immutability (no locking, safe concurrent reads).

question: How do delta-of-delta and XOR encoding compress time-series data so well (~1-2 bytes/point)?

choices.1: Timestamps are near-evenly spaced, so delta-of-delta is mostly zero
choices.2: They use a global dictionary of every possible value
choices.3: They store the full 16-byte values but simply in a faster file format
choices.4: They drop most data points and interpolate later

explanation: Values change slowly, so XOR-ing each with the previous yields mostly zero bits — both store only the few differing bits.

question: What does time-based partitioning (blocks by time window) enable?

choices.1: Storing all series in a single giant file
choices.2: Avoiding the need for any index
choices.3: Query pruning and cheap retention
choices.4: Updating historical points in place efficiently

explanation: Deleting old data is dropping a whole block/file, with no row-by-row deletes or tombstones. A 'last 1h' query touches only the most recent block(s), skipping older ones.

question: What do downsampling / rollups achieve in a TSDB?

choices.1: They increase the resolution of old data
choices.2: They replicate data to other nodes
choices.3: They compress the entire WAL into a single record
choices.4: Pre-aggregated coarse buckets, expiring raw data

explanation: They store min/max/sum/count per bucket and expire fine-grained originals over time — cheap long-term history and fast wide-range queries.

question: How do block-level metadata and Bloom filters speed up reads?

choices.1: They replace the need for a WAL
choices.2: They guarantee every block is read exactly once
choices.3: They store the full decompressed data in memory for every query
choices.4: They let queries skip irrelevant blocks

explanation: Each block's min/max timestamp lets the engine time-prune blocks outside the range; a per-block Bloom filter skips blocks without the queried series; min/max stats can answer some queries from metadata alone.

question: What is a vector database specialized for?

choices.1: Full-text keyword search with BM25 only
choices.2: Exact key/range lookups and joins like a relational database
choices.3: Nearest-neighbor search over embeddings
choices.4: Storing timestamped metrics queried by time range

explanation: Storing high-dimensional vectors and finding those most similar to a query — 'closest in meaning', not exact matches.

question: What is an embedding, and what is the division of labour with the database?

choices.1: A hash of the item used as a primary key
choices.2: A vector (often 384-1536+ dims) from an ML model that maps items
choices.3: A compressed copy of the original source document stored as bytes
choices.4: A relational row with one column per feature

explanation: So semantic similarity equals geometric closeness; the model does the 'understanding' and the database does the geometry (nearest-neighbor search).

question: Which distance/similarity metric is the usual default for text embeddings, and why?

choices.1: Edit distance, because text is string-based
choices.2: Cosine similarity — direction carries meaning
choices.3: Hamming distance, because embeddings are binary
choices.4: Euclidean (L2) distance, because text is spatial

explanation: It measures the angle between vectors (ignoring magnitude), and for most text models meaning corresponds to direction; the metric must match how the model was trained.

question: Why can't exact kNN scale, and why can't you just use a B-tree?

choices.1: Exact kNN requires a GPU that most servers lack
choices.2: Brute-force kNN is O(N·D) per query
choices.3: Exact kNN is fast but uses too much disk
choices.4: B-trees work fine; vector DBs use them under the hood

explanation: E.g. 100M × 768 dims ≈ 77 billion ops); and there's no total ordering in high-dimensional space, so B-trees don't apply and traditional spatial indexes collapse (curse of dimensionality.

question: What is the central bargain of Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search?

choices.1: It returns ~95-99% of the true neighbors in sub-linear time
choices.2: It returns random vectors to save computation
choices.3: It always returns all true neighbors faster than brute force
choices.4: It guarantees exact results but uses less memory

explanation: Trading a little accuracy for orders-of-magnitude speed, tunable on a recall ↔ speed ↔ memory knob. Measured as recall = true neighbors returned / k.

question: What does a vector DB typically store per item, and what does the metadata enable?

choices.1: A B-tree index over every dimension
choices.2: Id, vector, metadata, optional payload
choices.3: The full neural network weights for each item
choices.4: Only the raw vector with no other fields

explanation: The metadata (scalar fields/tags) enables filtered/hybrid queries like 'similar to X but only tagged team=eng from the last 30 days'.

question: What is the universal trade-off ANN indexes balance?

choices.1: Recall (accuracy) vs speed vs memory
choices.2: Latency vs throughput only
choices.3: Consistency vs availability vs partition tolerance
choices.4: Write amplification vs read amplification only

explanation: You can't maximize all three; each index and its parameters picks a point in that triangle.

question: How does HNSW (the most popular ANN index) search for nearest neighbors?

choices.1: It compresses vectors into 1-byte codes and compares codebooks
choices.2: It clusters vectors with k-means and probes the nearest clusters
choices.3: Greedy descent through a layered graph
choices.4: It hashes vectors so similar ones collide in buckets

explanation: Sparse long-range top layers and a dense bottom layer form a navigable small-world graph; the search walks greedily toward the query, descending layer by layer in ~O(log N) hops.

question: How does an IVF (Inverted File) index find neighbors?

choices.1: It compares the query to every vector exactly
choices.2: It hashes the query into a single bucket
choices.3: It builds a navigable multi-layer graph and walks it greedily
choices.4: Search only the nearest clusters

explanation: k-means partitions vectors around centroids; a query compares to centroids and searches just the nprobe nearest clusters.

question: How does Product Quantization (PQ) reduce memory, and what is the cost?

choices.1: It removes dimensions at random with no accuracy cost
choices.2: It increases recall while raising memory use
choices.3: It stores full floats but in a faster layout, lossless
choices.4: Sub-vector codebooks trading recall for memory

explanation: Each vector is split into sub-vectors, a small codebook is learned per sub-space, and each sub-vector becomes its nearest centroid's 1-byte ID (e.g. 3 KB to ~96 bytes, ~30x smaller); distances become lossy and recall drops (often combined as IVF-PQ).

question: What do the HNSW parameters M, efConstruction, and efSearch control?

choices.1: Edges, build width, query width
choices.2: They are all synonyms for the number of clusters
choices.3: They control compression ratio, not recall
choices.4: M is the metric, efConstruction the dimension, efSearch the batch size

explanation: M is edges per node (recall vs memory), efConstruction the search width while building (better graph, slower build), efSearch the query-time width (recall vs speed).

question: What is re-ranking in ANN search?

choices.1: Rebuilding the index after each query
choices.2: Exact re-scoring of a bigger candidate set
choices.3: Re-sorting the entire dataset by distance on every query
choices.4: Replacing the index with brute force permanently

explanation: Retrieve more approximate candidates with the ANN index, then re-score them with exact distances on the full vectors — recovering recall cheaply, common when vectors were compressed with PQ.

question: How does a vector DB typically lay out its storage?

choices.1: Index in RAM, vectors split, metadata separate
choices.2: The index is on disk and vectors are never stored
choices.3: Everything (index, vectors, metadata) is stored in one row-oriented table
choices.4: Only the metadata is persisted; vectors are recomputed each query

explanation: The ANN index (especially HNSW) is held in memory, raw vectors live in memory and/or on disk (for exact re-ranking and rebuilds), and metadata/payload sits in a separate KV/columnar store with its own scalar indexes — plus a WAL/segments for durability.

question: How are updates and deletes handled in a vector DB?

choices.1: Updates are forbidden; you must rebuild the whole index
choices.2: Deletes physically remove the node immediately from every single segment
choices.3: Append-mostly: an update is typically delete (tombstone) + re-insert
choices.4: Updates edit the HNSW graph node in place instantly

explanation: Because editing an HNSW node's position in place is expensive, and deletes use a tombstone/soft-delete reclaimed during compaction.

question: What is the difference between pre-filtering, post-filtering, and single-stage filter-aware traversal for hybrid queries?

choices.1: Pre-filter searches first; post-filter filters first
choices.2: Only post-filtering truly preserves recall for selective filters
choices.3: Pre-filter finds the matching subset first then searches it
choices.4: They are three names for the same approach

explanation: Exact, but can break graph connectivity when selective); post-filter searches then drops non-matches (may return fewer than k for rare filters); single-stage filter-aware traversal evaluates the filter during graph traversal (best of both.

question: What is hybrid search (in the information-retrieval sense)?

choices.1: Fusing vector and keyword rankings
choices.2: Running the same query on two replicas and merging
choices.3: Mixing two embedding models for one query
choices.4: Searching two vector indexes and averaging the vectors

explanation: Dense vector similarity and sparse keyword search (BM25) each produce a ranked list, fused (e.g. Reciprocal Rank Fusion) to capture both semantic meaning and exact term matches.

question: Why is distributed (sharded) vector search a scatter-gather that can't prune shards?

choices.1: Because only one shard ever contains relevant vectors
choices.2: Because each shard holds the same full copy of the data
choices.3: A near neighbor could be on any shard
choices.4: Because shards are pruned by hashing the query vector

explanation: So unlike key-based sharding the query must fan out to every shard; each returns its local top-k and a coordinator merges them into the global top-k, bound by the slowest shard.

question: What is the sweet spot for a vector database?

choices.1: Storing timestamped metrics for range queries
choices.2: Exact key/range queries with joins and transactions
choices.3: Aggregations and analytics over large tabular datasets
choices.4: Semantic similarity at scale

explanation: Approximate high-recall search over embeddings plus metadata filtering — 'find things like this'.

question: Why did vector databases explode in popularity, and what is RAG?

choices.1: RAG means re-architecting graphs; vector DBs store the graph
choices.2: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
choices.3: They became the default cache for web pages
choices.4: They replaced relational databases for transactions

explanation: Embed a knowledge base, retrieve the most relevant chunks for a user's question, and feed them to an LLM as context — giving grounded, up-to-date answers without retraining; the vector DB is the retrieval/'memory' layer.

question: What primarily bottlenecks the quality of vector search results?

choices.1: The database's disk speed
choices.2: The choice of programming language
choices.3: The embedding model, not the database
choices.4: The number of CPU cores on the query node

explanation: The DB only does geometry, so garbage embeddings give garbage neighbors; changing models means re-embedding and re-indexing everything.

question: Which is a genuine limitation of vector databases?

choices.1: They guarantee exact nearest neighbors at all scales
choices.2: They cannot store metadata alongside vectors
choices.3: Approximate, hungry, and specialized
choices.4: They excel at ad-hoc range and aggregation queries

explanation: Approximate (not exact) results, memory-hungry, awkward updates/deletes, and not general databases (no joins, transactions, or analytics).

question: When might you NOT need a dedicated vector database?

choices.1: When a library or pgvector suffices
