explanation: So it speculatively executes instructions that should never run (e.g. a bounds-check bypass), loading secret-dependent data into the cache; a timing side channel (Flush+Reload) then recovers the secret, since microarchitectural state isn't rolled back.

question: Why does ASLR sometimes fail to stop ROP attacks?

choices.1: ASLR only randomizes the heap, never libraries
choices.2: ASLR randomizes base addresses
choices.3: ASLR cannot randomize shared libraries at all
choices.4: ASLR is disabled whenever NX is enabled

explanation: So gadgets are hard to locate, but if the attacker can leak a pointer (e.g. via a format-string bug or pointer in an error message), they recover the base and locate gadgets.

question: What is the buddy allocator?

choices.1: A garbage collector that compacts memory automatically
choices.2: A memory allocator that manages memory in power-of-two (2^k) sized blocks
choices.3: An allocator that gives every request the exact number of bytes asked for
choices.4: A linked-list allocator that never splits blocks

explanation: Fast allocation/free, low external fragmentation, and easy coalescing of free blocks.

question: How does the buddy allocator satisfy a request?

choices.1: It searches a sorted tree for the best-fit arbitrary size
choices.2: It always returns the largest available block
choices.3: It allocates the exact byte count from a contiguous arena
choices.4: It rounds the request up to the next power of two

explanation: If no block of that size is free, it splits a larger block into two equal buddies, returns one, and puts the other on the free list.

question: On freeing a block, how is its buddy found and merged?

choices.1: The buddy is the next block in address order, regardless of size
choices.2: address XOR block size
choices.3: The allocator scans the whole free list to find a matching block
choices.4: Buddies are never merged; freed blocks stay split

explanation: If the buddy is also free it is merged, and merging continues up to higher orders. Block address XOR block size. Buddies differ by exactly the block size, so one XOR finds the partner; merged pairs recurse upward.

question: How does Linux use the buddy allocator?

choices.1: As the filesystem's free-block tracker
choices.2: As the physical page-frame allocator
choices.3: Only for swapping pages to disk
choices.4: As the user-space malloc implementation directly

explanation: Kmalloc uses it underneath via the slab/slub allocators. Managing contiguous 4 KB pages in orders from 0 (1 page) up to 10/11 (1024 pages).

question: What is the main disadvantage of the buddy allocator?

choices.1: It cannot free memory once allocated
choices.2: External fragmentation that prevents any coalescing
choices.3: Power-of-two internal fragmentation
choices.4: Allocation is O(n) in the number of blocks

explanation: A 257-byte request gets a 512-byte block, wasting memory.

question: What is the key benefit of the buddy system's coalescing?

choices.1: It allows arbitrary multi-megabyte contiguous allocations easily
choices.2: Cheap merging into big chunks
choices.3: It guarantees zero internal fragmentation
choices.4: It eliminates the need for free lists

explanation: A trivial XOR finds the buddy; split and merge are O(log n).

question: What is the difference between external and internal fragmentation?

choices.1: External: free space is split into many small holes
choices.2: External occurs only in the kernel; internal only in user space
choices.3: They are the same thing measured differently
choices.4: External is wasted slack inside allocations; internal is scattered holes

explanation: So a large request can't be satisfied despite sufficient total free bytes; internal: you get more memory than requested (rounding to size classes/page alignment), so the slack is wasted.

question: Why does fragmentation hurt performance?

choices.1: Memory pressure, no huge pages, TLB churn
choices.2: It only affects disk I/O, never memory
choices.3: It has no real performance impact, only aesthetic
choices.4: It always speeds up allocation by spreading data out

explanation: It raises RSS (paging pressure), prevents allocating large blocks like 2 MiB/1 GiB huge pages, and multiplies TLB entries (many 4 KiB pages instead of few large ones).

question: Which is a common cause of memory fragmentation?

choices.1: Mixed lifetimes in one pool
choices.2: Using a single object size for everything
choices.3: Allocating all memory at program start and never freeing
choices.4: Running on a 64-bit instead of 32-bit system

explanation: Also many sizes with low reuse, per-thread caches holding pages, and frequent small mmap/munmap regions.

question: How does the Linux kernel proactively avoid fragmentation?

choices.1: It disables huge pages entirely
choices.2: Buddy merging plus migratetype lists
choices.3: It compacts memory on every single allocation
choices.4: It refuses any allocation larger than one page

explanation: The buddy allocator merges adjacent free blocks, and pages classified MOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE/UNMOVABLE live on separate free lists so unmovable pages don't pepper movable areas.

question: What is memory compaction, and how does it work?

choices.1: Deleting unused files to free disk space
choices.2: Compressing page contents to fit more in RAM
choices.3: Swapping all pages to disk and reloading them contiguously
choices.4: Moving movable pages to coalesce free holes

explanation: A compactor moves movable pages out of scattered areas into a target region, updates their PTEs, and performs a TLB shootdown so freed holes merge into larger blocks (kcompactd in the background, or direct compaction on the allocation path).

question: Why can compaction sometimes fail to defragment memory?

choices.1: Because movable pages cannot have their PTEs updated
choices.2: Because compaction only works on 32-bit systems
choices.3: Because the buddy allocator forbids any page movement
choices.4: Pinned pages are immovable rocks

explanation: RDMA pinning, get_user_pages, mlock, and device MMIO block coalescing, and aggressive compaction can cause latency stalls.

question: What core problem does free-space management solve across kernels, malloc, and filesystems?

choices.1: Compressing allocated data to save space
choices.2: Deciding the scheduling order of processes
choices.3: Tracking which regions are free
choices.4: Encrypting free memory so it can't be read

explanation: All efficiently; the underlying strategies are the same across domains. Finding a region that fits a request, splitting/coalescing regions, and avoiding fragmentation.

question: What are segregated free lists (bins), and why are they fast?

choices.1: A tree keyed by allocation timestamp
choices.2: Multiple free lists, one per size class
choices.3: A single sorted list searched linearly for each allocation
choices.4: A bitmap with one bit per byte of memory

explanation: Very fast O(1) allocation for common sizes and low fragmentation (used by ptmalloc/jemalloc/tcmalloc and slab allocators); the tradeoff is harder coalescing.

question: What is the slab allocator used for?

choices.1: Tracking free disk blocks in a filesystem
choices.2: Managing swap space on disk
choices.3: Allocating arbitrary variable-sized user-space buffers
choices.4: Fixed-size kernel object caches

explanation: Inodes, dentries, file descriptors — divided into slabs, giving no fragmentation for fixed-size objects, fast allocation, and object reuse with hot caches.

question: What is the Two-Level Segregated Fit (TLSF) allocator designed for?

choices.1: Real-time and embedded systems
choices.2: Maximizing throughput on batch workloads with no latency concern
choices.3: Allocating only powers of two like the buddy system
choices.4: Eliminating the need for any metadata

explanation: It provides guaranteed constant-time O(1) allocation, low fragmentation, and deterministic latency using two-level size indexes.

question: How do the first-fit and best-fit allocation strategies differ?

choices.1: First fit takes the largest block; best fit takes the first block
choices.2: First fit takes the first block large enough
choices.3: They are identical except in name
choices.4: Best fit is always faster than first fit

explanation: Fast, but fragments the start of memory); best fit takes the smallest block that fits (minimizes leftover fragments but is slow unless using a size-ordered tree.

question: Which is a key challenge in free-space management?

choices.1: Ensuring every allocation is exactly one page
choices.2: Preventing the CPU from caching free blocks
choices.3: Guaranteeing allocations never need to be freed
choices.4: Coalescing speed versus fragmentation

explanation: Balancing merge cost against fragmentation, plus handling external/internal fragmentation and multi-threaded contention (mitigated by per-thread arenas).

question: How is the 64-bit Linux virtual address space split?

choices.1: Kernel and user space share one single flat region with no protection at all
choices.2: The top half is user space and the bottom half is kernel space
choices.3: The top half is kernel space, the bottom half is per-process user space
choices.4: Each process gets a completely separate kernel mapping

explanation: Kernel pages are marked supervisor-only so user code can't access them. The same mapping shared by all processes.

question: What is the direct physical map (physmap) in the Linux kernel address space?

choices.1: A region where all physical RAM is mapped 1:1 into kernel space
choices.2: A per-process mapping unique to each task
choices.3: The region holding loadable kernel modules
choices.4: A mapping of non-contiguous physical memory into a contiguous virtual range

explanation: Used by the buddy allocator, slab allocator, and page cache. Virt = phys + PAGE_OFFSET. Giving fast physical-to-virtual translation without page-table walks.

question: What is the vmalloc region used for?

choices.1: Holding the kernel's executable code and rodata
choices.2: Contiguous virtual over scattered physical
choices.3: Mapping all physical RAM 1:1 for fast translation
choices.4: Per-process user heap allocations

explanation: Used for loadable kernel modules, large kernel buffers, and ioremap (MMIO); slower than the direct map.

question: What does the kernel heap (slab/slub) allocate?

choices.1: The page cache for file data
choices.2: Swap space on disk
choices.3: Small kernel objects
choices.4: User-space malloc allocations

explanation: task_struct, vm_area_struct, dentry, inode — backed by physical pages from the buddy allocator.

question: Why do all processes share the same kernel address-space layout?

choices.1: Because user space and kernel space use the same page table entries with the same permissions
choices.2: Switching kernel mappings on every context switch would be far too slow
choices.3: Because the kernel has no memory protection
choices.4: Because each process needs its own copy of the kernel code

explanation: Instead the kernel mapping is identical and stable, and access is blocked by supervisor-only page protection bits — enabling fast syscalls/interrupts and shared kernel text/data.

question: What is the fixmap region?

choices.1: A cache of recently freed kernel objects
choices.2: A region that maps all of physical RAM directly 1:1
choices.3: Fixed virtual addresses, changeable mappings
choices.4: The user-space stack region

explanation: Compile-time fixed VAs whose underlying mappings change at runtime — used for APIC/LAPIC, early-boot temporary mappings, IO devices, and CPU GDT/TSS structures.

question: Why does page replacement exist?

choices.1: To decide which process to schedule next
choices.2: To compress pages before writing them to disk
choices.3: A full RAM needs a victim on each fault
choices.4: To choose which file to cache in RAM

explanation: On a page fault with physical memory full, the OS must evict a page before bringing in the needed one; the policy that picks the victim is the replacement algorithm.

question: What is the Optimal (Bélády's OPT) algorithm and why isn't it used?

choices.1: It evicts the page that will be used farthest in the future
choices.2: It evicts the oldest page and is widely used for simplicity
choices.3: It evicts a random page and is optimal on average
choices.4: It evicts the most recently used page

explanation: The lowest possible faults; it's unimplementable because it needs perfect knowledge of the future, so it's only a benchmark.

question: What is Bélády's anomaly, and which algorithm exhibits it?

choices.1: More frames, more faults — under FIFO
choices.2: It is a hardware bug in the TLB
choices.3: It is when LRU performs worse than FIFO with more frames
choices.4: It is when more frames always reduce faults, as expected

explanation: Increasing frames can increase page faults in FIFO (a non-stack algorithm); it never happens in stack algorithms like LRU or OPT.

question: How does the Clock (second-chance) algorithm work?

choices.1: It evicts the page with the highest access count
choices.2: It maintains a perfectly ordered list of every memory access
choices.3: It evicts pages strictly in arrival order with no recency info
choices.4: It approximates LRU using a per-page reference bit

explanation: Evicting one with R=0, and clearing the R bit of pages with R=1 (giving them a second chance) — all at O(1) overhead. A clock hand sweeps the pages.

question: Why isn't true LRU used directly in real operating systems?

choices.1: Because LRU requires future knowledge
choices.2: Tracking every access is too expensive
choices.3: Because LRU performs worse than FIFO in practice
choices.4: Because LRU suffers from Bélády's anomaly

explanation: True LRU needs a timestamp per memory access or a fully ordered page list, which hardware can't maintain cheaply — so OSes approximate with NRU, aging, or clock.

question: How does Linux's real page-replacement approach work?

choices.1: It maintains two LRU lists
choices.2: It uses true per-access-timestamp LRU
choices.3: It uses pure FIFO across all pages
choices.4: It evicts random pages with no lists

explanation: An active list (hot pages) and an inactive list (cold pages); recently used pages stay/promote to active, untouched pages demote to inactive, and eviction comes from the inactive list, with the reference bit simulating LRU.

question: Why do page-replacement algorithms prefer evicting clean pages over dirty ones?

choices.1: Clean pages take longer to reload, so evicting them is cheaper
choices.2: There is no cost difference; the choice is arbitrary
choices.3: Dirty pages are always more recently used, so they're kept
choices.4: Dirty pages cost a writeback first

explanation: A clean page can just be discarded (its disk copy is identical); the enhanced clock uses the (R, D) bits, evicting R=0,D=0 pages first.

question: What is a page swap?

choices.1: Moving a page between RAM and swap
choices.2: Copying a page from one process to another
choices.3: Swapping the order of two pages in RAM
choices.4: Compressing a page to fit more in RAM

explanation: Page-out is RAM to swap, page-in is swap to RAM — part of demand paging.

question: Why does swapping exist?

choices.1: Virtual space is bigger than RAM
choices.2: To encrypt pages before storing them
choices.3: To make every page accessible to every process
choices.4: To eliminate the need for page tables

explanation: The OS keeps active pages in RAM, moves inactive ones to disk, and brings them back on demand.

question: What does the page table entry contain for a page that has been swapped out?

choices.1: A swap slot index instead of a physical frame number
choices.2: The disk's full file path for the page
choices.3: The physical frame number, since the page is still mapped
choices.4: Nothing — the PTE is deleted when a page is swapped

explanation: So a fault on that page triggers a swap-in.

question: What is thrashing?

choices.1: Working sets exceeding RAM cause fault storms
choices.2: When the TLB is flushed too often
choices.3: When pages are never evicted from RAM
choices.4: When the CPU runs far too many threads at once

explanation: Processes continuously page-fault and the CPU spends most of its time waiting for disk I/O, collapsing throughput.

question: What does the kswapd background reclaim daemon do?

choices.1: It only runs when the system is shutting down
choices.2: It compacts the page tables to save memory
choices.3: It synchronously evicts a page on every single page fault
choices.4: Asynchronous watermark-driven eviction

explanation: It scans the inactive LRU lists when free memory drops below a watermark, evicting pages and writing dirty ones — so page faults don't block on disk I/O.

question: What is page prefetching (readahead), and when does it fail?

choices.1: Writing dirty pages to disk early, which fails on SSDs
choices.2: Evicting pages before they're used, which always helps
choices.3: Loading every single page of a file into RAM at open time, which never fails
choices.4: Loading pages into RAM before they're referenced based on predicted access

explanation: It fails for random or pointer-chasing access with no locality. E.g. sequential readahead grows a per-file window; swap readahead loads neighboring swapped-out pages.

question: Why is a single-level (linear) page table impractical for 64-bit address spaces?

choices.1: It's an array indexed by VPN
choices.2: It only works for physical addresses
choices.3: It's too fast for the TLB to keep up with
choices.4: It cannot store protection bits

explanation: For a 64-bit space with 4 KB pages you'd need 2^52 entries (tens of petabytes), most of them unused — wasteful for sparse address spaces.

question: How do hierarchical (multi-level) page tables save memory, and what's the cost?

choices.1: They store the whole table in the TLB, eliminating walks
choices.2: Sparse trees; multi-access TLB misses
choices.3: They use one global table for all processes
choices.4: They remove the need for an offset field

explanation: The virtual address indexes a tree of page-table pages, so only branches for used regions are allocated — but a TLB miss requires walking 4-5 levels.

question: How is a virtual address translated in the x86-64 4-level page table scheme?

choices.1: The VA is hashed to find the frame in one step
choices.2: The 48-bit VA is split into four 9-bit indices plus a 12-bit offset
choices.3: A single 48-bit index directly selects the frame
choices.4: The top 16 bits directly select the frame and all the rest is the offset

explanation: PML4 -> PDPT -> PD -> PT. Walking each level to reach the final PTE and physical frame.

question: What is an inverted page table, and what is its main drawback?

choices.1: One table per process indexed by VPN, which uses the most memory
choices.2: One frame-indexed global table
choices.3: A table that maps physical to physical addresses
choices.4: A table stored entirely in the TLB with no memory cost

explanation: It's very small (size = number of frames) but translation needs a slower hash lookup and it can't easily support shared pages. Each entry holds PID, VPN, flags. Translation needs hash(PID, VPN) — the drawback is slower, hash-based lookups.

question: What are nested/extended page tables (EPT/NPT) used for?

choices.1: Speeding up single-process translation by caching walks
choices.2: Replacing the TLB entirely
choices.3: Virtualization — they add a second translation layer
choices.4: Mapping files into memory without the page cache

explanation: Guest VA -> guest PA via guest tables, then guest PA -> host PA via host tables. Enabling hardware-assisted VMs at the cost of two-dimensional page walks.

question: What is the difference between a minor (soft) and major (hard) page fault?

choices.1: A minor fault is for reads and a major fault is for writes
choices.2: A minor fault needs no disk I/O
choices.3: They are the same; the names differ by architecture
choices.4: A minor fault crashes the process; a major fault is recoverable

explanation: The data is already in RAM (first-touch zero page, copy-on-write, or a file page already in the page cache); a major fault requires disk/swap I/O and has orders-of-magnitude higher latency.

question: What is paging?

choices.1: Storing all of a process's memory in the TLB
choices.2: Swapping the entire process to disk at once
choices.3: Dividing memory into variable-sized logical segments
choices.4: Fixed-size pages mapped to arbitrary frames

explanation: The virtual address space is divided into pages and physical memory into equal-size frames, mapped by page tables; any page can map to any frame (not necessarily contiguous), which eliminates external fragmentation.

question: How does the MMU translate a virtual address under paging?

choices.1: It searches RAM linearly for the matching page
choices.2: Split VPN/offset, TLB first, walk on miss
choices.3: It hashes the entire address to find a frame directly
choices.4: It adds a fixed base register to the VA with no lookup

explanation: The VPN is looked up in the TLB; a hit forms the physical address immediately, a miss triggers a page-table walk.

question: What is the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)?

choices.1: A page-translation cache
choices.2: A buffer holding pages about to be swapped to disk
choices.3: A queue of pending page faults
choices.4: A table mapping filenames to inodes

explanation: Recently used page-table entries avoid a full walk on every access.

question: Which fields does a page table entry (PTE) typically contain?

choices.1: Frame number plus status bits
choices.2: Only the frame number and nothing else
choices.3: The filename and offset of the backing file
choices.4: The process ID and thread ID

explanation: Present bit, protection (read/write/execute), dirty bit, and accessed/reference bit.

question: What is the purpose of the NX/XD (execute-disable) bit in a PTE?

choices.1: It makes the page read-only
choices.2: When set, the page cannot be executed
choices.3: It marks the page as dirty
choices.4: It fully disables caching for the page

explanation: Critical for W^X (write-xor-execute) and preventing stack/heap code-execution attacks (DEP).

question: What is the tradeoff of using large/huge pages (2 MiB or 1 GiB)?

choices.1: They increase the number of TLB entries needed
choices.2: They always make every workload faster with no downside
choices.3: They reduce TLB pressure and page-table overhead
choices.4: They eliminate internal fragmentation entirely

explanation: Fewer entries/levels. Lowering TLB misses and page faults, but cause more internal fragmentation and are harder to allocate as large contiguous physical regions.

question: What is a segmentation fault?

choices.1: When a program runs out of stack space and pauses
choices.2: An access the process isn't allowed
choices.3: When the CPU executes an invalid opcode
choices.4: When two segments of a program overlap in memory

explanation: SIGSEGV — an unmapped address or a permission violation; the hardware faults and the OS typically terminates the process.

question: How does the hardware first detect a segfault-causing access?

choices.1: The disk controller reports the bad access
choices.2: The OS scans all memory accesses in software
choices.3: The MMU raises a page fault on translation
choices.4: The compiler inserts a runtime bounds check

explanation: On a memory access the MMU translates the VA via the TLB/page table; if the address is unmapped or the access violates permissions (e.g. writing read-only memory), it raises a #PF exception — before the OS is involved.

question: After a page fault, how does control reach the kernel?

choices.1: Kernel mode, save state, IDT dispatch
choices.2: The process is immediately killed with no handler
choices.3: The MMU fixes the access itself with no kernel involvement
choices.4: The faulting instruction directly calls a user-space handler

explanation: The CPU switches to kernel mode, saves registers and execution state, and jumps to the page-fault handler via the interrupt descriptor table.

question: How does the kernel's page-fault handler decide whether a fault is recoverable?

choices.1: It always retries the instruction until it succeeds
choices.2: It checks the faulting address and error code
choices.3: It picks recoverability at random
choices.4: Every page fault is unrecoverable and ends in a segfault

explanation: A swapped-out page is loaded (soft fault), a copy-on-write first write makes a private copy, a stack-growth fault allocates a page — but an invalid address or wrong permission is unrecoverable and leads to a segfault.

question: How is a segmentation violation delivered to the process?

choices.1: The kernel sends the SIGSEGV signal
choices.2: The CPU retries the faulting instruction automatically
choices.3: The kernel rolls back the process to its last checkpoint
choices.4: The kernel pauses the process indefinitely

explanation: If the process registered a handler it runs, otherwise the default action is to terminate the process (with an optional core dump).

question: Why is the NULL page left unmapped, and is the faulting instruction retried?

choices.1: The lowest page (around 0x0) is intentionally unmapped
choices.2: The faulting instruction is always retried after the handler
choices.3: The NULL page is mapped read-write for safety, and the instruction is always retried
choices.4: The NULL page holds the interrupt vector table, and faults are ignored

explanation: So a NULL dereference faults instead of corrupting memory; the OS does not retry the faulting instruction for an invalid access (it's invalid by definition) — control only returns to run a signal handler.

question: What is segmentation as a memory-management scheme?

choices.1: Dividing memory into fixed-size pages mapped to frames
choices.2: Splitting a file into blocks for storage
choices.3: Dividing the address space into logically meaningful segments
choices.4: Storing the whole process on disk and loading it all on demand

explanation: Code, data, heap, stack, shared libraries), each contiguous with its own base, limit, and permissions; memory is addressed as (segment number, offset.

question: How does segmentation translate a logical address mechanically?

choices.1: It hashes (segment, offset) to find the physical frame
choices.2: It adds the offset to the program counter
choices.3: base[segment] + offset, limit-checked
choices.4: It walks a multi-level page table

explanation: Hardware verifies offset <= limit[segment], else raises a segmentation fault.

question: What is a key motivation for segmentation?

choices.1: Per-segment protection and independent growth
choices.2: To eliminate the need for a base register
choices.3: To force all memory to be the same fixed size
choices.4: To use one giant flat region with uniform permissions

explanation: Code read-only/executable, heap and stack read-write/non-executable; stack grows down while heap grows up; plus easy sharing of code/libraries across processes.

question: Why did pure segmentation decline in favor of paging?

choices.1: Segmentation could not support more than one process
choices.2: Segmentation provided no memory protection at all
choices.3: External fragmentation killed it
choices.4: Paging is simpler to implement than segmentation in every way

explanation: Variable-sized segments fragment and are hard to compact; fixed-size pages avoid external fragmentation.

question: What role does segmentation play on x86-64 today?

choices.1: Mostly disabled; FS/GS remain
choices.2: It has been removed entirely with no remaining use
choices.3: It is used for every memory access alongside paging
choices.4: It fully replaces paging for all translation

explanation: FS and GS survive for thread-local storage; paging does all the address translation.

question: What is the typical layout of a process's virtual address space (high to low)?

choices.1: Heap at the very top growing downward into the stack
choices.2: A single flat region with no distinct segments
choices.3: Text at the top, then stack, heap, and kernel at the bottom
choices.4: Kernel, stack, mmap, heap, BSS, data, text

explanation: The stack grows down; the mmap region holds shared libraries and thread stacks; the heap grows up; BSS holds zero-initialized globals, data holds initialized globals, text is read-only code, and the NULL page is unmapped.

question: What are the core motivations for virtual memory?

choices.1: Forcing programs to know their exact physical load address
choices.2: Eliminating the need for any disk storage
choices.3: Making every process share one physical address space directly
choices.4: Giving each process a private isolated address space

explanation: Paging to disk. Allowing programs to use more memory than physical RAM, simplifying relocation/linking, enabling efficient sharing, and enforcing protection/security.

question: How does the base-and-bounds mechanism work?

choices.1: It walks a multi-level page table for each access
choices.2: It hashes the virtual address to a physical frame
choices.3: Trap if VA >= bounds, else PA = base + VA
choices.4: It maps each page independently to any frame

explanation: A base register holds the physical start of the process's region and a bounds/limit register its size; the hardware checks every access.

question: Why was base-and-bounds replaced by paging?

choices.1: It provided no memory protection whatsoever
choices.2: One contiguous region is too rigid
choices.3: It was too slow because every access required a page-table walk
choices.4: It could not relocate programs in memory

explanation: No sharing between processes, no lazy allocation, no swapping/overcommit, and all-or-nothing protection — paging fixes all of these.

question: Why does x86-64 use only 48 of the 64 address bits for virtual addressing?

choices.1: Four 9-bit indices plus a 12-bit offset
choices.2: Because the TLB can only hold 48 entries
choices.3: Because the CPU registers are only 48 bits wide
choices.4: Because RAM chips physically can't address more than 48 bits

explanation: That's the 4-level page-table format = 48 bits (256 TB per process); using all 64 bits would need vastly larger page-table structures for no practical benefit.

question: What are canonical addresses on x86-64?

choices.1: Addresses that are aligned to a 4 KB page boundary
choices.2: Addresses whose top 16 bits are always zero
choices.3: Bits 63-48 must be sign-extended copies of bit 47
choices.4: Addresses that fall entirely within the heap

explanation: Non-canonical addresses cause a general protection fault, which simplifies pointer arithmetic and leaves room for future expansion (LA57 -> 57 bits). Low: 0x0000... and high: 0xFFFF8... Creating two canonical halves.

question: How is the base-and-bounds region enforced across a context switch?

choices.1: The kernel swaps privileged registers
choices.2: Base and bounds are recomputed from the page table each time
choices.3: User code reloads the base and bounds registers itself
choices.4: The bounds register is shared by all processes

explanation: On a switch the OS saves the outgoing process's base/bounds and loads the incoming one's; only the kernel can change these registers, so enforcement is entirely in hardware.

question: What is consistent hashing, and what problem does it solve?

choices.1: It encrypts keys before distributing them across nodes
choices.2: Keys and nodes share a ring; churn moves ~K/N
choices.3: It eliminates the need for any hashing
choices.4: It guarantees every node holds exactly the same keys

explanation: Both are mapped onto the same circular keyspace, so adding or removing a node moves only ~K/N keys instead of nearly all of them — solving the resharding problem of naive hash(key) % N.

question: Why is hash(key) % N problematic when the number of nodes changes?

choices.1: It only works for fewer than 4 nodes
choices.2: It leaves most keys unassigned
choices.3: It requires a central coordinator to compute every lookup
choices.4: Changing N remaps almost every key

explanation: E.g. going from 4 to 5 nodes reshuffles ~80%. Causing a cache-miss storm or a massive data migration.

question: On the consistent-hashing ring, which node owns a given key?

choices.1: The node whose hash value is numerically the closest to the key's own hash
choices.2: The node with the most free capacity
choices.3: The first node found going clockwise from the key's position on the ring
choices.4: A randomly chosen node, rebalanced periodically

question: What happens to key assignments when a node is added or removed?

choices.1: All keys are redistributed evenly across every node
choices.2: Keys are unaffected; only metadata changes
choices.3: Half of all keys move to the new node
choices.4: Only one arc's keys change hands

explanation: Adding the Nth node steals ~K/N keys from its clockwise successor; removing a node reassigns only its keys to the next clockwise node — not a global reshuffle.

question: What problem do virtual nodes (vnodes) solve in consistent hashing?

choices.1: They make hot keys distribute across nodes automatically
choices.2: They remove the ring's clockwise assignment rule
choices.3: Uneven arcs and lumpy failover
choices.4: They eliminate the need for replication

explanation: Placing each physical node at many ring points averages out the arcs (even load) and spreads a removed node's load across many successors instead of one neighbor; it also enables weighting for heterogeneous capacity.

question: Does consistent hashing (even with vnodes) solve the hot-key problem?

choices.1: Yes, vnodes spread a single hot key across all nodes
choices.2: No — the ring always sends one key to one node
choices.3: Yes, the clockwise rule balances hot keys automatically
choices.4: Hot keys cannot occur under consistent hashing

explanation: Hot keys need separate mitigations like key salting/splitting, replicating/caching the hot key, or bounded loads.

question: Roughly how do these latencies order from fastest to slowest?

choices.1: All of these are within the same order of magnitude
choices.2: SSD ~1 ns, RAM ~100 us, L1 ~10 ms
choices.3: L1, RAM, SSD, DC hop, HDD, WAN
choices.4: Main memory ~1 ns, L1 cache ~100 ns, HDD seek ~1 us, network ~1 ms

explanation: ~1 ns, ~100 ns, ~tens of µs, ~0.5 ms, ~5-10 ms, ~150 ms respectively.

question: What is the key ratio that explains why caching exists?

choices.1: Memory is about 2x faster than a network call
choices.2: A network call is faster than an L1 cache hit
choices.3: Disk is faster than memory for random access
choices.4: ~100,000x memory versus network

explanation: Memory is roughly five orders of magnitude faster than a cross-country network call.

question: How do successive CPU cache levels relate in size and speed?

choices.1: Each level is roughly 10x smaller and 10x faster than the one directly above it
choices.2: All cache levels have the same size and latency
choices.3: Each level is roughly 10x larger and 10x slower than the one above it
choices.4: L3 is faster than L1 because it is larger

explanation: A full RAM access is ~100x an L1 hit.

question: What is the handy 'seconds per day' constant for QPS estimation?

choices.1: About 3,600, so daily requests / 3,600 ~ average QPS
choices.2: About 86,400 ~ 10^5, so daily requests / 10^5 ~ average QPS
choices.3: About 1,000,000, so that daily requests / 10^6 ~ average QPS
choices.4: About 1,440, the number of minutes per day

question: How should you relate peak QPS to average QPS when provisioning?

choices.1: Peak QPS is always lower than average QPS
choices.2: Peak QPS equals average QPS, so they are interchangeable
choices.3: Peak QPS is roughly 2-3x average QPS
choices.4: Peak QPS is about 100x average QPS

explanation: So provision for the peak rather than the mean.

question: How much faster is a cache hit than the database query it replaces?

choices.1: Roughly 100-1000x faster
choices.2: Slower, but more consistent
choices.3: About 2x faster
choices.4: Roughly the same speed, just cheaper

explanation: The whole reason to cache.

question: How do REST, GraphQL, and gRPC model operations?

choices.1: REST uses binary RPC; gRPC uses JSON resources; GraphQL uses HTTP verbs
choices.2: All three model resources with HTTP verbs
choices.3: REST models resources acted on with HTTP verbs
choices.4: They are three names for the same protocol

explanation: GraphQL is a query language over a typed graph where clients fetch exactly the fields they need; gRPC models functions/methods (RPC) called over a fast binary protocol.

question: What are REST's two classic fetching weaknesses?

choices.1: It can only return binary data and cannot stream
choices.2: It cannot be cached by CDNs
choices.3: It requires HTTP/2 and a proxy for browsers
choices.4: Over-fetching and under-fetching / N+1

explanation: You get the whole resource even if you need one field. One screen needs several round trips.

question: What is GraphQL's main strength, and its main weakness?

choices.1: Strength: the client asks for exactly the fields it needs in one round trip
choices.2: Strength: trivial HTTP caching; weakness: it simply can't express nested queries
choices.3: Strength: binary compactness; weakness: no typed schema
choices.4: Strength: browser-only support; weakness: no field selection

explanation: Weakness: caching is hard (a single dynamic POST endpoint), and naive resolvers cause N+1 queries unless batched. Killing over/under-fetching.

question: What characterizes gRPC?

choices.1: Typed Protobuf contracts over HTTP/2
choices.2: A query language letting clients pick exact fields
choices.3: Human-readable JSON over HTTP/1.1 with easy curl debugging
choices.4: A resource-oriented model using HTTP status codes

explanation: Fast, compact binary serialization with multiplexing and bidirectional streaming, plus codegen — but not human-readable, and browsers need grpc-web/a proxy.

question: How do the three paradigms compare on caching?

choices.1: All three are equally easy to cache via HTTP
choices.2: GraphQL caches for free; REST cannot be cached
choices.3: gRPC is the easiest to cache; REST the hardest
choices.4: REST is easy to cache (HTTP caching/CDNs)

explanation: GraphQL and gRPC are both hard to cache.

question: What is the rule of thumb for choosing a paradigm?

choices.1: Public/cacheable/broad-reach -> REST
choices.2: Always use REST for internal low-latency service calls
choices.3: Always use gRPC for public web APIs
choices.4: GraphQL for everything; the others are obsolete

explanation: Client-driven field selection or aggregating many backends or varied frontends -> GraphQL; internal service-to-service with low latency and streaming -> gRPC.

question: How does the cache-aside (lazy loading) strategy work?

choices.1: The app writes only to the cache, which flushes to the DB later
choices.2: The app talks to both cache and DB
choices.3: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously
choices.4: The cache loads from the DB itself on every miss

explanation: On a read it checks the cache, and on a miss reads the DB, writes the value into the cache, then returns it; on a write it updates the DB and invalidates the cache entry.

question: What distinguishes cache-aside from read-through caching?

choices.1: Cache-aside writes synchronously to the DB; read-through writes asynchronously
choices.2: In cache-aside the application repopulates the cache on a miss
choices.3: Read-through caches all data eagerly; cache-aside never caches
choices.4: They are identical strategies

explanation: In read-through the cache itself loads from the DB via a provider/loader, so the app only ever talks to the cache.

question: What are the trade-offs of write-through caching?

choices.1: It risks losing writes if the cache crashes
choices.2: It gives the lowest possible write latency
choices.3: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously
choices.4: It leaves the cache and DB eventually consistent

explanation: Strong consistency and no stale data for cached keys, but at the cost of higher write latency and caching data that may never be read.

question: What are the benefits and the core risk of write-behind (write-back) caching?

choices.1: Fast async writes; loss if the cache dies first
choices.2: It is the simplest strategy with synchronous DB writes
choices.3: It always reads stale data from the DB
choices.4: It gives strong consistency with no risk of data loss

explanation: The app writes only to the cache and returns immediately, with the cache flushing to the DB asynchronously (batched/coalesced) — very low write latency and high throughput, but data is lost if the cache crashes before flushing.

question: What is the cache miss penalty shared by cache-aside and read-through?

choices.1: The cache must be fully warmed before any read succeeds
choices.2: Every read, hit or miss, goes to the database
choices.3: Writes are slow because they hit cache and DB synchronously
choices.4: The first read of any key is slow

explanation: Because a miss requires fetching from the DB and populating the cache (three trips for cache-aside).

question: Which is a sensible common strategy combination?

choices.1: Cache-aside with no invalidation for strong consistency
choices.2: Write-behind + write-through combined together for guaranteed durability
choices.3: Read-through alone guarantees the cache never goes stale
choices.4: Cache-aside + TTL is the simplest, most common general-purpose setup

explanation: Read-through + write-through gives a fully cache-fronted, strongly consistent system at the cost of slower writes.

question: What is the difference between eviction and expiration?

choices.1: Eviction is triggered by capacity (the cache is full, so make room)
choices.2: Eviction is time-based; expiration is capacity-based
choices.3: They are the same mechanism with different names
choices.4: Eviction applies only to reads, while expiration applies only to writes

explanation: Expiration (TTL) is triggered by time (the entry is too old) regardless of how full the cache is — they are orthogonal and usually combined.

question: How does the FIFO eviction policy behave, and what is its main drawback?

choices.1: It never evicts; it only expires entries by TTL
choices.2: It evicts the earliest-inserted entry regardless of usage (a simple queue)
choices.3: It evicts the single least recently accessed entry, thereby exploiting locality
choices.4: It evicts the most frequently used entry

explanation: It's cheap but ignores access patterns, so a frequently used hot entry gets evicted just because it's old, and it can suffer Belady's anomaly.

question: What is the main vulnerability of the LRU eviction policy?

choices.1: It is vulnerable to scans/flooding
choices.2: It always keeps the oldest items forever
choices.3: It cannot be implemented in O(1) time
choices.4: It ignores recency entirely

explanation: A one-time sweep over many cold items (e.g. a full table scan) evicts the genuinely hot working set, tanking the hit rate afterward.

question: What are the drawbacks of the LFU eviction policy?

choices.1: It requires no per-entry state at all
choices.2: It evicts the most recently used item, which hurts locality
choices.3: It is identical to FIFO in behavior
choices.4: It suffers stale popularity and punishes new items

explanation: So it needs aging/decay to adapt. An item popular long ago keeps a high count and lingers even after demand disappears. Which start with a low count and get evicted quickly.

question: What does TTL (time-to-live) provide, and what does it NOT do?

choices.1: It bounds staleness and is self-cleaning, but it is not size-aware
choices.2: It guarantees the cache never serves stale data and never fills up
choices.3: It evicts the least recently used entry when full
choices.4: It caps memory usage, removing the need for eviction

explanation: The cache can still fill up before anything expires, so you still need a capacity-driven eviction policy. Data is never older than the TTL.

question: Which production-grade or specialized eviction policies are worth knowing?

choices.1: Only FIFO and LRU exist in real systems
choices.2: MRU, ARC, and W-TinyLFU
choices.3: MRU is the default for locality-heavy workloads
choices.4: Random replacement is never used in practice

explanation: MRU evicts the most recently used (good for sequential scans); ARC self-tunes between recency and frequency; W-TinyLFU (Caffeine) pairs a frequency sketch with an LRU window.

question: What is a cache stampede (thundering herd), and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: When one key overloads a single node; fixed by replication
choices.2: When the cache returns stale data after a write; fixed by versioning
choices.3: When requests hit keys absent from cache and DB; fixed by Bloom filters
choices.4: A hot-key miss stampede; coalesce and jitter

explanation: When a hot key expires, many concurrent requests all rush to recompute the same expensive value, flooding the DB; mitigate with request coalescing/single-flight, early/probabilistic refresh, TTL jitter, and stale-while-revalidate.

question: Where does cache inconsistency (stale reads) come from?

choices.1: The cache and DB are separate copies that diverge
choices.2: The cache always perfectly mirrors the DB; staleness is impossible
choices.3: It is caused solely by network packet loss
choices.4: Stale reads only happen when the cache is empty

explanation: E.g. the DB is updated without invalidating the cache, a slow repopulating read races with a concurrent write, or a write-behind cache lags the DB; mitigations include TTLs, invalidate-on-write, write-through, and versioned keys.

question: What is the hot-key problem and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: A key that doesn't exist anywhere; fixed by negative caching
choices.2: A key that expires too quickly; fixed by raising its TTL only
choices.3: A single key gets a hugely disproportionate share of traffic
choices.4: A key that is too large; fixed by compression

explanation: Mitigations include local/client-side caching, key replication/fan-out, random suffixes, and hotspot detection. Concentrating load on one cache node/shard and defeating horizontal scaling.

question: What is cache penetration and how is it addressed?

choices.1: One single key overloading a node; fixed by replication
choices.2: The cache lagging the DB; fixed by write-through
choices.3: Misses for keys absent from cache AND the database
choices.4: Many keys expiring at once; fixed by TTL jitter

explanation: Often malicious or buggy requests that always miss and hit the DB pointlessly. Fixes: negative caching ('not found' with a short TTL) or a Bloom filter rejecting keys that definitely don't exist.

question: What is a cache avalanche?

choices.1: A request for a nonexistent key
choices.2: Mass simultaneous expiry of cached keys
choices.3: A single hot key expiring under load
choices.4: The cache serving up slightly stale data

explanation: A large fraction of the cache expires or fails at once (or the whole tier goes down), dumping massive load on the DB. Fixes: TTL jitter, layered caches, circuit breakers, and a highly-available cache tier.

question: What is the cold-start cache problem?

choices.1: An empty cache has a 0% hit rate
choices.2: A cache that is too cold to fit in RAM
choices.3: A key that is accessed only once and never again
choices.4: A cache that never expires any entries

explanation: The fix is to pre-warm critical keys on startup. After a deploy, restart, or flush. So all traffic hits the DB until it warms up.

question: What is the typical ordering of caching layers from client to origin?

choices.1: Client to origin, absorbing as they go
choices.2: There is only one cache layer between the app and the DB
choices.3: Disk -> database -> Redis -> app -> CDN -> browser, in that request order
choices.4: All caches sit at the same layer with equal latency

explanation: Browser/client cache -> CDN/edge -> reverse-proxy -> app in-process -> distributed cache -> database buffer pool -> disk; each layer shrinks the traffic the next one sees.

question: What is the key benefit and limitation of a client-side/browser cache?

choices.1: It is shared across all users and easy to invalidate centrally
choices.2: It gives the fastest possible hit
choices.3: It requires a network round trip for every hit
choices.4: It survives across all servers in the fleet

explanation: Zero network, governed by HTTP headers like Cache-Control/ETag), but you can't invalidate it directly once shipped — you wait for the TTL or use versioned URLs (app.v3.js.

question: What is the main risk of an application in-process (local) cache?

choices.1: It is shared across instances and never drifts
choices.2: It requires a network hop for every access
choices.3: It is always slower than a distributed cache
choices.4: N instances hold N drifting copies

explanation: Per-instance caches can drift out of sync and don't survive restarts — though ns-µs access with no network makes them a good hot-key shield.

question: What are the strengths and the risk of a distributed cache (Redis/Memcached)?

choices.1: It lives inside each app process with no network cost at all
choices.2: It is a shared sub-ms source of truth for the whole fleet
choices.3: It is per-instance and cannot be shared
choices.4: It cannot be sharded across nodes

explanation: Consistent across instances, surviving app restarts, scaling independently, and sharded via consistent hashing — but it adds a network hop and the cache tier itself needs HA.

question: What are database/storage caches, and their limitation?

choices.1: Caches inside the data tier
choices.2: Browser caches governed by HTTP headers
choices.3: CDN edge caches near the user
choices.4: External Redis clusters shared by all services

explanation: The buffer pool/page cache (hot pages in RAM skip disk), query/result caches, and materialized views; they're 'free' but per-database and don't offload the DB the way an external cache does.

question: What is the two-tier (L1 in-process + L2 distributed) caching pattern, and its cost?

choices.1: It uses two distributed caches to double throughput, with no invalidation cost
choices.2: L1 (per-instance, ns) absorbs hot keys with no network
choices.3: It replaces the database entirely with two cache tiers
choices.4: It is slower than a single cache because of the extra layer

explanation: On a miss L2 (shared Redis, sub-ms) absorbs the rest fleet-wide; on an L2 miss the DB is hit — mirroring the CPU's L1/L2/L3 hierarchy — at the cost of harder invalidation (two caches to keep coherent).

question: What does two-phase locking (2PL) guarantee, and what should it not be confused with?

choices.1: It guarantees durability by flushing the write-ahead log
choices.2: It guarantees atomic commit across nodes, the same as 2PC
choices.3: It guarantees serializability within a database
choices.4: It guarantees availability during network partitions

explanation: It should not be confused with two-phase commit (2PC), which is an atomic-commit protocol across multiple nodes. The isolation I in ACID.

question: What is the defining rule of two-phase locking?

choices.1: A transaction cannot acquire any lock after it has released one
choices.2: A transaction may acquire locks only after releasing all previous ones
choices.3: A transaction acquires and releases locks in any order it likes
choices.4: A transaction must release each lock immediately after using it

explanation: It has a growing phase (acquire only) and a shrinking phase (release only), separated by the 'lock point'.

question: What is the difference between shared (S) and exclusive (X) locks?

choices.1: A shared lock is for writing; an exclusive lock is for reading
choices.2: Both can be held by multiple transactions simultaneously
choices.3: An exclusive lock allows concurrent shared locks
choices.4: Many readers or one writer

explanation: Shared locks coexist for reading; an exclusive lock is for writing, held by one transaction, excluding shared locks too.

question: How does Strict 2PL improve on Basic 2PL?

choices.1: It releases all of the locks as early as it possibly can to maximize concurrency
choices.2: It acquires all locks before the transaction starts
choices.3: It holds all exclusive (write) locks until the transaction commits or aborts
choices.4: It removes the two-phase rule entirely

explanation: The most common variant. Preventing other transactions from reading uncommitted writes and thus eliminating cascading aborts.

question: What is the property and cost of Conservative (Static) 2PL?

choices.1: It is the same as Strict 2PL with shared locks released early
choices.2: It guarantees no isolation but high availability
choices.3: A transaction acquires all its locks up front before it begins
choices.4: It releases locks during the growing phase, improving throughput

explanation: Makes it deadlock-free, but it requires knowing the full lock set in advance and reduces concurrency.

question: How does 2PL compare with MVCC?

choices.1: They are identical approaches to concurrency control
choices.2: MVCC blocks readers against writers while 2PL never blocks
choices.3: 2PL keeps multiple versions while MVCC uses locks
choices.4: Pessimistic blocking versus snapshot versions

explanation: 2PL blocks on conflict (readers and writers block each other); MVCC keeps multiple versions so readers never block (they see a snapshot) — many databases combine MVCC reads with 2PL-style locking for writes.

question: What is the Saga pattern?

choices.1: A locking protocol that holds locks across all services until commit
choices.2: A single atomic transaction spanning all the services at once
choices.3: A caching strategy for distributed reads
choices.4: Local transactions undone by compensations

explanation: A sequence of local transactions, each committing independently, where a failure triggers compensating transactions that semantically undo prior work — maintaining consistency across services without a distributed transaction, trading atomicity for availability.

question: What is a compensating transaction in a saga?

choices.1: A new transaction that semantically undoes a previously committed step
choices.2: A lock held until the entire saga completes
choices.3: A plain database rollback that simply erases the original committed step
choices.4: A read that re-fetches the latest value

explanation: It must be idempotent and safe to retry. Not a database rollback, since the original commit is already durable and visible.

question: What is the difference between choreography and orchestration in sagas?

choices.1: Both require a two-phase commit coordinator
choices.2: They differ only in the programming language used
choices.3: Events and reactions versus a central coordinator
choices.4: Choreography uses a central coordinator; orchestration uses events

explanation: Choreography: each service publishes events others react to — loose coupling but hard to trace. Orchestration: a central coordinator tells each service what to do and invokes compensations — explicit and monitorable, but another component to operate.

question: What is the pivot transaction in a saga?

choices.1: The point of no return: once it commits the saga will complete
choices.2: The compensating transaction for the final step
choices.3: A lock acquired across all participating services
choices.4: The very first step of the whole saga, which can always be undone

explanation: Everything before it is compensatable, and everything after it is retryable (must eventually succeed).

question: Which ACID property do sagas NOT provide, and what problem does that cause?

choices.1: They provide full ACID including isolation
choices.2: They lack durability, so committed steps can vanish
choices.3: Isolation — intermediate states are visible
choices.4: They lack consistency, so data is always corrupt

explanation: Sagas provide atomicity (via compensation), consistency, and durability, but each step commits immediately, causing anomalies like dirty reads and lost updates (mitigated by semantic locks marking records as PENDING).

question: When should you use a saga versus two-phase commit?

choices.1: They are interchangeable in all situations
choices.2: Sagas across services; 2PC inside one boundary
choices.3: Sagas are for short transactions; 2PC for long-running ones
choices.4: Always use 2PC for microservices and sagas for single databases

explanation: Use a saga for long-running, microservice, or cross-shard operations tolerating brief inconsistency; reach for 2PC only within a single database/trust boundary needing strict isolation.

question: What does a circuit breaker do?

choices.1: It caches responses from a slow dependency
choices.2: It load-balances requests across healthy replicas
choices.3: It cuts traffic to an unhealthy dependency
choices.4: It encrypts traffic between two services

explanation: Wrapping calls and failing fast instead of endlessly retrying or waiting on a broken dependency.

question: What are the three states of a circuit breaker?

choices.1: On, Off, and Standby
choices.2: Closed, Open, and Half-open
choices.3: Read, Write, and Idle
choices.4: Healthy, Degraded, and Failed, with no recovery testing

explanation: The dependency is unhealthy, requests are blocked or fail fast. A few test requests check whether it has recovered. Normal operation, requests allowed through.

question: Why is 'failing fast' a key benefit of circuit breakers?

choices.1: It makes the failing service respond faster
choices.2: It retries the request more aggressively to force success
choices.3: It stops burning resources on doomed waits
choices.4: It hides the error from monitoring so alerts don't fire

explanation: Instead of waiting seconds for each request to time out, the breaker immediately returns an error or fallback, preserving request threads, DB connections, memory, CPU, and sockets — many outages come from resource exhaustion while waiting on a failed service.

question: How does a circuit breaker help an already-overloaded service recover?

choices.1: By sending it more requests to keep it warm
choices.2: By restarting the service automatically
choices.3: By increasing the timeout so that requests wait longer
choices.4: By opening the circuit it reduces the request load

explanation: The struggling service breathing room to recover instead of hammering it until it fully collapses.

question: How does a circuit breaker isolate failure and degrade gracefully?

choices.1: It shuts down the entire application to contain the failure
choices.2: It creates a boundary around the failure
choices.3: It retries until the recommendation service comes back
choices.4: It forces every dependent service to fail together

explanation: So e.g. a down recommendation service doesn't hang the whole product page — the page still loads without recommendations (degraded, not catastrophic).

question: What fallback behaviors can a system use while a circuit is open?

choices.1: Permanently disable the affected feature
choices.2: Block all requests until the dependency recovers, with no response
choices.3: Crash the caller to free its resources
choices.4: Serve something cheaper immediately

explanation: Cached data, partial results, defaults, skipped features, queued work, or a fast clear error.

question: What does it mean for an operation to be idempotent?

choices.1: It always returns a different result each time
choices.2: Once equals many times
choices.3: It must be executed inside a transaction
choices.4: It can only ever be executed once

explanation: Repeated attempts don't create extra side effects.

question: Why does idempotency matter during failures?

choices.1: It makes operations run faster
choices.2: Failures are ambiguous; retries must be safe
choices.3: It guarantees the operation never fails
choices.4: It encrypts the request so it can't be tampered with

explanation: Timeouts, dropped connections, lost responses, and redeliveries mean the client often can't tell if the operation succeeded — idempotency makes retrying safe.

question: Why is retrying a non-idempotent POST /payments dangerous?

choices.1: A lost response means a double charge
choices.2: It will always be rejected by the server
choices.3: It corrupts the database schema
choices.4: It is impossible to retry a POST request

explanation: If the first request succeeded but the reply was lost, the retry pays again.

question: How does an idempotency key make a payment retry safe?

choices.1: The key forces the server to fully process the same payment again
choices.2: The key tells the server to skip the payment entirely
choices.3: The client sends a unique key (e.g. Idempotency-Key: abc-123)
choices.4: The key encrypts the payment amount so it can't change

explanation: If it retries with the same key, the server recognizes the already-processed operation and returns the original result instead of creating a second payment.

question: Why is idempotency especially important with message queues and microservices?

choices.1: Microservices never retry messages
choices.2: It makes messages deliver faster
choices.3: Queues guarantee exactly-once delivery, so idempotency is unnecessary
choices.4: Many systems provide at-least-once delivery

explanation: So a message may be delivered more than once (e.g. a consumer crashes before acknowledging); an idempotent handler detects the already-processed event and avoids duplicating the action.

question: What does a timeout do, and why is it needed?

choices.1: It guarantees the operation eventually succeeds
choices.2: It encrypts the request to prevent tampering
choices.3: It controls how long to wait before retrying again after a failure
choices.4: It sets a maximum time you're willing to wait for an operation

explanation: Without it a program could wait forever on a slow call, tying up threads, memory, and sockets — a timeout turns 'maybe it'll respond eventually' into 'it didn't respond in time'.

question: What does a backoff control, and what is exponential backoff?

choices.1: A backoff is the act of giving up after the first failure
choices.2: A backoff caches the failed response
choices.3: A backoff controls how long you wait before retrying after a failure
choices.4: A backoff sets the maximum total time to wait for one single operation

explanation: Exponential backoff waits increasing delays (e.g. 1s, 2s, 4s) instead of retrying instantly.

question: Why does exponential backoff help a struggling system?

choices.1: It makes each retry faster than the last
choices.2: It removes the need for timeouts
choices.3: It guarantees the request succeeds on the second attempt
choices.4: It prevents retry storms

explanation: Clients stop hammering the service, the failing system gets time to recover, wasted work drops, and transient failures succeed on later tries.

question: How do timeouts and backoffs solve different parts of the same problem?

choices.1: They are two names for the same mechanism
choices.2: A timeout stops you from waiting too long
choices.3: Both control how long to wait before retrying
choices.4: Both set the maximum time to wait for one operation

explanation: A backoff stops you from retrying too quickly — together (with a max retry count) they form a safe retry pattern.

question: Why should jitter be added to backoff delays?

choices.1: In large systems many clients may fail at the same time
choices.2: Jitter makes the retries happen faster on average
choices.3: Jitter encrypts the retry timing for security
choices.4: Jitter guarantees all clients retry at the same instant

explanation: If they all retry after exactly 1s, 2s, 4s they overload the service again in synchronized waves — jitter adds randomness to spread retries out and reduce traffic spikes.

question: What is client-side load balancing?

choices.1: A load balancer that runs directly inside the client's operating system kernel
choices.2: Balancing load between a client's CPU cores
choices.3: A technique where the client chooses which server instance to call directly
choices.4: A technique where the server decides which client to serve next

explanation: Instead of sending every request through a central load balancer.

question: How does a client discover available server instances in client-side load balancing?

choices.1: It asks the central load balancer for the list
choices.2: It hardcodes a single fixed server address
choices.3: A service registry lists healthy instances
choices.4: It broadcasts to every IP on the network

explanation: Consul, Eureka, etcd, or Kubernetes service discovery provides the list; the client then picks one with a strategy like round robin.

question: What is a key benefit of client-side load balancing?

choices.1: Simpler clients that need no extra logic
choices.2: Centralized control that's easy to update everywhere at once
choices.3: No extra hop, no central bottleneck
choices.4: Guaranteed perfectly even traffic distribution

explanation: The client calls the backend directly, plus better service-discovery integration and more routing control.

question: What is a key drawback of client-side load balancing?

choices.1: It requires a dedicated hardware appliance
choices.2: Clients become more complex
choices.3: It always adds an extra network hop
choices.4: It cannot integrate with service discovery

explanation: Each must do service discovery, health tracking, server selection, retries, and timeouts — and updating the load-balancing behavior may require updating many clients, risking inconsistent behavior.

question: How does client-side load balancing differ from server-side?

choices.1: In client-side the client makes the routing decision and knows the backend instances
choices.2: In client-side the external load balancer decides, while in server-side the client decides
choices.3: Both always add an extra network hop
choices.4: Server-side requires the client to know all backends

explanation: Usually no extra hop, but higher client complexity); in server-side the load balancer decides and the client only knows the LB (usually an extra hop, lower client complexity.

question: How can a sidecar proxy relate to client-side load balancing?

choices.1: A sidecar proxy is a hardware appliance in the data center
choices.2: A sidecar proxy forces all traffic through a central load balancer
choices.3: A sidecar proxy replaces the need for any backend servers
choices.4: The proxy does the balancing for you

explanation: A sidecar like Envoy gives client-side benefits without putting the logic in application code.

question: What information does a Layer 4 load balancer use to route traffic?

choices.1: Transport-layer information
choices.2: The request body and API version
choices.3: The user's session and authentication token
choices.4: HTTP URL paths, cookies, and headers

explanation: Source/destination IP, source/destination port, and protocol (TCP/UDP) — without inspecting application content like HTTP paths, headers, or cookies.

question: What can a Layer 7 load balancer do that Layer 4 cannot?

choices.1: Route by URL, host, cookies, or headers
choices.2: Route purely by IP and port with minimal overhead
choices.3: Handle raw TCP and UDP with the least processing
choices.4: Balance load without understanding any protocol

explanation: E.g. send /api to API servers; plus TLS termination, web-application-firewall integration, and rate limiting.

question: When is a Layer 4 load balancer the better choice?

choices.1: When you need a web application firewall and TLS termination
choices.2: When routing HTTP requests to microservices by header
choices.3: When you need URL-path-based and cookie-based routing
choices.4: Raw TCP/UDP at high throughput

explanation: Databases, DNS, VoIP — where minimal application awareness and simple connection distribution are needed.

question: What is an example of Layer 7 routing?

choices.1: Forwarding a TCP connection on port 443 to a server with no path awareness
choices.2: Distributing UDP DNS packets across resolvers
choices.3: Path, host, or cookie routing
choices.4: Balancing PostgreSQL connections across replicas by IP

explanation: /api to API servers, /static to asset servers, admin.example.com to admin servers, or cookie-based stickiness.

question: How do hardware and software load balancers compare?

choices.1: They are identical except for their names
choices.2: Hardware load balancers are cheaper and more flexible than software ones
choices.3: Appliance performance versus software flexibility
choices.4: Software load balancers can only operate at Layer 4

explanation: Hardware LBs (F5, Citrix) give high, predictable performance but are expensive, less flexible, and vendor-locked; software LBs (NGINX, HAProxy, Envoy) run on general-purpose servers, are flexible, scalable, and cloud-native but consume server resources.

question: What is the purpose of load-balancer health checks?

choices.1: They balance load across the load balancer's own CPU cores
choices.2: They cache responses from healthy backends
choices.3: Probing backends, dropping the sick
choices.4: They encrypt traffic to each backend server

explanation: Layer 4: can the server accept a TCP connection? Layer 7: does /health return HTTP 200? Failures are pulled from rotation.

question: How does the round robin load-balancing algorithm work, and what does it ignore?

choices.1: It picks the fastest-responding server each time
choices.2: It sends each request to whichever server has the fewest active connections
choices.3: It routes by hashing the client IP
choices.4: It sends requests to servers one after another in a fixed rotating order

explanation: It assumes all servers are equal and ignores current load, active connections, CPU, and response time.

question: What does weighted round robin add over plain round robin?

choices.1: It always sends traffic to the fastest server
choices.2: It routes based on the requested URL
choices.3: Capacity-proportional traffic
choices.4: It picks two random servers and routes to the less loaded one

explanation: Stronger servers receive more requests according to a configured weight.

question: When is the least-connections algorithm a better fit than round robin?

choices.1: When routing cached objects to the same server
choices.2: For long-lived connections or requests with uneven durations
choices.3: When you need sticky sessions without cookies
choices.4: For short identical requests where sheer simplicity matters most

explanation: WebSockets, database connections. Because it sends new traffic to the server with the fewest active connections rather than assuming equal load.

question: What does the least-response-time algorithm optimize for?

choices.1: It keeps each client on the same server
choices.2: It rotates through servers equally
choices.3: The currently fastest backend
choices.4: It routes to the server using the least network bandwidth

explanation: Routing to the lowest-latency server suits latency-sensitive, user-facing applications.

question: How does the 'power of two choices' algorithm work, and why is it useful?

choices.1: Compare two random servers, pick the lighter
choices.2: It picks the two fastest servers and alternates between them
choices.3: It hashes the request across exactly two shards
choices.4: It always checks all servers and picks the best one

explanation: It avoids heavily loaded servers without checking every backend — very scalable for large systems.

question: What is the IP-hash algorithm good for, and what is its weakness?

choices.1: It hashes the client IP to a server
choices.2: It distributes load perfectly evenly regardless of client IPs
choices.3: It routes based on the requested URL for caching
choices.4: It picks a random server each request, ignoring the client

explanation: So the same client consistently reaches the same backend (basic session persistence without cookies); but it becomes uneven when many users share an IP (NAT/VPN/proxy) and remaps many clients when servers are added or removed.

question: What is kernel bypass?

choices.1: Moving network I/O out of the OS kernel into user space
choices.2: Disabling the network card to reduce interrupts
choices.3: Caching network packets in the CPU's L1 cache
choices.4: Running the whole application inside the kernel for speed

explanation: So packets travel between the NIC and the application without the kernel's TCP/IP stack, system calls, copies, and interrupts on the path.

question: Why is the normal kernel network path slow and jittery for latency-critical work?

choices.1: Because the NIC cannot do DMA in the normal path
choices.2: Every packet crosses the user/kernel boundary
choices.3: Because the kernel compresses every packet
choices.4: Because user space is always slower than kernel space

explanation: A syscall), is delivered via interrupts and softirqs, is copied from kernel to user buffers, and is processed by a generic stack — adding latency and, worse, variance that wrecks the tail (p99.9.

question: How does DPDK achieve kernel bypass?

choices.1: It runs the standard kernel TCP stack but at higher priority
choices.2: It intercepts the BSD sockets API and keeps the kernel stack
choices.3: User-space poll-mode drivers on hugepages
choices.4: It writes packets directly into remote machine memory

explanation: A PMD busy-polls the NIC rings directly from user space (no interrupts, burning a core), packets are DMA'd zero-copy into hugepage-backed buffers, and the app brings its own UDP/TCP stack.

question: What is distinctive about Solarflare/Onload compared to DPDK?

choices.1: It writes directly into the remote peer's memory
choices.2: It requires rewriting the application around a custom stack
choices.3: It runs entirely inside the kernel with no user-space component
choices.4: It keeps the BSD sockets API

explanation: Onload intercepts recv()/send() and runs the stack in user space — kernel bypass without rewriting the application, unlike DPDK.

question: How does RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) work?

choices.1: The NIC writes directly into remote application memory
choices.2: It batches syscalls using shared submission/completion rings
choices.3: It runs an eBPF program at the earliest point in the driver
choices.4: It polls the local NIC from user space with its own TCP stack

explanation: And often the remote CPU entirely. Bypassing both CPUs' kernels for ultra-low latency and near-zero-copy, via the verbs API over transports like InfiniBand, RoCE, or iWARP.

question: What do you give up by using kernel bypass?

choices.1: The ability to use DMA at all
choices.2: Nothing; it is strictly better in every way
choices.3: All networking — the machine can no longer reach the network
choices.4: A burned core, kernel features, debuggability

explanation: Busy-polling is 100% CPU by design; you lose the firewall, tcpdump, routing, and the mature TCP stack; plus hardware lock-in and operational complexity — only worth it for genuinely latency-critical paths.

question: What is the governing idea of low-latency design?

choices.1: Maximize average throughput at any cost to variance
choices.2: Prioritize correctness over predictability on the hot path
choices.3: Always scale horizontally by adding more threads to the hot path
choices.4: Mechanical sympathy plus low variance

explanation: Latency isn't just being fast on average but having low variance, so you optimize the tail (p99.9), not the mean.

question: How should the hot path and cold path be separated?

choices.1: Both paths should share the same threads and code for simplicity
choices.2: Keep the hot path bare
choices.3: The cold path should be made as fast as the hot path
choices.4: Logging and stats should run inline on the hot path

explanation: The cold path (startup, logging, recovery, stats) prioritizes correctness and is kept out of the hot instruction-cache footprint and off the critical path. The per-event critical path. Allocation-free, branch-light, lock-free, cache-hot; push logging and stats to the cold path.

question: What is the recommended threading model for low latency?

choices.1: One thread handling all stages sequentially with blocking I/O
choices.2: Thread-per-core on isolated cores
choices.3: A thread pool that migrates threads across cores freely
choices.4: Many threads sharing mutable state behind a global mutex

explanation: One busy thread per critical stage pinned to a core isolated from the scheduler/IRQs, avoiding locks via single-writer designs and lock-free handoff (e.g. SPSC rings) between stages.

question: Why do latency-critical loops busy-poll instead of blocking?

choices.1: Blocking is impossible on modern hardware
choices.2: Spin-polling avoids the need for the NIC entirely
choices.3: Busy-polling uses less CPU than blocking
choices.4: Blocking adds wakeup and context-switch jitter

explanation: An interrupt or syscall wakeup costs microseconds of jitter, so spin-polling the NIC/queue trades a burned CPU core for predictable, low, jitter-free latency (throughput systems do the opposite to save CPU).

question: Why eliminate allocation and syscalls on the hot path?

choices.1: Allocation is free, so this is only about code style
choices.2: Object pools are slower than calling new/delete repeatedly
choices.3: Syscalls are faster than function calls on the hot path
choices.4: Both cause jitter and kernel transitions

explanation: Allocation means a lock, maybe a syscall, cache-cold memory, and tail spikes — so pre-allocate and reuse via pools/arenas; each syscall is a user/kernel transition, which for networking motivates kernel bypass.

question: Why warm everything up before the critical period (e.g. market open)?

choices.1: It permanently lowers the CPU's clock to reduce variance
choices.2: Cold caches and predictors hit event #1 hardest
choices.3: It reduces the total number of events processed
choices.4: Warming up is only needed for managed runtimes with a JIT

explanation: Un-faulted pages and first-call costs too — so warm up by touching memory, running the hot path with dummy data, pre-faulting pages, and priming caches, making event #1 as fast as event #1000.

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: What are tail latency and jitter?

choices.1: Tail latency is network bandwidth; jitter is packet loss
choices.2: Tail latency is the average response time; jitter is the median
choices.3: They both refer to the fastest 1% of requests
choices.4: Tail latency is how slow your slowest requests are

explanation: Jitter is the variability itself — the spread between the fast common case and the slow outliers. The p99, p99.9, p99.99 of the distribution, not the average.

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: Why does the mean latency 'lie'?

choices.1: Because the mean is always larger than the maximum
choices.2: Because the median always equals the mean for latency
choices.3: Latency distributions are right-skewed with a long tail
choices.4: Because averages are impossible to compute over latency

explanation: Most requests are fast and a few are very slow, so the rare slow ones drag the mean while hiding in it — you should report a distribution (p50/p90/p99/p99.9/max).

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: How does fan-out amplify tail latency?

choices.1: Fan-out averages out the tails, making them disappear
