choices.2: Fan-out reduces the tail because requests run in parallel
choices.3: The slowest of many parallel calls sets latency
choices.4: Fan-out only affects throughput, never latency

explanation: With 100 services each at p99=10ms, ~63% of top-level requests hit at least one slow service — a service's p99 becomes its caller's median.

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: What is coordinated omission in latency measurement?

choices.1: When two threads coordinate to skip the same request
choices.2: When the load generator sends too many requests at once
choices.3: A trap where naive benchmarks under-measure the tail
choices.4: When percentiles are averaged instead of merged

explanation: When the system stalls they stop sending requests during the stall, so the requests that would have been slow are never recorded; the fix is to measure against the intended schedule (e.g. wrk2 or HdrHistogram's CO correction).

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: Which are common sources of jitter (latency variance)?

choices.1: Only the programming language's syntax
choices.2: Only the number of CPU cores available
choices.3: GC pauses, preemption, and page faults
choices.4: Only network bandwidth limits

explanation: Also swapping, TLB misses, NUMA-remote access, context switches, CPU frequency scaling, interrupts and syscalls, lock contention, false sharing, network retransmits, head-of-line blocking, and cold starts.

source: C++/System Design/Low Latency/Tail Latency and Jitter.md
question: How should tail latency be measured properly?

choices.1: Use a low-resolution clock to reduce overhead
choices.2: Full histograms, merged — never averaged
choices.3: Average the p99 values reported by each shard
choices.4: Track only the average and the median

explanation: Record a full histogram (e.g. HdrHistogram) rather than a running mean, merge histograms across shards rather than averaging percentiles, use the highest-resolution clock (hardware NIC timestamps for tick-to-trade), and watch p99.9/p99.99 and max.

question: What is the distinction between ordering events and measuring real time across machines?

choices.1: Both always require GPS-disciplined hardware clocks
choices.2: Ordering needs physical sync, while measuring needs only logical clocks
choices.3: Ordering events across machines is solved with logical/vector clocks
choices.4: Neither requires any form of clock

explanation: Measuring real latency, timestamping for audit/regulation, and correlating logs need physical clocks agreeing to a tight bound. No physical sync needed.

question: What two sub-problems arise because machine clocks drift?

choices.1: Cardinality and sampling rate
choices.2: Latency and bandwidth
choices.3: Offset and drift/skew
choices.4: Jitter and packet loss

explanation: Offset is how far a clock is from the reference (corrected by stepping/slewing); drift/skew is the rate difference (corrected by continuously disciplining the frequency). Measuring offset requires estimating uncertain one-way network delay.

question: How do NTP and PTP differ in accuracy and mechanism?

choices.1: PTP works only over the internet while NTP works only on LANs
choices.2: NTP is far more accurate than PTP mainly because it uses hardware timestamps everywhere
choices.3: NTP uses software timestamps in the OS and achieves ~1-10 ms over the internet
choices.4: Both achieve identical nanosecond accuracy

explanation: PTP (IEEE 1588) uses hardware assistance on LANs to reach sub-microsecond to nanosecond accuracy with a GPS-disciplined grandmaster clock. Sub-ms on a good LAN.

question: Where does the big accuracy jump from NTP to PTP actually come from?

choices.1: From PTP using TCP instead of UDP
choices.2: From PTP using a faster cryptographic algorithm
choices.3: From PTP polling the time server more frequently
choices.4: From where the timestamp is taken

explanation: Hardware timestamping has the NIC stamp the packet at the wire (PHY), removing host-side software jitter (interrupts, scheduling, kernel-stack time) and giving nanosecond-grade determinism — not from the protocol math itself.

question: How is a high-precision local clock kept aligned to a reference, and why?

choices.1: GPS provides a PPS reference; the clock is disciplined, not stepped
choices.2: The reference is taken from the application's wall-clock reads
choices.3: The clock is stepped abruptly to the reference on every single update
choices.4: The local clock is left free-running and never corrected

explanation: A GPS/GNSS receiver provides UTC plus a pulse-per-second edge as the grandmaster reference, and the local clock is continuously frequency-adjusted toward it — stepping could make time go backwards, breaking ordering and measurement.

question: Why does high-frequency trading care about precise time synchronization?

choices.1: It does not; HFT ignores clock synchronization
choices.2: Latency measurement, audit, and regulation
choices.3: Only to display the correct local time to traders
choices.4: Only to schedule batch jobs at night

explanation: Tick-to-trade needs clocks agreeing to nanoseconds; sequencing/audit needs to reconstruct who saw what when; MiFID II RTS 25 mandates clock sync to UTC within tight bounds (e.g. 100 us); plus fairness/forensics.

question: What are the three pillars of observability?

choices.1: Dashboards, alerts, and runbooks
choices.2: CPU, memory, and disk
choices.3: Metrics, logs, and traces
choices.4: Latency, throughput, and availability

explanation: The path of one request across services. Aggregate numbers over time. Discrete event records.

question: How does observability differ from monitoring?

choices.1: Monitoring watches known dashboards/alerts for known problems
choices.2: Monitoring uses traces while observability uses only metrics
choices.3: They are identical; observability is just a newer word
choices.4: Observability watches known problems; monitoring finds unknown ones

explanation: 'is the thing I expected to break, broken?'); observability is about debugging the unknown-unknowns ('why is this request slow, given I never anticipated this failure?'.

question: Why is cardinality 'the enemy' for metrics?

choices.1: Because cardinality only affects logs, never metrics
choices.2: Because low cardinality makes dashboards slow
choices.3: Because metrics cannot store any labels at all
choices.4: Each unique label combination is a new time series

explanation: So high-cardinality labels (user_id, order_id) explode storage — those high-cardinality details belong in logs/traces, not metric labels.

question: What is a trace, and what does it make debuggable?

choices.1: A tree of timed spans for one request
choices.2: A trace is a single aggregated latency number per service
choices.3: A trace is a counter of total requests
choices.4: A trace is a log line with a timestamp

explanation: Spans (one per operation/service) linked by a propagated trace id localize which hop is slow, making distributed latency (tail amplification) debuggable.

question: What do the RED and USE methods measure?

choices.1: Both measure only error rates
choices.2: RED is for databases and USE is for caches
choices.3: RED measures resources; USE measures requests
choices.4: RED measures Rate, Errors, and Duration

explanation: The user-facing golden signals; USE (per resource) measures Utilization, Saturation, and Errors — to find a bottleneck resource like a CPU, disk, or connection pool. Per service, request-driven.

question: What is the relationship between SLI, SLO, SLA, and the error budget?

choices.1: The error budget equals the SLO itself
choices.2: SLIs are contracts with customers and SLAs are measured numbers
choices.3: SLA is the internal target and SLO is the external contract
choices.4: Measurement, target, contract, and slack

explanation: An SLI is a measured number (% of requests < 200ms), an SLO is the internal target, an SLA is the external contract with consequences; error budget = 100% - SLO, and you alert on SLO burn rather than every cause.

question: Why doesn't a multi-GB file fit the normal request/response model?

choices.1: Because HTTP cannot transfer binary data
choices.2: Because the database row limit is exceeded
choices.3: Memory, timeouts, bandwidth, and restart cost
choices.4: Because blob stores cannot hold files larger than 1 GB

explanation: It won't fit in RAM on the app server, a single HTTP request times out partway, streaming through your servers wastes bandwidth and CPU, and one network blip wastes the whole multi-GB transfer.

question: What is the central insight for handling large blobs?

choices.1: Stream every byte through the app server so it can validate them
choices.2: Don't move blob bytes through your application servers
choices.3: Store the entire blob inside the application database
choices.4: Compress the blob so it fits in a single request

explanation: Move them directly between the client and a blob store (S3/GCS/Azure), and let your servers handle only small metadata and permissions.

question: How does a presigned (pre-authorized) URL work for blob access?

choices.1: It is a permanent public URL anyone can use forever
choices.2: A temporary scoped capability to hit the store directly
choices.3: It routes the upload through the app server for validation
choices.4: It encrypts the blob so only the app can read it

explanation: The app server grants the client short-lived permission (pinned to key, content-type, max size, expiry) to talk to the blob store directly, so bytes never touch your servers while the app still controls who can upload/download what.

question: What does multipart upload provide for large files?

choices.1: Parallel part uploads with per-part retry
choices.2: It stores each part in a different database
choices.3: It uploads the whole file in one atomic request
choices.4: It compresses the file into a single part

explanation: Parts upload independently in any order, each acknowledged with an ETag, then stitched on 'complete' — giving throughput via parallelism, resumability (retry one failed part, not the whole 5 GB), and pausability across sessions.

question: How are large blob downloads accelerated and made resumable?

choices.1: By disabling resumption to keep transfers atomic
choices.2: HTTP Range requests let a client fetch specific byte ranges
choices.3: By converting the blob to text before sending
choices.4: By streaming the whole blob through the app server with no caching

explanation: So most downloads come from nearby rather than one central region. Enabling parallel/segmented downloads and resume-after-disconnect, and a CDN caches blobs at edge locations near users.

question: How is the metadata/blob split handled, and what is the two-phase consistency fix?

choices.1: The metadata is stored inside the blob itself, so they can't diverge
choices.2: The two systems are always kept strongly consistent with a distributed transaction
choices.3: The blob bytes live in the blob store while queryable metadata lives in your DB
choices.4: The blob store is queried directly for all metadata, with no database

explanation: Since they are two separate systems, a status field ('pending' -> 'available') with a finalize step handles divergence — a sweeper cleans up pending rows whose upload never completed, and a reconciliation job GCs orphaned blobs. Keyed by a blob key.

question: What is the fundamental move for handling long-running tasks?

choices.1: Running the work inside the request thread with a longer timeout
choices.2: Storing the task in memory and processing it on the next request
choices.3: Decoupling acceptance from execution
choices.4: Making the user wait synchronously for the work to finish

explanation: The server enqueues a job and immediately returns 202 Accepted with a job id, while background workers drain the queue asynchronously.

question: How does a visibility timeout keep job processing reliable?

choices.1: It locks the entire queue until the worker finishes
choices.2: It guarantees exactly-once delivery with no redelivery
choices.3: Pulled jobs hide until acked or expired
choices.4: It deletes the job immediately on pull so it can never be reprocessed

explanation: The broker makes a pulled job invisible for the timeout (two workers can't grab it); success acks and deletes it, while failure or expiry makes it visible again for redelivery.

question: What is the purpose of a dead-letter queue (DLQ)?

choices.1: It permanently deletes failed jobs to save space
choices.2: It is the main queue workers pull from
choices.3: After N failed attempts, a poison job is moved aside to a DLQ
choices.4: It stores all of the successfully completed jobs purely for auditing

explanation: E.g. bad input. So it doesn't retry infinitely and block the pipeline, while being preserved for inspection, manual replay, and alerting.

question: Why do retries demand idempotency?

choices.1: Because idempotency makes retries faster
choices.2: Because queues always guarantee exactly-once delivery, so retries never duplicate anything
choices.3: A retried job may be one that actually succeeded but failed to ack, so it runs again
choices.4: Because retries always run on a different worker that can't see prior state

explanation: Retries are only safe if the work is idempotent (running it twice equals running it once).

question: What is the crucial caveat about queue backpressure?

choices.1: Queues eliminate the need to ever scale workers
choices.2: Backpressure means the producers automatically slow down with no configuration
choices.3: A large enough queue can absorb any sustained overload indefinitely
choices.4: A queue absorbs temporary bursts, not a permanently higher load

explanation: If average arrival rate exceeds average drain rate, no queue saves you; you need more capacity (workers/shards) or less load (shedding/rate limiting).

question: How do you prevent slow bulk jobs from starving fast urgent ones?

choices.1: Increase the visibility timeout for bulk jobs
choices.2: Make all jobs the same priority to be fair
choices.3: Use separate queues per priority/class plus dedicated worker pools
choices.4: Use one single shared queue and always process strictly in arrival order

explanation: So a flood of slow bulk jobs can't block fast urgent ones (job-level head-of-line blocking).

question: What is the dual-write problem?

choices.1: Two services writing to the same database row concurrently
choices.2: Writing the same row twice in one transaction
choices.3: Writing to a primary and a replica that disagree
choices.4: DB write and event publish share no transaction

explanation: Writing the DB then publishing can lose the event on a crash; publishing then writing can emit a phantom event for a change that rolled back. No ordering of two independent writes is safe.

question: How does the transactional outbox pattern solve the dual-write problem?

choices.1: It stores events only in the broker, never the database
choices.2: The event commits with the business change
choices.3: It publishes the event first and rolls it back if the DB write fails
choices.4: It uses a distributed two-phase commit across the DB and broker

explanation: The event goes into an outbox table in the same database, in the same local transaction, so one atomic commit lands both or neither; a separate relay process then publishes the unsent rows to the broker.

question: What delivery guarantee does the outbox relay provide, and what does it require of consumers?

choices.1: Ordered delivery with no possibility of duplicates
choices.2: Exactly-once delivery, so consumers need no deduplication
choices.3: At-most-once delivery, so some events are silently dropped
choices.4: At-least-once; consumers dedupe

explanation: The relay can publish a row then crash before marking it sent, republishing later — consumers must be idempotent and dedupe by event id.

question: How does Change Data Capture (CDC) read changes compared to a polling relay?

choices.1: CDC is identical to polling but runs more frequently
choices.2: CDC queries the broker for missing events
choices.3: CDC writes events directly from the application with no database involvement
choices.4: It tails the WAL instead of polling

explanation: CDC reads the database's own replication log (WAL/binlog) and turns committed row-changes into a stream (e.g. Debezium to Kafka) — low latency, no app-side query load.

question: What is the key insight behind log-based CDC?

choices.1: The broker's log is the source of truth, not the database
choices.2: CDC only works with NoSQL databases
choices.3: The database's write-ahead log IS the event stream
choices.4: CDC requires rewriting all application queries

explanation: Log-based CDC can capture changes to business tables directly (sometimes removing the need for an explicit outbox) and syncs the DB to a search index, cache, or warehouse without dual writes.

question: Why not just use a distributed transaction (2PC) across the database and broker?

choices.1: Because brokers always support 2PC but databases do not
choices.2: Because 2PC cannot guarantee atomicity
choices.3: A distributed transaction / two-phase commit across the DB and broker is slow
choices.4: Because 2PC across a DB and broker is actually the recommended standard approach

explanation: Blocking, and usually unsupported by message brokers.

question: What does a durable execution engine let you do?

choices.1: Run stateless request/response handlers faster
choices.2: Write a long-running, multi-step process as ordinary code
choices.3: Define a process as a declarative JSON state machine run by a managed service
choices.4: Store domain events that you manually fold into state

explanation: Loops, conditionals, function calls) while the engine guarantees it survives crashes, restarts, and failures and resumes exactly where it left off (e.g. Temporal, Azure Durable Functions.

question: How does a durable execution engine resume a process after a crash?

choices.1: It replays the code, feeding back recorded results
choices.2: It checkpoints the entire process memory to disk and reloads it
choices.3: It keeps the process in memory and never needs to resume
choices.4: It restarts the whole process from scratch, redoing all work

explanation: The workflow function re-runs from the top on another worker, but each completed step's result is fed back from a durable event history (memoization), fast-forwarding to the first step with no recorded result.

question: Why must durable-execution workflow code be deterministic?

choices.1: Because non-deterministic code cannot be compiled
choices.2: Replay must reproduce identical decisions
choices.3: Because deterministic code runs faster
choices.4: Because the engine only supports pure functions for performance

explanation: The engine re-runs workflow code to rebuild state from history, so the code must decide the same given the same history — no now()/random()/uuid() or direct I/O inside workflow code; non-determinism goes into activities (whose results are recorded) or engine-provided deterministic APIs.

question: Which capability is a hallmark of durable execution engines?

choices.1: Eliminating the need for any datastore
choices.2: Guaranteed sub-microsecond latency on every step
choices.3: Running arbitrary non-deterministic code in the workflow body
choices.4: Durable timers surviving restarts

explanation: 'Sleep for 30 days' is one line the engine persists and wakes even after deploys, holding no thread — plus automatic retries, signals/queries, and Saga-style compensation.

question: What is a notable drawback of durable execution engines?

choices.1: They require no infrastructure at all
choices.2: They cannot run processes longer than a few seconds
choices.3: Versioning mid-flight executions breaks replay
choices.4: They cannot retry failed steps

explanation: Changing workflow code while executions are in flight can make the new code produce a different history; plus the determinism constraint trips up newcomers, and self-hosting means running a stateful cluster.

question: Where does a durable execution engine sit among the alternatives?

choices.1: It is a simple cron job with retries
choices.2: It is the 'workflow as code' point on the spectrum
choices.3: It is the lowest-code, fully-managed option with the least flexibility
choices.4: It is a pattern, not a runtime, where you fold events yourself

explanation: More power and expressiveness than a declarative workflow DSL, but more structure and reliability guarantees than rolling your own with event sourcing.

question: What is the core idea of event sourcing?

choices.1: Caching the current state in memory for fast reads
choices.2: Storing only the latest value and overwriting it on each change
choices.3: Store the events, derive the state
choices.4: Storing events in a broker and discarding them after delivery

explanation: The full sequence of immutable events is the source of truth; current state is a left-fold (replay) — append-only, never updating or deleting in place.

question: How do events differ from commands in event sourcing?

choices.1: Commands are stored append-only; events are mutable
choices.2: They are the same thing with different names
choices.3: Events are requests that may fail; commands are immutable facts
choices.4: Happened versus requested

explanation: An event is an immutable past fact (OrderPlaced); a command is an intent that may still fail (PlaceOrder).

question: What are snapshots used for in event sourcing?

choices.1: Snapshots replace the event log as the new source of truth
choices.2: Snapshots store commands rather than state
choices.3: Snapshots are immutable events that can never be rebuilt
choices.4: Replaying thousands of events on every read is slow

explanation: So you periodically persist the folded state and only replay events since the last snapshot; the events remain the source of truth and snapshots are a disposable performance optimization.

question: Why does event sourcing pair with CQRS and projections?

choices.1: Folds over events are awkward to query directly
choices.2: Because CQRS stores events while event sourcing stores current state
choices.3: Because projections replace the need for an event store
choices.4: Because CQRS makes reads strongly consistent with writes

explanation: So you separate the write model (append events) from read models: subscribers consume the stream and maintain read-optimized projections (SQL tables, search indexes, caches); new projections can be built later by replaying history.

question: What is a key benefit of event sourcing?

choices.1: A complete audit trail — every change is a stored fact
choices.2: Strong read-after-write consistency everywhere
choices.3: Easy deletion of data to satisfy GDPR erasure
choices.4: Much simpler querying than any traditional CRUD database

explanation: So 'how did we get here?' is answerable by construction — plus time travel (reconstruct any past state by replaying events) and the ability to build new read models by replaying history.

question: What is a significant drawback of event sourcing?

choices.1: It makes simple CRUD apps simpler
choices.2: Event schema evolution is painful
choices.3: It loses historical information on every write
choices.4: It cannot support audit trails

explanation: Because events are immutable and live forever, so changing their shape needs versioning/upcasting of old events; querying is also hard (needs projections, introducing eventual consistency between write and read sides).

question: What is a managed workflow system?

choices.1: A pattern for storing domain events and folding them into state
choices.2: A library you embed to run workflows as ordinary code in your process
choices.3: A managed service running declarative flows
choices.4: A message queue with workers

explanation: You define the multi-step process as a graph/state machine in JSON/YAML/DSL and the service executes it, handling retries, state, and progress visibility (AWS Step Functions, Airflow, Argo).

question: What is the defining split in a managed workflow system?

choices.1: The split between read models and write models
choices.2: The split between the database and the message broker
choices.3: The split between definition and execution
choices.4: The split between the client and the server

explanation: The process is data, not code. The managed service owns durable state between steps, dispatching, retries, scheduling, and a visual run history. What you author: the steps, order, branching, retry/timeout policy, error handling.

question: What are the two common flavors of managed workflow systems?

choices.1: Push-based and pull-based message queues
choices.2: State machines and DAG schedulers
choices.3: Synchronous and asynchronous request handlers
choices.4: Relational and NoSQL databases

explanation: State-machine/orchestration systems (Step Functions, Cloud Workflows) for event-driven per-request business processes; DAG schedulers (Airflow, Argo, Dagster) for scheduled data pipelines and batch jobs.

question: What is a key benefit of a managed workflow system?

choices.1: The lowest possible cost at very high volume
choices.2: No stateful cluster to run or patch
choices.3: Maximum expressiveness for complex dynamic logic
choices.4: No vendor lock-in, since definitions are fully portable

explanation: Plus fast development via declarative definitions and prebuilt connectors, built-in reliability, and an excellent visual run history.

question: What is the main drawback of managed workflow systems?

choices.1: They have no visibility into running executions
choices.2: They require you to run and scale a stateful cluster yourself
choices.3: DSL clumsiness, lock-in, per-transition cost
choices.4: They cannot retry failed steps or persist state

explanation: A JSON/DSL state machine is awkward for complex logic, loops, and dynamic behavior compared to real code — exactly where durable-execution 'workflow as code' wins.

question: Where does a managed workflow system sit relative to durable execution engines and event sourcing?

choices.1: It is the 'low-code, fully-managed' end
choices.2: It is identical to event sourcing
choices.3: It offers the most control and expressiveness of the three
choices.4: It is a pattern with no runtime, where you fold events yourself

explanation: Least operational burden and least code, but least flexible and most lock-in; when the logic outgrows the declarative DSL, you move to a durable execution engine (workflow as code).

question: How does the fixed window counter rate-limiting algorithm work, and what is its main flaw?

choices.1: It counts requests in a fixed interval
choices.2: It leaks requests at a constant rate to smooth traffic
choices.3: It fills a bucket with tokens at a steady rate
choices.4: It keeps a timestamp of every request and is perfectly precise

explanation: It's simple, fast, and low-memory but allows boundary bursts — a client can send 100 at 12:00:59 and another 100 at 12:01:00, i.e. 200 in ~2 seconds. E.g. 100/minute. Resetting the counter at each boundary.

question: What are the trade-offs of the sliding window log algorithm?

choices.1: It is the lowest-memory algorithm but allows boundary bursts
choices.2: It approximates the window by weighting the previous window's count
choices.3: It allows controlled bursts via accumulated tokens
choices.4: Exact but one timestamp per request

explanation: It keeps every request's timestamp, drops those older than the window, and counts the rest — accurate and burst-free, but memory-hungry and more expensive for high-traffic users.

question: How does the sliding window counter approximate a true sliding window?

choices.1: Weighted blend of current and previous windows
choices.2: It stores every request timestamp for perfect accuracy
choices.3: It resets the counter abruptly at each window boundary
choices.4: It leaks tokens at a constant rate

explanation: It combines the current fixed-window count with the previous window's count weighted by the fraction of the window not yet elapsed — much less memory than the log and smoother than the fixed window, but only approximate.

question: How does the token bucket algorithm work?

choices.1: It stores a timestamp per request and counts the recent ones
choices.2: A bucket fills with tokens at a steady rate up to a maximum capacity
choices.3: Requests enter a queue and are processed at a fixed leak rate
choices.4: It counts requests per fixed interval and simply resets at boundaries

explanation: Each request consumes a token, so accumulated tokens allow controlled bursts while enforcing a smooth long-term average rate.

question: What does the leaky bucket algorithm achieve?

choices.1: It resets a counter at fixed window boundaries
choices.2: It allows large bursts by accumulating tokens
choices.3: Requests enter a bucket that leaks at a constant rate
choices.4: It stores every single request timestamp for precision

explanation: But queueing introduces latency. Or is processed. Smoothing outgoing traffic to a steady pace and protecting downstream systems from bursts.

question: What is the key difference between token bucket and leaky bucket?

choices.1: They are identical in behavior
choices.2: Token bucket smooths output; leaky bucket allows bursts
choices.3: Token bucket stores every timestamp; leaky bucket uses a counter
choices.4: Token bucket controls the average rate while allowing bursts

explanation: Leaky bucket controls the output rate by smoothing requests so they leave at a steady pace — token bucket suits APIs tolerating bursts, leaky bucket suits downstreams needing steady flow. Save up tokens, spend them quickly later.

question: What core problem does a CDN solve?

choices.1: Physical distance means delay
choices.2: Databases are too slow for read-heavy workloads
choices.3: Encryption is too slow over long distances
choices.4: Servers run out of memory under load

explanation: Without a CDN every user fetches from a distant origin, giving higher latency, heavy origin load during spikes, higher bandwidth cost, and more DDoS exposure. The internet is physically large; a CDN moves content near users.

question: What is the basic idea of a CDN?

choices.1: A faster protocol that replaces HTTP
choices.2: A single powerful origin server that handles all global traffic
choices.3: Edge servers caching near users
choices.4: A database replicated to every user's device

explanation: A geographically distributed cache: users are routed to a nearby edge server instead of always reaching the origin.

question: What is the difference between a CDN cache hit and a cache miss?

choices.1: A miss permanently disables caching for that file
choices.2: On a hit the edge contacts the origin; on a miss it serves from cache
choices.3: A hit skips the origin; a miss fetches from it
choices.4: Both hit and miss always contact the origin

explanation: On a miss the edge fetches from the origin, returns it to the user, and usually stores a copy so the next request is a hit.

question: How are users routed to a nearby CDN edge server?

choices.1: DNS, Anycast, or CDN routing logic
choices.2: By the origin redirecting every request after processing it
choices.3: By the user manually selecting the closest server
choices.4: By broadcasting the request to all edges and taking the first reply

explanation: The pick weighs geographic/network proximity, health, and ability to serve the content.

question: What content are CDNs especially good at caching, and how do they help dynamic content?

choices.1: They excel at static content (images, video, CSS, JS, fonts, downloads)
choices.2: They only cache database query results
choices.3: They cannot serve any dynamic content at all
choices.4: They cache absolutely everything including per-user dynamic pages perfectly

explanation: For dynamic content they may not fully cache the response but still help via faster routing, TLS termination, compression, HTTP/2 or HTTP/3, and edge compute.

question: How does a CDN act as a shield for the origin?

choices.1: It encrypts the origin's disk to prevent data theft
choices.2: Traffic flows through the CDN before reaching the origin
choices.3: It exposes the origin's IP directly to all clients for speed
choices.4: It replaces the origin entirely so it can never be attacked

explanation: So the CDN can block or absorb DDoS attacks, bad bots, suspicious traffic, excessive request rates, and some web exploits (via WAF features), reducing unwanted load on the origin.

question: How does regional partitioning differ from plain replication?

choices.1: Replication gives every region a copy of ALL the data (for read scaling/failover)
choices.2: Replication splits data by region while partitioning copies everything
choices.3: Regional partitioning caches only static content; replication serves dynamic content
choices.4: They are the same; both copy all data everywhere

explanation: Regional partitioning has each region OWN a slice of the data (the users who belong to it) and run the full application near them.

question: What is a 'home region' in regional partitioning?

choices.1: A backup region used only during failover
choices.2: The region where the CDN edge is located
choices.3: The region responsible for a given piece of data
choices.4: The region with the most servers, which owns all data

explanation: So a European user's data lives in Europe and most requests stay entirely within one region (short paths, low latency, the local DB is the source of truth).

question: How are users routed to the correct region?

choices.1: The database decides the region after processing the query
choices.2: GeoDNS, Anycast, or an edge routing layer
choices.3: All requests go to one central region that forwards them
choices.4: Users manually pick a region at login

explanation: DNS can answer by resolver location; Anycast announces the same IP from many regions with BGP delivering to the nearest; or an edge layer forwards to a user's home region.

question: What is the usual pragmatic consistency design across regions?

choices.1: All regions accept writes independently with no reconciliation
choices.2: Reads always go cross-region to the home region for every request
choices.3: Home-region writes, async copies elsewhere
choices.4: Every write uses synchronous multi-region consensus for strong consistency everywhere

explanation: Each user is pinned to a home region (fast, strongly consistent locally, read-your-writes); other regions get asynchronous, eventually-consistent copies for reads.

question: Besides latency, what is a major driver of regional partitioning?

choices.1: Reducing the number of servers needed
choices.2: Eliminating the need for a CDN
choices.3: Making all data globally readable with no restrictions
choices.4: Data residency / compliance

explanation: Regulations like GDPR require citizens' data to physically reside within geographic borders, which regional partitioning makes natural since the EU region owns EU users' data.

question: How do CDN, replication, and regional partitioning relate?

choices.1: They are three names for the same technique
choices.2: A CDN handles writes while regional partitioning handles static assets
choices.3: A CDN caches static content near users (no app logic or writes)
choices.4: Regional partitioning replaces the need for both CDN and replication

explanation: Replication gives every node all data for read-scaling/failover; regional partitioning has each region own a slice of user data and run the full app — the real fix for dynamic, write-heavy workloads near users, and they stack together.

question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

choices.1: They are the same thing with different names
choices.2: Authentication enforces permissions; authorization proves identity
choices.3: Authentication (authN) is 'who are you?' — proving identity
choices.4: Authorization always comes before authentication

explanation: Authorization (authZ) is 'what are you allowed to do?' — enforcing permissions on a proven identity; authN comes first and authZ acts on its result (401 = not authenticated, 403 = not authorized).

question: What is the trade-off between stateful sessions and stateless JWT tokens?

choices.1: Server-side record versus signed claims
choices.2: They are functionally identical
choices.3: JWTs are easy to revoke while sessions cannot be revoked
choices.4: Sessions are stateless and JWTs are stateful

explanation: Easy to revoke but requiring a lookup every request and a shared store; JWTs are signed, self-contained tokens any service verifies locally without a lookup (scaling horizontally) but are hard to revoke before expiry (mitigated by short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens). Sessions store a server-side record with a random cookie id (easy revocation, needs a lookup); JWTs are self-contained signed claims (no lookup, hard to revoke before expiry).

question: What is a key caveat about JWT payloads?

choices.1: Don't put secrets in the payload
choices.2: The payload is encrypted, so secrets are safe inside it
choices.3: The signature encrypts the payload for confidentiality
choices.4: JWTs cannot carry any claims like roles or expiry

explanation: It is base64-encoded and only signed, not encrypted, so anyone can read it; the signature only proves integrity, and the token should always travel over TLS.

question: What is the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC)?

choices.1: OIDC is an older version of OAuth2
choices.2: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework for delegated access
choices.3: They are competing, unrelated protocols
choices.4: OAuth2 proves user identity while OIDC grants delegated access

explanation: It is NOT about identity; OIDC is an identity layer on top of OAuth2 that adds an ID token (a JWT) saying who the user is — what 'Log in with Google' uses. Letting a user grant App A limited access to their data on Service B without sharing their password. Issuing an access token.

question: How do RBAC and ABAC authorization models differ?

choices.1: RBAC keys on roles; ABAC on attribute policies
choices.2: ABAC is simpler and coarser than RBAC
choices.3: RBAC is attribute-based; ABAC is role-based
choices.4: Both simply grant every user full access by default

explanation: RBAC attaches permissions to roles and gives users roles (admin/editor/viewer) — simple and coarse. ABAC evaluates policies over attributes (user department, resource owner, time, location) — fine-grained and flexible but more complex.

question: What are the roles of access, refresh, and ID tokens?

choices.1: Authorize, renew, and identify
choices.2: All three are identity tokens used interchangeably
choices.3: The refresh token is short-lived and the access token is long-lived
choices.4: The access token mints refresh tokens and the ID token is sent to APIs

explanation: The access token authorizes API calls (short-lived), the refresh token mints new access tokens, the OIDC ID token carries the user's identity for the client.

question: Why are cross-shard queries (scatter-gather) problematic?

choices.1: They are impossible, so sharded systems forbid all queries
choices.2: They only affect writes, never reads
choices.3: The slowest shard gates the whole query
choices.4: They are faster than single-shard queries because they parallelize

explanation: A scatter-gather must fan out to every relevant shard and merge results, so one slow or failed shard degrades everything — JOINs and aggregations across shards are especially costly.

question: Why do distributed transactions across shards lose single-database ACID guarantees, and what's the common alternative?

choices.1: Sharded systems automatically provide complete full ACID across all shards for free
choices.2: The fix is to never write to a sharded database
choices.3: A transaction writing rows on different shards can't rely on one database's ACID
choices.4: Cross-shard transactions are faster than single-shard ones

explanation: Maintaining atomicity needs two-phase commit (slow, blocking, coordinator-dependent), so many sharded systems instead use the Saga pattern — local transactions with compensating actions — accepting eventual consistency.

question: Why is rebalancing/resharding hard, and how is it mitigated?

choices.1: Naive hash % N remaps nearly all keys on reshard
choices.2: There is no way to rebalance a sharded system
choices.3: Rebalancing is instant and requires no data movement
choices.4: Resharding only moves a single key at a time

explanation: That means a huge risky migration that must happen online. Mitigations: consistent hashing (only ~1/N keys move), directory-based mapping (move data then update the map), and over-provisioning logical shards up front.

question: Why are global constraints like uniqueness hard in a sharded system?

choices.1: Uniqueness is checked globally for free by every shard
choices.2: Sharding makes constraints easier, not harder
choices.3: Uniqueness and auto-increment can only be checked within a shard
choices.4: The database enforces all global constraints transparently across shards

explanation: So global uniqueness needs a distributed ID scheme (UUIDs, Snowflake IDs, or a central allocator); foreign keys across shards aren't enforced by the database (integrity moves to the app); and secondary indexes must be local (scatter-gather to query) or global (a separate sharded structure).

question: What causes hotspots even with many shards?

choices.1: Hotspots are caused by too many distinct key values
choices.2: Hotspots only occur with exactly one shard
choices.3: Even data distribution always prevents any hotspot
choices.4: Data skew, access skew, sequential keys

explanation: One range or tenant can hold far more rows than others; a 'celebrity' key can get disproportionate traffic regardless of even data spread; and a sequential shard key sends all new writes to one shard.

question: What is the golden rule of sharding?

choices.1: Always shard on a sequential/auto-increment key
choices.2: Spread every entity's data across all shards for balance
choices.3: One request, one shard
choices.4: Use as many shards as possible regardless of the key

explanation: Pick a shard key that keeps the data a single request needs together — cross-shard operations are where the pain lives.

question: What is the difference between native and application-level sharding?

choices.1: Native sharding requires the app to pick the shard manually
choices.2: They are identical approaches
choices.3: Who routes: the app versus the database
choices.4: Application-level sharding hides the shards from the app

explanation: Application-level sharding has the app or a middleware proxy (Vitess, Citus) decide the shard — maximum control and complexity; native sharding has the database manage partitioning, routing, and rebalancing so the app talks to a logical endpoint.

question: Why does sharding terminology vary across systems?

choices.1: Because only relational databases use the word 'shard'
choices.2: Different names, same idea
choices.3: Because each term means a fundamentally different mechanism
choices.4: Because the terms refer to replication, not sharding

explanation: Shard, partition, vnode, region, tablet, chunk — all mean splitting data by a key across nodes.

question: How do Cassandra and ScyllaDB approach sharding?

choices.1: They use a central coordinator that assigns each row to a shard
choices.2: They are built around consistent hashing
choices.3: They store all data on one master node with read replicas
choices.4: They require manual range-boundary configuration per shard

explanation: The partition key is hashed onto a token ring, each node owns ranges of tokens subdivided into virtual nodes for even distribution, and they are masterless/peer-to-peer so adding a node automatically reassigns a fraction of the ring — sharding and replication are unified.

question: How do NewSQL systems (Spanner, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB) shard?

choices.1: They avoid sharding entirely and run on a single node
choices.2: Auto-split key ranges replicated via consensus
choices.3: They forbid cross-shard transactions like NoSQL stores
choices.4: They shard manually via application code only

explanation: Data is split into ranges/tablets by primary-key ranges, automatically split and moved as they grow or get hot, with each range replicated via Paxos/Raft and first-class distributed ACID transactions (Spanner uses TrueTime; CockroachDB hybrid logical clocks).

question: How does Redis Cluster shard data?

choices.1: It hashes keys onto a token ring with virtual nodes
choices.2: It stores all keys on one node with no partitioning
choices.3: It uses a fixed set of 16384 hash slots
choices.4: It splits tables by primary-key ranges into tablets

explanation: Resharding moves slots (and their keys) between nodes live, and hash tags {...} force related keys into the same slot for multi-key operations. Mapping each key to a slot via CRC16(key) % 16384 and distributing slots across nodes.

question: What is the industry trend in database sharding?

choices.1: Toward eliminating sharding in favor of single huge servers
choices.2: Toward transparent, automatic sharding
choices.3: Toward fully manual application-level sharding everywhere
choices.4: Toward exposing all physical shard details to the application

explanation: Pushing partitioning, rebalancing, and even distributed transactions into the database so applications can scale without re-implementing the hard parts by hand.

question: How does sharding differ from replication?

choices.1: Replication copies the whole dataset to multiple nodes
choices.2: Sharding copies all data everywhere; replication splits it
choices.3: Replication is for writes and sharding is for reads
choices.4: They are the same technique

explanation: Sharding splits different data onto different nodes (for write/storage scaling) — they are complementary and usually combined, with each shard itself replicated. For availability/read scaling.

question: What are the trade-offs of range-based sharding?

choices.1: It distributes keys perfectly evenly and never has hotspots
choices.2: It makes all range queries expensive and scatter across many shards
choices.3: It requires no choice of shard key
choices.4: Rows are assigned to shards by contiguous ranges of the shard key

explanation: Excellent range queries and easy splitting of a hot range — but sequential keys (timestamps, auto-increment IDs) send all new writes to the last shard (hotspots) and ranges can be skewed/unbalanced.

question: What are the strengths and weaknesses of hash-based (mod N) sharding?

choices.1: Even distribution but painful ranges and resharding
choices.2: It excels at range queries but distributes unevenly
choices.3: It keeps logically adjacent data physically together
choices.4: It makes resharding trivial with no key movement

explanation: Hashing gives even spread (even with sequential keys) and fast deterministic routing — but range queries scatter across all shards, and changing N remaps almost every key, forcing a massive migration.

question: What is the trade-off of directory-based (lookup) sharding?

choices.1: It requires no lookup and routes by pure hashing
choices.2: A lookup service maps each shard key to a shard
choices.3: It cannot be rebalanced once set up
choices.4: It eliminates all latency from routing

explanation: Maximum flexibility (any key can map to any shard, rebalancing is easy — move data then update the map) — but the directory is a single point of failure and bottleneck that must be replicated/cached, and it adds a lookup hop per request.

question: What are the benefits and risks of geo/entity-based sharding?

choices.1: It scatters related data across all shards
choices.2: It is the same as hash-based sharding
choices.3: Partitioning along a business dimension
choices.4: It guarantees perfectly even distribution across shards

explanation: Region, tenant, customer) gives data locality (related rows co-located, avoiding cross-shard joins) and supports data residency/compliance — but a single large tenant or popular region can overwhelm one shard (the noisy-neighbor problem.

question: What makes a good shard key?

choices.1: High cardinality, even access, matching query patterns
choices.2: A sequential auto-increment value for predictable ordering
choices.3: Any column at random, since it can easily be changed later
choices.4: Low cardinality so keys group together

explanation: Many distinct values spread data finely, even access avoids hotspots, and matching query patterns keeps most queries on one shard — while avoiding monotonic values like timestamps for hash routing. It's the single most important, hard-to-change decision.

question: What is the FIX protocol, and what are its two layers?

choices.1: A proprietary multicast market-data feed format
choices.2: The standard order-entry protocol; session plus application layers
choices.3: A consensus protocol for replicating executed trades across exchanges
choices.4: A binary RPC framework for microservices

explanation: The session layer is a reliable, ordered, recoverable conversation over TCP (sequence numbers, heartbeats, resend); the application layer carries business messages (new order, cancel, execution report).

question: What is the FIX wire format, and what is its downside?

choices.1: A JSON document over HTTP/2
choices.2: SOH-separated tag=value pairs
choices.3: A fixed-layout binary struct parsed by overlay, very fast
choices.4: A compressed columnar format optimized for analytics

explanation: It is self-describing (every field tagged), human-readable, and flexible — but verbose on the wire and slow to parse (string scanning, integer parsing, no fixed layout). Flat ASCII fields — flexible but slow to parse.

question: How does the FIX session layer provide reliable delivery over TCP?

choices.1: It uses UDP multicast with no recovery
choices.2: It relies purely on TCP with no extra reliability
choices.3: It re-sends every message continuously regardless of acknowledgment
choices.4: Per-direction sequence numbers with resend

explanation: Every message carries a MsgSeqNum, heartbeats prove liveness when idle, and a ResendRequest replays missed messages — after a drop both sides reconcile by sequence number with no loss or duplication (persisting sent messages is mandatory to answer a resend).

question: What is the role of the ExecutionReport (35=8) in the FIX order lifecycle?

choices.1: It reports every order state transition
choices.2: It carries only market-data price updates
choices.3: It is the message a client sends to place a new order
choices.4: It authenticates the session at logon

explanation: New/acked, partially filled, filled, canceled, rejected, replaced — with ClOrdID (tag 11) tying acks and fills back to the originating order as the client's idempotency key.

question: Why do HFT firms often move off classic FIX for latency-critical order entry?

choices.1: Because FIX text is faster than binary on the hot path
choices.2: Because binary protocols are easier to debug with curl
choices.3: Tag=value text parses too slowly on the hot path
choices.4: Because FIX cannot represent orders at all

explanation: Classic FIX is ubiquitous and self-describing (great for integration) but verbose; exchanges offer binary protocols (FIX SBE/FAST or native binary) with fixed layouts parsed by struct overlay — orders of magnitude faster to encode/decode.

question: When HFT swaps FIX text for a binary encoding, what typically survives?

choices.1: Everything changes, including the session model
choices.2: The session concepts usually survive
choices.3: Binary encodings remove the need for any session layer
choices.4: The text encoding survives but sequence numbers are dropped

explanation: Only the encoding changes from self-describing text to packed binary; HFT order entry uses binary while back-office, allocations, and cross-firm integration stay on FIX. Sequence numbers, heartbeats, resend.

question: Why are market data feeds pushed over UDP multicast rather than TCP?

choices.1: One multicast stream fans out to all subscribers
choices.2: UDP guarantees ordered, reliable delivery that TCP lacks
choices.3: TCP cannot carry binary market data
choices.4: UDP multicast retransmits lost packets automatically on the hot path

explanation: The network fabric replicates the stream to thousands of subscribers simultaneously with minimal latency; TCP would serialize the same data per subscriber and add retransmit/ordering head-of-line blocking — but the app must then handle UDP's drops/reorders itself.

question: How does a feed handler detect packet loss?

choices.1: It compares checksums on every packet
choices.2: Every message carries a monotonically increasing sequence number
choices.3: It simply waits for the exchange to send an explicit 'loss' notification
choices.4: It assumes no loss occurs over multicast

explanation: The handler tracks the next expected value, and a gap (e.g. receiving 1004 when expecting 1002) means dropped packets — it must NOT apply the ahead-of-gap message (the book would be wrong), so it buffers and triggers recovery.

question: What is A/B line arbitration?

choices.1: Two redundant feeds; first packet per sequence wins
choices.2: It encrypts the feed twice for security
choices.3: It compresses the stream into two halves
choices.4: It load-balances orders across two matching engines

explanation: Exchanges publish the same sequenced stream over two independent network paths (A and B); the handler listens to both and takes whichever arrives first per sequence number, so a drop on one line is covered with zero recovery latency.

question: How does a feed handler recover when a real gap occurs on both lines?

choices.1: It switches to a TCP connection for all future data
choices.2: It ignores the gap and continues applying messages
choices.3: Buffer live data and re-sync from a snapshot
choices.4: It restarts the entire trading system from scratch

explanation: It keeps buffering live incrementals, obtains a snapshot tagged with the sequence number it's valid as of (or replayed messages up to seq S), discards buffered incrementals <= S, applies those > S, and resumes the live feed in sync.

question: What is conflation in a market data feed, and when is it acceptable?

choices.1: Collapsing updates for slow consumers
choices.2: Conflation is applied on the hot path to speed up matching
choices.3: Conflation merges two exchanges' feeds into one and is always safe
choices.4: Conflation deduplicates the A and B lines

explanation: Multiple updates to the same price level collapse into the latest value (state, not history) — acceptable only for GUIs and slow strategies, never on the trading hot path.

question: Which techniques minimize a feed handler's latency?

choices.1: Allocating new book structures on every parse
choices.2: Kernel bypass, busy-polling, NIC timestamps
choices.3: Routing all packets through the kernel TCP stack
choices.4: Blocking on interrupts to save CPU on the hot path

explanation: Solarflare/Onload or DPDK receive multicast straight into user space; busy-poll the NIC; take hardware timestamps on ingress; pre-allocate cache-aligned book structures; and measure with percentiles rather than means.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What is an order book and a matching engine?

choices.1: An order book is a log of completed trades; the matching engine compresses it
choices.2: Resting orders by price; the matcher trades them
choices.3: An order book is a relational database table; the matching engine runs SQL queries
choices.4: They are two names for the market data feed

explanation: The book holds all resting bids and asks organized by price; the matching engine takes incoming orders, matches against the book, generates trades, and publishes results — a single-threaded, in-memory, deterministic state machine.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What are the best bid, best ask, and spread?

choices.1: The mid is the most recently traded price
choices.2: Highest buy, lowest sell, and the gap between
choices.3: The best bid is the lowest buy and the best ask the highest sell
choices.4: The spread is the total quantity available in the book

explanation: Top of book; the midpoint is the mid. A marketable order crosses the spread and trades immediately; a passive/resting order provides liquidity.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: How does price-time (FIFO) priority determine matching?

choices.1: The most recently placed order trades first
choices.2: Best price first, then earliest at that price
choices.3: The largest order at any price always trades first
choices.4: Orders trade in random order to be fair

explanation: Price priority comes first, then a time tiebreak within a level — rewarding being early, which drives the latency race. Pro-rata instead splits fills proportionally to order size.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: Why must the cancel operation be O(1) in a matching engine?

choices.1: Cancels dominate real HFT order flow
choices.2: Because the exchange charges per cancel
choices.3: Because cancels must be slower than adds for fairness
choices.4: Because cancels are rare and only happen at end of day

explanation: The hot-path operations are add, cancel, and match, and HFT cancels the vast majority of orders — so cancel must be O(1), achieved via an order_id -> Order* hash map plus intrusive FIFO lists for O(1) unlink.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: What is the price-ladder (price-indexed array) data structure trick?

choices.1: Keeping levels in a balanced binary search tree for O(log n) access
choices.2: Storing the book on disk and memory-mapping it
choices.3: Index levels directly by price over tick size
choices.4: Storing all price levels in a hash table keyed by order id

explanation: For bounded tick ranges, a flat array indexed by (price / tick_size) gives O(1), branch-light, cache-hot access, with best-bid/ask pointers scanned to the next non-empty level on depletion — the classic HFT approach.

source: C++/System Design/Trading/Order Books and Matching Engines.md
question: How is a matching engine made crash-recoverable and scalable?

choices.1: By using a relational database with ACID transactions for each match
choices.2: By multithreading a single book across many cores with locks
choices.3: By writing the full book state to disk after every order
choices.4: Journal the inputs, replay to rebuild, shard instruments

explanation: The book lives in RAM; persistence is an append-only journal of the input event stream (not book state), so replay rebuilds the book after a crash. Scale out by sharding instruments across engines and replicate the deterministic state machine for HA.

question: What is Memcached, and what is it deliberately NOT?

choices.1: A relational database with SQL support
choices.2: A message broker with durable queues
choices.3: A persistent database with rich data structures and replication
choices.4: A high-performance, distributed, multi-threaded in-memory cache

explanation: And only a cache: a pure volatile key-to-opaque-bytes store with no persistence, no replication, and no data structures beyond blobs.

question: How does Memcached's threading model differ from Redis's?

choices.1: Both are single-threaded for command execution
choices.2: Memcached is single-threaded while Redis is multi-threaded
choices.3: Memcached uses one thread per key
choices.4: Memcached is multi-threaded

explanation: Scaling across CPU cores within one node for high throughput on big multi-core boxes, unlike Redis whose command execution is single-threaded.

question: What is Memcached's slab allocator, and its downside?

choices.1: Fixed-size slab classes; waste via calcification
choices.2: It compresses values to fit any size, with no waste
choices.3: It allocates exact-size blocks per value, causing fragmentation
choices.4: It stores values on disk to avoid memory limits

explanation: Memory is pre-carved into slab classes to avoid fragmentation, values placed in the smallest class that fits. Downside: a value slightly larger than a class wastes the remainder, and memory assigned to one class is hard to reassign.

question: How is data distributed across Memcached nodes?

choices.1: A master node routes every request to the right shard
choices.2: Nodes gossip to agree on key ownership
choices.3: Memcached nodes don't know about each other
choices.4: All nodes hold a full replica of every key

explanation: The client library shards keys across nodes, ideally using consistent hashing so adding/removing a node only remaps ~1/N of keys instead of flushing the whole cache.

question: What happens to data in Memcached on a restart?

choices.1: Data is restored from an append-only log
choices.2: Data is replicated from a peer node
choices.3: Data is restored from a periodic on-disk snapshot file
choices.4: Everything is lost — Memcached has no persistence

explanation: It is purely a volatile cache, with LRU eviction per slab class when memory is full.

question: When should you pick Memcached over Redis?

choices.1: When you need persistence and replication out of the box
choices.2: When you need a built-in clustering protocol
choices.3: When you need sorted sets and streams
choices.4: A plain volatile blob cache at scale

explanation: Memcached wins for multi-core throughput and dead-simple ops; reach for Redis the moment you need data structures, persistence, pub/sub, locks, or built-in HA.

question: Why is Redis being single-threaded a source of speed rather than slowness?

choices.1: No locks, no switches, atomic commands
choices.2: Single-threading is slow, which is why Redis needs persistence
choices.3: Single-threading requires locks around every command
choices.4: Single-threading lets Redis use all CPU cores in parallel

explanation: Single-threaded execution means no lock contention or context switches, and every command is atomic by construction; the bottleneck is network/memory not CPU, and you scale past one core by sharding.

question: What makes Redis far more than a key-value cache?

choices.1: It stores data exclusively on disk for durability
choices.2: It only stores opaque blobs like Memcached
choices.3: It is a full relational database with JOINs
choices.4: Its rich data structures

explanation: Strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets (great for leaderboards/rate limiters), streams (a Kafka-like log with consumer groups), bitmaps, and HyperLogLogs — so you pick the structure to fit the job.

question: How do Redis's RDB and AOF persistence options differ?

choices.1: Neither persists data; Redis is always purely volatile
choices.2: RDB logs every command; AOF takes periodic snapshots
choices.3: RDB is a periodic point-in-time snapshot
choices.4: Both guarantee zero data loss on any crash

explanation: Compact, fast restart, but can lose the last few minutes on a crash); AOF (Append-Only File) logs every write command and replays it on restart (far less data loss, but larger files and slower restart.

question: How does Redis Cluster shard the keyspace?

choices.1: It assigns each key to a random node with no fixed mapping
choices.2: It splits the keyspace into 16384 hash slots distributed across primary nodes
choices.3: It hashes keys onto a large token ring using virtual nodes, much like Cassandra
choices.4: It replicates every key to all nodes

explanation: Hash tags {...} force related keys into the same slot so multi-key operations can span them. Slot = CRC16(key) % 16384. Each with replicas.

question: What provides Redis high availability for a single-primary (non-sharded) setup?

choices.1: Sentinel failover plus async replication
choices.2: A master election via an external ZooKeeper cluster
choices.3: Redis Cluster is the only HA option for a single primary
choices.4: Synchronous replication to every replica before acknowledging writes

explanation: Sentinel monitors primaries, performs automatic failover, and handles client discovery; replication asynchronously copies the primary to replicas (read scaling plus a possibly-lagging failover copy).

question: What is the caveat with using Redis as a distributed lock?

choices.1: Redis locks are always perfectly safe with no caveats
choices.2: Redis cannot implement locks at all
choices.3: No fencing: a stalled holder acts on a lost lock
choices.4: Locks require the AOF to be disabled

explanation: SET key val NX PX ttl gives a lock, but without fencing tokens a GC-paused holder can think it still owns the lock after it expired and was granted to someone else — unsafe for correctness-critical mutual exclusion.

question: What is Cassandra's 'tunable consistency'?

choices.1: Per-query replica-count choice
choices.2: Consistency is fixed cluster-wide and cannot be changed
choices.3: It is a CP system that rejects writes during any partition
choices.4: It always enforces strong consistency on every operation

explanation: Fundamentally AP (eventually consistent by default), but each query chooses how many replicas must respond — trading consistency, latency, and availability.

question: What does the QUORUM consistency level require?

choices.1: A majority of all replicas to respond
choices.2: Only a minority of replicas to respond
choices.3: Exactly one replica to respond
choices.4: Every single replica to respond

explanation: Floor(RF/2) + 1 (e.g. 2 of 3 with RF=3) — while LOCAL_QUORUM requires a quorum only within the local datacenter to avoid cross-DC latency.

question: What is the R + W > RF rule in Cassandra?

choices.1: It only applies to writes, not reads
choices.2: R + W > RF guarantees the lowest possible latency
choices.3: R + W must be less than RF for strong consistency
choices.4: Read and write replica sets must overlap

explanation: If R + W > RF, the sets share at least one node, guaranteeing the read sees the latest acknowledged write (strong consistency); e.g. RF=3 with W=2 and R=2 gives 4 > 3.

question: How does Cassandra resolve write conflicts, and what is the consequence?

choices.1: It uses two-phase commit to serialize all conflicting writes
choices.2: Last-Write-Wins by column timestamp
choices.3: It merges conflicting values by summing them
choices.4: It rejects any write that conflicts with another

explanation: The highest timestamp wins with no locks or coordination, so writes are fast and never block — but there's no read-modify-write safety by default, so two concurrent increment-style updates can lose data.

question: What is hinted handoff in Cassandra?

choices.1: It routes reads to the fastest replica
choices.2: It rejects writes until all replicas are up
choices.3: If a replica is down during a write, the coordinator stores a hint
choices.4: It hands off the entire data partition to a brand new node permanently

explanation: A pending write) and replays it when the replica recovers, so writes succeed even with a replica temporarily offline (hints have a time limit, after which repair fixes the gap.

question: When and why would you use a Cassandra Lightweight Transaction (LWT)?

choices.1: To make reads faster by skipping replicas
choices.2: For conditional writes needing compare-and-set
choices.3: For every write, to guarantee strong consistency by default
choices.4: To resolve Last-Write-Wins conflicts automatically

explanation: Operations like 'insert only if not exists' or 'update only if unchanged' need linearizable semantics; LWT runs a Paxos round giving compare-and-set — expensive (multiple round trips), so use it sparingly.

question: How does Cassandra data modeling differ fundamentally from relational modeling?

choices.1: In Cassandra you model the QUERIES first and design tables to serve them
choices.2: You normalize all of the data fully and rely on JOINs like a relational DB
choices.3: You model entities first and let Cassandra optimize the queries
choices.4: There is no difference; CQL is identical to SQL

explanation: The opposite of relational normalization where you model the data then query it however you like. One table per query pattern. Denormalizing and duplicating data freely.

question: Which capabilities does Cassandra's wide-column model NOT provide?

choices.1: It allows arbitrary WHERE clauses on any column efficiently
choices.2: JOINs, foreign keys, ad-hoc WHERE
choices.3: It supports full JOINs and foreign keys like a relational database
choices.4: It provides full SQL with subqueries and window functions

explanation: You query mostly by primary key, with only limited aggregations.

question: What are the two parts of a Cassandra primary key and their roles?

choices.1: PRIMARY KEY ((partition key), clustering columns)
choices.2: Both parts just uniquely identify the row with no effect on layout
choices.3: The first part is the column name and the second is its value
choices.4: The partition key sorts rows and the clustering columns decide the node

explanation: The partition key decides WHERE the row lives (which node/partition), and the clustering columns decide HOW rows are sorted WITHIN that partition.

question: What does the partition key determine?

choices.1: It is only a uniqueness constraint with no effect on storage location
choices.2: It selects which datacenter handles the query
choices.3: It determines the sort order of rows within a partition
choices.4: Which node stores the row

explanation: It is hashed for placement, and all rows sharing the key live together in one partition.

question: What do clustering columns control?

choices.1: They enforce foreign-key relationships between tables
choices.2: They decide which node stores the row
choices.3: They have no effect on how data is stored
choices.4: Sort order within a partition

explanation: Rows are physically stored sorted by the clustering columns, making range scans and ordered reads inside the partition cheap.

question: What are the two competing goals (and cardinal sins) when designing a partition key?

choices.1: You need even distribution AND bounded partition size
choices.2: You need low cardinality and unbounded partitions for efficiency
choices.3: Partition key choice doesn't affect performance
choices.4: You need a sequential key and a single global partition

explanation: The two cardinal sins are hot partitions and unbounded partitions. High cardinality so load spreads, avoiding hot partitions. E.g. add a time bucket so partitions don't grow unbounded.

question: How does Cassandra propagate cluster state without a master?

choices.1: A master node broadcasts state to all others
choices.2: Each node polls a central registry on every request
choices.3: A ZooKeeper cluster keeps the authoritative membership
choices.4: Gossip: random peer exchanges until convergence

explanation: Roughly every second each node picks a few random peers and exchanges its view of every node's state; information spreads exponentially with no central coordinator.

question: What information does gossip carry about each node?

choices.1: The SQL query plans the node has executed
choices.2: Only whether the node is up or down, nothing else
choices.3: The full contents of every partition the node stores
choices.4: Status, a heartbeat/generation version counter

explanation: Up/down/joining/leaving/normal. So newer info wins over stale info, the token ranges owned, schema version, datacenter/rack, and load.

question: What are seed nodes in Cassandra?

choices.1: The nodes that hold the only writable copy of the data
choices.2: Coordinator nodes that route every query
choices.3: Bootstrap gossip contacts
choices.4: The nodes that run the failure detector for everyone else

explanation: Well-known addresses a joining node first gossips with — not special masters.

question: How does Cassandra's Phi Accrual failure detector work?

choices.1: It computes a rising suspicion value from heartbeat history
choices.2: It marks a node as dead after exactly three missed heartbeats
choices.3: It relies on the node self-reporting that it has failed
choices.4: It pings every node from a central monitor every second

explanation: Instead of a fixed 'missed N heartbeats = dead' rule, it tracks per-node heartbeat inter-arrival history and computes Phi relative to that node's normal timing, marking it down past a threshold — adapting to network conditions and avoiding false positives on slow links.

question: How does Cassandra keep serving when a node holding a replica goes down?

choices.1: A leader election is triggered to pick a replacement before any request is served
choices.2: The other replicas keep serving
choices.3: The whole cluster halts until the node recovers
choices.4: All writes are rejected until the node returns

explanation: Every partition has RF replicas and no master: RF=3 with QUORUM tolerates one node down, hinted handoff covers the down replica's writes, and read/anti-entropy repair reconcile divergence later.

question: Why does Cassandra have no single point of failure?

choices.1: No master; any node coordinates any request
choices.2: Because a ZooKeeper quorum guarantees a coordinator is always available
choices.3: Because every node holds a full copy of all data
choices.4: Because one master node is replicated three times

explanation: There is no leader election to stall on and nothing whose loss stops writes; gossip provides decentralized membership; RF replicas plus hinted handoff and repair self-heal; multi-DC replication survives a datacenter outage.

question: In Cassandra, what do partitioning and replication each decide?

choices.1: Partitioning encrypts data; replication compresses it
choices.2: Partitioning decides which node OWNS a given row (via consistent hashing on a token ring)
choices.3: Both decide only the sort order of rows
choices.4: Partitioning decides the total number of data copies, while replication decides the owning node

explanation: Replication decides how many COPIES exist and where (via replication factor and strategy).

question: How does Cassandra place a row on the token ring?

choices.1: The row is stored on a randomly chosen node
choices.2: A master node assigns each row to a partition
choices.3: The row simply goes to whichever node has the most free space
choices.4: The partition key is hashed to produce a token on the ring

explanation: Each node owns a contiguous range of tokens, and the row is stored on the node whose range contains its token (then replicated clockwise). Default Murmur3.

question: What benefit do virtual nodes (vnodes) provide?

choices.1: They eliminate the need for replication
choices.2: They let one node own the entire ring for simplicity
choices.3: Many scattered small ranges each
choices.4: They store the data in memory instead of on disk

explanation: And bigger nodes can be given more vnodes. Rather than one big range. Faster, smoother. So load is balanced evenly and, when a node fails or joins, many nodes share the rebalancing. Even load, spread failover, and capacity weighting.

question: How are replicas placed for a partition with RF=3, and is there a master replica?

choices.1: The replicas are the next RF-1
choices.2: One replica is the master that all reads and writes must go through
choices.3: All replicas are placed on the same node for locality
choices.4: Replicas are chosen at random across the cluster

explanation: Here 2) nodes clockwise from the primary token owner, and crucially all RF copies are EQUAL masterless peers — any of them can serve a read or accept a write (unlike Kafka/ZooKeeper which have a leader per partition.

question: What does NetworkTopologyStrategy provide over SimpleStrategy?

choices.1: It removes the need for a replication factor
choices.2: It stores all replicas in a single rack for speed
choices.3: Per-DC replication across distinct racks
choices.4: It just walks the ring clockwise ignoring topology

explanation: You set the replication factor per datacenter and replicas are placed on different racks within each DC so a rack failure doesn't destroy all copies — enabling multi-region clusters.

question: What is the role of the snitch in Cassandra?

choices.1: It tells Cassandra the rack/datacenter topology
choices.2: It elects the coordinator node for each request
choices.3: It hashes partition keys to tokens
choices.4: It compacts SSTables in the background

explanation: So replicas are spread across racks for fault isolation and reads are routed to the nearest/healthiest replica.

question: What is the coordinator in a Cassandra request?

choices.1: A ZooKeeper process that assigns requests to nodes
choices.2: The single master that owns all the data
choices.3: A single dedicated router node that every request must pass through
choices.4: Because Cassandra is masterless, a client connects to any node

explanation: Becomes the coordinator for that request — it hashes the partition key to a token, finds the RF replicas (knowing the ring via gossip), contacts as many as the consistency level requires, and assembles the reply, even if it doesn't own the data.

question: What is token-aware routing, and what does it require?

choices.1: Drivers send straight to an owning node
choices.2: It routes by hashing the entire row contents
choices.3: The driver picks a random node and lets it forward the request
choices.4: The coordinator always adds an extra hop to reach the replicas

explanation: Knowing the ring eliminates the extra coordinator-to-replica hop (lower latency, evenly spread coordinator load) — which requires the client to supply the partition key.

question: How does Cassandra's read path resolve replicas at consistency level > ONE?

choices.1: One full read plus digests; repair on mismatch
choices.2: It reads from every replica fully and returns the first response
choices.3: It reads from a single master replica only
choices.4: It returns whichever value the most replicas agree on, ignoring timestamps

explanation: The coordinator sends a DATA read to the closest replica and DIGEST reads to the others; matching digests return immediately, otherwise it fetches full data, returns the newest by timestamp, and triggers read repair.

question: How does a write propagate in Cassandra relative to the consistency level?

choices.1: The write is buffered until all replicas are confirmed up
choices.2: The write is sent only to the number of replicas equal to the consistency level
choices.3: The write goes to one master replica that forwards it later
choices.4: The write is sent to ALL RF replicas in parallel

explanation: The consistency level only governs how many ACKs the coordinator waits for before reporting success, and a down replica gets a hinted handoff. Each writing to its commit log + memtable and ACKing.

question: What is the production-standard driver load-balancing policy for Cassandra?

choices.1: Random selection of any node in any datacenter
choices.2: Always route to a single fixed coordinator node
choices.3: TokenAwarePolicy wrapping DCAwareRoundRobinPolicy
choices.4: Always route to the node with the lowest IP address
