question: How does SBE (Simple Binary Encoding) handle schema evolution?

choices.1: Append-only fields plus a schema version field
choices.2: Self-describing tags on every field like FIX
choices.3: A negotiation handshake that downgrades both sides to the older schema
choices.4: It doesn't — any schema change requires simultaneous upgrades

explanation: Old readers simply ignore the appended tail.

question: Why do binary protocols over TCP add a length prefix to each message?

choices.1: TCP requires application-level framing hints for Nagle's algorithm
choices.2: TCP is a byte stream with no message boundaries
choices.3: The length field doubles as a checksum
choices.4: Routers use the prefix to prioritise small control messages

explanation: A length prefix lets the reader split messages without scanning, handling frames that arrive split across packets.

question: Why must a wire format be specified explicitly rather than inferred from a C++ struct's layout?

choices.1: C++ structs cannot contain fixed-width integer types
choices.2: Struct layout is always randomised for security
choices.3: Compilers pad structs differently
choices.4: The standard forbids memcpy from structs with private members

explanation: The wire format is an ABI: endianness, offsets, and padding must be pinned down exactly, never inferred from a struct layout.

question: How is a binary heap stored and where are node i's children?

choices.1: As a linked binary tree with parent pointers; children are dereferenced
choices.2: As a sorted array; children are at i-1 and i+1
choices.3: As a hash table keyed by priority
choices.4: Implicitly in an array as a complete tree

explanation: Children of i at 2i+1 and 2i+2, so navigation is arithmetic, not pointer chasing.

question: Why is building a heap from n elements O(n) rather than O(n log n)?

choices.1: Sifting down from the last internal node to the root
choices.2: Because comparisons are constant-time, unlike sorting
choices.3: It isn't — build-heap is Θ(n log n) in the worst case
choices.4: The array is already a valid heap half the time on random input

explanation: Most nodes are near the leaves and sift only a little, and the total work telescopes to O(n).

question: Which is a real limitation of std::priority_queue?

choices.1: It cannot hold move-only types
choices.2: Its top() operation is O(log n)
choices.3: No decrease-key and no element handles
choices.4: It heap-allocates a separate node on every push

explanation: Dijkstra either pushes duplicates and skips stale pops, or needs an indexed heap; equal priorities also pop in unspecified order.

question: How do you get FIFO tie-breaking for equal priorities with std::priority_queue?

choices.1: Enable the stable flag in the comparator
choices.2: Push (priority, monotonically increasing sequence number) pairs
choices.3: Use std::stable_sort on the underlying container after each push
choices.4: Switch the underlying container from vector to deque

explanation: So ties break on insertion order.

question: Why do 4-ary heaps often beat binary heaps in practice, and Fibonacci heaps lose despite better asymptotics?

choices.1: 4-ary heaps have O(1) push; Fibonacci heaps have O(n) top
choices.2: 4-ary heaps avoid the comparator entirely on half the levels
choices.3: Fibonacci heaps are not available in the standard library
choices.4: Cache lines beat asymptotics

explanation: A shallower tree with children sharing cache lines means fewer misses per sift; Fibonacci's O(1) decrease-key drowns in constants and pointer-chasing.

question: Why is a heap the wrong structure for an order book?

choices.1: It can't do ordered levels, FIFO, or cancels
choices.2: Heaps cannot store prices as integer keys
choices.3: Heap memory cannot be shared between processes
choices.4: Heaps are too slow for the required O(log n) inserts

explanation: A book needs ordered iteration over price levels, FIFO within a level, and O(1) cancel by id — a heap exposes only the extreme element.

question: In SwissTable, how is the 64-bit hash split and what does each part do?

choices.1: Two independent 32-bit hashes are computed; one for the group, one for collision resolution
choices.2: H1 (top 57 bits) selects WHERE to look — the starting 16-slot group via H1 % num_groups
choices.3: The low 32 bits index the slot array directly; the high 32 bits are stored as a checksum
choices.4: H1 (low 7 bits) picks the slot within a group; H2 (top 57 bits) picks the group

explanation: While H2 (low 7 bits) is the fingerprint stored in the control bytes saying WHAT to expect there.

question: How does a SwissTable lookup know it can stop probing, and why don't tombstones stop it?

choices.1: It stops after a fixed maximum of four groups, the bound tombstones are designed to maintain
choices.2: It stops when the probe returns to its starting group after wrapping the table
choices.3: Finding any EMPTY control byte in the group proves the key was never displaced past that point
choices.4: It stops when the H2 match mask is zero for the current group, since the key would have matched there

explanation: DELETED bytes mark slots that were occupied at insertion time, so the sought key may have probed past them — which is exactly the chain information tombstones preserve.

question: What is the copy-and-swap implementation of assignment?

choices.1: Copy by value, swap, let it die
choices.2: operator=(const T& rhs) locks both objects, copies field by field, then unlocks
choices.3: operator=(T&& rhs) moves from rhs and leaves it empty
choices.4: A free function assign(T&, const T&) that clears the target then rebuilds it from the source

explanation: operator=(T other) — the copy/move constructor does the copying, swap steals the copy's guts, and its destructor releases the old state.

question: Why does copy-and-swap provide the strong exception guarantee where naive assignment fails?

choices.1: The swap operation catches and re-throws any exceptions from the copy
choices.2: It performs the copy inside a try block and rolls back on failure
choices.3: The compiler enforces noexcept on the whole operator when the parameter is by value
choices.4: All fallible work happens before *this changes

explanation: The copy is built in the by-value parameter first; naive assignment deletes the old state before the fallible allocation, so a throw leaves *this dangling.

question: How does copy-and-swap handle self-assignment (x = x)?

choices.1: It requires the usual this == &rhs guard at the top of the operator
choices.2: It just works, with no check
choices.3: It is undefined behaviour and must be prevented by the caller
choices.4: The swap detects aliasing and becomes a no-op

explanation: x is copied into the parameter, swapped, and destroyed — and skipping the explicit check avoids a branch in the common non-self case.

question: Why does the single operator=(T other) also serve as move assignment in C++11?

choices.1: The compiler synthesises a separate move overload from the copy one
choices.2: std::swap moves its arguments internally
choices.3: The by-value parameter binds an rvalue argument via the MOVE constructor
choices.4: Move assignment is unnecessary once swap is defined, because the compiler rewrites moves as swaps

explanation: So x = std::move(y) constructs the parameter by move and never allocates; writing a separate operator=(T&&) alongside would be ambiguous.

question: What requirement does the idiom place on swap, and how is it typically provided?

choices.1: A noexcept swap of raw members
choices.2: A virtual swap member so derived classes can override it
choices.3: A swap specialisation in namespace std for the class
choices.4: swap must allocate a temporary buffer to guarantee atomicity

explanation: Usually a hidden friend found by ADL, exchanging pointers/sizes only — if swap can throw, the exception-safety story collapses.

question: Why does std::vector::operator= NOT use copy-and-swap?

choices.1: Copy-and-swap would invalidate iterators, which vector assignment must preserve
choices.2: vector predates the idiom and is frozen by ABI compatibility
choices.3: swap on vectors is O(n), making the idiom quadratic
choices.4: vector reuses its existing capacity

explanation: Copy-and-swap always allocates a new buffer; element-wise copy into sufficient existing capacity does zero allocations — vector trades the strong guarantee for that efficiency.

question: Why can't a heterogeneous collection of CRTP-based objects be held behind one base pointer?

choices.1: CRTP base classes are abstract, so they can never be instantiated or even pointed to
choices.2: The static_cast inside the base erases the pointer's static type at run time
choices.3: Each Base<Derived> is a distinct unrelated type, so no shared base pointer exists
choices.4: CRTP objects live only on the stack and therefore never keep a stable address

explanation: There is no shared base to point at, so heterogeneous storage needs type erasure or std::variant.

question: You must store a mixed set of unrelated types in one container and dispatch at run time. Since CRTP can't, what fits?

choices.1: Switch every Base<Derived> to a shared non-template base with static methods
choices.2: Add a dynamic_cast in the base to recover each concrete derived type
choices.3: Give Base a virtual destructor so the instantiations share one vtable
choices.4: Type erasure, or a std::variant over the types visited with std::visit

explanation: Type erasure or a variant gives one runtime type over unrelated types; CRTP cannot.

question: Why is calling the CRTP interface from within the base class constructor undefined behaviour?

choices.1: The Derived subobject isn't built yet, so the downcast hits a nonexistent object
choices.2: static_cast is disallowed inside any constructor body by the C++ standard
choices.3: Constructors can never be inlined, which breaks CRTP's static dispatch entirely
choices.4: The base's vtable pointer still refers to Base while its construction is in progress

explanation: The Derived part is constructed after Base, so the downcast dereferences a not-yet-built object - UB, like calling a virtual from a constructor.

question: Why does a CRTP base use static_cast rather than dynamic_cast to reach the derived object?

choices.1: dynamic_cast is measurably faster than static_cast for downcasts on modern CPUs
choices.2: The derived type is fixed by the pattern and the base has no vtable for dynamic_cast
choices.3: static_cast performs a hidden run-time check that dynamic_cast skips for speed
choices.4: dynamic_cast would allocate heap memory, which CRTP is specifically designed to avoid

explanation: The concrete type is fixed by the one-derived contract, and a non-polymorphic base has no RTTI for dynamic_cast.

question: What does C++23 'deducing this' (an explicit object parameter) offer over classic CRTP?

choices.1: It adds a vtable so that the base and derived share a stable runtime interface
choices.2: It moves CRTP's dispatch from compile time to run time for extra flexibility
choices.3: A base method deduces the derived type without Base<Derived> or a static_cast
choices.4: It finally makes CRTP bases storable together in one heterogeneous container

explanation: this Self&& self deduces the caller's type, giving static polymorphism without self-inheritance or a cast.

question: What does std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource do when an individual object is deallocated?

choices.1: It returns the freed block to a size-class free list for reuse on a later request
choices.2: It hands the freed block straight back to the configured upstream memory resource
choices.3: Nothing - deallocate is a no-op; memory is freed only on release or destruction
choices.4: It rewinds its internal bump pointer back past the most recently freed block

explanation: monotonic_buffer_resource only bumps a pointer; it reclaims nothing until release() or destruction.

question: You move-assign one std::pmr::vector to another whose memory_resource differs. What happens?

choices.1: The buffer pointer is stolen in O(1) just like a normal vector move
choices.2: The elements are moved one by one into the destination's own resource
choices.3: The destination adopts the source's resource and then steals its buffer
choices.4: The assignment is ill-formed and the program fails to compile at that line

explanation: polymorphic_allocator does not propagate on assignment, so unequal resources force an element-wise move into the destination's resource.

question: What is the lifetime relationship between a std::pmr container and its memory_resource?

choices.1: The container must outlive the resource that it allocates all memory from
choices.2: Their lifetimes are unrelated because the container copies the resource in
choices.3: The resource is reference-counted and freed with the last live container
choices.4: The resource must outlive the container, which only holds a pointer to it

explanation: The container stores a bare memory_resource*, so destroying the resource first dangles it.

question: In a std::pmr::vector<std::pmr::string> built on an arena, where do the inner strings allocate?

choices.1: From the same arena - the outer resource is propagated to each inner string
choices.2: From the global default resource, wholly independent of the outer vector's arena
choices.3: Each inner string quietly allocates from its own private monotonic buffer
choices.4: Nested pmr containers are forbidden, so this declaration fails to compile

explanation: polymorphic_allocator is scoped, so uses-allocator construction passes the outer resource into each inner string.

question: What is the main per-allocation runtime cost of PMR versus a template allocator?

choices.1: A full context switch into the kernel taken on every single allocation
choices.2: A mutex lock acquired on every allocation regardless of which resource is used
choices.3: One virtual do_allocate call per allocation instead of an inlined direct call
choices.4: An extra heap allocation just to store the polymorphic_allocator object itself

explanation: The resource is reached through a virtual do_allocate, unlike a template allocator that inlines.

question: In the single-producer LMAX Disruptor, when does claim() spin instead of returning immediately?

choices.1: Whenever any consumer's sequence number exceeds the producer's own cursor value
choices.2: While the slot it is about to reuse has not been passed by the gating sequence
choices.3: Until it wins a CAS on the shared cursor against the other active producers
choices.4: While the ring is still less than half full, to accumulate a bigger batch

explanation: claim() spins until the gating (slowest consumer) sequence has passed seq - N, the slot about to be overwritten - that spin is the back-pressure.

question: With multiple producers, why can a Disruptor consumer no longer trust the cursor alone to mean a slot is ready?

choices.1: Claiming a seq and writing its slot are separate steps, so it may be unpublished
choices.2: The cursor is only updated once per batch, lagging behind individual publishes
choices.3: Multiple producers store the cursor with relaxed ordering, so it may read as stale
choices.4: The consumer reads the cursor before the producer's fetch_add has completed

explanation: With multiple producers, claim and publish are distinct, so an availability buffer (lap number per slot) is needed; the cursor alone can point at an unwritten slot.

question: How many acquire loads of the cursor does a Disruptor consumer need to process a burst of 40 newly published events?

choices.1: Forty acquire loads - one load guards each individual event it consumes
choices.2: Two acquire loads - one at the start and one to confirm none arrived since
choices.3: One acquire load - it reads the cursor once, then drains all forty slots
choices.4: Zero - the producer pushes the events straight into the consumer's buffer

explanation: The consumer loads the cursor once and drains the whole batch, amortising one fence over all events.

question: In the worked EBR scheme, when can the global epoch advance from e to e+1?

choices.1: As soon as any single reader announces epoch e and then exits its region
choices.2: After exactly three readers have retired an object into the limbo bucket
choices.3: Whenever the writer holds the epoch lock and no active readers are registered yet
choices.4: Only once every active reader has announced epoch e, none still in an older one

explanation: try_advance returns early unless every active reader has announced the current epoch; a reader still in an older epoch blocks the advance.

question: What does an EBR reader's read_lock do on entering a read-side critical region?

choices.1: It copies the current global epoch into its record and sets its in_read flag
choices.2: It takes a shared lock on the epoch counter that writers then must wait behind
choices.3: It increments the global epoch so that writers can tell a reader is active
choices.4: It copies the whole protected structure into a thread-local snapshot buffer

explanation: read_lock stores the current global epoch into the thread's record and sets in_read - a cheap announce, no CAS or shared write.

question: Why is the limbo bucket two epochs behind safe to delete when the epoch advances?

choices.1: Those nodes were reference-counted down to zero before entering the bucket
choices.2: Any reader that could have seen them entered in that older epoch and has exited
choices.3: Deletion is deferred to a background thread that first scans all hazard pointers
choices.4: The bucket is guarded by the writer mutex, so no reader is able to reach it

explanation: The bucket two epochs back holds nodes retired then; any reader that saw them entered in that epoch and has since exited and re-announced, so none can hold them.

question: In the seqlock reader loop, which two conditions each force a retry?

choices.1: The payload is larger than one cache line, or the writer holds the lock
choices.2: The sequence counter overflowed, or a second reader is active concurrently
choices.3: The two sequence samples differ, or the first sample read comes back odd
choices.4: The first sample is even, or the acquire fence failed to establish ordering

explanation: The reader retries if its two samples of seq_ differ (a write happened mid-read) or if the first sample is odd (a write is in progress).

question: Why must a seqlock's payload be a trivially copyable value rather than hold a pointer to follow?

choices.1: A torn read could yield a wild pointer that crashes when it is dereferenced
choices.2: Pointers simply cannot be stored inside a struct guarded by a sequence counter
choices.3: Following a pointer would force the reader to take the writer's spinlock first
choices.4: The sequence counter can only guard objects smaller than one machine word

explanation: Torn values are discarded on retry, but a torn pointer could be wild and crash on dereference - so a seqlock stores trivially-copyable values, not pointers.

question: How do you support multiple writers with a seqlock while keeping reads cheap?

choices.1: Give each writer its own private sequence counter and have readers check them all
choices.2: Serialise the writers with a mutex among themselves; readers stay lock-free
choices.3: Promote every payload field to std::atomic so that writers can race safely
choices.4: Have readers take a shared lock so that writers can acquire it exclusively

explanation: Serialise writers with a mutex among themselves; the reader side stays lock-free, preserving cheap reads.

question: What is false sharing in a multithreaded program?

choices.1: Two threads reading one shared variable through separate mutexes
choices.2: Two threads writing distinct variables that share one cache line
choices.3: A thread observing stale data after a lock is released too early
choices.4: Two threads deadlocking by taking their locks in a different order

explanation: There is no logical sharing, but MESI bounces the line between cores on every write, killing performance.

question: How do you eliminate false sharing between two hot per-thread counters?

choices.1: Wrap both counters in a single shared std::mutex to serialize their updates
choices.2: Mark both counters volatile so the compiler reloads them on every access
choices.3: Pad each onto its own line with alignas(hardware_destructive_interference_size)
choices.4: Place both counters together in one tightly packed struct that shares a cache line

explanation: std::hardware_destructive_interference_size (C++17) gives the padding needed to keep each variable on its own cache line.

question: For a plain statistics counter incremented by many threads, which memory ordering minimises fence cost while staying correct?

choices.1: relaxed - no cross-thread ordering is required for a counter
choices.2: seq_cst - the safe default that every atomic operation should use
choices.3: acquire on every increment to publish the running total
choices.4: release paired with a mutex taken on each increment

explanation: relaxed still guarantees atomicity; acquire/release are only needed to publish data to other threads, and seq_cst emits the most expensive fences.

question: When is spinning preferable to blocking while waiting on a lock?

choices.1: When many threads must contend and wait on the lock for a long time
choices.2: When the waiting thread currently holds no other resources
choices.3: When the lock is shared across two separate NUMA nodes
choices.4: When the critical section is held for only a few nanoseconds

explanation: A context switch costs far more than a nanosecond-scale wait; long waits should block via a futex to free the core.

question: Under Linux's first-touch policy, which NUMA node backs a freshly allocated page?

choices.1: The node holding the most free physical memory currently
choices.2: A node chosen round-robin across all of the system's sockets
choices.3: The node of the thread that first writes to the given page
choices.4: The node on which the process's main thread was started

explanation: Allocation only reserves virtual address space; the physical page lands on the node of the first writer, so touch memory on the thread that will use it.

question: For a CPU-bound workload, why size the pool to about one thread per physical core rather than per logical (SMT) core?

choices.1: Siblings share execution units, so 2x logical cores is not 2x compute
choices.2: Each logical core owns fully private ALUs, doubling arithmetic throughput
choices.3: The OS scheduler refuses to place threads on SMT sibling cores
choices.4: Physical cores must stay idle so they can service interrupts

explanation: Oversubscription causes scheduler thrash, and compute-bound SMT siblings contend for the same execution units.

question: On which workload is SMT most likely to reduce per-thread performance?

choices.1: One thread that stalls frequently on DRAM cache misses
choices.2: Two threads that mostly sleep waiting on network I/O
choices.3: Two compute-bound threads contending for the same ALUs
choices.4: A single thread with its sibling context left fully idle

explanation: SMT helps when threads stall (siblings fill idle slots) but hurts when both are compute-bound and fight for shared execution units.

question: Why do many low-latency and HFT systems disable SMT?

choices.1: A busy sibling adds jitter and steals slots, hurting tail latency
choices.2: SMT roughly halves the count of physical cores the process can use
choices.3: Turning off SMT doubles the L1 data cache on each core
choices.4: Hardware threads cannot run affinity-pinned threads at all

explanation: Latency-sensitive systems want predictability; a sibling's activity injects jitter, so they disable SMT or pin one thread per core.

question: Why do many cloud providers disable SMT for untrusted multi-tenant workloads?

choices.1: SMT lets one tenant read another tenant's private page tables directly
choices.2: Siblings share microarch state, enabling side channels like L1TF/MDS
choices.3: Hardware threads bypass the hypervisor's memory isolation fully
choices.4: SMT turns off ASLR on every logical core across the system

explanation: Siblings share caches, buffers and ports, so side-channel attacks (L1TF/Foreshadow, MDS) can leak data across them.

question: How does running two SMT siblings affect the per-thread effective cache?

choices.1: Each sibling gets the full L1/L2, doubling total cache capacity
choices.2: Each sibling receives a private L2 but shares only the L3
choices.3: Siblings share registers but each keeps a fully private L1
choices.4: Siblings share one L1/L2, halving effective cache per thread

explanation: Because siblings share the core's L1/L2, a cache-sensitive workload sees more misses as each thread effectively has less cache.

question: What does the Instruction Register (IR) hold?

choices.1: The address of the next instruction to be fetched
choices.2: The instruction word currently being decoded
choices.3: The result of the most recent ALU operation
choices.4: The control signals emitted by the control unit

explanation: The PC holds the address of the next instruction; the IR holds the fetched instruction word while it is decoded.

question: The naive fetch-decode-execute model would leave most of a core idle. What do modern cores do to keep the hardware busy?

choices.1: Raise clock speed until one instruction finishes per nanosecond
choices.2: Execute one instruction fully before the next is ever fetched
choices.3: Disable caches so each stage reads straight from main memory
choices.4: Pipeline and issue multiple instructions out of order per cycle

explanation: Pipelining overlaps stages, superscalar issue runs several per cycle, and OOO plus branch prediction reorder around stalls, all preserving the cycle's semantics.

question: What does an IPC (instructions per cycle) greater than 1 tell you about a core?

choices.1: It is superscalar, retiring several instructions each clock cycle
choices.2: It is currently running below its rated clock frequency to save power
choices.3: Its pipeline is stalled waiting on memory for most cycles
choices.4: It executes exactly one instruction on every clock cycle

explanation: The naive single-cycle model implies CPI much greater than 1 (IPC < 1); a superscalar core with multiple execution units sustains IPC > 1.

question: In the C++14 generic lambda [](auto a, auto b){ return a + b; }, how are the two auto parameters related?

choices.1: They must both deduce to one common type, like a single typename T
choices.2: They share a type only when both happen to be arithmetic
choices.3: They are two independent template parameters, so types may differ
choices.4: They are ordinary runtime parameters with no template involved

explanation: Each auto becomes its own template parameter, so add(1, 2.0) is valid; use []<typename T>(T,T) if you need the arguments to match.

question: What is the purpose of a lambda written []<typename... Ts>(Ts&&... args){ f(std::forward<Ts>(args)...); }?

choices.1: To copy each of the arguments once before passing them along to f
choices.2: To forward any argument pack to f, preserving value categories
choices.3: To restrict f to exactly one argument of a deduced type
choices.4: To capture all of the arguments by reference for later use

explanation: Ts&&... are forwarding references; std::forward keeps lvalues as lvalues and rvalues as rvalues, the generic wrapper idiom.

question: How can you require a C++20 lambda's parameter to satisfy a concept such as std::integral?

choices.1: Write the constraint in the list: []<std::integral T>(T x){...}
choices.2: Partially specialize the lambda's closure type for integral T
choices.3: Use a static_assert only, since lambdas cannot take constraints
choices.4: Overload the lambda twice, one per concept, in the same scope

explanation: A lambda's operator() is a function template, so it accepts concepts/requires-clauses; partial specialization is not available for closures.

question: Which lambdas convert implicitly to a plain function pointer?

choices.1: Any lambda at all, since every lambda is just a function pointer
choices.2: Only lambdas whose operator() is explicitly templated over T
choices.3: Only captureless lambdas; capturing ones stay unique closures
choices.4: Only lambdas that declare an explicit trailing return type

explanation: Capturing lambdas hold state and remain unique closure objects; only captureless ones offer the function-pointer conversion.

question: This implementation always heap-allocates via make_unique. What does the real std::function add to avoid that?

choices.1: Reference counting so that all of the copies share one heap block
choices.2: Small Buffer Optimization: small callables are stored inline
choices.3: A global object pool that recycles the Impl allocations
choices.4: Compile-time evaluation that removes the callable at build

explanation: SBO stores small callables (captureless lambdas, function pointers) inside a fixed inline buffer, spilling only large callables to the heap.

question: What runtime costs does std::function's type erasure impose that a template callable parameter avoids?

choices.1: A virtual call plus a possible heap allocation per callable
choices.2: A full deep copy of the arguments on every single invocation made
choices.3: A mutex acquisition to guard the erased callable pointer
choices.4: A recompilation of the callable each time it is invoked

explanation: Erasure dispatches through a virtual operator() and may allocate; hot paths often take the callable as a template parameter or function_ref to avoid both.

question: How does Gustafson's Law reframe Amdahl's pessimistic speedup ceiling?

choices.1: It proves that the serial fraction shrinks all by itself as more cores are added
choices.2: It assumes the problem grows with the core count, giving near-linear speedup
choices.3: It shows the cache, not the serial fraction, is the true scaling limit
choices.4: It swaps processors for threads, so the formula stops applying entirely

explanation: Amdahl fixes problem size (strong scaling); Gustafson grows the workload with N (weak scaling), so the serial share stays small and speedup stays near-linear.

question: As the processor count N goes to infinity, what does Amdahl's Law say speedup approaches?

choices.1: N, because speedup keeps growing without any upper bound
choices.2: 1/(1-P), the reciprocal of the program's serial fraction
choices.3: P times N, scaling with the parallelisable fraction
choices.4: Exactly P, the parallelisable fraction of the program

explanation: The P/N term vanishes as N grows, leaving the ceiling 1/(1-P); e.g. P=0.9 caps speedup at 10x no matter how many cores.

question: What distinguishes the assumptions behind Amdahl's Law and Gustafson's Law?

choices.1: Amdahl fixes the problem size; Gustafson grows it with the machine
choices.2: Amdahl applies to CPUs while Gustafson applies only to GPUs
choices.3: Amdahl counts threads while Gustafson counts physical cores
choices.4: Amdahl ignores serial work entirely while Gustafson doubles it at each step

explanation: Strong scaling (fixed work) vs weak scaling (work grows with cores) is the whole difference between the two laws.

question: Why is measured parallel speedup often worse than Amdahl's Law predicts?

choices.1: Parallelism adds overhead: synchronisation, coherency traffic, scheduling
choices.2: The serial fraction is always exactly zero in real programs
choices.3: Compilers silently serialise every single loop just to preserve strict ordering
choices.4: Adding extra cores lowers the clock speed of the whole chip

explanation: Amdahl only models the serial fraction; real runs also pay synchronisation, cache-coherency, and scheduling costs the formula ignores.

question: C++20 coroutines are described as 'stackless'. What does that mean?

choices.1: They cannot use any of their local variables across a single suspension point
choices.2: Their frame must always live on the stack, never on the heap
choices.3: Only the coroutine's own frame is saved on suspend, not a full call stack
choices.4: They run without any stack at all, using only CPU registers

explanation: A stackless coroutine saves just its own frame, so it is cheap to suspend but can only suspend from its own body, not from a nested callee.

question: What does returning std::suspend_always from initial_suspend make a coroutine?

choices.1: Lazy - it does nothing until the first resume() call
choices.2: Eager - it runs fully before returning to the caller
choices.3: Detached - it runs on a separate OS thread at once
choices.4: Invalid - initial_suspend must return suspend_never

explanation: suspend_always at the start means the body runs nothing until resumed (lazy); suspend_never would make it eager, running up to the first real suspension.

question: Why can coroutines handle far more concurrent mostly-waiting tasks than threads?

choices.1: They are preempted by the OS scheduler on a fixed time slice
choices.2: They are cooperative and stackless, avoiding per-task stacks and kernel switches
choices.3: Each coroutine transparently runs on its own dedicated core
choices.4: They skip the promise_type entirely, so there is absolutely no bookkeeping cost at all

explanation: Cooperative single-threaded scheduling means no kernel context switch and no reserved stack per task, so thousands can be in flight cheaply.

question: The coroutine frame is normally heap-allocated. What can eliminate that allocation?

choices.1: HALO, when the frame's lifetime is bounded by the caller
choices.2: Marking the whole coroutine noexcept so it cannot throw
choices.3: Declaring the promise_type constexpr at namespace scope
choices.4: Using co_return rather than co_yield to end the coroutine

explanation: Heap Allocation eLision Optimization removes the allocation when the compiler can prove the frame does not outlive the caller.

question: A single init thread touches a large shared array on a NUMA box. Why might every worker then suffer?

choices.1: The array is silently copied once for each and every worker, exhausting all of system memory
choices.2: First-touch placed all pages on the init thread's node, so others read them remotely
choices.3: Touching the memory locks its pages, blocking all concurrent readers
choices.4: The allocator interleaved the pages, so no worker gets a local copy

explanation: First-touch pins each physical page to the node of the thread that first writes it; a lone initialiser makes the whole array remote for everyone else.

question: What does running a process under numactl --interleave=all do?

choices.1: Binds every thread and all its memory to node 0 only
choices.2: Migrates all pages to whichever node currently has free RAM
choices.3: Spreads the process's pages round-robin across all NUMA nodes
choices.4: Disables remote memory access across the interconnect entirely

explanation: Interleaving evens out bandwidth for a globally-accessed dataset instead of concentrating it on one node's controller.

question: What does Linux AutoNUMA balancing do, and why is it sometimes disabled?

choices.1: It doubles memory bandwidth, but only on single-socket machines
choices.2: It interleaves pages once at boot and never moves them afterward
choices.3: It pins every single thread to node 0 in order to guarantee purely local accesses
choices.4: It migrates pages toward the accessing node; the overhead can hurt latency

explanation: Sampling faults and migrating pages/threads helps average workloads, but the migration cost adds jitter, so latency-sensitive systems prefer explicit pinning.

question: In a cache-coherent NUMA (ccNUMA) system, what does the hardware keep consistent across sockets?

choices.1: The clock frequency of every core running across the whole system
choices.2: Cached copies of memory, kept coherent by the coherency protocol
choices.3: The latency to reach any memory location from any core
choices.4: The amount of local DRAM physically attached to each socket

explanation: ccNUMA keeps cache lines coherent across sockets via the coherency protocol; latency stays non-uniform, which is the whole point of NUMA.

question: Into which three fields does a set-associative cache split a memory address?

choices.1: Page number, frame number, and a per-line dirty bit flag
choices.2: Tag, set index, and the byte offset within the line
choices.3: Segment selector, page, and permission bits
choices.4: Tag, a valid bit, and a replacement counter

explanation: The offset picks the byte in the line, the index picks the set, and the tag confirms which block occupies the matched way.

question: For a 32 KiB, 8-way set-associative cache with 64-byte lines, how many index bits select the set?

choices.1: 9 bits, because the cache holds 512 lines in total
choices.2: 3 bits, because the cache is 8-way set-associative
choices.3: 6 bits, because 512 lines / 8 ways gives 64 sets
choices.4: 12 bits, matching the offset of a 4 KiB page

explanation: 512 lines / 8 ways = 64 sets, and log2(64) = 6 index bits (with 6 offset bits for the 64-byte line).

question: Which category of cache miss does higher associativity specifically reduce?

choices.1: Compulsory misses, on the very first access to a block
choices.2: Capacity misses, when the working set exceeds the cache
choices.3: Coherency misses from another core writing the line
choices.4: Conflict misses, where too many blocks map into one set

explanation: Associativity gives a block more candidate slots per set, so it targets conflict misses; compulsory and capacity misses need prefetch or a bigger cache.

question: Why aren't large caches made fully associative despite having no conflict misses?

choices.1: Comparing every tag needs costly, power-hungry CAM that is slow at scale
choices.2: Fully associative caches can only ever store one single cache line at a time
choices.3: They would require the address to carry no tag field at all
choices.4: Operating systems forbid fully associative caches for security

explanation: Checking all tags in parallel needs content-addressable memory, which is expensive, hot, and slow for large line counts, so only tiny structures (TLBs) use it.

question: What does 'encapsulation' mean as data moves down the network stack?

choices.1: Each layer encrypts the payload so that the lower layers cannot ever read it
choices.2: Each layer wraps the payload from the layer above in its own header
choices.3: Each layer compresses the data so it fits onto the physical medium
choices.4: Each layer removes its own header before passing the data downward

explanation: Going down, every layer adds a header; going up, the receiver strips one header per layer. Each layer treats the layers above as opaque payload.

question: What is the Transport then Internet then Link sequence of PDU names?

choices.1: Segment, then packet, then frame
choices.2: Packet, then frame, then segment
choices.3: Frame, then packet, then segment
choices.4: Datagram, then frame, then packet

explanation: Transport produces a segment (datagram for UDP), the Internet layer a packet, and the Link layer a frame; the Physical layer sends bits.

question: Why is the TCP/IP model, rather than OSI, described as what the internet actually uses?

choices.1: OSI simply has too few layers to describe modern encrypted traffic
choices.2: TCP/IP defines the seven physical layers that every router implements
choices.3: OSI is a teaching model; real protocols map onto TCP/IP's four layers
choices.4: OSI was simply never standardised by any networking body at all

explanation: OSI is useful vocabulary, but deployed protocols compose along TCP/IP's four layers; TLS and QUIC don't fit OSI's boundaries cleanly.

question: What role does the link layer's MTU (for example 1500 bytes) play?

choices.1: It sets the maximum number of TCP connections allowed per host
choices.2: It defines how many hops a packet may traverse before it expires
choices.3: It fixes the port range available to the transport layer protocol
choices.4: It caps frame size, forcing IP fragmentation or TCP's MSS choice

explanation: The MTU limits how big a frame can be, so IP may fragment larger packets and TCP negotiates an MSS to stay within it.

question: Which of these is a non-type template parameter?

choices.1: template <int N>, as used in std::array<T, N>
choices.2: template <class T> for a generic container type
choices.3: template <template <class> class Container>
choices.4: A concept used to constrain a type parameter

explanation: Non-type parameters are compile-time values (like N); type parameters take types and template-template parameters take templates.

question: Why can an unused member function of a class template contain code that wouldn't compile for some T?

choices.1: The compiler eagerly type-checks every member at definition time
choices.2: Member functions of class templates are never type-checked at all
choices.3: The compiler instantiates only the template members you actually use
choices.4: Unused members still get compiled, but their errors are downgraded to warnings

explanation: Instantiation is lazy: an unused member is never instantiated, so it is never fully checked for that T.

question: In what sense is a C++ template not itself code?

choices.1: It is interpreted at runtime once its arguments become known
choices.2: It is a pattern the compiler stamps into real code on instantiation
choices.3: It is really just a macro that the preprocessor expands purely textually first
choices.4: It is a virtual interface the linker resolves at program load time

explanation: A template is a compile-time code generator; each instantiation produces separate, fully-optimised concrete code.

question: How does if constexpr simplify a recursive variadic template?

choices.1: It runs the whole recursion at runtime rather than at compile time
choices.2: It lets you partially specialize the recursive function template
choices.3: It unrolls the entire recursion into a single machine instruction
choices.4: It collapses the recursion into one function with no base overload

explanation: The discarded branch when the pack is empty removes the need for a separate base-case overload.

question: Which C++17 feature eliminates hand-written recursion for pack operations entirely?

choices.1: Fold expressions, such as ((std::cout << xs), ...)
choices.2: Explicit template instantiation of the whole pack
choices.3: Partial specialization applied over the parameter pack
choices.4: A recursive constexpr lambda iterating over the pack

explanation: A fold expression applies an operator across the whole pack in one expression, with no recursive instantiation.

question: For a recursive function template, why is the base case a less-variadic overload rather than a specialization?

choices.1: Overloads happen to instantiate faster than specializations would
choices.2: Function templates cannot be partially specialized in the language
choices.3: Specializations of function templates are illegal in every single form
choices.4: Overloads dodge the compiler's template instantiation depth limit

explanation: Partial specialization is only allowed for class templates, so functions use overload resolution to terminate the recursion.

question: What is a practical downside of deep template recursion?

choices.1: Each step allocates a stack frame, risking a runtime stack overflow
choices.2: The recursion runs at runtime, adding a call overhead at each level
choices.3: Each step is a separate instantiation, costing compile time and depth
choices.4: The compiler is unable to inline any recursively instantiated template

explanation: Recursion happens through instantiation at compile time, so it grows compile time/binary size and can hit the instantiation depth limit.

question: What problem does a trailing return type like auto f(A a, B b) -> decltype(a + b) solve?

choices.1: It forces the return value to be computed at compile time only
choices.2: It makes the function a template without any type parameters
choices.3: It gives the function a return type that depends on its parameters
choices.4: It quietly suppresses any implicit conversions applied to the returned value

explanation: decltype(a + b) can name a type derived from the parameters, which a leading return type cannot reference yet.

question: Why is decltype(auto) f() { int x; return (x); } a bug?

choices.1: The parens make the return type int&, returning a dangling reference
choices.2: decltype(auto) is simply not permitted as any function's return type
choices.3: It returns a copy, silently slicing off part of a derived object
choices.4: The variable x would need to be declared static in order to compile

explanation: decltype((x)) is int& (lvalue expression), so decltype(auto) returns a reference to a destroyed local. Return the bare name instead.

question: How is decltype used for expression SFINAE, e.g. a signature ending in -> decltype(c.size())?

choices.1: It evaluates c.size() once to cache the resulting return type
choices.2: It forces c.size() to be marked noexcept before it will compile
choices.3: It converts the c.size() call into a compile-time constant value
choices.4: It enables the overload only when that expression is well-formed

explanation: If the expression is ill-formed the overload is silently removed from the set rather than causing a hard error.

question: std::vector::push_back gives the strong guarantee only when what is true of the element type?

choices.1: It has a trivial default constructor
choices.2: Its move constructor is marked noexcept
choices.3: It is copyable but is not movable at all
choices.4: It declares a virtual destructor for cleanup

explanation: On reallocation the vector can move elements only if that move can't throw; otherwise it must copy to preserve the strong guarantee.

question: If an element's move constructor can throw, what does vector reallocation do instead, and why?

choices.1: It moves them anyway and silently catches any exception that is thrown
choices.2: It refuses to reallocate and immediately throws std::bad_alloc instead
choices.3: It copies the elements, so a throw leaves the original buffer intact
choices.4: It moves half and copies the other half to balance the total cost

explanation: Copying is non-destructive, so if construction throws mid-reallocation the source buffer is untouched and the strong guarantee holds.

question: Why must a destructor not throw during stack unwinding?

choices.1: A second exception raised while one is active will call std::terminate
choices.2: The compiler deletes any destructor that is allowed to throw at all
choices.3: A throwing destructor automatically leaks the object's whole memory
choices.4: Stack unwinding is completely unable to run any destructor code at all

explanation: Throwing while another exception is propagating triggers std::terminate, so destructors are implicitly noexcept.

question: In the copy-and-swap strong-guarantee idiom, why does the swap come last?

choices.1: The swap must always run before the copy so as to reserve enough memory first
choices.2: The swap first validates the object's invariants before the copying
choices.3: Swapping first lets the later copy step reuse the freed-up storage
choices.4: All throwing work is done first, so the noexcept swap can't fail to commit

explanation: Every operation that might throw happens on the temporary before the swap; once you reach the noexcept swap the commit cannot fail.

question: Why must the base class be polymorphic (have a virtual function) for dynamic_cast to work?

choices.1: Because non-polymorphic types are stored entirely without any type information at all
choices.2: So that the whole conversion can be resolved at compile time instead
choices.3: It reads the runtime type from the vtable, which only polymorphic types have
choices.4: Because only polymorphic types are ever allocated on the heap safely

explanation: dynamic_cast performs a runtime type check using RTTI reached through the vtable; a class without a virtual function has no vtable to consult.

question: What is the risk of using static_cast to downcast a Base* to a Derived*?

choices.1: It is unchecked, so it is UB if the object isn't really that derived type
choices.2: It always throws std::bad_cast whenever the object's type is wrong
choices.3: It silently copies the base subobject, slicing off the derived part
choices.4: It fails to even compile unless the base class explicitly declares a virtual function

explanation: static_cast does no runtime check; if the object isn't actually the derived type the result is undefined behaviour. Use dynamic_cast for a checked downcast.

question: Why must a copyable Pimpl class define its own copy constructor rather than use the default?

choices.1: Because Impl is a class template that cannot be instantiated more than once
choices.2: Because unique_ptr isn't copyable, so the defaulted copy would be deleted
choices.3: Because copying changes the public class's size and breaks the stable ABI
choices.4: Because the compiler forbids copying any class that declares a destructor

explanation: std::unique_ptr has no copy, so the implicit copy is deleted; a copyable Widget must deep-copy *impl_ by hand (where Impl is complete).

question: Can a const member function of a Pimpl class mutate the hidden Impl object, and why?

choices.1: No, a const method cannot call anything on the hidden Impl at all
choices.2: No, the compiler makes the Impl object const inside const methods
choices.3: Yes, but only if Impl is declared mutable in the header
choices.4: Yes, because constness does not propagate through the unique_ptr

explanation: A const method only makes the pointer const, not the pointee, so *impl_ stays mutable; use propagate_const if you want const-correctness to reach through.

question: How does the Meyers singleton avoid the static-initialisation-order fiasco?

choices.1: The instance is constructed on its first use, not at program startup
choices.2: The compiler orders all of the global constructors alphabetically by their name
choices.3: Function-local statics are always constructed before global objects
choices.4: The linker guarantees one deterministic init order across all files

explanation: Construct-on-first-use means the object is created when instance() is first called, so it is always ready before use, regardless of TU init order.

question: What lifetime hazard still exists with a Meyers singleton?

choices.1: Its constructor may run twice if instance() is called recursively
choices.2: Two separate instances may exist briefly during concurrent first calls
choices.3: It is destroyed at program exit, so using it from another static's destructor is UB
choices.4: It is simply never destroyed at all, leaking all of its resources when the program finally ends

explanation: Local statics are destroyed in reverse order of construction at exit; touching a destroyed singleton from another static object's destructor is undefined.

question: In a detection trait, what does std::void_t let you do?

choices.1: It evaluates the detected expression and caches its resulting value
choices.2: It forces every candidate template to share one common return type
choices.3: It turns a failed template substitution straight into a hard compile-time error instead
choices.4: It maps well-formed types to void, so a bad one drops the specialisation

explanation: void_t<...> is void whenever its arguments are well-formed; if the probed expression is ill-formed, the partial specialisation is discarded and the primary template is used.

question: What C++20 feature replaces enable_if for constraining templates, with clearer errors?

choices.1: Explicit template instantiation of each candidate overload
choices.2: Concepts, which constrain a template with far clearer diagnostics
choices.3: Runtime type introspection through typeid and std::type_info
choices.4: Preprocessor macros that carefully expand to the single correct overload

explanation: Concepts express requirements directly (e.g. template <std::integral T>) and produce readable errors, replacing most enable_if-style SFINAE constraints.

question: How can double-checked locking be made correct in modern C++?

choices.1: Wrap the entire function body in a recursive mutex instead of a plain one
choices.2: Mark the shared pointer volatile so the compiler reloads it on each read
choices.3: Use an atomic pointer with a release store paired with an acquire load
choices.4: Add a second mutex so that the two null checks can never race together

explanation: The release store publishes the fully-constructed object; a matching acquire load guarantees a reader that sees the pointer also sees the constructor's writes.

question: After initialisation has happened, what is the cost of a std::call_once call?

choices.1: Very cheap, since the flag is already set from the first call
choices.2: As expensive as acquiring the mutex on every single call made
choices.3: Undefined, because call_once might run the initialiser once more
choices.4: Proportional to the number of threads that ever called it

explanation: Once the once_flag is set, subsequent call_once calls just observe that and return, so the fast path is very cheap.

question: What do Factory patterns primarily decouple?

choices.1: The code that uses an object from the choice of concrete type created
choices.2: The destructor of an object from the allocation in its constructor
choices.3: A class's interface from its member names
choices.4: The compilation of a header from its separately built implementation file

explanation: Callers depend on an interface and the factory decides the concrete type, so adding a type touches one place.

question: What does Abstract Factory add over a plain Factory Method?

choices.1: It caches every object it creates in a registry
choices.2: It produces whole families of related products guaranteed to be compatible
choices.3: It guarantees that only a single shared instance of the product ever exists
choices.4: It assembles one object step by step

explanation: Abstract Factory groups several factory methods so a matching product family (e.g. per-theme widgets) stays consistent.

question: When is a factory most warranted?

choices.1: When every field of the object is fixed and never changes after creation
choices.2: When the program needs exactly one shared instance of the type globally
choices.3: When the concrete type is decided at runtime from configuration or input
choices.4: When two collaborating classes expose completely unrelated interfaces

explanation: Factories shine when the concrete type isn't known until runtime or when construction must be centralised.

question: For a fixed, closed set of product types, what avoids a virtual factory entirely?

choices.1: Allocating the products from a global arena
choices.2: Marking every one of the product classes as both final and noexcept
choices.3: Making the shared base class a template parameterised on each product type
choices.4: Returning a std::variant of the concrete types by value

explanation: A std::variant plus a factory returning it avoids heap allocation and virtual dispatch when the type set is closed.

question: What is a drawback of an if/switch-based factory function?

choices.1: It must be edited for each new type unless a registry mechanism is used
choices.2: It is completely unable to return objects through a std::unique_ptr owner
choices.3: It forces every product in the hierarchy to share one base constructor
choices.4: It makes the individual concrete product classes impossible to unit test

explanation: A hard-coded dispatch changes for every new type; a registry or self-registration keeps it open for extension.

question: What does the Builder pattern separate?

choices.1: The declaration of a class from its friends
choices.2: The construction of a complex object from its final representation
choices.3: The allocation of an object's memory from the cleanup its destructor performs
choices.4: The abstract interface of a class from its subclasses

explanation: Builder assembles an object step by step, replacing telescoping constructors with a readable incremental API.

question: When is Builder most useful?

choices.1: When a single class must expose exactly one globally shared instance
choices.2: When two mutually incompatible interfaces have to be bridged together
choices.3: When an object has many optional or order-sensitive parameters to set
choices.4: When one algorithm among several must be swapped out live at runtime

explanation: Builder handles many optional fields cleanly instead of a long, unreadable positional argument list.

question: What enables the fluent chaining in b.url(...).method(...).build()?

choices.1: The builder overloads operator>> once for each configurable parameter
choices.2: Every configuring setter is declared static on the builder's own class
choices.3: The whole object is rebuilt again from scratch after each setter call
choices.4: Each configuring step returns a reference to the builder object itself

explanation: Returning *this from each step lets the calls chain into one readable expression.

question: Which C++20 feature often replaces Builder for simple configuration objects?

choices.1: Designated initialisers used on a plain aggregate configuration type
choices.2: Virtual inheritance from one common shared configuration base class
choices.3: Template specialisation written separately for each configuration field
choices.4: A consteval constructor that accepts every configuration parameter

explanation: Config{.timeout=5, .retries=3} gives named optional fields with zero boilerplate; Builder wins when steps need logic.

question: Where does a Builder typically enforce the finished object's invariants?

choices.1: Inside the destructor of the fully constructed finished object
choices.2: Once, in the final build() step, just before it returns the object
choices.3: Inside every individual setter, re-checking them on each single call
choices.4: Nowhere, because builders only ever collect raw unvalidated values

explanation: Validating once in build() means the object is only produced when it is complete and valid.

question: How does the Prototype pattern create new objects?

choices.1: By constructing each fresh from a parameter list
choices.2: By handing back one shared global instance to every single caller that asks
choices.3: By cloning an existing instance through a virtual clone() method
choices.4: By selecting the concrete type to build from a key

explanation: A polymorphic clone() copies the concrete type through a base pointer, without the caller knowing the exact class.

question: Prototype's clone() method is an example of what technique?

choices.1: A static factory helper on the base
choices.2: Guaranteed copy elision performed by the compiler right at the call site
choices.3: A user-provided conversion operator down to the common shared base type
choices.4: A virtual copy constructor that duplicates the concrete type

explanation: clone() is the virtual-copy-constructor idiom: copy an object polymorphically through its base interface.

question: When is Prototype most useful?

choices.1: When you need a copy through a base pointer without knowing its type
choices.2: When exactly one instance of a type must exist across the whole program
choices.3: When two APIs expose mismatched signatures
choices.4: When one algorithm among many must be swapped live at runtime

explanation: It duplicates an object whose concrete type is hidden, and avoids re-running expensive construction.

question: What is a key hazard when implementing clone()?

choices.1: Accidentally leaving the surrounding class entirely non-polymorphic at runtime
choices.2: Getting deep-versus-shallow copy semantics of the members wrong
choices.3: Forcing every clone to alias one global
choices.4: Preventing the constructed object from ever being destroyed safely

explanation: A defaulted copy of pointer members shares state; clone() must deep-copy where required (see the rule of five).

question: Which standard C++ facility relies on Prototype's virtual-clone trick?

choices.1: The comparator that std::sort takes
choices.2: The contiguous element storage that std::vector maintains internally for speed
choices.3: A copyable type-erased wrapper such as the standard std::function
choices.4: The iteration a range-based for loop performs

explanation: Type erasure uses a virtual clone() so the erased object stays copyable despite its concrete type being hidden.

question: What does the Adapter pattern do?

choices.1: It adds stackable extra behaviour to an object at runtime
choices.2: It controls access to an expensive underlying real object
choices.3: It notifies many observers whenever a subject's state changes
choices.4: It wraps a class in the different interface clients expect

explanation: Adapter implements the target interface and forwards to the adaptee, translating names, types, or arguments.

question: When should you reach for an Adapter?

choices.1: To fit a third-party or legacy class you are not able to modify
choices.2: To give one simple entry point to a whole complicated subsystem
choices.3: To add optional features to an object without a subclass explosion
choices.4: To turn a request into a queueable and undoable command object

explanation: Adapter integrates an incompatible class into code expecting a specific interface, editing neither side.

question: Which Adapter form is preferred in modern C++?

choices.1: A class adapter using multiple inheritance from the adaptee class
choices.2: An object adapter that holds the adaptee by internal composition
choices.3: A preprocessor macro that mechanically renames the adaptee methods
choices.4: A reinterpret_cast performed directly between the two interfaces

explanation: The object adapter composes the adaptee, avoiding the tight coupling of inheriting from it.

question: How does Adapter differ from Decorator?

choices.1: Adapter builds objects; Decorator destroys them
choices.2: Adapter adds new behaviour, whereas Decorator only controls access to it
choices.3: Adapter changes the interface; Decorator keeps it and adds behaviour
choices.4: Adapter works only at compile time; Decorator at runtime

explanation: Both wrap an object, but Adapter's purpose is interface translation, not adding responsibilities.

question: What is a cost of using an Adapter?

choices.1: It quietly forces the wrapped adaptee to become a singleton object
choices.2: It requires the client code to depend directly on the adaptee class
choices.3: It stops the adaptee class from ever being unit tested in isolation
choices.4: It adds an indirection layer and one more class to keep maintained

explanation: The translation layer is an extra class and a level of indirection; it grows if many methods need adapting.

question: What does the Decorator pattern do?

choices.1: It wraps a same-interface object to add behaviour that can stack
choices.2: It exposes one simplified interface over a large and complex subsystem
choices.3: It selects a product type from input
choices.4: It guarantees a class has exactly one shared global instance that is reused

explanation: Each decorator shares the wrapped object's interface and adds work before/after, so features compose at runtime.

question: What problem does Decorator prevent?

choices.1: Two incompatible interfaces failing to connect
choices.2: A subclass explosion arising from every possible feature combination
choices.3: An object being accidentally constructed more than one single time over
choices.4: The notification order among listeners being unspecified

explanation: Instead of a class per feature combination, you stack small single-purpose wrappers at runtime.

question: How does Decorator differ from Proxy?

choices.1: Decorator changes the interface, whereas Proxy keeps it fully identical
choices.2: Decorator is creational, whereas Proxy is a purely behavioural pattern
choices.3: Decorator adds behaviour, whereas Proxy controls access to the object
choices.4: Decorator works only at compile time, whereas Proxy works at runtime

explanation: Both share the subject's interface, but Decorator adds responsibilities while Proxy governs access to it.

question: What is a downside of a deep decorator stack?

choices.1: It quietly forces every wrapper in the stack to be a global singleton
choices.2: It changes the presented interface at each successive wrapping layer
choices.3: It prevents the innermost base object from ever being constructed at all
choices.4: It is harder to debug, and the order of wrapping can change the result

explanation: Many small wrappers make a deep stack hard to trace, and ordering (e.g. compress-then-encrypt) can change results.

question: What must a decorator share with the object it wraps?

choices.1: The same public interface that the wrapped object exposes
choices.2: The exact same concrete class as the object being wrapped
choices.3: A single shared heap allocation holding both of the objects
choices.4: An identical constructor signature to the wrapped object's

explanation: Because it presents the same interface, a decorated object is a drop-in for the original from the client's view.

question: What does a Facade provide?

choices.1: A step-by-step way to build one object
choices.2: One simplified high-level interface over a complex internal subsystem
choices.3: A same-interface stand-in that controls access to the real underlying object
choices.4: A freshly cloned copy of an existing object

explanation: Clients use the facade for common cases instead of wiring the subsystem's classes together themselves.

question: What is the main benefit of a Facade?

choices.1: It allows optional features to be layered onto objects at runtime
choices.2: It guarantees only a single instance of the subsystem ever exists
choices.3: It shrinks the client's dependency surface down to a single class
choices.4: It converts one class's interface into a different expected one

explanation: Callers depend on one small surface, so subsystem internals can change without breaking them.

question: How does Facade differ from Adapter?

choices.1: Facade changes an interface; Adapter hides one class
choices.2: Facade is a creational pattern, whereas Adapter is a behavioural one at heart
choices.3: Facade controls access, whereas Adapter adds behaviour
choices.4: Facade simplifies a whole subsystem, whereas Adapter reshapes one interface

explanation: Adapter matches one class to an expected interface; Facade fronts many classes with a simple entry point.

question: What is the main risk when a Facade grows unchecked?

choices.1: It becomes a god object that accumulates every possible operation
choices.2: It starts translating between two mismatched incompatible interfaces
choices.3: It duplicates all of the subsystem's internal objects on each call
choices.4: It ends up exposing far too many internal classes to its clients

explanation: Keep a facade focused on common workflows so it doesn't swell into an unmaintainable god object.

question: Which class-level C++ idiom is analogous to a Facade?

choices.1: CRTP, which hides shared behaviour inside a common base template
choices.2: Pimpl, which hides a class's implementation behind a single pointer
choices.3: RAII, which ties a resource's lifetime to the owning object's scope
choices.4: SFINAE, which removes candidate overloads during argument substitution

explanation: Pimpl hides one class's implementation and headers behind a single pointer, much as a facade hides a subsystem.

question: What does the Proxy pattern provide?

choices.1: One simplified single door into a large and complicated subsystem
choices.2: A way to add several stacked behaviours to an object at runtime
choices.3: A same-interface stand-in that controls access to a real object
choices.4: A builder that assembles a complex object one small step at a time

explanation: The proxy mirrors the subject's interface and intercepts calls to add access control, laziness, caching, or remoting.

question: Which of these is a standard kind of Proxy?

choices.1: A creational proxy that clones the underlying subject fresh on each new demand
choices.2: A visitor proxy that adds operations across a hierarchy
choices.3: A builder proxy that validates parameters in steps
choices.4: A virtual/lazy proxy that defers creating an expensive object

explanation: Common proxies include virtual/lazy, protection, caching, remote, and smart-reference proxies.

question: How does Proxy differ from Decorator?

choices.1: Proxy controls access to the object, whereas Decorator adds new behaviour
choices.2: Proxy changes the interface, whereas Decorator keeps it identical throughout
choices.3: Proxy is creational; Decorator is structural
choices.4: Proxy needs multiple inheritance, whereas Decorator manages without any

explanation: Both keep the subject's interface, but a proxy governs access rather than adding client-requested behaviour.

question: Which standard C++ type is a smart-reference proxy?

choices.1: std::optional, which stands in for a possibly-absent single value
choices.2: std::shared_ptr, which reference-counts and manages the real object
choices.3: std::string_view, which merely borrows another string's character data
choices.4: std::tuple, which simply aggregates several values of differing types

explanation: shared_ptr is a smart-reference proxy managing the real object's lifetime via reference counting.

question: What does a virtual (lazy) proxy achieve?

choices.1: It notifies every subscriber whenever the underlying object changes
choices.2: It translates the underlying object into a different expected interface
choices.3: It defers creating an expensive object until it is first actually used
choices.4: It reverses the most recent operation that was performed on the object

explanation: The proxy constructs the costly real object only when a method is first actually called.

question: What does the Strategy pattern do?

choices.1: It reverses past operations through a stored history for undo support
choices.2: It notifies a set of many listeners whenever a subject changes state
choices.3: It wraps an existing class inside a different interface clients expect
choices.4: It makes a family of interchangeable algorithms selectable at runtime

explanation: Strategy puts a family of algorithms behind a common interface so the context can swap behaviour at runtime.

question: What does Strategy most directly replace?

choices.1: Sprawling mode-switching conditional logic scattered across the code
choices.2: A deep runtime hierarchy of many stacked decorator wrapper objects
choices.3: A whole subsystem's many entangled and tightly coupled inner classes
choices.4: A telescoping series of overloaded constructors with long arg lists

explanation: Each algorithm becomes an object, eliminating scattered if/switch-on-mode branches.

question: What is the lightest form of a single-method strategy in modern C++?

choices.1: A CRTP base class parameterised statically on the algorithm type
choices.2: A std::function or a small lambda that simply holds the algorithm
choices.3: A virtual interface exposing several separate algorithm methods
choices.4: A preprocessor macro that expands directly to the chosen algorithm

explanation: For one operation a std::function/lambda avoids a whole class hierarchy; virtuals pay off for multi-method strategies.

question: How does State differ from Strategy despite sharing structure?

choices.1: State is strictly compile-time, whereas Strategy is strictly runtime
choices.2: State creates the objects, whereas Strategy is what destroys them again
choices.3: State swaps its own behaviour over time through explicit transitions
choices.4: State never needs an interface, whereas Strategy always requires one

explanation: Both delegate to a swappable object, but a State machine changes its own current state as it runs.

question: What does Strategy reify, in contrast with the Command pattern?

choices.1: A single event; Command reifies a listener
choices.2: One concrete state, whereas the Command pattern instead reifies a transition
choices.3: A pending request, whereas the Command pattern reifies a complete algorithm
choices.4: An algorithm, whereas the Command pattern reifies a request or an action

explanation: Strategy turns an algorithm into a pluggable object; Command turns a request/action into an object.

question: What relationship does the Observer pattern define?

choices.1: A one-to-many dependency that notifies observers when state changes
choices.2: A one-to-one adapter bridging two mismatched interfaces
choices.3: A chain of nested wrappers each adding a little more behaviour on top
choices.4: A single shared instance made globally accessible

explanation: When the subject's state changes, all registered observers are notified automatically.

question: Observer is the basis of which kind of system?

choices.1: Compile-time metaprogramming resolved entirely by the templates
choices.2: Publish/subscribe and event or notification systems such as UIs
choices.3: Reference-counted automatic management of shared heap memory
choices.4: Step-by-step assembly of one complex object from many parts

explanation: Pub/sub, UI event handling, and model-view updates are all built on Observer's notify-many mechanism.

question: What is the main lifetime hazard in a naive Observer implementation?

choices.1: The whole subject object is copied once per registered observer that exists
choices.2: Observers get notified before the subject exists
choices.3: A destroyed observer left still registered becomes a dangling pointer
choices.4: The subject can never be destroyed while any observers still exist elsewhere

explanation: If an observer is destroyed without unsubscribing, the subject holds a dangling pointer; weak_ptr or unsubscribe fixes it.

question: Besides lifetime, what is a common Observer pitfall?

choices.1: Only a single observer is ever allowed to subscribe at one time
choices.2: Every observer is forced to share the subject's own concrete type
choices.3: The subject must be fully recreated after every single notification
choices.4: Notification order is unspecified and updates can re-enter the list

explanation: Unspecified order plus modifying the observer list mid-notification causes subtle re-entrancy bugs.

question: What is a common modern-C++ way to store observers?

choices.1: A vector of std::function callbacks, or weak_ptrs to the observers
choices.2: A single raw pointer that always points at the latest observer only
choices.3: A compile-time type list enumerating every observer type in advance
choices.4: One global mutex that guards a single shared observer object slot

explanation: Callbacks as std::function (and weak_ptr to avoid dangling) are more flexible than hand-rolled observer interfaces.

question: What does the Command pattern turn a request into?

choices.1: One simplified high-level interface sitting over a whole subsystem
choices.2: A standalone object exposing a uniform execute() method to run it
choices.3: A same-interface stand-in that controls access to the real object
choices.4: A family of interchangeable algorithms chosen freely at runtime

explanation: Bundling the operation, receiver, and arguments as an object makes the request storable and replayable.

question: What capability does making a request an object enable?

choices.1: Automatic deep copying of the receiver on every method invocation
choices.2: Compile-time selection of the concrete receiver type to dispatch to
choices.3: Undo/redo, queuing, logging, and macro recording of the operations
choices.4: A guarantee that exactly one receiver instance exists in the program

explanation: Because the action is a value, it can be queued, logged, scheduled, or reversed with undo().

question: How does a Command typically support undo?

choices.1: By simply re-running its own execute() method a second time over
choices.2: By deleting its receiver entirely and then rebuilding it from scratch
choices.3: By notifying its observers so they each roll their own state back
choices.4: By capturing enough state to reverse the effect it originally had

explanation: Each command stores what it needs to invert its effect (or a snapshot/memento), which can be memory-heavy.

question: When you only need deferred execution (no undo or logging), what is a lightweight Command?

choices.1: A std::function<void()> that captures the arguments it will later need
choices.2: A virtual base class exposing a single overridable execute() method to run
choices.3: A singleton that runs the action later
choices.4: A class template specialised separately on each action's own concrete type

explanation: A lambda/std::function capturing its args is a minimal command, exactly what a thread-pool task is.

question: A thread-pool task is essentially an instance of which pattern?

choices.1: The Observer pattern, notifying the pool
choices.2: The Command pattern, an action packaged as a runnable object for later
choices.3: The Adapter pattern, matching the pool's expected interface for submission
choices.4: The Prototype pattern, cloned onto the queue

explanation: An enqueued unit of work with a uniform run/execute interface is a Command in disguise.

question: What does the State pattern let an object do?

choices.1: Expose one simple interface over many classes
choices.2: Add several stacked extra responsibilities onto itself dynamically at runtime
choices.3: Change its behaviour by swapping an internal state object that it holds
choices.4: Produce whole families of related objects

explanation: Behaviour is delegated to a state object that is swapped on transitions, so the context appears to change class.

question: What does the State pattern most directly replace?

choices.1: A telescoping set of many overloaded constructors with long args
choices.2: A deep runtime stack of nested decorator wrappers around an object
choices.3: A one-to-many mechanism that notifies observers about every change
choices.4: A sprawling switch over the current state repeated in every method

explanation: One class per state localises behaviour and transitions, replacing scattered switch(currentState) logic.

question: How does State relate to Strategy?

choices.1: State swaps its own strategy over time as it moves between states
choices.2: State is a creational pattern; Strategy is structural
choices.3: State forbids using any interface, whereas Strategy always requires one of them
choices.4: State runs only at compile time, whereas Strategy runs purely at runtime

explanation: State shares Strategy's structure but the object changes its own active state during execution.

question: What is an idiomatic, allocation-free modern C++ state machine?

choices.1: A single enum value that is switched on inside each and every method
choices.2: A std::variant of the state structs combined with a std::visit call
choices.3: A vector holding a std::function transition callback for every edge
choices.4: A CRTP base class statically parameterised on the current state type

explanation: variant + visit keeps states as values, avoids heap allocation, and makes transition handling compile-checked.

question: What is a downside of the classic State pattern?

choices.1: It quietly prevents the surrounding context object from being copied
